## audit sink

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/adapters/audit-sink

---
title: 'Log command execution with the Manifest audit sink'
description: 'Wire in an AuditSink to receive one AuditRecord per runCommand call — success or failure — for compliance, debugging, and observability.'
sidebarTitle: 'Audit Sink'
---

Every call to `RuntimeEngine.runCommand` produces a single `AuditRecord`, regardless of whether the command succeeds, is denied by a guard or policy, hits a constraint, or throws an unexpected error. You receive that record by providing an `AuditSink` in `RuntimeOptions`.

## What gets recorded

The runtime emits exactly one record per `runCommand` invocation. The record is produced in a `finally` block, so even unhandled errors produce an audit entry before propagating.

The `AuditRecord` shape, as defined in `@angriff36/manifest/audit`:

```typescript
interface AuditRecord {
  /** Stable record id. Generated via RuntimeOptions.generateId (falls back to crypto.randomUUID). */
  recordId?: string;
  /** Wall-clock timestamp — milliseconds since epoch. From RuntimeOptions.now. */
  occurredAt: number;
  /** Tenant the command ran against, when known. */
  tenantId?: string;
  /** Organization the actor belonged to (e.g. Clerk orgId). */
  orgId?: string;
  /** Acting user id. */
  actorId?: string;
  /** Caller-supplied request id for trace correlation. */
  requestId?: string;
  /** Origin surface ('route', 'job', 'cli', ...). */
  source?: string;
  /** Entity name the command targeted, when entity-scoped. */
  entity?: string;
  /** Command name. */
  command: string;
  /** Canonical "<entity>.<command>" identifier, when available. */
  commandId?: string;
  /** Outcome classification. */
  outcome: CommandOutcome;
  /** Structured runtime diagnostics, if any. */
  diagnostics?: unknown;
  /** Names of events emitted by the command, when the outcome was success. */
  emittedEventNames?: string[];
  /** IR provenance hash at execution time, for drift detection. */
  irHash?: string;
}
```

### Outcome values

The `outcome` field is one of:

| Value                    | Meaning                                                      |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `success`                | Command completed; state was mutated and events emitted.     |
| `guard_denied`           | A guard expression evaluated to false.                       |
| `policy_denied`          | A policy check rejected the command.                         |
| `rate_limit_denied`      | A command or policy rate limiter blocked execution.          |
| `constraint_failed`      | One or more constraint validations failed.                   |
| `concurrency_conflict`   | Optimistic concurrency check failed.                         |
| `missing_tenant_context` | The command required a tenant context that was not supplied. |
| `error`                  | An unexpected error occurred during evaluation.              |

<Note>
  `diagnostics` carries the structured failure details — `policyDenial`, `guardFailure`,
  `concurrencyConflict`, `constraintOutcomes`, or `error` — so downstream tooling can reason about a
  denial without reshaping the runtime contract.
</Note>

## First-party sinks

Two sinks ship with the package:

<CodeGroup>

```typescript MemoryAuditSink (development)
import { MemoryAuditSink } from '@angriff36/manifest/audit/memory';

const sink = new MemoryAuditSink();

// Retrieve records after commands run:
const records = sink.getRecords();
```

```typescript PostgresAuditSink (production)
import { PostgresAuditSink } from '@angriff36/manifest/audit/postgres';
import { Pool } from 'pg';

const pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });
const sink = new PostgresAuditSink({ pool });
```

</CodeGroup>

`MemoryAuditSink` is idempotent by `recordId` — re-emitting a record with the same `recordId` does not create a duplicate. Use it in tests and sample applications.

`PostgresAuditSink` is backed by `pg` and uses `INSERT … ON CONFLICT (record_id) DO NOTHING` for idempotency. Apply the included schema before using it:

<Tip>
  The schema file `postgres.sql` is included in the published package. Find it at
  `node_modules/@angriff36/manifest/src/manifest/audit/sinks/postgres.sql` and run it against your
  database once during deployment.
</Tip>

## Wiring in an audit sink

Pass your sink via `RuntimeOptions.auditSink`:

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { PostgresAuditSink } from '@angriff36/manifest/audit/postgres';
import { Pool } from 'pg';

const pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { actorId: session.userId, tenantId: session.tenantId },
  { auditSink: new PostgresAuditSink({ pool }) },
);

// Every runtime.runCommand(...) call now writes one AuditRecord.
const result = await runtime.runCommand('createOrder', input, {
  entityName: 'Order',
  instanceId: orderId,
});
```

> **Correction (2026-07-22) @RYANSIGNED:** The `RuntimeEngine` constructor is
> `(ir, context, options)`. `auditSink` is a `RuntimeOptions` field and must go in
> the **options** (3rd) arg, not `context`. Passing it in `context` typechecks —
> `RuntimeContext` has an open `[key: string]` index signature — but the engine
> only reads `this.options.auditSink`, so the sink is silently never wired and no
> `AuditRecord` is written. Identity (`actorId`/`tenantId`) stays in `context`.

## Import paths

| What                                         | Import path                          |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `AuditSink` interface and `AuditRecord` type | `@angriff36/manifest/audit`          |
| `MemoryAuditSink`                            | `@angriff36/manifest/audit/memory`   |
| `PostgresAuditSink`                          | `@angriff36/manifest/audit/postgres` |

## Implementing a custom sink

You can implement `AuditSink` against any backend — OpenTelemetry, a file logger, a remote webhook, or your own table schema:

```typescript
import type { AuditSink, AuditRecord } from '@angriff36/manifest/audit';

export class OpenTelemetryAuditSink implements AuditSink {
  async emit(record: AuditRecord): Promise<void> {
    // Write to your observability backend
    telemetry.track('manifest.command', {
      outcome: record.outcome,
      entity: record.entity,
      command: record.command,
      actorId: record.actorId,
      tenantId: record.tenantId,
    });
  }
}
```

Sinks **must** be idempotent against `recordId` when one is supplied, so retries and at-least-once delivery from upstream workers do not double-write.

## Failure policy: fail-open

<Warning>
If `AuditSink.emit` throws, the runtime catches the error, logs it to `stderr`, and **continues normally**. The command result returned to your caller is not affected.

The audit subsystem is observability, not enforcement. Monitor for the log line:

```
[Manifest Runtime] AuditSink.emit failed
```

Alert on it in production so you know when audit records are being lost.

</Warning>


---

## custom stores

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/adapters/custom-stores

---
title: 'Connect Manifest to your database with custom stores'
description: 'Implement the Store interface and provide it via storeProvider to persist entity state in any database — Postgres, Prisma, Drizzle, or your own backend.'
sidebarTitle: 'Custom Stores'
---

By default, `RuntimeEngine` stores entity state in memory. That works for tests and local development, but production applications need durable storage. Manifest exposes the `Store` interface and a `storeProvider` hook so you can plug in any backend without changing your domain model.

## The Store interface

Every custom store must implement the following interface, exported from `@angriff36/manifest`:

```typescript
interface Store<T extends EntityInstance = EntityInstance> {
  getAll(): Promise<T[]>;
  getById(id: string): Promise<T | undefined>;
  create(data: Partial<T>, tx?: TransactionHandle): Promise<T>;
  update(id: string, data: Partial<T>, tx?: TransactionHandle): Promise<T | undefined>;
  delete(id: string, tx?: TransactionHandle): Promise<boolean>;
  clear(): Promise<void>;
}
```

Each method maps directly to a runtime operation:

| Method    | When the runtime calls it                        |
| --------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| `getAll`  | Hydrating entity collections                     |
| `getById` | Loading an instance before a command runs        |
| `create`  | Persisting a new entity instance                 |
| `update`  | Writing state mutations after a command succeeds |
| `delete`  | Removing an instance                             |
| `clear`   | Resetting all instances (typically in tests)     |

## Wiring in a custom store

Pass a `storeProvider` function in `RuntimeOptions` when you construct
`RuntimeEngine`. The function receives the entity name and returns either your
store or `undefined`. `undefined` uses built-in behavior only for `memory` and
browser `localStorage`; durable and custom targets fail instead of falling back
to memory.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { MyCustomStore } from './my-custom-store';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { actorId: 'user-123', tenantId: 'tenant-456' },
  {
    storeProvider: (entityName) =>
      entityName === 'Recipe' ? new MyCustomStore({/* config */}) : undefined,
  },
);
```

Generated runtime companions can load the same bindings from
`manifest.config.ts` through `runtimeConfigImport`. See the
[complete PostgreSQL setup](/language/stores#complete-generated-companion-postgresql-setup).

## Complete example: Prisma store

Here is a full implementation backed by Prisma. Adapt the delegate type and field mapping to match your schema.

```typescript
import type { Store, EntityInstance } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import type { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

export class PrismaStore<T extends EntityInstance> implements Store<T> {
  constructor(
    private readonly prisma: PrismaClient,
    private readonly model: string,
  ) {}

  private get delegate(): any {
    return (this.prisma as any)[this.model];
  }

  async getAll(): Promise<T[]> {
    return this.delegate.findMany();
  }

  async getById(id: string): Promise<T | undefined> {
    const record = await this.delegate.findUnique({ where: { id } });
    return record ?? undefined;
  }

  async create(data: Partial<T>): Promise<T> {
    return this.delegate.create({ data });
  }

  async update(id: string, data: Partial<T>): Promise<T | undefined> {
    try {
      return await this.delegate.update({ where: { id }, data });
    } catch {
      return undefined;
    }
  }

  async delete(id: string): Promise<boolean> {
    try {
      await this.delegate.delete({ where: { id } });
      return true;
    } catch {
      return false;
    }
  }

  async clear(): Promise<void> {
    await this.delegate.deleteMany();
  }
}
```

Wire it in alongside `RuntimeEngine`:

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
import { PrismaStore } from './prisma-store';

const prisma = new PrismaClient();

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { actorId: session.userId, tenantId: session.tenantId },
  {
    storeProvider: (entityName) => {
      switch (entityName) {
        case 'Recipe':
          return new PrismaStore(prisma, 'recipe');
        case 'Order':
          return new PrismaStore(prisma, 'order');
        default:
          return undefined;
      }
    },
  },
);
```

## Built-in storage targets

The runtime ships with first-party support for the following targets:

| Target          | Notes                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| --------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `memory`        | Default. In-process; state is lost on restart.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| `postgres`      | Server-side only. Uses `pg`. Import from `@angriff36/manifest/stores`.                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| `supabase`      | Server-side only. Requires the optional `@supabase/supabase-js` peer dependency. Import from `@angriff36/manifest/stores`.                                                                                                                                                               |
| `localStorage`  | Browser only.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| `dynamodb`      | AWS DynamoDB. Single-table design with composite keys. See [DynamoDB adapter](/adapters/dynamodb).                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| `redis`         | Redis string/JSON entity storage (`RedisStore`). ~~with pub/sub events~~ **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** store pub/sub methods throw — use `RedisEventBus` via `RuntimeOptions.eventBus`. See [Redis adapter](/adapters/redis). |
| `turso`         | Turso / libSQL edge database. See [Turso adapter](/adapters/turso).                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| `eventSourced`  | ~~`event-sourced`~~ **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** IR target string is `eventSourced` (camelCase). Still not a working runtime store — passthrough metadata only. See [Event sourcing patterns](/adapters/event-sourced-store). |

## Unsupported targets throw — there is no silent fallback

<Warning>
If you pass an unsupported storage target (for example `postgres` in a browser environment), the runtime throws a clear error identifying the unsupported target and entity. It does **not** silently fall back to memory storage.

This behavior is required by the Manifest conformance spec. Always verify your target at startup and surface configuration errors early.

</Warning>

## Practical guidance

- **Keep methods deterministic** in test environments — avoid `Date.now()` or random IDs inside the store; let the runtime supply them via `RuntimeOptions.generateId` and `RuntimeOptions.now`.
- **Enforce tenant boundaries at the storage layer** when your domain requires row-level isolation. The runtime passes `tenantId` in context; your store can filter on it.
- **Surface failures explicitly.** Methods like `update` and `delete` return `undefined` / `false` on not-found, and throw on genuine errors. Do not swallow errors silently.


---

## dynamodb

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/adapters/dynamodb

---
title: 'DynamoDB store adapter'
sidebarTitle: 'DynamoDB'
description: 'Persist Manifest entity state in DynamoDB with single-table design, composite keys, optimistic locking, and a transactional outbox — plus CloudFormation, CDK, and Terraform infrastructure projections.'
---

The DynamoDB store adapter persists Manifest entity state in AWS DynamoDB using the `Store<T>` interface. Use it when your deployment target is AWS and you need a fully managed, low-latency key-value store. The adapter supports single-table design patterns, composite primary keys, optimistic locking, and a dedicated transactional outbox store for durable event delivery.

## Configuration

> **Correction (2026-07-22 @RYANSIGNED):** The examples below have been corrected to show the accurate `DynamoDBStore` and `buildDynamoDBKey` signatures. The constructor takes `(entityName, config, generateId?)` where config uses `partitionKey`/`sortKey` (not `partitionKeyName`/`sortKeyName`). The `buildDynamoDBKey` helper takes `(id, config, entityName)` where config uses `entityPrefix` (not `prefix`).

`DynamoDBStore` is exported from `@angriff36/manifest/stores` (Node.js only). It requires the `@aws-sdk/client-dynamodb` and `@aws-sdk/lib-dynamodb` packages as optional peer dependencies, loaded via dynamic import.

```typescript
import { DynamoDBStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';

const store = new DynamoDBStore('Order', {
  tableName: 'manifest-orders',
  region: 'us-east-1',
  partitionKey: 'pk',
  sortKey: 'sk',
  entityPrefix: 'ORDER', // single-table design prefix
});
```

The store declares itself in Manifest source as:

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property total: decimal
  property status: string
}

store Order in dynamodb
```

## Single-table design

DynamoDBStore supports the single-table design pattern through the `entityPrefix` option. When configured, all entities share a single DynamoDB table and are distinguished by their partition key prefix. The `buildDynamoDBKey()` helper constructs composite keys:

```typescript
import { buildDynamoDBKey } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';

// Composite key: "ORDER#123" / "ORDER#123#DETAIL"
const key = buildDynamoDBKey('order-123', { entityPrefix: 'ORDER' }, 'Order');
```

When `entityPrefix` is set, the store uses `ConditionExpression` for atomic create (`attribute_not_exists`) and delete (`attribute_exists`) operations. Single-table design is auto-detected when `config.singleTable === true` in any store declaration.

## Composite key handling

The store maps Manifest entity IDs to DynamoDB composite primary keys. Partition and sort key names are configurable via `partitionKey` (default `pk`) and `sortKey` (default `sk`) in the store config. Entity instances are serialized as JSON strings and stored as DynamoDB attribute values.

## Optimistic locking

~~DynamoDBStore implements optimistic locking via a `version` field. On `update`, the store includes a `ConditionExpression` that checks the current version matches the expected value.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-22 @RYANSIGNED):** DynamoDBStore does NOT implement optimistic locking — the `update` method is a plain `PutCommand` with no version check. For optimistic locking with version field enforcement, use **MongoDBStore** (`@angriff36/manifest/stores`), which includes a version check in its update filter. DynamoDB atomic create uses `attribute_not_exists` and delete uses `attribute_exists` for existence checks, but there is no version-based concurrency control.

## DynamoDBOutboxStore

A `DynamoDBOutboxStore` exists in the source tree — it implements the `OutboxStore` interface using DynamoDB's `TransactWriteItems` for atomic batch enqueue (up to 100 actions), conditional `UpdateItem` for concurrent claim, and `markDelivered`/`markFailed` for state transitions — but it is **not currently a public export**. The `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/dynamodb` subpath does not resolve, and the store has no test coverage yet, so it is not published on the `exports` map.

For durable at-least-once event delivery on AWS today, use one of the **exported** outbox stores: `PostgresOutboxStore` (`@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres`) or `RedisOutboxStore` (`@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis`).

<Note>
  Unlike the doc's older example, the source `DynamoDBOutboxStore` constructor takes a single
  options object whose `client` field is **required** — it does not accept a `region`. The `client`
  is an initialized AWS SDK document client that also carries the command constructors
  (`TransactWriteCommand`, `ScanCommand`, `UpdateCommand`), matching the convention used by
  `DynamoDBStore`. This shape is documented here for reference only; the store is not importable
  from the package until it is exported.
</Note>

## Infrastructure projections

The DynamoDB adapter includes a dedicated projection that generates infrastructure definitions from Manifest IR. The `DynamoDBProjection` is registered under the name `dynamodb` and emits three surfaces:

- `dynamodb.cloudformation` — CloudFormation YAML template with table definitions, key schema, attribute definitions, billing mode, and stream specification.
- `dynamodb.cdk` — AWS CDK TypeScript code with `dynamodb.Table` constructs.
- `dynamodb.terraform` — Terraform HCL with `aws_dynamodb_table` resources.

All three surfaces support per-entity and single-table designs, and always emit an outbox table.

```ts
import { getProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections';

const projection = getProjection('dynamodb');
const cfn = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'dynamodb.cloudformation' });
```

## Wiring in DynamoDB

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { DynamoDBStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';
import { PostgresOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres';
import { Pool } from 'pg';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { actorId: session.userId, tenantId: session.tenantId },
  {
    storeProvider: (entityName) =>
      entityName === 'Order'
        ? new DynamoDBStore('Order', { tableName: 'manifest-data', region: 'us-east-1' })
        : undefined,
    // The DynamoDB outbox store is not yet an exported subpath; use an exported
    // outbox store (Postgres or Redis) for durable delivery today.
    outboxStore: new PostgresOutboxStore(new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL })),
  },
);
```

## Import paths

| What                      | Import path                                                                               |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `DynamoDBStore` class     | `@angriff36/manifest/stores`                                                              |
| `buildDynamoDBKey` helper | `@angriff36/manifest/stores`                                                              |
| `DynamoDBOutboxStore`     | not exported (source-only) — use `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres` or `/outbox/redis` |
| `DynamoDBProjection`      | `@angriff36/manifest/projections`                                                         |

## Notes & limitations

<Note>
  The `@aws-sdk/client-dynamodb` and `@aws-sdk/lib-dynamodb` packages are optional peer dependencies
  loaded via dynamic import. If they are not installed, the constructor throws a clear error:
  "DynamoDBStore requires '@aws-sdk/client-dynamodb' and '@aws-sdk/lib-dynamodb' to be installed."
  The `clear()` method uses Scan + BatchWrite in groups of 25 (DynamoDB batch limit).
</Note>


---

## event sourced store

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/adapters/event-sourced-store

---
title: 'Event Sourced Store'
description: 'Append-only event log store with snapshotting for Manifest entities. Ships in the reference runtime — auto-wired for `store Entity in eventSourced { ... }` declarations.'
sidebarTitle: 'Event Sourced Store'
---

The `EventSourcedStore` is an in-memory `Store<T>` implementation that persists entity state as an append-only event log. Each `create`/`update`/`delete` operation appends an event to the log, and the current state is maintained by replaying events from the last snapshot.

This store **ships** in the reference runtime (`@angriff36/manifest/runtime-engine`) and is auto-wired for any entity declared with `store Entity in eventSourced { ... }`.

## How it works

1. **Events append to a log** — Each write operation creates an event (`create`/`update`/`delete`) with a sequence number and timestamp.
2. **State is projected** — The current entity state is maintained in memory by applying all events from the log.
3. **Snapshots** — Periodically (default: every 100 events), the current state is snapshotted. Replay starts from the last snapshot instead of the beginning.
4. **Optional event log access** — When `exposeEventLog: true`, `getEventLog(id)` returns the full append-only log for an entity.

## Declaration

```manifest
store Order in eventSourced {
  snapshotInterval: 100    // Take a snapshot every N events (default: 100)
  exposeEventLog: true     // Allow getEventLog() to return the event log (default: false)
}
```

## Usage in TypeScript

The `EventSourcedStore` class is exported from `@angriff36/manifest/stores/event-sourced`. You rarely instantiate it directly — the runtime auto-wires it based on the IR store declaration. For custom instantiation:

```typescript
import { EventSourcedStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores/event-sourced';

const store = new EventSourcedStore<Order>({
  snapshotInterval: 100,
  exposeEventLog: true,
  generateId: () => crypto.randomUUID(),
  now: () => Date.now(),
});

// Standard Store interface
await store.create({ id: 'order-1', total: 100 });
await store.update('order-1', { total: 150 });
await store.delete('order-1');
const order = await store.getById('order-1');
const all = await store.getAll();

// Event-sourced specific methods
const events = store.getEventLog('order-1');  // EventSourcedEvent[] | undefined
const snapshot = store.getSnapshot('order-1'); // EventSourcedSnapshot<Order> | undefined
```

## Event structure

Each event in the log has:

| Field    | Type                               | Description                                  |
| -------- | ---------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| sequence | number                             | Monotonically increasing event number      |
| kind     | `'create'` `'update'` `'delete'`     | Operation type                               |
| at       | number                             | Timestamp (milliseconds since epoch)        |
| payload  | `Record<string, unknown>` \| undefined | For `update` events, the changed fields |

## Snapshot structure

| Field    | Type   | Description                             |
| -------- | ------ | --------------------------------------- |
| sequence | number | Sequence number at snapshot time        |
| state    | T      | Complete entity state at that sequence |

## Limitations

- **In-memory only** — The event log and projected state live in process memory and are lost on restart. For durable event sourcing, use the outbox/audit sinks or build a custom adapter.
- **No distributed replay** — Each process maintains its own log; for multi-process scenarios, use an external event store.

## Related

- [Events and reactions](/language/events) — Event-driven architecture in Manifest
- [Outbox](/adapters/outbox) — Durable, at-least-once event delivery
- [Audit Sink](/adapters/audit-sink) — Compliance-grade command history

Package pin: `@angriff36/manifest@3.6.41+` (FULLY_IMPLEMENTED per `COMPLIANCE_MATRIX.md:133`)


---

## outbox

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/adapters/outbox

---
title: 'Durable event delivery with the Manifest outbox'
description: 'Use OutboxStore to persist emitted events for at-least-once delivery. Covers the enqueue/claim/markDelivered lifecycle, crash recovery, and known limitations.'
sidebarTitle: 'Outbox Store'
---

When a command emits one or more events, you often need to deliver those events to downstream consumers reliably — even if your process crashes immediately after the command succeeds. The `OutboxStore` adapter gives you a durable queue for emitted events that a background worker can drain with at-least-once delivery guarantees.

## How the outbox works

After a successful `runCommand` call that emits events, the runtime calls `outboxStore.enqueue(entries)` once, batching all events from that invocation into a single call. A separate delivery worker then polls the store using `claim`, delivers the events, and calls `markDelivered` or `markFailed`.

```
runCommand → success → enqueue(entries)
                            │
              ┌─────────────▼─────────────┐
              │      OutboxStore          │
              │  pending entries queue    │
              └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                            │
              delivery worker: claim → deliver → markDelivered
```

## The OutboxStore interface

The full interface, from `@angriff36/manifest/outbox`:

```typescript
interface OutboxStore {
  /**
   * Enqueue one or more outbox entries. Called by the runtime after a
   * successful command. Pass a tx handle to participate in the caller's
   * transaction; without one, enqueue runs independently (best-effort).
   */
  enqueue(entries: OutboxEntry[], tx?: unknown): Promise<void>;

  /**
   * Claim up to batchSize pending entries for delivery. Durable
   * implementations should use SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED so
   * concurrent workers receive disjoint batches.
   */
  claim(batchSize: number): Promise<OutboxEntry[]>;

  /** Mark entries as successfully delivered. */
  markDelivered(entryIds: string[]): Promise<void>;

  /** Mark entries as failed with a reason. */
  markFailed(entryIds: string[], error: string): Promise<void>;
}
```

## The OutboxEntry shape

Each entry the runtime enqueues has the following shape:

```typescript
interface OutboxEntry {
  /** Stable id assigned at enqueue time. Use this as your dedup token. */
  entryId: string;
  /** Timestamp the entry was enqueued (ms since epoch). */
  enqueuedAt: number;
  /** The full EmittedEvent payload from the command. */
  event: EmittedEvent;
  /** Current status: 'pending' | 'delivered' | 'failed'. */
  status: OutboxEntryStatus;
  /** Number of times this entry has been claimed. */
  attempts: number;
  /** Last error message, present when status === 'failed'. */
  lastError?: string;
}
```

## Delivery semantics: at-least-once

<Warning>
The outbox provides **at-least-once** delivery, not exactly-once. Your consumers **must be idempotent**.

A worker that delivers an event and then crashes before calling `markDelivered` will redeliver the same entry after stale-claim recovery. Use `entryId` — or a domain-level idempotency key — as a dedup token in every consumer.

</Warning>

The three reasons duplication can occur:

1. A worker claims an entry, delivers it, crashes before `markDelivered` runs, and `releaseStaleClaims` recovers the entry for redelivery.
2. `releaseStaleClaims` is called while the original worker is still alive, producing a second concurrent delivery.
3. Network retries from the consumer side can produce repeat reads within a single worker.

## First-party stores

<CodeGroup>

```typescript MemoryOutboxStore (development / tests)
import { MemoryOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/memory';

const outboxStore = new MemoryOutboxStore();
```

```typescript PostgresOutboxStore (production)
import { PostgresOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres';
import { Pool } from 'pg';

const pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });
const outboxStore = new PostgresOutboxStore({ pool });
```

```typescript RedisOutboxStore (Redis Streams)
import { RedisOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis';

const outboxStore = new RedisOutboxStore({
  url: process.env.REDIS_URL,
});
```

</CodeGroup>

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** First-party outbox stores also include
> `RedisOutboxStore` (`@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis`) — see [Redis adapter](/adapters/redis).

`MemoryOutboxStore` is an in-memory analogue of `SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` — `claim` skips entries that are already claimed or resolved. Use it in tests.

`PostgresOutboxStore` uses `SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` combined with a `claimed_at IS NULL` filter so concurrent workers receive disjoint batches. Apply the schema before first use:

<Tip>
  The schema file is included in the published package at
  `node_modules/@angriff36/manifest/src/manifest/outbox/stores/postgres.sql`. Run it against your
  database once during deployment.
</Tip>

## Wiring in an outbox store

Pass your store via `RuntimeOptions.outboxStore`:

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { PostgresOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres';
import { Pool } from 'pg';

const pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { actorId: session.userId, tenantId: session.tenantId },
  { outboxStore: new PostgresOutboxStore({ pool }) },
);

// After a successful command, entries are automatically enqueued.
const result = await runtime.runCommand('placeOrder', input, {
  entityName: 'Order',
  instanceId: orderId,
});
```

> **Correction (2026-07-22) @RYANSIGNED:** `outboxStore` is a `RuntimeOptions`
> field — put it in the **options** (3rd) arg of `new RuntimeEngine(ir, context,
> options)`, not in `context`. In `context` it typechecks (the open index
> signature) but is never read, so outbox entries are silently never enqueued.

Then run the shipped delivery worker. `runOutboxWorker` owns the
claim/deliver/mark loop — you supply only the `deliver` closure that publishes
each entry:

```typescript
import { runOutboxWorker } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/worker';

const worker = runOutboxWorker(store, async (entry) => {
  // Reject to have the entry marked failed with this error; resolve to have
  // it marked delivered. Delivery is at-least-once, so keep this idempotent.
  await publishToEventBus(entry.event);
});

// On shutdown, stop the loop and wait for the in-flight batch to settle.
await worker.stop();
```

Options tune batching and back-off: `batchSize` (default 100), `pollIntervalMs`
(default 1000, the wait between empty polls), an `onError` callback for
store/infrastructure failures, and a `signal: AbortSignal` as an alternative to
`stop()`. For a serverless or cron-driven deployment that wants a single pass
instead of a long-lived loop, call `drainOutboxOnce(store, deliver, { batchSize })`.

`runOutboxWorker` is safe to run in multiple replicas against `PostgresOutboxStore`:
its `claim` uses `SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`, so each worker gets a
disjoint batch.

## Crash recovery

If a worker claims entries and then crashes before calling `markDelivered` or `markFailed`, those rows are left with `status='pending'` but a non-null `claimed_at`. The `claim` method will not return them again (the `claimed_at IS NULL` filter excludes them).

Call `releaseStaleClaims(entryIds)` on `PostgresOutboxStore` to reset those entries so a follow-up `claim` picks them up:

```typescript
// Only call this after you are certain the original worker is dead.
await outboxStore.releaseStaleClaims(staleEntryIds);
```

<Warning>
  Only release claims when you are certain the original worker is no longer running. Releasing a
  claim held by a live worker produces a second delivery. The `attempts` counter increments on every
  successful claim, so you can use it to bound retries and dead-letter entries that exceed a
  threshold.
</Warning>

## Transaction boundary

By default — with no `transactionProvider` configured — `RuntimeEngine` does **not** share a transaction boundary between entity state mutation and outbox enqueue. The `enqueue` call runs on a separate database connection from the entity `update` inside `_executeCommandInternal`. In that mode, if the entity mutation succeeds but `outboxStore.enqueue` fails, the state change is persisted without a durable outbox row. The runtime logs the failure to `stderr` as `[Manifest Runtime] OutboxStore.enqueue failed`, and the `CommandResult` is not affected — the command still returns success to your caller.

<Note>
**Recommended: engine-managed atomicity.** Set a `transactionProvider` in `RuntimeOptions` and the engine wraps each command attempt — command body, its outbox enqueue, and its idempotency record — in a single `withTransaction` call, so those writes commit or roll back together. A failed enqueue now rolls the mutation back and surfaces `OUTBOX_ENQUEUE_FAILED` instead of silently dropping the event.

```typescript
import { Pool } from 'pg';
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { PostgresOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres';
import { PostgresTransactionProvider } from '@angriff36/manifest/transactions/postgres';

const pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, context, {
  outboxStore: new PostgresOutboxStore({ pool }),
  transactionProvider: new PostgresTransactionProvider({ pool }),
});
```

</Note>

**Adapter-level alternative:** `PostgresOutboxStore.enqueue(entries, tx)` also accepts a caller-supplied `PoolClient`. If you open your own transaction and pass the same client to both a `PostgresStore` write and `PostgresOutboxStore.enqueue(entries, sameClient)`, you get atomicity without a provider. Use this when you already manage transactions yourself.

## Import paths

| What                                           | Import path                           |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| `OutboxStore` interface and `OutboxEntry` type | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox`          |
| `MemoryOutboxStore`                            | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/memory`   |
| `PostgresOutboxStore`                          | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres` |

## Failure policy: fail-open

Like the audit sink, `OutboxStore.enqueue` errors are caught and logged to `stderr`. They do not alter `CommandResult` or block the caller. Monitor for:

```
[Manifest Runtime] OutboxStore.enqueue failed
```

Alert on this line in production if your application depends on durable event delivery.


---

## redis

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/adapters/redis

---
title: 'Redis store adapter'
sidebarTitle: 'Redis'
description: 'Persist Manifest entity state in Redis with key-based JSON storage, TTL management, and pub/sub event propagation.'
---

The Redis store adapter persists Manifest entity state in Redis using the `Store<T>` interface. Use it when you need high-throughput, low-latency entity storage with built-in TTL management and native pub/sub for event propagation. The adapter loads the `ioredis` library lazily as an optional dependency.

## Configuration

`RedisStore` is exported from `@angriff36/manifest/stores` (Node.js only). The constructor takes the **entity name** as its first argument, followed by a `RedisConfig` object.

```typescript
import { RedisStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';

const store = new RedisStore<User>('User', {
  url: 'redis://localhost:6379',
  keyPrefix: 'manifest:', // defaults to `<entityName>:` lowercased
  defaultTTL: 3600, // 1 hour in seconds (optional)
});
```

`RedisConfig` exposes exactly three optional fields: `url`, `keyPrefix`, and `defaultTTL`. When `keyPrefix` is omitted it defaults to the lowercased entity name followed by a colon (for example, `user:`).

The store declares itself in Manifest source as:

```manifest
entity Session {
  property required id: string
  property userId: string
  property data: string
}

store Session in redis
```

## Key naming conventions

Each entity instance is stored as its own Redis string key holding a JSON-serialized value. The key pattern is `<keyPrefix><id>`. With `keyPrefix: 'manifest:'`, an entity with ID `user-123` is stored at key `manifest:user-123`. Collection reads use `KEYS <keyPrefix>*` followed by `MGET`.

## Key-based JSON storage

`RedisStore` implements the `Store<T>` interface using Redis string commands:

- `getAll()` runs `KEYS <keyPrefix>*` then `MGET`, deserializing each JSON value.
- `getById(id)` runs `GET` on `<keyPrefix><id>`.
- `create(data)` assigns an ID (or uses `data.id`) and runs `SET` with the serialized entity, then `EXPIRE` when `defaultTTL` is set.
- `update(id, data)` reads the existing value, merges the partial data, and `SET`s it back.
- `delete(id)` runs `DEL` on the key.
- `clear()` runs `KEYS <keyPrefix>*` then `DEL` on the matched keys.

## TTL management

`RedisStore` supports automatic entity expiration through Redis TTL:

```typescript
// Set TTL at store construction (applies to newly created keys)
const store = new RedisStore('User', { url: 'redis://localhost:6379', defaultTTL: 3600 });

// Manage TTL at runtime (operates across the store's keyPrefix namespace)
await store.setTTL(7200); // Set a 2-hour TTL on this store's keys
await store.setTTL(undefined); // PERSIST — remove TTL
const ttl = await store.getTTL(); // Remaining TTL in seconds for the namespace
```

When `defaultTTL` is configured, newly created entity keys receive the TTL automatically via `EXPIRE`.

## Pub/Sub — use `RedisEventBus`, not `RedisStore`

~~`RedisStore` includes pub/sub helpers for event propagation:~~

~~```typescript
await store.publishEvent('OrderCreated', { orderId: 'order-123', total: 99.99 });
await store.subscribe('OrderCreated', (event) => { ... });
```~~

~~Treat the pub/sub helpers as a building block, not a turnkey event bus.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** `RedisStore.publishEvent` and `RedisStore.subscribe`
> were silent no-ops and now **throw**, pointing callers at `RedisEventBus`. Cross-process event
> fan-out is `RuntimeOptions.eventBus` + `RedisEventBus` from `@angriff36/manifest/events/redis`
> (see below). There is no auto-construct from env — wire the bus yourself.

## `RedisEventBus` (cross-process fan-out)

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { RedisEventBus } from '@angriff36/manifest/events/redis';

const eventBus = new RedisEventBus({
  url: process.env.REDIS_URL, // default redis://localhost:6379
  channel: 'manifest:events', // default
});

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, context, { eventBus });
await runtime.connectEventBus(); // subscribe this process to remote envelopes
```

`RedisEventBus` implements the same `EventBus` contract as `MemoryEventBus`
(`@angriff36/manifest/events`). Optional peer: `ioredis`. Inject `client` in tests to avoid loading
ioredis. Call `close()` to quit bus-owned connections.

## Outbox delivery

`RedisOutboxStore` is a first-party Redis Streams implementation of the `OutboxStore` contract, exported from `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis`. It uses Redis Streams (`XADD` / `XREADGROUP` / `XACK`) with a consumer group so multiple dispatcher workers can drain the outbox in parallel.

```typescript
import { RedisOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis';

const outboxStore = new RedisOutboxStore({
  url: process.env.REDIS_URL,
  keyPrefix: 'manifest:outbox:', // default
  consumerGroup: 'manifest-dispatcher', // default
});
```

Like `RedisStore`, it loads `ioredis` lazily as an optional peer dependency and throws a clear error if the package is not installed. The `enqueue` method accepts an optional Redis pipeline for batching event persistence alongside state mutations.

The package `exports` map exposes `./outbox` (the `OutboxStore` / `OutboxEntry` contract) plus the concrete stores:

| Outbox store          | Import path                           |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| `MemoryOutboxStore`   | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/memory`   |
| `PostgresOutboxStore` | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres` |
| `RedisOutboxStore`    | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis`    |

## Wiring in Redis

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { RedisStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';
import { RedisOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { actorId: session.userId, tenantId: session.tenantId },
  {
    storeProvider: (entityName) =>
      entityName === 'Session'
        ? new RedisStore(entityName, { url: process.env.REDIS_URL, defaultTTL: 3600 })
        : undefined,
    // Durable at-least-once event delivery via the Redis Streams outbox:
    outboxStore: new RedisOutboxStore({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL }),
  },
);
```

## Import paths

| What                                     | Import path                           |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| `RedisStore` class and `RedisConfig`     | `@angriff36/manifest/stores`          |
| `RedisEventBus`                          | `@angriff36/manifest/events/redis`    |
| `MemoryEventBus`                         | `@angriff36/manifest/events`          |
| `RedisOutboxStore`                       | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis`    |
| `MemoryOutboxStore`                      | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/memory`   |
| `PostgresOutboxStore`                    | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres` |
| `OutboxStore` / `OutboxEntry` interfaces | `@angriff36/manifest/outbox`          |

## Notes & limitations

<Note>
  `ioredis` is loaded lazily and is optional for `RedisStore`, `RedisOutboxStore`, and
  `RedisEventBus`. If it is not installed, those adapters throw a clear install hint. Prefer TLS
  (`rediss://`) in production. Package pin for the main SDK: `@angriff36/manifest@3.6.41`.
</Note>


---

## turso

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/adapters/turso

---
title: 'Turso / libSQL store adapter'
description: "Persist entity state in Turso's edge-optimized libSQL database with JSON blob rows and optional schema DDL helper."
sidebarTitle: 'Turso'
---

The Turso adapter persists entity state in a [Turso](https://turso.tech/) edge database using libSQL. It implements the `Store<T>` interface and stores each entity instance as a JSON blob in a single table.

## Configuration

`TursoStore` is exported from `@angriff36/manifest/stores` (Node.js only). Optional peer: `@libsql/client`.

```typescript
import { TursoStore, generateTursoSchema } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';

const store = new TursoStore({
  url: process.env.TURSO_DATABASE_URL!,
  authToken: process.env.TURSO_AUTH_TOKEN, // optional for local SQLite
  tableName: 'entities', // default
});
```

### Options

| Option      | Required | Description                                                              |
| ----------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `url`       | Yes      | Turso / libSQL URL (e.g. `libsql://…` or a local file URL)               |
| `authToken` | No       | Auth token (optional for local SQLite)                                   |
| `tableName` | No       | Single table for JSON blobs (default `entities`)                         |
| `client`    | No       | Pre-built LibSQL client (tests / custom setups)                          |

~~`schemaPrefix`~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** There is no `schemaPrefix` option. Use `tableName`.

## Wiring into the runtime

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { TursoStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { user: { id: 'user-1' } },
  {
    storeProvider: (entityName) =>
      new TursoStore({
        url: process.env.TURSO_DATABASE_URL!,
        authToken: process.env.TURSO_AUTH_TOKEN,
        tableName: entityName.toLowerCase(),
      }),
  },
);
```

## Schema helper (not a CLI projection)

~~```bash
manifest generate ir/ --projection turso --output schema/
```~~

~~Per-entity columnar DDL like `CREATE TABLE manifest_Order (id, status, total)`~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED — Documentation gap:** There is **no** `turso`
> projection in `listBuiltinProjections()`. Schema is a JSON-blob table produced by
> `generateTursoSchema(tableName)` (or automatically on first use via the store). Apply DDL in
> your app — there is no `manifest generate --projection turso`.

```typescript
import { generateTursoSchema } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';

const ddl = generateTursoSchema('entities');
// CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS "entities" (
//   id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
//   data TEXT NOT NULL,
//   created_at TEXT DEFAULT (...),
//   updated_at TEXT DEFAULT (...)
// );
```

## Edge deployment

- Turso databases are replicated to edge locations for low-latency reads
- Writes go to the primary and propagate to replicas
- Connection pooling is handled by `@libsql/client`

<Note>
  Install the peer: `npm install @libsql/client`. Package pin: `@angriff36/manifest@3.6.41`.
</Note>


---

## workspaces

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/concepts/workspaces

---
title: 'Documents, folders, and cloud projects'
sidebarTitle: 'Workspaces'
description: 'Learn how Builder handles single Manifest documents, local multi-file workspaces, drafts, templates, and cloud projects.'
---

Builder has three authoring scopes. Choose the narrowest one that matches your source layout.

## Single document

A single document is one editor buffer. You can create it from a built-in template, import a `.manifest` or plain-text file, drag a file into the app, or download the current source.

When the browser grants a writable file handle, Builder can save changes back to that file. Permission is always browser-controlled.

## Local folder workspace

**Open Manifest Folder** loads the `.manifest` files in a selected directory tree. Builder creates a logical workspace with:

- normalized relative paths;
- an active source file;
- a combined project compilation across all files;
- dirty-path tracking;
- direct saves back to granted file handles;
- navigation from cross-file diagnostics to the relevant source.

`use` directives resolve against the workspace file map. Builder rejects path traversal shapes such as `.` and `..` and ignores non-Manifest files during workspace discovery.

<Note>
  Folder access depends on the File System Access API. When directory iteration is unavailable,
  Builder can use a `manifest-workspace.txt` index supplied by the selected workspace.
</Note>

## Cloud projects

With Supabase configured and a user signed in, Builder can save named projects, keep revisions, create share links, and synchronize queued offline saves. If the cloud copy changed while you were offline, Builder presents a conflict instead of silently replacing either version.

Cloud features are optional. Local compilation and generation do not depend on them.

## Drafts and templates

Builder keeps recovery drafts in browser storage. On restart, you can restore or discard the pending draft.

User templates are also stored locally, separately from projects and drafts. Template JSON includes a kind and version marker; Builder validates the file before importing it.

## Saving safely

- Save all dirty workspace paths before closing a folder.
- Resolve cloud conflicts explicitly with **Keep mine**, **Use cloud version**, or **Save copy**.
- Treat browser storage as convenience state, not your only source control.
- Keep application regeneration separate from saving Manifest source. They have different ownership rules.


---

## assemble project

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/guides/assemble-project

---
title: 'Assemble a generated application'
sidebarTitle: 'Assemble a project'
description: 'Combine compatible Manifest projections into a project file map, inspect assembly diagnostics, and export only complete output.'
---

The Assemble panel combines generated projection artifacts with Builder-owned project wiring. It operates on an in-memory file map; it does not write into an existing application directory.

## Select compatible projections

Builder groups projections by category. ORM and framework selections are exclusive: selecting two ORMs or two frameworks creates an assembly conflict.

The assembler routes known outputs into conventional directories, merges generated dependency requirements, and detects artifact path collisions.

## Review the assembly report

The report separates:

- conflicts between selected projection families;
- generation errors that block export;
- warnings that require application configuration;
- informational verification checks;
- environment variable names required by selected outputs.

Generated `.env.example` files contain names only. Builder does not write actual environment values.

## Convex application preset

Selecting Convex uses the dedicated Manifest-backed Convex application preset and its assembly verification. Builder does not substitute generic multi-projection output for that preset.

Export is enabled only when the preset is complete. The output contains source and package requirements; it does not select or initialize a Convex deployment.

## Export options

When assembly is complete, you can:

- download the in-memory files as a ZIP for a new project; or
- open them in StackBlitz for temporary inspection.

<Warning>
  A ZIP export is not an ownership-aware update. Do not extract it over an existing application. Use
  the [safe regeneration workflow](/builder/guides/safe-regeneration) for existing projects.
</Warning>

## Generated authentication

Next.js assembly uses a fail-closed custom authentication seam. Implement the generated application's `lib/auth.ts` before runtime use. The generated stub is not anonymous production authentication.


---

## generate projections

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/guides/generate-projections

---
title: 'Generate and preview projections'
sidebarTitle: 'Generate projections'
description: 'Select Manifest projection surfaces in Builder, inspect generated artifacts, and understand generation diagnostics and environment limits.'
---

The Projections panel lists projections registered by the installed `@angriff36/manifest` package. Builder calls the Manifest projection API and displays returned artifacts and diagnostics.

## Preview a projection

1. Compile valid Manifest source.
2. Open **Projections**.
3. Select a projection and one of its advertised surfaces.
4. Review generated files, diagnostics, and any required options.

Builder caches projection generation by IR and request options. A model or option change invalidates the relevant cache entry.

## Projection versus assembly

A projection preview answers, “What does this Manifest surface emit?” Assembly answers, “Can these selected outputs form one coherent project?”

Assembly adds routing, collision checks, package requirements, framework wiring, and application-preset verification. Do not treat a successful projection preview as proof that a complete application is ready.

## Environment-specific tooling

Some Manifest tooling is intentionally unavailable in the browser:

- coverage commands require filesystem paths and a Node project root;
- generated test tooling requires a provider API key and a Node host;
- the MCP stdio server must run in a host process, not a browser tab.

Builder reports those host requirements instead of attempting to polyfill them.

## Projection catalog

The repository's generated `PROJECTIONS.md` is the source-backed catalog of registered projections and representative emitted surfaces. Run this check after changing the Manifest package pin:

```bash
npm run docs:projections:check
```

If it fails because the installed compiler changed, regenerate and review the catalog:

```bash
npm run docs:projections
```


---

## inspect model

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/guides/inspect-model

---
title: 'Inspect a compiled Manifest model'
sidebarTitle: 'Inspect the model'
description: 'Use Builder panels to understand entities, relationships, command flows, diagnostics, metrics, symbols, changes, and IR.'
---

Builder derives its inspection views from compiler output. Fix compiler errors before relying on projection or assembly results.

## Inspection panels

| Panel   | Use it for                                                                |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Model   | Browse compiled entities and their properties and commands.               |
| Graph   | Explore entity relationships visually.                                    |
| Flow    | Follow command, guard, mutation, and event flow.                          |
| Changes | Compare the current compiled IR with the previous successful compilation. |
| Checks  | Review compiler diagnostics and completeness findings.                    |
| Metrics | Inspect model size and complexity measurements.                           |
| Symbols | Navigate to declarations in the active source or workspace.               |
| IR      | Inspect the exact compiled intermediate representation.                   |

## Navigate from evidence

Use the Symbols panel or command palette to jump to a declaration. Workspace diagnostics include a logical path; selecting one opens the matching file before moving the editor cursor.

## Compare changes

Builder retains the previous successful IR in the current session. The Changes panel summarizes structural differences rather than comparing raw source lines. Use it to answer questions such as:

- Which entities or commands were added or removed?
- Did a property type or requiredness change?
- Which downstream projection surfaces may need regeneration?

The comparison is an authoring aid, not a database migration plan.

## Inspect generated meaning

The IR panel is the clearest boundary between Manifest and Builder. If a rule is absent from IR, Builder cannot make it authoritative by adding UI behavior. Correct the Manifest source or compiler semantics instead.

<Tip>
  When a projection looks wrong, inspect the IR first, then the projection diagnostics. This
  separates a source/compiler issue from a Builder routing or assembly issue.
</Tip>


---

## run commands

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/guides/run-commands

---
title: 'Run Manifest commands in Studio'
sidebarTitle: 'Run commands'
description: "Execute compiled commands in Builder's isolated runtime, provide inputs, and inspect state, policy failures, guard failures, and events."
---

The Run panel creates an isolated runtime session from the current compiled IR. Use it to inspect command behavior while authoring a model.

## Execution context

Studio currently creates the runtime with this fixed authoring context:

```typescript
{
  source: 'ui',
  user: { id: 'studio-user', role: 'admin' }
}
```

The panel lists entity commands and their parameters, guards, policies, and declared events. Enter parameter values and execute the command.

## Outcomes

Builder distinguishes:

- successful execution and resulting state;
- guard failures with the formatted failed condition;
- policy denials with policy name and message;
- general runtime errors;
- a two-second UI timeout.

Successful outcomes include state before and after execution when the runtime can read it, plus emitted event names and payloads.

<Warning>
  The timeout stops waiting in the UI; it does not cancel the underlying runtime promise. The
  command may still finish. Refresh the execution context before running another command after a
  timeout.
</Warning>

## What Run is not

The Studio runtime is not a production environment, database migration tool, seed runner, or deployment verifier. It does not connect to an existing Convex deployment or application database.


---

## safe regeneration

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/guides/safe-regeneration

---
title: 'Safely create or regenerate an application'
sidebarTitle: 'Safe regeneration'
description: 'Use Builder initial and update modes to create owned files, preview changes, detect conflicts, merge package requirements, and roll back failed updates.'
---

Filesystem project generation has two explicit modes. Both produce a machine-readable plan before writing.

## Initial mode

Use initial mode only for a new application directory. It may create the complete generated structure and initial package configuration.

Initial mode:

- refuses a non-empty or initialized target;
- requires `--destructive-override` before overwriting existing paths;
- writes `.builder/ownership.json` after a successful transaction;
- records a SHA-256 digest for each generated file;
- records Builder-owned dependency, development dependency, and script entries.

The ownership manifest is control metadata. Keep it in source control with the generated application.

## Update mode

Update mode requires `.builder/ownership.json`. It compares three states:

1. the previously recorded Builder-owned hash;
2. the current application file;
3. the newly generated desired content.

The resulting plan contains additions, modifications, conflicts, deletions, dependency-change status, and follow-up actions.

| Situation                                                     | Result                                    |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| New generated path does not exist                             | Addition                                  |
| Owned file still matches its recorded hash                    | Modification when desired content changed |
| Owned file contains an unrecognized edit                      | Conflict; no apply                        |
| New generated path collides with an app-owned file            | Conflict; Builder does not claim it       |
| Previously owned file is no longer generated and is unchanged | Deletion                                  |
| Previously owned stale file was edited                        | Conflict; file is preserved               |

## Transaction behavior

Builder stages desired writes and snapshots all affected target files in a sibling temporary directory. It verifies plan preconditions, applies only conflict-free plans, and restores the snapshot if any write or deletion fails.

If the target changes after planning, apply stops and asks you to create a new plan.

## Protected application state

Builder refuses to generate or delete:

- `.env` and `.env.*` files at any depth;
- `.convex` deployment state;
- `.vercel`, `.netlify`, and `.firebase` deployment identifiers;
- `package-lock.json`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `yarn.lock`, `bun.lock`, `bun.lockb`, and `npm-shrinkwrap.json`.

Application-owned routes, UI, authentication wiring, local configuration, and unrelated files are outside the ownership manifest and are never update targets.

## Package merging

`package.json` is merged by owned entry instead of replaced as a whole.

- Application-added dependencies and scripts remain.
- An unchanged Builder-owned requirement can be updated or removed.
- A Builder-owned entry changed by the application becomes a conflict when the new generated requirement differs.
- Unrelated top-level package fields remain.
- Lockfiles change only through an explicitly enabled dependency install after requirements changed.

## Installs and lifecycle scripts

`--install` has no effect unless generated dependency requirements changed. Install commands use `--ignore-scripts` by default. Add `--allow-lifecycle-scripts` only after reviewing the package changes.

## Convex follow-up

When Convex source changes, the plan reports a `convex-deploy` follow-up. Builder does not execute it. Review the generated source and deploy from the application repository using that application's established process.

<Warning>
  Builder never runs Convex deployment, schema deployment, migrations, seeds, resets, table
  clearing, environment-variable changes, or deployment initialization as part of generation.
</Warning>


---

## introduction

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/introduction

---
title: 'Builder: the visual control plane for Manifest applications'
sidebarTitle: 'Introduction'
description: 'Understand how Builder edits Manifest source, inspects compiled models, generates projections, assembles applications, and manages generated-file ownership.'
---

Builder is a browser-based studio and application-generation control plane for [Manifest](https://manifest.build). You edit one or more `.manifest` source files, compile them to Manifest IR, inspect the resulting model, generate projection artifacts, and assemble runnable application packages.

Builder does not replace Manifest. Manifest owns the language, compiler, IR, runtime semantics, and projection implementations. Builder owns the authoring environment, project orchestration, visualization, assembly presets, and generated-application lifecycle.

## What you can do

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="Author and compile" icon="code" href="/builder/concepts/workspaces">
    Edit a single document or open a folder of related Manifest source files with live compiler
    diagnostics.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Inspect the model" icon="diagram-project" href="/builder/guides/inspect-model">
    Browse entities, relationships, flows, symbols, metrics, changes, and compiled IR.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Generate projections" icon="layers" href="/builder/guides/generate-projections">
    Preview registered Manifest projections and their emitted source artifacts.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Assemble applications" icon="box" href="/builder/guides/assemble-project">
    Combine compatible projections with Builder-owned project wiring and package metadata.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Regenerate safely" icon="rotate" href="/builder/guides/safe-regeneration">
    Update only recognized Builder-owned files with conflict detection, package merging, and
    rollback.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Run commands" icon="play" href="/builder/guides/run-commands">
    Exercise compiled commands against an isolated Studio runtime and inspect state and events.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>

## The core workflow

```text
.manifest source
      |
      v
Manifest compiler -> IR + diagnostics
      |                    |
      |                    +-> graphs, checks, flows, metrics, symbols
      v
Manifest projections -> generated source artifacts
      |
      v
Builder assembly -> project file map + assembly report
      |
      v
initial build or ownership-aware update
```

<Info>
  Builder can run locally without a backend. Supabase configuration is optional and enables sign-in,
  cloud project synchronization, share links, and the authenticated AI edge function.
</Info>

## Safety boundary

Assembly creates a desired file map. Filesystem project generation is a separate operation with two explicit modes:

- **Initial** creates a new application and records every generated file in `.builder/ownership.json`.
- **Update** requires that ownership manifest, previews the plan, and changes only recognized Builder-owned files.

Builder never implicitly deploys Convex, initializes a deployment, changes environment variables, runs migrations or seeds, or clears application data.

## Next steps

- [Run Builder locally](/builder/quickstart)
- [Learn the workspace model](/builder/concepts/workspaces)
- [Understand safe regeneration](/builder/guides/safe-regeneration)


---

## quickstart

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/quickstart

---
title: 'Run Builder locally'
sidebarTitle: 'Quickstart'
description: 'Install Builder, start the local Vite application, open Manifest source, and inspect the compiled model.'
---

You can use Builder's local authoring, compilation, workspace, inspection, projection, assembly, and runtime features without configuring a cloud backend.

## Prerequisites

- Node.js 22 or later
- npm 10 or later
- A modern Chromium-based browser for folder access through the File System Access API

<Steps>
  <Step title="Clone and install">

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Angriff36/builder.git
cd builder
npm install
```

  </Step>
  <Step title="Start the development server">

```bash
npm run dev
```

Open the local URL printed by Vite, normally `http://localhost:5173`.

  </Step>
  <Step title="Create or open source">

Use **New document** to start from an empty editor, open a `.manifest` file, or choose **Open Manifest Folder** to load a multi-file workspace.

The editor compiles as you work. The header reports compiler state, and diagnostics appear in the editor and Checks panel.

  </Step>
  <Step title="Inspect the compiled model">

Open the right panel and switch between Model, Graph, Flow, Checks, Metrics, Symbols, and IR. Projection and assembly actions remain blocked while compilation has errors.

  </Step>
</Steps>

## Optional cloud configuration

Create a local `.env` file only when you need sign-in, cloud saves, share links, or AI generation:

```bash
VITE_SUPABASE_URL=https://your-project.supabase.co
VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=your-anon-key
```

<Warning>
  Do not put an Anthropic API key in a `VITE_*` variable. Builder sends authenticated AI requests
  through the `manifest-ai` Supabase Edge Function so the browser never receives the provider key.
</Warning>

## Repository checks

Before committing Builder changes, run:

```bash
npm run typecheck
npm run lint
npm test
npm run build
npm run docs:projections:check
```

The projection documentation check verifies that `PROJECTIONS.md` matches the installed Manifest compiler and registered projections.


---

## cloud and ai

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/reference/cloud-and-ai

---
title: 'Cloud, storage, and AI boundaries'
sidebarTitle: 'Cloud and AI'
description: 'Understand which Builder features are local, which require Supabase, what is stored in the browser, and how AI credentials remain server-side.'
---

Builder is local-first. The core studio does not require Supabase.

## Local features

Without cloud configuration you can:

- edit and compile Manifest source;
- open browser-granted files and folders;
- use inspection, projection, assembly, and Run panels;
- keep recovery drafts and user templates in browser storage;
- download source and assembled ZIP files.

## Supabase-backed features

Set both `VITE_SUPABASE_URL` and `VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` to enable the cloud client. Cloud features include:

- authentication;
- saved projects and revisions;
- share links;
- queued save synchronization and conflict handling;
- calls to the `manifest-ai` Edge Function.

Builder creates a stable random device identifier in local storage for synchronization behavior.

## AI request path

The browser sends the authoring intent, current IR, and any repair feedback to the authenticated `manifest-ai` Supabase Edge Function. The Edge Function holds the provider credential.

Returned source is compiled before insertion. In a folder workspace, validation uses the full workspace snapshot with the proposed active-file revision.

<Warning>
  Never expose provider API credentials through `VITE_*` variables. Vite embeds those variables in
  browser assets.
</Warning>

## Data handling checklist

- Review Manifest source before sending it to configured AI services.
- Use repository source control for durable history.
- Do not treat localStorage drafts or templates as backups.
- Keep application `.env` files and deployment identifiers outside generated-file ownership.


---

## panels

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/reference/panels

---
title: 'Builder panel reference'
sidebarTitle: 'Panel reference'
description: "Reference for Builder's model, graph, flow, changes, checks, metrics, symbols, assistant, projections, assemble, run, docs, help, and IR panels."
---

| Panel       | Source                                | Primary purpose                                    |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Model       | Compiled IR                           | Browse entity structure and commands.              |
| Graph       | Compiled IR                           | Visualize entity relationships.                    |
| Flow        | Compiled IR                           | Inspect command and event flow.                    |
| Changes     | Current and previous IR               | Review structural model changes.                   |
| Checks      | Diagnostics and completeness analysis | Find compiler and model issues.                    |
| Metrics     | Compiled model                        | Measure size and complexity.                       |
| Symbols     | Source index                          | Navigate declarations.                             |
| Assistant   | IR, source, optional cloud AI         | Scaffold or repair Manifest source.                |
| Projections | Manifest projection registry          | Preview individual generated surfaces.             |
| Assemble    | Selected projections and IR           | Build a coherent project file map and report.      |
| Run         | Isolated runtime session              | Exercise commands and inspect outcomes.            |
| Docs        | Generated domain documentation        | Preview and download model documentation.          |
| Help        | Repository `DSL.md`                   | Read the bundled Manifest language reference.      |
| IR          | Compiler output                       | Inspect the canonical intermediate representation. |

## Keyboard navigation

Open the command palette with `Ctrl+K`. The first ten panels also receive `Ctrl+Alt+<number>` shortcuts based on their displayed order. Use `F8` and `Shift+F8` to move between editor problems.

## Availability

Panels that depend on valid IR may show no result or disable generation when compilation has errors. Fix the source diagnostics first; Builder does not generate from the last successful IR while the active source is invalid.


---

## project generation cli

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/reference/project-generation-cli

---
title: 'Project generation CLI reference'
sidebarTitle: 'Generation CLI'
description: "Reference for Builder's Convex project-generation command, modes, dry runs, apply gates, install flags, and exit behavior."
---

Builder exposes the safe Convex application generation entrypoint through npm:

```bash
npm run project:generate:convex -- [options]
```

The command is a dry run unless `--apply` is present.

## Options

| Option                      | Default                                                   | Meaning                                                                    |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--mode initial\|update`    | `update`                                                  | Select new-project or ownership-aware update behavior.                     |
| `--ir <path>`               | `C:/projects/Manifest-source/generated/ir/merged.ir.json` | Manifest IR JSON used by the Convex preset.                                |
| `--target <path>`           | `C:/projects/CapsuleX`                                    | Application directory to plan against.                                     |
| `--name <name>`             | `capsule`                                                 | Generated project name.                                                    |
| `--apply`                   | off                                                       | Apply a conflict-free plan.                                                |
| `--destructive-override`    | off                                                       | Permit initial mode to overwrite generated paths in an initialized target. |
| `--install`                 | off                                                       | Install only when generated dependency requirements changed.               |
| `--allow-lifecycle-scripts` | off                                                       | Allow package lifecycle scripts during an explicitly enabled install.      |

## Create a new project

Review the initial plan:

```bash
npm run project:generate:convex -- \
  --mode initial \
  --ir C:/projects/manifest-source/generated/ir/merged.ir.json \
  --target C:/projects/new-application \
  --name new-application
```

After reviewing additions and conflicts, apply it:

```bash
npm run project:generate:convex -- \
  --mode initial \
  --ir C:/projects/manifest-source/generated/ir/merged.ir.json \
  --target C:/projects/new-application \
  --name new-application \
  --apply
```

## Update an owned project

Run the update without `--apply` first:

```bash
npm run project:generate:convex -- \
  --mode update \
  --ir C:/projects/manifest-source/generated/ir/merged.ir.json \
  --target C:/projects/existing-application \
  --name existing-application
```

The JSON output lists `additions`, `modifications`, `conflicts`, `deletions`, `dependencyRequirementsChanged`, and `followUps`. Resolve every conflict and rerun the plan before adding `--apply`.

## Exit behavior

- Invalid mode or incomplete Convex assembly exits with an error before planning.
- A plan with conflicts sets exit code `2` and is not applied.
- A dry run with no conflicts prints the plan and stops.
- Apply writes transactionally, then optionally runs the gated install.


---

## troubleshooting

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/builder/troubleshooting

---
title: 'Troubleshoot Builder'
sidebarTitle: 'Troubleshooting'
description: 'Resolve common Builder workspace, compiler, projection, assembly, regeneration, cloud, and runtime problems.'
---

<AccordionGroup>
  <Accordion title="Open Manifest Folder finds no files">
    Confirm the selected folder contains `.manifest` files. Builder ignores other extensions. In
    browser environments where directory iteration returns no entries, provide a valid
    `manifest-workspace.txt` index with normalized relative file paths.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="A use directive does not resolve">
    Open the containing folder as a workspace instead of importing one file. Check the directive's
    relative path and exact filename casing. Paths containing `.` or `..` traversal segments are
    rejected.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Projection or assembly buttons are disabled">
    Compile valid source first. Then select at least one projection and resolve exclusive
    ORM/framework conflicts. Convex export also requires every preset verification check to pass.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Update mode says ownership manifest missing">
    Update mode only works for applications created by initial mode and carrying
    `.builder/ownership.json`. Builder does not guess ownership for an existing untracked
    application because that could overwrite application code.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="An owned file reports a conflict">
    The file's current SHA-256 hash no longer matches the prior ownership record. Preserve the
    application edit, move the customization to an app-owned seam, or reconcile it with the newly
    generated content. Do not edit the ownership hash to bypass review.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="package.json reports package-entry-modified">
    An application changed a dependency or script that Builder previously owned, and the new
    generated requirement differs. Choose a compatible requirement explicitly. Builder will not
    replace the whole package file.
  </Accordion>
