  <Accordion title="--install does not run">
    Installs run only when the plan reports `dependencyRequirementsChanged: true` and `--install` is
    present. Lifecycle scripts remain disabled unless you also pass `--allow-lifecycle-scripts`.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Convex source changed but deployment did not">
    This is expected. Builder reports Convex deployment as a separate follow-up and never executes
    it. Deploy from the application repository after reviewing the source and using the
    application's established deployment process.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="Cloud save or AI is unavailable">
    Confirm both `VITE_SUPABASE_URL` and `VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` are configured, restart the Vite
    server, and sign in. Local Studio features continue to work without those values.
  </Accordion>
  <Accordion title="A Run command timed out">
    The panel stops waiting after two seconds, but the underlying execution may still finish.
    Refresh the runtime context before running another command. Treat Studio execution as an
    authoring aid, not production transaction evidence.
  </Accordion>
</AccordionGroup>


---

## Customize Node.js versions for the matrix build

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/cli/ci-cd

---
title: 'CI/CD: GitHub Actions and breaking change detection'
description: 'Generate GitHub Actions workflows, detect breaking changes between IR versions, and integrate Manifest validation into your CI pipeline.'
sidebarTitle: 'CI/CD'
---

Manifest provides CI/CD tooling for automated validation, breaking change detection, and regression testing in continuous integration pipelines. Use the workflow generator to bootstrap a GitHub Actions pipeline, and the breaking change detector to gate pull requests on incompatible IR changes.

## GitHub Actions workflow generator

Generate a complete GitHub Actions workflow file with a single command:

```bash
manifest init --ci github
```

This creates `.github/workflows/manifest-ci.yml` with two jobs:

**PR validation** (runs on every pull request):

- `manifest validate` — validates IR JSON against the IR schema
- `manifest scan` — scans the codebase for governance violations
- `npm test` — runs the full test suite

**Conformance regen** (runs on pushes to `main`):

- Auto-regenerates conformance fixtures
- Commits updated fixtures if they have changed
- Uses `[skip ci]` in the commit message to avoid infinite workflow loops

### Options

| Flag                         | Default    | Description                                               |
| ---------------------------- | ---------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--ci <provider>`            | (required) | CI provider to generate for. Currently supports `github`. |
| `--node-versions <versions>` | `18,20,22` | Comma-separated Node.js versions for matrix builds        |
| `--force`                    | `false`    | Overwrite existing workflow file                          |

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** The CLI flag default string is still `18,20,22`, but
> `@angriff36/manifest` declares `engines.node: ">=20"`. Prefer `--node-versions 20,22` (or newer
> LTS) so the generated matrix matches what the package supports.

```bash
# Customize Node.js versions for the matrix build
manifest init --ci github --node-versions 20,22
```
<Note>
  The command is invoked as `manifest init --ci github` — it is a flag on the existing `init`
  command, not a separate subcommand. It does not run the interactive setup; it only generates the
  workflow file.
</Note>

### Generated workflow structure

```yaml
name: Manifest CI
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        node-version: [20, 22]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx manifest validate
      - run: npx manifest scan
      - run: npm test

  conformance-regen:
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run conformance:regen
      - uses: stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action@v5
        with:
          commit_message: 'chore: regen conformance fixtures [skip ci]'
```

## Generated-code drift gate

`manifest generate --check` regenerates projection output **in memory** and compares it to the committed files without writing anything. It exits non-zero and lists the drifted files (a missing file or any byte difference counts as drift) — the codegen analogue of `prettier --check`. This lets CI assert that committed code equals freshly generated code, so stale checked-in output can never silently diverge from the IR.

```bash
# Fails the job if any committed file differs from a fresh generation
manifest generate ir/ --output generated/ --check
```

Add it as a PR step alongside `validate` and `scan`:

```yaml
codegen-drift:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
      with:
        node-version: 20
    - run: npm ci
    - name: Assert generated code is up to date
      run: npx manifest generate ir/ --output generated/ --check
```

When the job fails, the fix is to run `manifest generate` (without `--check`) locally and commit the result. Pair this with `manifest compile`'s idempotent output (unchanged source re-compiles byte-identically) so a clean `compile` + `generate --check` chain proves the whole `.manifest → IR → code` pipeline is committed and current.

## Breaking change detector

Classify changes between two IR versions as `compatible`, `deprecated`, or `breaking`:

```bash
manifest diff breaking old.ir.json new.ir.json
```

The detector is a pure function (`classifyBreakingChanges`) that compares two IR JSON files and produces a report of every change, classified by severity with consumer impact annotations.

### Classification rules

| Element                           | Change                        | Severity                    |
| --------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Entity added                      | `entity-added`                | compatible                  |
| Entity removed                    | `entity-removed`              | **breaking**                |
| Property added (optional/default) | `property-added`              | compatible                  |
| Property added (no details)       | `property-added`              | **breaking** (conservative) |
| Property removed                  | `property-removed`            | **breaking**                |
| Property type changed             | `property-type-changed`       | **breaking**                |
| Property made optional            | `property-made-optional`      | compatible                  |
| Property made required            | `property-made-required`      | **breaking**                |
| Computed expression changed       | `computed-expression-changed` | deprecated                  |
| Computed property removed         | `computed-removed`            | **breaking**                |
| Relationship removed              | `relationship-removed`        | **breaking**                |
| Relationship kind changed         | `relationship-kind-changed`   | **breaking**                |
| Constraint removed                | `constraint-removed`          | deprecated                  |
| Constraint severity raised        | `constraint-severity-raised`  | compatible                  |
| Command removed                   | `command-removed`             | **breaking**                |
| Command parameter added           | `command-param-added`         | **breaking**                |
| Command parameter removed         | `command-param-removed`       | **breaking**                |
| Command guards changed            | `command-guards-changed`      | deprecated                  |
| Command returns changed           | `command-returns-changed`     | **breaking**                |
| Policy removed                    | `policy-removed`              | **breaking**                |
| Policy expression changed         | `policy-expression-changed`   | deprecated                  |
| Store removed                     | `store-removed`               | **breaking**                |
| Store target changed              | `store-target-changed`        | **breaking**                |
| Event removed                     | `event-removed`               | **breaking**                |
| Event channel changed             | `event-channel-changed`       | **breaking**                |
| Module added                      | `module-added`                | compatible                  |
| Module removed                    | `module-removed`              | **breaking**                |

The conservative default for `property-added` (no details) is `breaking` because adding a required property without a default value breaks existing consumers. Properties added as optional or with defaults are classified as `compatible`.

### Output format

By default, the detector outputs a human-readable report with color-coded severity indicators:

```
BREAKING  entities.Order.properties.discount (property-removed)
  Impact: commands, routes, projections

DEPRECATED entities.Task.computed.dueDate (computed-expression-changed)
  Impact: projections
```

### CI mode

Use `--ci` to gate pipelines on unacknowledged breaking changes:

```bash
manifest diff breaking old.ir.json new.ir.json --ci
```

In CI mode, the command exits non-zero when there are breaking changes not listed in the acknowledgments file. This makes it suitable as a PR gate: if the PR introduces breaking changes, the CI job fails until the changes are acknowledged or the IR is updated to be compatible.

### Acknowledgments file

Create an acknowledgments file to track breaking changes that have been reviewed and accepted:

```json
{
  "acknowledged": [
    {
      "path": "entities.Order.properties.discount",
      "category": "property-removed",
      "reason": "Discount feature removed in v2, all consumers updated"
    },
    {
      "path": "entities.Task.commands.estimate.command-removed",
      "category": "command-removed",
      "reason": "Replaced by async estimateV2 command"
    }
  ]
}
```

Reference it with `--ack`:

```bash
manifest diff breaking old.ir.json new.ir.json --ack acknowledgments.json --ci
```

Acknowledged breaking changes are filtered from the report. Only unacknowledged breaking changes cause a non-zero exit in CI mode.

### Options

| Flag                  | Description                                           |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `--json`              | Output structured JSON instead of human-readable text |
| `--ack <path>`        | Path to acknowledgments file for filtering            |
| `--ci`                | Exit non-zero on unacknowledged breaking changes      |
| `-o, --output <path>` | Write output to a file instead of stdout              |

## Complete PR pipeline example

A complete GitHub Actions workflow combining validation, testing, and breaking change detection:

```yaml
name: Manifest CI
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        node-version: [20, 22]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx manifest validate
      - run: npx manifest scan --strict
      - run: npm test

  breaking-changes:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - run: npm ci
      - name: Compare IR against main branch
        run: |
          git stash
          git checkout origin/main -- ir/
          cp -r ir/ old-ir/
          git stash pop || true
          git checkout - -- ir/
          npx manifest diff breaking old-ir/ ir/ --ci --ack acknowledgments.json
```

This pipeline runs validation and tests across three Node.js versions, then checks for breaking changes against the main branch. The `--ci` flag ensures the job fails if the PR introduces unacknowledged breaking changes.

See [CLI overview](/cli/overview) for the full set of CLI commands, [Governance and audit](/cli/governance) for `manifest scan` and `enforce-surface`, and [Configuration](/cli/configuration) for storing default flags in `manifest.config.yaml`.


---

## Interactive setup

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/cli/commands

---
title: 'Manifest CLI command reference'
sidebarTitle: 'Commands'
description: 'Full syntax, options, and usage examples for core Manifest CLI commands: manifest init, compile, generate, build, and validate — with flags and defaults.'
---

Every command reads `manifest.config.yaml` from your project root when it exists. CLI flags always take precedence over config values. Run any command with `--help` to see its options inline.

---

## `manifest init`

Runs an interactive prompt that collects your project settings and writes a `manifest.config.yaml` to the project root. If a config file already exists, the command exits without overwriting it unless you pass `--force`.

```bash
manifest init
```

### Questions asked

| Prompt                                  | Default         |
| --------------------------------------- | --------------- |
| Where are your `.manifest` files?       | `**/*.manifest` |
| Where should IR output go?              | `ir/`           |
| Will you be generating code from IR?    | No              |
| What projection target?                 | `nextjs`        |
| Where should generated code be written? | `app/api`       |

If the target is `nextjs` or `remix`, the generated code output defaults to `app/api`. For any other target it defaults to `generated`.

### Options

<ParamField path="-f, --force" type="boolean" default="false">
  Overwrite an existing `manifest.config.yaml` (or CI workflow file when `--ci` is used). The
  interactive setup requires a TTY; in non-interactive shells it writes a minimal config with
  defaults.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--ci" type="string">
  Generate a CI workflow for the given provider (currently `github`) instead of running the
  interactive config setup. See [CI/CD](/cli/ci-cd).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--node-versions" type="string">
  Comma-separated Node.js versions for the generated CI matrix (default: `18,20,22`). Only
  meaningful with `--ci`. Prefer `20,22` — package `engines.node` is `>=20` (Correction
  2026-07-15 @RYANSIGNED).
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Interactive setup
manifest init

# Overwrite an existing config
manifest init --force
```

<Warning>
  `manifest init --force` requires an interactive terminal. In headless or non-TTY environments the
  command writes a minimal default config instead of prompting.
</Warning>

---

## `manifest compile [source]`

Parses one or more `.manifest` source files and writes the compiled IR as `.ir.json` files. When no source is provided, the CLI uses the glob pattern from your config (or `**/*.manifest` by default).

```bash
manifest compile [source] [options]
```

`source` can be a single `.manifest` file path, a directory, or omitted to use the config glob.

### Options

<ParamField path="-o, --output" type="string">
  Output directory or file path. The flag itself has **no default** — when omitted, the CLI falls
  back to the config's `output` field if a config file is present, and otherwise writes alongside
  the source / to stdout depending on the input. (The `ir/` value is the documented config-level
  fallback, not a flag default.) When the output is a directory, each source file produces a
  matching `.ir.json` file inside it.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-g, --glob" type="string">
  Glob pattern to select multiple source files. Use this when you pass a directory as `source` and
  want a non-default pattern, or when you want to specify patterns without a config file.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-d, --diagnostics" type="boolean" default="false">
  Print compiler diagnostics (errors, warnings, and informational messages) to the console after
  each file is compiled.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--pretty" type="boolean" default="true">
  Pretty-print the IR JSON output with two-space indentation. Disable for compact output.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--merge" type="boolean" default="false">
  Merge multiple source files into a single IR, resolving `use` declarations across files. Produces
  one combined IR rather than one `.ir.json` per source.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--entry" type="string...">
  Entry file(s) for merge compilation (repeatable). The merge starts from these files and follows
  their `use` declarations. Auto-detected when omitted. Only meaningful together with `--merge`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--all" type="boolean" default="false">
  Read all settings from `manifest.config.yaml` and use the config-driven flow instead of individual flags.
  When set, the command uses config values for `output`, `glob`, `diagnostics`, and other options.
  This is the preferred workflow for projects with a config file.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--fail-on <policy>" type="string" default="block">
  CI exit policy: `block` (errors only), `warn` (errors or warnings), or `never` (report-only).
</ParamField>

<Note>
  **Idempotent output.** When an output `.ir.json` already exists and was produced from
  byte-identical source (matching `provenance.contentHash`), the compiler reuses the prior
  `provenance.compiledAt` (recomputing `irHash` against it) so re-compiling unchanged source is
  byte-identical — no spurious git diff. A fresh `compiledAt` lands only when the source actually
  changes. Applies to single-file and `--merge` compilation.
</Note>

<Tip>
  **Duplicate event names** produce a compile warning (shown with `--diagnostics` and in the LSP):
  two declarations of the same event name collide in the event registry, so one shadows the other.
  Rename one.
</Tip>

### Examples

```bash
# Compile a single file; output goes to the config output dir if set
manifest compile modules/recipe.manifest

# Compile a single file to a specific directory
manifest compile modules/recipe.manifest --output ir/

# Compile all .manifest files matching a glob
manifest compile --glob "modules/**/*.manifest" --output ir/

# Compile and show diagnostics
manifest compile modules/recipe.manifest --output ir/ --diagnostics

# Use all config defaults (finds all .manifest files via config src pattern)
manifest compile
```

---

## `manifest generate <ir>`

Reads one or more compiled IR files and generates code using the specified projection and surface. `<ir>` can be a single `.ir.json` file or a directory; when it is a directory, the CLI processes every `.ir.json` file found inside it.

```bash
manifest generate <ir> [options]
```

### Options

<ParamField path="-p, --projection" type="string" default="nextjs">
  Projection to use for code generation. Accepts **any registered projection name** — dispatch is
  generic via the projection registry, so this is not a fixed list. Registered projections include
  `nextjs`, `routes`, `prisma`, `prisma-store`, `zod`, `drizzle`, `convex`, `openapi`, `kysely`,
  `graphql`, `react-query`, `express`, `hono`, `mermaid`, `jsonschema`, `storybook`, `sveltekit`,
  `pydantic`, `dynamodb`, `dart`, `terraform`, `elasticsearch`, `analytics`, `materialized-views`,
  and more. Adding a new projection means registering it, not editing the CLI.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-s, --surface" type="string" default="all">
  Which surface(s) to generate. Valid surface names are projection-specific; pass `all` to emit every surface the projection declares.

**nextjs** (default): `route` · `detail` · `command` · `dispatcher` · `subscribe` · `subscriptionHook` · `sharedRuntime` · `schedule` · `webhook` · `companions` · `ts.types` · `ts.client` · `all` (everything except concrete per-command routes, which are opt-in)

For other projections the CLI default `all` resolves to the projection's declared surface set (e.g. the `prisma` projection emits a single `schema.prisma` artifact). An unknown surface name is reported as a diagnostic, not silently ignored.

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-o, --output" type="string">
  Output directory for generated files. Falls back to the config's `projections.<name>.output` value (e.g. `projections.nextjs.output`, `projections.prisma.output`) when not provided.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--auth" type="string">
  *(nextjs only)* Auth provider name or import path (e.g. `clerk` or `@repo/auth/server`). Falls
  back to `projections.nextjs.options.authImportPath`, then `authProvider`, then the `authProvider`
  default of `none` (no auth wrapper).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--database" type="string">
  *(nextjs only)* Database import path used in generated files (e.g. `@/lib/database` or
  `@repo/database`). Falls back to `projections.nextjs.options.databaseImportPath`, then defaults to
  `@/lib/database`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--runtime" type="string">
  *(nextjs only)* Runtime import path (e.g. `@/lib/manifest-runtime` or `@repo/manifest/runtime`).
  Falls back to `projections.nextjs.options.runtimeImportPath`, then defaults to
  `@/lib/manifest-runtime`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--response" type="string">
  *(nextjs only)* Response helpers import path (e.g. `@/lib/manifest-response` or
  `@repo/manifest/response`). Falls back to `projections.nextjs.options.responseImportPath`, then
  defaults to `@/lib/manifest-response`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--check" type="boolean" default="false">
  Drift mode: regenerate code in memory and compare it to the committed files **without writing
  anything**. Exits non-zero and lists the drifted files (a missing file or any byte difference
  counts as drift). Use it in CI to assert that committed code equals freshly generated code — the
  codegen analogue of `prettier --check`. See [CI/CD](/cli/ci-cd).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--all" type="boolean" default="false">
  Read all settings from `manifest.config.yaml` and use the config-driven flow instead of individual flags.
  When set, the command uses config values for `output`, `projection`, `surface`, and other options.
  This is the preferred workflow for projects with a config file.
</ParamField>

#### Examples

```bash
# Default: nextjs, all surfaces
manifest generate ir/

# Just the canonical dispatcher route
manifest generate ir/ --surface dispatcher --output app/api

# Prisma schema from compiled IR
manifest generate ir/ --projection prisma --surface prisma.schema --output prisma

# CI drift gate: fail if committed code is stale vs. the IR
manifest generate ir/ --output generated/ --check
```

Projection-specific options (table names, column mappings, decimal precision, indexes, type overrides, foreign-key names for prisma; auth provider, tenant filters, dispatcher mode for nextjs) live in `manifest.config.yaml` under `projections.<name>.options`. See [Configuration](/cli/configuration) and the per-projection guides ([Next.js](/integration/nextjs), [Prisma](/integration/prisma)).

#### Exit codes

`manifest generate` exits **non-zero** when any projection returns a diagnostic with `severity: 'error'`. Example trigger conditions for the Prisma projection: bare `number`-typed properties (`PRISMA_AMBIGUOUS_NUMBER`), unknown `type.name` with no `typeMappings` override (`PRISMA_UNKNOWN_TYPE`), or an unknown surface name (`UNKNOWN_SURFACE`). The failing diagnostic codes are surfaced in stdout/stderr so CI logs are actionable. Info / warning severities log but do not fail the command.

### Examples

```bash
# Generate using all config defaults (config file must exist)
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json

# Override the output directory
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --output apps/api/app/api/

# Generate only TypeScript types
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface types --output generated/types.ts

# Generate only the client SDK
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface client --output generated/client.ts

# Generate from a directory of IR files with workspace imports
manifest generate ir/ \
  --output apps/api/app/api/ \
  --auth @repo/auth/server \
  --database @repo/database \
  --runtime @repo/manifest/runtime \
  --response @repo/manifest/response
```

---

## `manifest build [source]`

Runs compile and generate as a single pipeline. The source `.manifest` files are compiled to IR in `--ir-output`, then code is generated from that IR into `--code-output`. All config defaults apply to both stages.

```bash
manifest build [source] [options]
```

`source` can be a single `.manifest` file, a glob pattern, or omitted to use the config `src` field.

### Options

<ParamField path="-p, --projection" type="string" default="nextjs">
  Projection name for the generate stage. Accepts any registered projection (e.g. `nextjs`,
  `prisma`, `zod`, `drizzle`, `convex`, `openapi`, `kysely`, …) — see [`manifest
  generate`](#manifest-generate-ir) for the full list.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-s, --surface" type="string" default="all">
  Surface(s) to generate. Same values as `manifest generate` — projection-specific, with `all`
  emitting every declared surface (nextjs: `route`, `detail`, `command`, `dispatcher`, `types`,
  `client`).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--ir-output" type="string">
  Directory to write compiled IR files. Falls back to the config `output` field, then defaults to
  `ir/`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--code-output" type="string">
  Directory to write generated code files. Falls back to `projections.nextjs.output` in config, then
  defaults to `generated/`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-g, --glob" type="string">
  Glob pattern for selecting multiple source files during the compile stage.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--auth" type="string">
  Auth provider or import path, passed through to the generate stage.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--database" type="string">
  Database import path, passed through to the generate stage.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--runtime" type="string">
  Runtime import path, passed through to the generate stage.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--response" type="string">
  Response helpers import path, passed through to the generate stage.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--all" type="boolean" default="false">
  Read all settings from `manifest.config.yaml` and use the config-driven flow instead of individual flags.
  When set, the command uses config values for compile and generate stages (output, projection, surface, etc.).
  This is the preferred workflow for projects with a config file.
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Use all config defaults (recommended after manifest init)
manifest build

# Build a single source file with explicit outputs
manifest build modules/recipe.manifest \
  --ir-output ir/ \
  --code-output app/api/

# Override the code output directory for a staging target
manifest build modules/recipe.manifest --code-output apps/staging/app/api/

# Build multiple files via glob with workspace imports
manifest build \
  --glob "modules/**/*.manifest" \
  --ir-output ir/ \
  --code-output apps/api/app/api/ \
  --auth @repo/auth/server \
  --database @repo/database \
  --runtime @repo/manifest/runtime \
  --response @repo/manifest/response
```

---

## `manifest docs [source]`

Generates a static API reference site from one or more Manifest sources. Inputs can be `.manifest` (compiled first) or `.ir.json` files (loaded directly).

```bash
manifest docs [source] [options]
```

`source` can be a single file, a directory, or a glob.

### Options

<ParamField path="-o, --output" type="string" default="docs-site">
  Output directory for generated docs.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-f, --format" type="string" default="html">
  Output format: `html` or `markdown`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-t, --title" type="string" default="Manifest API Reference">
  Site title used in generated pages.
</ParamField>

### IR merge workflow (`mergeIrs` behavior)

When `manifest docs` receives multiple inputs, it merges all compiled/loaded IR objects into one aggregate IR for page generation.

Expected merge contract:

1. Initialize all required IR arrays before merging: `modules`, `values`, `entities`, `enums`, `stores`, `events`, `commands`, `policies`.
2. Append each input IR array into the merged array.
3. Propagate `values` exactly like the other top-level arrays.

```ts
const mergedIR: IR = {
  version: '1.0',
  provenance: firstProvenance,
  modules: [],
  values: [],
  entities: [],
  enums: [],
  stores: [],
  events: [],
  commands: [],
  policies: [],
};

for (const ir of inputIRs) {
  mergedIR.modules.push(...ir.modules);
  mergedIR.values.push(...ir.values);
  mergedIR.entities.push(...ir.entities);
  mergedIR.enums.push(...ir.enums);
  mergedIR.stores.push(...ir.stores);
  mergedIR.events.push(...ir.events);
  mergedIR.commands.push(...ir.commands);
  mergedIR.policies.push(...ir.policies);
}
```

<Note>
  `values` is required by the IR v1 schema even when empty. Any merge path that does not initialize
  and populate `values` can produce nonconformant merged IR and break downstream tooling.
</Note>

### Examples

```bash
# Build docs from all manifest/IR files under src
manifest docs src/ --format markdown --output docs-site

# Build docs from a single compiled IR
manifest docs ir/app.ir.json --output docs-site
```

---

## `manifest validate [ir]`

Validates one or more IR JSON files against the IR v1 JSON schema using [Ajv](https://ajv.js.org). When `ir` is omitted, the CLI searches the current directory for all `*.ir.json` files (excluding `node_modules`, `dist`, and `.next`).

```bash
manifest validate [ir] [options]
```

`ir` can be a single `.ir.json` file, a directory of IR files, or omitted to scan the current directory.

### Options

<ParamField path="--schema" type="string">
  Path to a custom JSON schema file. When omitted, the CLI uses the IR v1 schema bundled with the
  installed package automatically.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--strict" type="boolean" default="false">
  Alias for `--fail-on warn` (fail the process on warnings).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--fail-on <policy>" type="string" default="block">
  CI exit policy: `block` (errors only), `warn` (errors or warnings), or `never` (report-only).
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Validate all IR files in the ir/ directory
manifest validate ir/

# Validate a specific IR file
manifest validate ir/recipe.ir.json

# Fail on warnings as well as errors
manifest validate ir/ --strict

# Validate against a custom schema
manifest validate ir/ --schema custom-schema.json
```

<Note>
  The bundled schema path is resolved relative to the installed package, so `manifest validate`
  works correctly whether you run it from inside the Manifest repo or from a consumer project.
</Note>

---

## `manifest config`

Inspection surface for `manifest.config.{yaml,yml,ts,js}`. Three subcommands let you prove what Manifest will actually do before regenerating code — the cornerstone of the CI drift workflow described in [Configuration](/cli/configuration).

```bash
manifest config <subcommand> [options]
```

### `manifest config validate`

JSON-schema validates the build config against [`docs/spec/config/manifest.config.schema.json`](https://github.com/Angriff36/Manifest/blob/main/docs/spec/config/manifest.config.schema.json), the same schema referenced via `$schema:` in your config file.

```bash
manifest config validate
manifest config validate --json
```

<ParamField path="--json" type="boolean" default="false">
  Emit structured JSON (`{ configPath, ok, diagnostics }`) instead of human-readable output. Useful for CI pipelines that need machine-parseable failures.
</ParamField>

The command exits non-zero on the first schema violation and prints a dotted-path diagnostic for each error, e.g.:

```text
  - projections.nextjs.options.authProvider: must be equal to one of the allowed values (allowed: clerk, nextauth, custom, none)
  - projections.nextjs.options: unknown property "unknownOption" (additionalProperties: false)
```

### `manifest config print-defaults`

Prints the canonical defaults snapshot — every value Manifest applies when an option is unset. Sourced from `src/manifest/projections/nextjs/defaults.ts` so the projection, the schema, and this command can never disagree.

```bash
manifest config print-defaults
```

Output is key-sorted JSON across six blocks: `nextjs`, `tenantProvider`, `dispatcher`, `concreteCommandRoutes`, `readRoutes`, `routes`. See the [configuration reference](/cli/configuration) for what each key controls.

### `manifest config inspect`

Aliased as `print-effective`. Prints the **effective** configuration — defaults merged with user overrides from your config file. The output is deterministic and key-sorted, making it safe to snapshot in CI.

```bash
manifest config inspect
manifest config inspect --json
```

```json
{
  "configPath": "/work/app/manifest.config.yaml",
  "build": { "output": "ir/", "src": "**/*.manifest" },
  "projections": {
    "nextjs": {
      "output": "generated/",
      "options": {
        "appDir": "apps/api/app/api",
        "authProvider": "clerk",
        "concreteCommandRoutes": {
          "enabled": false,
          "legacyAliasesOnly": true
        },
        "dispatcher": {
          "deriveInstanceId": true,
          "enabled": true,
          "executionMode": "inline",
          "executorImportName": "executeManifestCommand",
          "executorImportPath": "@/lib/manifest-executor",
          "path": "/manifest/[entity]/commands/[command]/route.ts"
        },
        "readRoutes": {
          "directDbReads": true,
          "enabled": true
        },
        "unauthorizedStatus": 401,
        ...
      }
    }
  }
}
```

<Tip>
  Commit the inspect output as a snapshot under VCS (e.g.
  `.manifest/effective-config.snapshot.json`) and gate CI on `git diff --exit-code` against it.
  Drift in either Manifest defaults or your local config will then surface as a diff before it
  changes emitted code.
</Tip>

---

## `manifest watch [source]`

Watches `.manifest` files and rebuilds (compile + generate) on every change. Useful during local development so generated code stays in sync with your source.

```bash
manifest watch [source] [options]
```

### Options

`watch` accepts the same flags as `manifest build` — `-p, --projection`, `-s, --surface`, `--ir-output`, `--code-output`, `-g, --glob`, and the `--auth`/`--database`/`--runtime`/`--response` import-path flags — plus:

<ParamField path="--debounce" type="number" default="300">
  Debounce delay in milliseconds before a detected change triggers a rebuild.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--events" type="boolean" default="false">
  Emit structured JSON change events to stdout, useful for piping into other tooling.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--clear" type="boolean" default="false">
  Clear the terminal on each rebuild.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--all" type="boolean" default="false">
  Read all settings from `manifest.config.yaml` and use the config-driven flow instead of individual flags.
  When set, the command uses config values for compile and generate stages (output, projection, surface, etc.).
  This is the preferred workflow for projects with a config file.
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Watch a directory and rebuild on change
manifest watch src/

# Watch with a custom debounce and JSON change events
manifest watch src/ --debounce 300 --events
```

---

## `manifest validate-ai [source]`

Validates a `.manifest` or IR file and returns scored diagnostics tuned for AI agents, so an agent can gate on a numeric pass threshold rather than parsing free-form output.

```bash
manifest validate-ai [source] [options]
```

`source` can be a `.manifest` file, an `.ir.json` file, a directory, or a glob.

### Options

<ParamField path="-f, --format" type="string" default="text">
  Output format: `text` or `json`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--schema" type="string">
  Path to a custom JSON schema file.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--min-score" type="number" default="100">
  Minimum score required to pass. The command exits non-zero when the score falls below this value.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--verbose" type="boolean" default="false">
  Include info-level diagnostics in the output.
</ParamField>

### Diagnostic categories

Every diagnostic carries a `category`: `schema`, `compile`, `semantic`, `structural`, or `domain`. The **`domain`** category surfaces compile-time domain- and reaction-wiring checks against the IR with coded diagnostics (each includes a fix suggestion):

| Code                   | Severity | Meaning                                                                                                                                                                  |
| ---------------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `DOMAIN_UNWIRED_FK`    | error    | A `{parent}Id`-style foreign key with no `belongsTo`/`ref` and no command that sets it from `self.id`.                                                                   |
| `DOMAIN_ORPHAN_CREATE` | error    | A child entity with no command or reaction that wires its parent FK.                                                                                                     |
| `REACTION_UNWIRED`     | error    | A reaction whose event is never emitted by a command, or whose payload references can't be satisfied by emitter params, `payload._subject`, or create-result properties. |

Non-error domain findings are reported as `DOMAIN_COMPLETENESS` / `REACTION_COMPLETENESS`. These checks mirror the compiler's domain-completeness semantics (see `docs/spec/semantics.md`) — a half-wired model fails validation.

### Examples

```bash
# Score an IR file as JSON, passing at 80 or above
manifest validate-ai generated.ir.json --format json --min-score 80
```

---

## `manifest fmt [source]`

Formats `.manifest` source files using deterministic whitespace normalization. Pairs well with a pre-commit hook to keep source style consistent.

```bash
manifest fmt [source] [options]
```

`source` can be a file, directory, or glob.

### Options

<ParamField path="--check" type="boolean" default="false">
  Fail if any file would change. When set, writing is disabled — ideal for CI.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--write" type="boolean">
  Write formatted output. This is the default when `--check` is not set.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-g, --glob" type="string">
  Glob pattern to use when `source` is a directory.
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Check formatting without writing (CI mode)
manifest fmt src/ --check

# Format and write changes in place
manifest fmt src/ --write
```

---

## `manifest diagram [source]`

Generates Mermaid diagrams from IR — entity-relationship, state, and sequence views — so you can visualize a program's shape.

```bash
manifest diagram [source] [options]
```

`source` can be a `.manifest`, `.ir.json`, directory, or glob.

### Options

<ParamField path="-o, --output" type="string" default="diagrams">
  Output directory for generated diagram files.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-t, --type" type="string" default="all">
  Diagram type: `er`, `state`, `sequence`, or `all`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-e, --entity" type="string">
  Filter output to a single entity.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--markdown" type="boolean" default="false">
  Wrap output in fenced code blocks for embedding in Markdown.
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Generate an ER diagram wrapped in Markdown fences
manifest diagram src/app.manifest -t er --markdown
```

---

## `manifest coverage`

Reports command/guard/policy/constraint coverage by correlating compiled IR with conformance and unit test evidence found in the project.

```bash
manifest coverage [options]
```

### Options

<ParamField path="--ir" type="string">
  Path to a compiled IR JSON file.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-s, --source" type="string">
  Path to `.manifest` source, compiled on the fly.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-r, --root" type="string" default=".">
  Root directory to scan for test evidence.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-f, --format" type="string" default="text">
  Output format: `text` or `json`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--min-coverage" type="number">
  Minimum coverage percentage required to pass.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--strict" type="boolean" default="false">
  Exit non-zero when coverage falls below `--min-coverage`.
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Fail CI if coverage drops below 80%
manifest coverage -s src/app.manifest --min-coverage 80 --strict
```

---

## `manifest diff`

`diff` is a parent command for comparing source against IR, comparing two IR files, and classifying breaking changes.

```bash
manifest diff <subcommand> [options]
```

### `manifest diff source-vs-ir <entityName>`

Compares source-manifest parse output against precompiled IR for a single entity.

<ParamField path="--json" type="boolean" />
<ParamField path="--src" type="string">
  Source glob pattern.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="--ir-root" type="string">
  One or more IR root paths.
</ParamField>

### `manifest diff ir-vs-ir <oldIR> <newIR>`

Compares two IR JSON files and produces a diff report, optionally rendering migration SQL or Prisma steps.

<ParamField path="--json" type="boolean" />
<ParamField path="--sql" type="boolean">
  Render the diff as migration SQL.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="--prisma" type="boolean">
  Render the diff as Prisma migration steps.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="-o, --output" type="string">
  Output path.
</ParamField>

```bash
manifest diff ir-vs-ir old.ir.json new.ir.json --sql
```

---

## `manifest diff breaking`

Classifies the changes in an IR diff as compatible, deprecated, or breaking, with consumer impact. Run it as a `diff` subcommand. Use `--ci` to gate pipelines on unacknowledged breaking changes.

```bash
manifest diff breaking <oldIR> <newIR> [options]
```

### Options

<ParamField path="--json" type="boolean" />

<ParamField path="--ack" type="string">
  Path to an acknowledgement file listing breaking changes that have been reviewed and accepted.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--ci" type="boolean" default="false">
  Exit non-zero when there are unacknowledged breaking changes.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-o, --output" type="string">
  Output path.
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Fail CI on unacknowledged breaking changes
manifest diff breaking old.ir.json new.ir.json --ci
```

---

## `manifest migrate`

Analyzes an IR diff to produce a database migration plan, with optional SQL/Prisma preview and reversibility checks.

```bash
manifest migrate [options]
```

### Options

<ParamField path="--old-ir" type="string" required>
  Path to the previous IR file.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--new-ir" type="string" required>
  Path to the new IR file.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--dry-run" type="boolean" default="false">
  Show the plan without applying it.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--preview" type="boolean" default="false">
  Show the generated SQL and Prisma steps.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--force" type="boolean" default="false">
  Apply despite warnings or unacknowledged breaking changes.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--tool" type="string" default="prisma">
  Migration tool: `prisma` or `drizzle`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--no-check-reversibility" type="boolean">
  Skip reversibility validation.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--json" type="boolean" />
<ParamField path="-o, --output" type="string">
  Output path.
</ParamField>

### Examples

```bash
# Preview a migration plan between two IR versions
manifest migrate --old-ir old.ir.json --new-ir new.ir.json --preview
```

---

## `manifest changelog <from-ref> [to-ref]`

Generates a Markdown changelog from IR diffs between two Git refs. `to-ref` defaults to `HEAD`.

```bash
manifest changelog <from-ref> [to-ref] [options]
```

### Options

<ParamField path="-s, --source" type="string" default="**/*.manifest">
  Source glob pattern.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-o, --output" type="string">
  Output path for the changelog file.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="-t, --title" type="string">
  Changelog title.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="--json" type="boolean" />

### Examples

```bash
# Generate a changelog from v0.3.0 to HEAD
manifest changelog v0.3.0 HEAD -o CHANGELOG.md
```


---

## or:

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/cli/configuration

---
title: 'Configure Manifest with manifest.config.yaml'
sidebarTitle: 'Configuration'
description: 'Full reference for manifest.config.yaml: source globs, IR output, projection options (nextjs, routes, prisma, prisma-store), preflight env declarations, hooks, plugins, naming convention, auth providers, and monorepo import paths.'
---

`manifest.config.yaml` is the project-level config file that the CLI loads from your project root before executing any command. You create it by running `manifest init` or by writing it by hand. Every field is optional — the CLI falls back to built-in defaults for any field you omit.

## Config file locations

The CLI searches for a config file in the following order, using the first one it finds:

1. `manifest.config.yaml`
2. `manifest.config.yml`
3. `.manifestrc.yaml`
4. `.manifestrc.yml`

All four files live at your project root (the directory where you run `manifest` commands).

Runtime bindings that contain JavaScript values—store classes, store instances,
factories, and `resolveUser`—belong in `manifest.config.ts` or
`manifest.config.js`. A TypeScript config may also contain a `build` block; its
build values override YAML.

## Durable storage for generated runtime companions

`DATABASE_URL` only supplies connection details. It does not tell Manifest
which entity uses which adapter or table. Put those bindings in
`manifest.config.ts`:

```ts
import { defineConfig } from '@angriff36/manifest/config';
import { PostgresStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';

const connectionString = process.env.DATABASE_URL;
if (!connectionString) throw new Error('DATABASE_URL is required');

export default defineConfig({
  stores: {
    Customer: { implementation: new PostgresStore({ connectionString, tableName: 'customers' }) },
    Invoice: { implementation: new PostgresStore({ connectionString, tableName: 'invoices' }) },
  },
});
```

Then point the Next.js runtime companion at that file:

```yaml
projections:
  nextjs:
    output: generated
    options:
      # Relative to the emitted lib/manifest-runtime.ts file.
      runtimeConfigImport: '../manifest.config'
```

Only `memory` and browser `localStorage` work without this binding. Generated
runtimes containing `postgres`, `supabase`, `durable`, or custom stores fail
clearly when `runtimeConfigImport` is absent. See [Stores](/language/stores#complete-generated-companion-postgresql-setup) for the full source-to-runtime example.

## Option priority

When resolving any value, the CLI checks three sources in this order:

1. **CLI flag** (highest priority) — value passed directly on the command line
2. **Config file** — value from `manifest.config.yaml`
3. **Built-in default** (lowest priority) — fallback hardcoded in the CLI

You can always override a config value for a single run without modifying the file.

## Top-level fields

<ParamField path="$schema" type="string">
  Optional pointer to the config JSON Schema for editor IntelliSense. Manifest does **not** publish a resolvable schema URL, and `manifest config validate` loads the schema bundled with the package — it never fetches a URL. For autocomplete without a dead link, map the bundled schema in `.vscode/settings.json`:

```jsonc
{
  "yaml.schemas": {
    "./node_modules/@angriff36/manifest/docs/spec/config/manifest.config.schema.json": [
      "manifest.config.yaml",
      ".manifestrc.yaml",
    ],
  },
}
```

If you do set `$schema`, point it at a real local path — never a public URL Manifest doesn't host.

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="src" type="string" default="**/*.manifest">
  Glob pattern that selects your `.manifest` source files. Used by `manifest compile` and `manifest build` when no `source` argument is passed on the command line.

```yaml
src: modules/**/*.manifest
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="output" type="string" default="ir/">
  Directory where compiled IR JSON files are written. Used by `manifest compile` as the default `--output` value, and by `manifest build` as the default `--ir-output` value.

```yaml
output: ir/
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="naming" type="string | object">
  Global identifier-casing convention inherited by projections that map IR names to physical database names (currently the Prisma projection). Opt-in; omit to emit IR names verbatim. A per-projection `projections.<name>.options.naming` overrides this default.

The string shorthand `snake_case` expands to `{ table: snake_case, column: snake_case, pluralizeTables: true }` — e.g. `createdAt` → `@map("created_at")`, `Widget` → `@@map("widgets")`. The object form tunes each axis (`table`: `snake_case` | `camelCase` | `PascalCase` | `preserve`; `column`: `snake_case` | `camelCase` | `preserve`; `pluralizeTables`: boolean, default `true`).

```yaml
naming: snake_case
# or:
naming:
  table: snake_case
  column: snake_case
  pluralizeTables: true
```

Only `@map`/`@@map` attributes are added — Prisma model names and field identifiers stay as the IR name, so relations and indexes are unaffected. Explicit `tableMappings`/`columnMappings` always win and are the escape hatch for irregular plurals.

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="prismaSchema" type="string">
  Path to your Prisma schema file, used by property alignment checks. When omitted, the CLI searches `prisma/schema.prisma`, `schema.prisma`, and `db/schema.prisma` in that order.

```yaml
prismaSchema: prisma/schema.prisma
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="hooks" type="object">
  Hook settings: Git pre-commit settings consumed by `manifest install-hooks`, plus optional build lifecycle scripts.

<ParamField path="hooks.skipInCi" type="boolean" default="true">
  Skip running the generated pre-commit hook in CI environments.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="hooks.provider" type="string" default="husky">
  Git hook manager the pre-commit hook is installed into. Accepted values: `husky`,
  `simple-git-hooks`.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="hooks.runFmt" type="boolean" default="true">
  Run `manifest fmt` from the generated pre-commit hook.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="hooks.runValidate" type="boolean" default="true">
  Run `manifest validate` from the generated pre-commit hook.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="hooks.lifecycle" type="object">
  Build lifecycle scripts (not git hooks). These are shell scripts or Node scripts run at specific points in the build process. Paths are relative to the project root.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="hooks.lifecycle.beforeCompile" type="array">
  Scripts (paths) run before `manifest compile`. Executes in the order listed. Fails the build if any script exits non-zero.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="hooks.lifecycle.afterGenerate" type="array">
  Scripts (paths) run after a successful `manifest generate` or `manifest generate --all`. Executes in the order listed. Fails the build if any script exits non-zero.
</ParamField>

```yaml
hooks:
  # Git pre-commit hooks
  provider: husky
  skipInCi: true
  runFmt: true
  runValidate: true
  # Build lifecycle scripts
  lifecycle:
    beforeCompile:
      - scripts/pre-compile.sh
    afterGenerate:
      - scripts/post-generate.js
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="plugins" type="array">
  Third-party plugin declarations. At CLI startup any declared plugins are loaded and the CLI commands they contribute are registered, so they appear in `manifest --help` and are dispatchable; `manifest plugins list` reports their real load status and diagnostics. Loading is conditional — a project with no `plugins` declared loads nothing. A plugin that fails to load surfaces a diagnostic and is skipped; it never bricks the CLI. For runtime use (custom store adapters, expression builtins, audit sinks), compose the loaded registries into your runtime options with `pluginRegistriesToRuntimeOptions` from `@angriff36/manifest/plugin-loader` — see [Plugin API](/extensibility/plugin-api). Each entry requires a `module`; `enabled`, `options`, `order`, and `capabilities` are optional.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="plugins[].module" type="string" required>
  npm package name or relative file path to the plugin module.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="plugins[].options" type="object">
  Plugin-specific options forwarded to the plugin's `onLoad` hook and to its store-adapter and
  audit-sink factories.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="plugins[].enabled" type="boolean" default="true">
  Whether the plugin is active.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="plugins[].order" type="integer">
  Load order for this plugin. Lower numbers load first. Ties break by `module` string (localeCompare). Missing `order` sorts after any explicit order (stable relative to other unordered entries by module). Use this when plugin A depends on plugin B being loaded first.
</ParamField>
<ParamField path="plugins[].capabilities" type="array">
  Well-known capability tags the plugin advertises. Accepted values: `storeAdapter`, `auditSink`, `builtin`, `cliCommand`, `projection`. Unknown tags are kept (host extension) but surface an info diagnostic. Used for plugin discovery and dependency checking.
</ParamField>

```yaml
plugins:
  - module: '@my-org/manifest-plugin-audit'
    enabled: true
    order: 10
    capabilities:
      - auditSink
    options:
      level: strict
  - module: './local-plugins/naming-lint.js'
    order: 20
    capabilities:
      - cliCommand
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="env" type="object">
  Structured environment-variable mapping for **preflight validation** — grouped definitions of the variables your stores, auth, adapters, and custom settings require. Consumed by `manifest preflight` to check the environment and to generate `.env.example`.

~~This is distinct from the `env(VAR)` expression syntax…~~
> **Correction (2026-07-22):** There is **no** `env(VAR)` config-load inliner. This `env` block
> only _declares_ variable metadata for `manifest preflight` / `.env.example`. Inject secrets with
> `process.env` in `manifest.config.ts` (see [Durable storage](#durable-storage-for-generated-runtime-companions)).

The block has four optional groups — `stores`, `auth`, `adapters`, `custom` — each a map of an arbitrary key to an `EnvVarDefinition`. Each definition requires `name` and may set `description`, `required` (default `true`), `default`, and `example`.

```yaml
env:
  stores:
    primaryDb:
      name: DATABASE_URL
      description: Postgres connection string
      required: true
      example: postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/app
  auth:
    clerk:
      name: CLERK_SECRET_KEY
      required: true
  adapters:
    outbox:
      name: OUTBOX_TABLE
      required: false
      default: manifest_outbox
  custom:
    region:
      name: AWS_REGION
      default: us-east-1
```

</ParamField>

## `mergeIntegrity`

<ParamField path="mergeIntegrity" type="object">
  Controls multi-file compilation behavior when duplicate names or cross-module references appear. Only applies when compiling multiple `.manifest` files (e.g. `manifest compile --all` or multi-module repos).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="mergeIntegrity.onDuplicateEntity" type="string" default="error">
  Policy when multiple files declare an entity with the same name.

  - `error` (default): emit a diagnostic and fail compilation.
  - `lastWins`: silently use the last declaration (file load order wins).

  ```yaml
  mergeIntegrity:
    onDuplicateEntity: lastWins  # Allow duplicate entity names; last file wins
  ```
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="mergeIntegrity.onDuplicateCommand" type="string" default="error">
  Policy when multiple files declare a command with the same name (on the same entity or across entities). Accepted values: `error`, `lastWins`. Defaults to `error`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="mergeIntegrity.allowCrossModuleRefs" type="boolean" default="true">
  Whether relationships (`hasMany`, `hasOne`, `belongsTo`, `ref`) may target entities defined in other modules (other `.manifest` files). When `false`, cross-module relationships emit a diagnostic and fail compilation. Defaults to `true`.

  ```yaml
  mergeIntegrity:
    allowCrossModuleRefs: false  # Block relationships across modules
  ```
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="mergeIntegrity.forbidCycles" type="boolean" default="true">
  Whether circular relationship chains are rejected. When `true`, the compiler emits a diagnostic if a cycle is detected (e.g. `A hasMany B`, `B hasMany A`). This is a **required invariant** — setting it to `false` is rejected at config resolution time. Defaults to `true`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="mergeIntegrity" type="object">
  **Why this exists.** In multi-file projects, duplicate names and cross-file references can introduce silent bugs or ambiguous semantics. `mergeIntegrity` makes these conditions explicit and configurable. Use `lastWins` during rapid prototyping with split files; use `error` in production to enforce uniqueness. Use `allowCrossModuleRefs: false` to enforce module boundaries.
</ParamField>

## `validation`

<ParamField path="validation" type="object">
  Preflight validation rules that emit diagnostics when certain structural conditions are detected. These are **compile-time checks only** — they do not affect runtime behavior. Rules are disabled by default; enable the ones you want.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="validation.rules" type="array">
  List of validation rule names to enable. Accepted values: `missing-policy`, `unused-entity`, `orphan-relationship`.

  <ParamField path="validation.rules" type="array">
  **`missing-policy`** — Emit a diagnostic when an entity has commands but no policies target it **and** `defaultPolicies` is empty. Helps catch missing authorization for command-bearing entities.

  **`unused-entity`** — Emit a diagnostic for "bare declaration" entities: not external, have no store, no entity-scoped commands, and are not referenced by any other entity's relationships. Helps identify dead code.

  **`orphan-relationship`** — Emit a diagnostic for one-sided relationships where an entity has `belongsTo` or `ref` pointing to a parent, but the parent lacks a corresponding `hasMany` or `hasOne` inverse. Helps catch typos and missing inverse declarations.
  </ParamField>

  ```yaml
  validation:
    rules:
      - missing-policy    # Flag entities with commands but no policies
      - unused-entity     # Flag entities that are never referenced
      - orphan-relationship  # Flag one-sided relationships
  ```
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="validation.failOn" type="array">
  Severity levels that should fail compilation. Accepted values: `ok`, `warn`, `block`. When omitted, all severities pass (diagnostics are emitted but compilation continues). Set to `['block']` or `['warn', 'block']` to halt on specific severities.
</ParamField>

## `provenance`

<ParamField path="provenance" type="object">
  Controls IR provenance stamps and lockfile behavior. IR always carries required provenance fields (schema version, compiler version, content hash). This config determines whether timestamps are deterministic and whether a lockfile is written.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="provenance.stamp" type="boolean" default="true">
  When `false`, skip writing the provenance lockfile. IR is still stamped with required fields (this flag only controls the lockfile write). Defaults to `true`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="provenance.fields" type="array" default="['sourceHash', 'generatorVersion', 'irSchemaVersion', 'gitSha']">
  Requested stamp fields. Required IR fields are always stamped regardless of this list. `gitSha` is accepted but not yet written (deferred). Defaults to all four fields.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="provenance.deterministic" type="boolean" default="false">
  When `true`, use a fixed `compiledAt` timestamp (`1970-01-01T00:00:00.000Z`) instead of wall-clock time. Required for reproducible builds. Defaults to `false`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="provenance.lockfile" type="string">
  Path for the provenance lockfile (relative to the project root). When omitted, the default is `.manifest/versions.json`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="provenance.failIfStale" type="boolean" default="false">
  When `true` and a lockfile exists: if source content (`contentHash`) matches the lock but the IR hash (`irHash`) differs under deterministic mode, compilation fails. This catches hand-edited IR or non-deterministic compiler behavior. Defaults to `false`.
</ParamField>

```yaml
provenance:
  deterministic: true  # Use fixed timestamp for reproducible builds
  lockfile: .manifest/versions.json
  failIfStale: true    # Fail if IR drifts despite unchanged sources
```

## `runtime`

<ParamField path="runtime" type="object">
  Central runtime build knobs that affect generated companion factories and execution mode. These settings are consumed by projections (Next.js, Express, Hono, Remix, SvelteKit) and by the generated runtime helpers.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="runtime.executionMode" type="string" default="inline">
  Dispatcher execution mode for the Next.js projection. Accepted values: `inline`, `externalExecutor`.

  - `inline` (default): Generated handlers call `createManifestRuntime(...)` then `runtime.runCommand(...)` per request.
  - `externalExecutor`: Handlers import a configured executor and delegate — no `createManifestRuntime` or `runtime.runCommand` appears in emitted code.

  See [`projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher`](#projectionsnextjsoptionsdispatcher) for the executor migration guide.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="runtime.determinism" type="object">
  Determinism settings for the generated runtime factory.

  <ParamField path="runtime.determinism.deterministicMode" type="boolean" default="false">
    When `true`, the factory injects `RuntimeOptions.deterministicMode` (fixed clock, seeded `generateId`). Useful for reproducible tests.
  </ParamField>

  <ParamField path="runtime.determinism.forbidWallClock" type="boolean" default="false">
    When `true`, the factory requires `RuntimeOptions.now` to be provided (no `Date.now` fallback). Enforces explicit time control.
  </ParamField>

  <ParamField path="runtime.determinism.seed" type="number">
    Fixed clock milliseconds when `forbidWallClock` is `true`. Also seeds the `generateId` sequence. Ignored unless `forbidWallClock` is set.
  </ParamField>
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="runtime.stores" type="string">
  Import path for store bindings (`ManifestRuntimeConfig` module). Fans into projection `runtimeConfigImport` when that projection-specific key is unset. Used by Next.js, Express, Hono, Remix, and SvelteKit projections.

  ```yaml
  runtime:
    stores: './manifest.config'  # Points to the TS config with store bindings
  ```
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="runtime.defaultContext" type="object">
  Default runtime context merged into `createManifestRuntime` calls (caller-provided context wins on conflict). Useful for global constants like `appVersion` or `region`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="runtime.concurrency" type="object">
  Parallel execution settings.

  <ParamField path="runtime.concurrency.maxParallelCommands" type="integer">
    Maps to `RuntimeOptions.maxParallelCommands` on the generated factory. Positive integer only; invalid values are ignored (unlimited parallelism).
  </ParamField>
</ParamField>

## `projections`

The `projections` block configures code generation per target. Each key matches a registered projection name. The keys validated by the config schema are `nextjs`, `routes`, `prisma`, and `prisma-store`; the `nextjs` projection is documented in full below, with `prisma`/`prisma-store` summarized at the end of this section. (Many more projections are registered — zod, drizzle, convex, openapi, kysely, graphql, and others — and are driven entirely by CLI flags; only the four above accept structured config blocks today.)

### `projections.nextjs.output`

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.output" type="string" default="generated/">
  Directory where the Next.js projection writes generated route files. Used by `manifest generate` and `manifest build` as the default `--output` / `--code-output` value.

```yaml
projections:
  nextjs:
    output: app/api/
```

</ParamField>

### `projections.nextjs.options`

These options control the import paths and filtering behavior written into generated route files.

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.authProvider" type="string" default="none">
  Auth provider name. Used when `authImportPath` is not set. Accepted values: `clerk`, `nextauth`, `custom`, `none`. Defaults to `none` so a basic entity generates runnable code with no auth wrapper — opt into a provider explicitly.

```yaml
options:
  authProvider: clerk
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.authImportPath" type="string">
  Import path for your auth helper. When set, this takes precedence over `authProvider`. Can be a local alias (`@/lib/auth`) or a workspace package (`@repo/auth/server`).

```yaml
options:
  authImportPath: '@repo/auth/server'
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.databaseImportPath" type="string" default="@/lib/database">
  Import path for your database client, injected into generated route files.

```yaml
options:
  databaseImportPath: '@repo/database'
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.runtimeImportPath" type="string" default="@/lib/manifest-runtime">
  Import path for the Manifest runtime helper, injected into generated route files.

```yaml
options:
  runtimeImportPath: '@repo/manifest/runtime'
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.responseImportPath" type="string" default="@/lib/manifest-response">
  Import path for the Manifest response helper, injected into generated route files.

```yaml
options:
  responseImportPath: '@repo/manifest/response'
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.includeTenantFilter" type="boolean" default="false">
  When `true`, generated read routes include a `WHERE tenantId = <tenant>` filter and POST handlers resolve a `tenantId` before executing. Defaults to `false` (single-tenant). Set to `true` for multi-tenant applications.

```yaml
options:
  includeTenantFilter: true
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.includeSoftDeleteFilter" type="boolean" default="false">
  When `true`, generated read routes include a `WHERE deletedAt IS NULL` filter. Defaults to `false`. Set to `true` if your data model uses soft deletes.

```yaml
options:
  includeSoftDeleteFilter: true
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.tenantIdProperty" type="string" default="tenantId">
  Property name used for tenant scoping in generated filters.

```yaml
options:
  tenantIdProperty: tenantId
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.deletedAtProperty" type="string" default="deletedAt">
  Property name used for soft-delete filtering in generated queries.

```yaml
options:
  deletedAtProperty: deletedAt
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.appDir" type="string" default="app/api">
  App Router base directory. Every generated route's `pathHint` is relative to this. The default `app/api` suits a standard (non-monorepo) Next.js project. For a monorepo, set this to e.g. `apps/api/app/api`.

```yaml
options:
  appDir: apps/api/app/api
```

</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.strictMode" type="boolean" default="true">
  Whether generated TypeScript is written to be strict-mode friendly. Leave on unless you have a
  specific compatibility reason to disable it.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.includeComments" type="boolean" default="true">
  Whether to emit explanatory comments above generated handlers. Set `false` to slim generated
  files.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.indentSize" type="integer" default="2">
  Spaces of indentation in generated code. Must be between 1 and 8.
</ParamField>

### `projections.nextjs.options.tenantProvider`

Replaces the default `userTenantMapping.findUnique` pattern with a project-supplied lookup helper. When set, generated handlers import `functionName` from `importPath` and call it with the auth field named by `lookupKey`.

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.tenantProvider.importPath" type="string" required>
  Module path that exports the tenant lookup helper.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.tenantProvider.functionName" type="string" required>
  Named export to call (e.g. `getTenantIdForOrg`).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.tenantProvider.lookupKey" type="string" required>
  Which auth-context field is passed as the lookup argument. Accepted values: `orgId`, `userId`.
</ParamField>

```yaml
projections:
  nextjs:
    options:
      tenantProvider:
        importPath: '@my-app/data'
        functionName: 'getTenantIdForOrg'
        lookupKey: 'orgId'
```

### `projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher`

The dispatcher is the canonical Manifest write surface at `POST /api/manifest/[entity]/commands/[command]`. Two execution modes are supported, and either can be selected from config alone — no projection-source edits required.

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher.enabled" type="boolean" default="true">
  When `false`, the `nextjs.dispatcher` surface emits no artifact and the CLI surfaces an
  info-diagnostic.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher.executionMode" type="string" default="inline">
  Where command execution happens.

- `inline` (default, back-compat): handler calls `createManifestRuntime(...)` then `runtime.runCommand(...)` per request.
- `externalExecutor`: handler imports the configured executor and delegates — **no `createManifestRuntime` or `runtime.runCommand` appears in the emitted code.**
  </ParamField>

<ParamField
  path="projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher.executorImportPath"
  type="string"
  default="@/lib/manifest-executor"
>
  Module path for the external executor. Only used when `executionMode` is `externalExecutor`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField
  path="projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher.executorImportName"
  type="string"
  default="executeManifestCommand"
>
  Named export to call on the external executor module.
</ParamField>

<ParamField
  path="projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher.deriveInstanceId"
  type="boolean"
  default="true"
>
  When `true` (default), the dispatcher extracts an `instanceId` from `body.instanceId` or `body.id`
  and forwards it to `runCommand` (inline mode) or the configured executor (`externalExecutor`
  mode). Non-create commands (release, archive, update, …) need this to address the target instance;
  create commands ignore it harmlessly. Override to `false` only with strong reason.
</ParamField>

<ParamField
  path="projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher.path"
  type="string"
  default="/manifest/[entity]/commands/[command]/route.ts"
>
  Dispatcher route path relative to `appDir`. Override only for non-canonical prefixes (e.g.
  `/api/v1/manifest/...`).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.unauthorizedStatus" type="integer" default="401">
  HTTP status returned when the auth helper rejects the request OR when it throws (invalid/expired
  token). Must be in the 400–499 range. Auth failures MUST NEVER surface as 500. Override only if
  you standardise on 403 to avoid user-existence leak.
</ParamField>

### `projections.nextjs.options.concreteCommandRoutes`

Policy for the deprecated per-command "concrete" routes (the `nextjs.command` surface). These are **opt-in** in current releases — the canonical dispatcher is the only write surface by default.

<ParamField
  path="projections.nextjs.options.concreteCommandRoutes.enabled"
  type="boolean"
  default="false"
>
  **Default false (opt-in).** When `false`, `nextjs.command` artifacts are suppressed entirely and
  `manifest generate --surface all` does not emit them. Opt in only if you still need per-command
  routes for legacy callers.
</ParamField>

<ParamField
  path="projections.nextjs.options.concreteCommandRoutes.legacyAliasesOnly"
  type="boolean"
  default="true"
>
  When `true` (default) and `enabled` is `true`, generated concrete routes carry the `DEPRECATED
  ALIAS` banner pointing callers at the dispatcher. Set `false` only if you intentionally treat
  per-command routes as a first-class surface.
</ParamField>

### `projections.nextjs.options.readRoutes`

Policy for direct database read routes (`nextjs.route` for list, `nextjs.detail` for single-entity GET). Direct reads bypass the runtime engine for read performance and assume a Prisma-compatible client at `databaseImportPath`.

<ParamField path="projections.nextjs.options.readRoutes.enabled" type="boolean" default="true">
  When `false`, both list and detail read routes are suppressed (info diagnostic only).
</ParamField>

<ParamField
  path="projections.nextjs.options.readRoutes.directDbReads"
  type="boolean"
  default="true"
>
  When `false`, read route handlers are emitted but contain no inline Prisma call — useful for
  projects that route reads through a separate query layer.
</ParamField>

<Note>
**Prisma 7 note.** When `includeTenantFilter` and/or `includeSoftDeleteFilter` are true, the detail route emits `findFirst` (not `findUnique`). The previous template emitted `findUnique({ where: { id, tenantId, deletedAt: null } })`, which fails type-check on Prisma 7+. This is fixed in current releases.
</Note>

### `projections.prisma`

Configuration for the Prisma schema projection (emits `schema.prisma`). All option keys are optional.

<ParamField path="projections.prisma.output" type="string" default="schema.prisma">
  Path hint for the emitted `schema.prisma` artifact.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.prisma.options" type="object">
  Per-entity overrides for the emitted schema. Keys include `provider` (emits a datasource block plus a `prisma.config.ts` companion; one of `postgresql`, `mysql`, `sqlite`, `sqlserver`, `mongodb`, `cockroachdb`), `urlEnvVar` (default `DATABASE_URL`), `tableMappings`, `columnMappings`, `precision`, `indexes`, `typeMappings`, `foreignKeys`, `dbAttributes`, `fieldAttributes`, and a projection-local `naming` (see the top-level [`naming`](#param-naming) field — explicit `tableMappings`/`columnMappings` always win).

```yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    output: prisma/schema.prisma
    options:
      provider: postgresql
      urlEnvVar: DATABASE_URL
      naming: snake_case
      tableMappings:
        OrderLine: order_lines
```

</ParamField>

### `projections.prisma-store`

Configuration for the Prisma store metadata/registry projection (`GenericPrismaStore`). It emits a model-metadata module and a store-registry module, and extends every `projections.prisma.options` key for consistent naming with `schema.prisma` emission.

<ParamField
  path="projections.prisma-store.options.metadataOutput"
  type="string"
  default="prisma-model-metadata.generated.ts"
>
  Path hint for the emitted `prisma-model-metadata.generated.ts`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField
  path="projections.prisma-store.options.registryOutput"
  type="string"
  default="prisma-store-registry.generated.ts"
>
  Path hint for the emitted `prisma-store-registry.generated.ts`.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.prisma-store.options.accessorNames" type="object">
  Per-entity Prisma client delegate override (e.g. `{ "OrderLine": "orderLines" }`).
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.prisma-store.options.storeImportPath" type="string">
  Module path for the `GenericPrismaStore` import in generated output.
</ParamField>

<ParamField path="projections.prisma-store.options.metadataImportPath" type="string">
  Module path for the generated metadata import in the registry output.
</ParamField>

```yaml
projections:
  prisma-store:
    options:
      naming: snake_case
      metadataOutput: src/generated/prisma-model-metadata.generated.ts
      registryOutput: src/generated/prisma-store-registry.generated.ts
      accessorNames:
        OrderLine: orderLines
```

## externalExecutor migration

Use `executionMode: externalExecutor` when your app owns Manifest runtime construction (shared executor, custom store wiring, extra context) and wants the dispatcher to be a thin transport adapter.

**Step 1.** Author an executor in your app. Place it where `executorImportPath` points; the call shape below is the contract.

```ts
// apps/api/lib/manifest-executor.ts
import { createManifestRuntime } from './manifest-runtime';

export interface ManifestExecutorCall {
  entityName: string;
  commandName: string;
  input: unknown;
  instanceId?: string;
  context: {
    tenantId?: string;
    orgId?: string;
    actorId?: string;
    requestId?: string;
    source?: string;
    user?: Record<string, unknown>;
  };
}

export async function executeManifestCommand(call: ManifestExecutorCall) {
  const runtime = await createManifestRuntime(call.context);
  return runtime.runCommand(call.commandName, call.input, {
    entityName: call.entityName,
    ...(call.instanceId ? { instanceId: call.instanceId } : {}),
  });
}
```

**Step 2.** Flip config to point at your executor:

```yaml
projections:
  nextjs:
    options:
      dispatcher:
        executionMode: externalExecutor
        executorImportPath: '@my-app/manifest-executor'
        executorImportName: 'executeManifestCommand'
```

**Step 3.** Regenerate. The dispatcher route now imports your executor and delegates to it — no `createManifestRuntime` or `runtime.runCommand` in the emitted code.

**Step 4.** Confirm with the CLI:

```bash
manifest config inspect --json | jq '.projections.nextjs.options.dispatcher'
```

## CI / drift workflow

Downstream repos should commit `manifest.config.yaml` and pin generated output under VCS. The recommended four-step CI gate catches both Manifest default changes and local config drift:

```bash
# 1. Fail on invalid config.
manifest config validate

# 2. Snapshot the effective config (commit the snapshot under VCS).
manifest config inspect --json > .manifest/effective-config.snapshot.json
git diff --exit-code .manifest/effective-config.snapshot.json

# 3. Regenerate code from IR.
manifest build

# 4. Fail on drift between checked-in and just-generated code.
git diff --exit-code
```

This flow guarantees that the effective config has not silently changed and that emitted code matches what the current Manifest + config combination produces.
