Regenerate it after every compile, or set `emitCompanions: false` and maintain your own — for example to enrich the `user` object with fields your guards and policies read (e.g. `role`).

  </Step>

  <Step title="Wire up Clerk auth">
    If you chose `--auth clerk`, install the Clerk SDK and configure your middleware:

    ```bash
    npm install @clerk/nextjs
    ```

    Add `clerkMiddleware` to `middleware.ts`:

    ```typescript
    import { clerkMiddleware } from '@clerk/nextjs/server';

    export default clerkMiddleware();

    export const config = {
      matcher: ['/((?!_next|[^?]*\\.(?:html?|css|js(?!on)|jpe?g|webp|png|gif|svg|ttf|woff2?|ico|csv|docx?|xlsx?|zip|webmanifest)).*)', '/(api|trpc)(.*)'],
    };
    ```

    Set your environment variables in `.env.local`:

    ```bash
    NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY=pk_...
    CLERK_SECRET_KEY=sk_...
    ```

    The generated routes call `auth()` imported from `@clerk/nextjs` to resolve `userId` (and `orgId`). Use the `authImportPath` option to import it from somewhere else (e.g. `@clerk/nextjs/server`). No additional configuration is needed in the route files.

  </Step>
</Steps>

## Multi-tenant setup

Tenant filtering is **opt-in**: `includeTenantFilter` defaults to `false`, so generated routes contain no tenant logic until you enable it. Turn it on in `manifest.config.yaml` (use `tenantIdProperty` to filter on a different column, e.g. `orgId`):

```yaml
# manifest.config.yaml
projections:
  nextjs:
    output: .
    options:
      authProvider: clerk
      includeTenantFilter: true
      tenantIdProperty: tenantId
      tenantProvider:
        importPath: '@/lib/tenant'
        functionName: 'getTenantIdForOrg'
        lookupKey: 'orgId' # or "userId"
```

Then regenerate with `manifest generate --all`.

When enabled, every generated route resolves the tenant through your `tenantProvider` function before touching data:

```typescript
// Emitted into generated routes when includeTenantFilter is true
const tenantId = await getTenantIdForOrg(orgId);

if (!tenantId) {
  return manifestErrorResponse({ error: 'Tenant not found', diagnostics: [] }, 400);
}

const recipes = await database.recipe.findMany({
  where: { tenantId },
  orderBy: { id: 'desc' },
});
```

You implement the provider function (shape: `(id: string) => Promise<string | null>`) — for example, backed by a `userTenantMapping` table keyed by `userId` if you set `lookupKey: "userId"`.

## Workspace / monorepo setup

In a monorepo, it is common to compile the IR once and share it across packages. A typical layout:

```
apps/
  web/           # Next.js app — consumes generated routes and client SDK
packages/
  domain/        # recipe.manifest, build script, generated IR artifact
  api-types/     # generated ts.types output, shared across apps
```

In `packages/domain`, declare the sources and projection targets in `manifest.config.yaml` instead of writing a build script by hand:

```yaml
# packages/domain/manifest.config.yaml
src: '*.manifest'
output: 'ir/'

projections:
  nextjs:
    output: '../../apps/web'
    options:
      authProvider: clerk
      # Redirect shared artifacts into the types package
      # (path is relative to the projection output above).
      paths:
        typesFile: '../../packages/api-types/src/recipe.ts'
```

Then the whole pipeline is two config-driven commands:

```bash
cd packages/domain
manifest compile --all     # compiles every source into ir/merged.ir.json
manifest generate --all    # runs every projection declared in the config
```

Routes land in `apps/web/app/api/`, the dispatcher in `apps/web/app/api/manifest/[entity]/commands/[command]/route.ts`, and types in `packages/api-types/src/recipe.ts`.

In `apps/web`, import the compiled IR directly:

```typescript
// apps/web/lib/manifest-ir.ts
import ir from '@repo/domain/ir/merged.ir.json';
export { ir };
```

## Listening to events in route handlers

Register an `onEvent` listener before calling `runCommand` to tap into emitted events. This is useful for publishing to an outbox or triggering webhooks:

```typescript
const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: userId, role: 'author' },
});

const unsubscribe = runtime.onEvent((event) => {
  console.log(event.name, event.channel, event.payload);
  // Enqueue to your outbox, publish to Pub/Sub, etc.
});

try {
  const result = await runtime.runCommand(
    'publishRecipe',
    {},
    { entityName: 'Recipe', instanceId: params.id },
  );

  if (!result.success) {
    return manifestErrorResponse(result.error ?? 'Command failed', 422);
  }

  return manifestSuccessResponse({ events: result.emittedEvents });
} finally {
  unsubscribe();
}
```

## Realtime subscriptions (SSE)

Entities flagged with the `realtime` modifier unlock three additional surfaces: an SSE subscription route (`nextjs.subscribe`), a typed auto-reconnecting React hook (`nextjs.subscriptionHook`), and a shared singleton runtime accessor (`nextjs.sharedRuntime`) that lets command routes and subscriptions observe the same event stream:

```manifest
entity Recipe {
  realtime
  property required id: string
  // ...
}
```

```typescript
const projection = new NextJsProjection();
const sseRoute = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'nextjs.subscribe', entity: 'Recipe' });
const hook = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'nextjs.subscriptionHook', entity: 'Recipe' });
const shared = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'nextjs.sharedRuntime' });
```

When any entity is realtime, generated command routes (inline mode) automatically use the shared runtime via `getSharedRuntime()` instead of constructing one per request, so subscribers see events emitted by commands. This requires a long-lived, single-instance server. See [Realtime Subscriptions](/extensibility/realtime-subscriptions) for the full contract.

## Common issues

<AccordionGroup>
  <Accordion title='"Entity not found in IR"'>
    Entity names are case-sensitive. Verify that the name you pass to `runCommand` exactly matches the entity name declared in your `.manifest` file.
  </Accordion>

<Accordion title="Guard fails even with the correct user">
  Guards reference `user.*` from the runtime context, not from the request body. The generated
  `@/lib/manifest-runtime` forwards the context it receives; if your guards read fields the
  dispatcher's auth step doesn't supply (e.g. `role`), enrich the context — either in a custom
  runtime module (`emitCompanions: false`) or by adjusting your auth provider wiring. If the guard
  references `user.role` and you did not supply it, the failure is correct behavior.
</Accordion>

<Accordion title="Generated route imports are wrong">
  The generated code uses import paths like `@/lib/database`, `@/lib/manifest-response`, and
  `@/lib/manifest-runtime`. The generator emits these companion modules itself (the `companions`
  surface, included in `--surface all`), so a fresh generate compiles without hand-written files —
  this error usually means the companions were generated into a different directory than your `@/*`
  alias points at, or generation ran with `emitCompanions: false`. Custom `databaseImportPath`,
  `responseImportPath`, and `runtimeImportPath` options relocate both the imports and the emitted
  modules; paths pointing at npm packages are left to you.
</Accordion>

<Accordion title="Routes don't match my react-query hooks">
  Both projections derive each entity's default URL segment from its name, and the casing must
  agree. Pass the same `routeCasing` option to both generators: `'lowercase'` (default — `PrepTask`
  → `preptask`), `'kebab-case'` (`prep-task`), `'snake_case'` (`prep_task`), or `'preserve'`
  (`PrepTask`). Explicit `routeSegments` / `entityRoutes` overrides still take precedence. Use
  `dateSerialization: 'iso-string'` (default `'date'`) when `date`/`datetime` fields cross the wire
  as ISO-8601 strings rather than `Date` instances.
</Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Tenant filtering returns no results">
    Tenant filtering is off by default (`includeTenantFilter: false`). If you enabled it and get no results, confirm that your database rows have the tenant column populated and that your `tenantProvider` function returns the correct value. To rule the filter out during development, remove `includeTenantFilter: true` and regenerate.
  </Accordion>
</AccordionGroup>


---

## overview

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/integration/overview

---
title: 'Integrating Manifest into your application'
sidebarTitle: 'Integration overview'
description: 'Choose between Projections and the Embedded Runtime to integrate Manifest. Learn when to use each pattern and how to keep identity fields safe.'
---

Manifest gives you two distinct integration patterns: **Projections**, which generate platform-specific code from your compiled IR, and the **Embedded Runtime**, which lets you drive command execution directly inside your own handlers. Most production applications use both.

This page explains the boundary between them, how to decide which to reach for first, and a security rule that applies to both.

## The two patterns

<Tabs>
  <Tab title="Projections">
    Projections are a **code-generation tool**, not a change to language semantics. They read your compiled Intermediate Representation (IR) and emit artifacts—Next.js App Router routes, TypeScript types, and a client SDK—that you can drop straight into your project.

    ```bash
    # Compile + generate all surfaces for your .manifest file
    manifest build recipe.manifest --projection nextjs --code-output app/api/

    # Or use the CLI to generate individual surfaces from compiled IR
    manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface route --output app/api/
    manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface command --output app/api/
    ```

    ~~The four generated surfaces are:~~

    ~~| Surface | What it produces |
    | --- | --- |
    | `nextjs.route` | GET handlers with direct database queries |
    | `nextjs.command` | POST/PUT/DELETE handlers that call `RuntimeEngine.runCommand` |
    | `ts.types` | TypeScript interfaces derived from your entity definitions |
    | `ts.client` | Typed fetch wrappers for calling your routes from client code |~~

    > **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** The Next.js projection registers **twelve** surfaces
    > (`nextjs.route`, `nextjs.detail`, `nextjs.command`, `nextjs.dispatcher`,
    > `nextjs.subscribe`, `nextjs.subscriptionHook`, `nextjs.sharedRuntime`, `nextjs.schedule`,
    > `nextjs.webhook`, `nextjs.companions`, `ts.types`, `ts.client`). Default `--surface all`
    > writes go through the **command dispatcher** (`nextjs.dispatcher` →
    > `app/api/manifest/[entity]/commands/[command]/route.ts`); per-command concrete routes
    > (`nextjs.command`) are opt-in via `concreteCommandRoutes.enabled`. Other registered
    > projections (`prisma`, `convex`, `zod`, …) are selected with `-p` / `manifest.config.yaml`.

    **Read operations** in generated routes use direct database queries and intentionally bypass the runtime for performance. **Write operations** always flow through `RuntimeEngine.runCommand` so that guards, policies, constraints, and event emissions run in the order the spec requires.

  </Tab>
  <Tab title="Embedded Runtime">
    The Embedded Runtime lets you instantiate `RuntimeEngine` directly in your own handlers and call `runCommand` yourself. You control request parsing, response shaping, event routing, and any custom side-effect pipeline.

    ```typescript
    import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';

    const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
      user: { id: 'user-1', role: 'admin' },
      requestId: 'req-1',
    });

    const result = await runtime.runCommand(
      'approve',
      { reason: 'validated' },
      { entityName: 'Invoice', instanceId: 'inv-1' }
    );

    if (!result.success) {
      // result.error, result.guardFailure, result.policyDenial
      // Surface these in your response — do not alter execution semantics.
    }
    ```

    The runtime enforces the full execution order on every `runCommand` call:

    1. Build evaluation context (`self`, `this`, params, runtime context)
    2. Evaluate rate limits — returns `rateLimitExceeded` immediately if exceeded
    3. Evaluate applicable policies
    4. Evaluate command constraints — `block`-severity violations halt execution
    5. Evaluate guards in declared order — halts on the first failure
    6. Execute actions in declared order
    7. Emit declared events
    8. Return `CommandResult`

  </Tab>
</Tabs>

## Decision guide

Use **Projections** when:

- You want generated route code that follows Next.js App Router conventions without writing boilerplate by hand.
- Your mutation flow can be expressed as a named Manifest command and you are comfortable with the generated handler structure.

Use the **Embedded Runtime** when:

- You need custom orchestration around command execution — for example, composing multiple commands, injecting computed context, or conditionally routing results.
- You need a custom event-handling pipeline: publishing to a queue, writing to an outbox, or triggering side effects in a specific order.

<Note>
  These patterns are not mutually exclusive. A common setup uses generated routes for
  straightforward CRUD entities and the Embedded Runtime for commands that require complex
  orchestration.
</Note>

## Security: never trust client-supplied identity fields

This rule applies regardless of which pattern you use.

When a command is executed from an HTTP handler, the request body is **untrusted**. A client can supply any value for `id`, `userId`, `tenantId`, `orgId`, or `user`. If those values flow unmodified into `runCommand`, a caller can act on behalf of another user or mutate a resource they do not own.

**Always strip client-supplied identity fields and inject authoritative values from your auth context and URL path parameters before calling `runCommand`.**

```typescript
// Correct: derive identity from trusted server-side sources
const { userId } = await auth(); // from your auth provider
const { id: entityId } = params; // from the URL path param

const body = await request.json();

// Strip any identity fields the client may have sent
const {
  id: _id,
  userId: _userId,
  tenantId: _tenantId,
  orgId: _orgId,
  user: _user,
  ...safeInput
} = body;

// Inject authoritative identity before execution
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'update',
  { ...safeInput, userId, entityId },
  { entityName: 'Invoice', instanceId: entityId },
);
```

<Warning>
  Do not pass `await request.json()` directly to `runCommand` without stripping identity fields.
  This is the most common source of privilege-escalation bugs in Manifest-backed APIs.
</Warning>

## Next steps

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="Projections" icon="wand-sparkles" href="/integration/projections">
    Learn how to use the projection API programmatically and understand the four generated surfaces.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Embedded Runtime" icon="cpu" href="/integration/embedded-runtime">
    Instantiate RuntimeEngine, handle CommandResult variants, and wire up event handlers.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Next.js guide" icon="triangle" href="/integration/nextjs">
    End-to-end walkthrough: install, write a `.manifest` file, generate routes, and add auth.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>


---

## Single source file

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/integration/prisma

---
title: 'Prisma schema projection'
description: "Generate a Prisma schema from Manifest IR. Compile-time only, app-agnostic, with full @relation wiring, decimal defaults for money, and structured diagnostics for everything the projection can't safely emit."
sidebarTitle: 'Prisma'
---

The **Prisma projection** reads compiled Manifest IR and emits a `schema.prisma` artifact. It is compile-time only — it does not connect to a database, run migrations, or know anything about any specific application. All relational concepts (table names, column names, decimal precision, composite indexes, foreign-key field names) live in projection config; Manifest core stays backend-neutral.

The projection ships **inside the main `@angriff36/manifest` package** — no separate install needed. Running `manifest generate -p prisma` works out of the box.

<Note>
  An earlier pre-release explored shipping the projection as `@manifest/projection-prisma`. That
  package was never published. Since v1.0.0 the projection is bundled in `@angriff36/manifest` with
  the same internal separation (`src/manifest/projections/prisma/`) as the Next.js and routes
  projections.
</Note>

For programmatic use, import from the main package:

```ts
import { PrismaProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections/prisma';
```

## Quickstart

A minimal `.manifest` source:

```manifest widget.manifest
entity Widget {
  property required id: string
  property required name: string
  property required qty: int
  property required unitCost: money
}

store Widget in durable
```

A minimal `manifest.config.yaml`:

```yaml manifest.config.yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    output: generated/
    options:
      provider: postgresql
```

Compile and generate:

```bash
manifest compile widget.manifest
manifest generate ir/ -p prisma -s prisma.schema -o generated/
```

Emitted `generated/schema.prisma`:

```prisma
// Auto-generated by @angriff36/manifest
// DO NOT EDIT — regenerate with the projection.

datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"
}

generator client {
  provider = "prisma-client-js"
}

model Widget {
  id String @id
  name String
  qty Int
  unitCost Decimal @db.Decimal(12, 2)
}
```

Emitted `generated/prisma.config.ts` (Prisma 7+ — URL lives here, not in `schema.prisma`):

```typescript
// Auto-generated by @angriff36/manifest
// Prisma 7+: connection URL lives here, not in schema.prisma.
import { defineConfig } from 'prisma/config';

export default defineConfig({
  datasources: { db: { url: process.env.DATABASE_URL } },
});
```

## What gets emitted, what gets skipped

| IR shape                                                               | Projection behavior                                        |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `entity X { ... }` + `store X in durable` (or `postgres` / `supabase`) | Emits a Prisma `model`                                     |
| `entity X { ... }` + `store X in memory` (or `localStorage`)           | **Skipped** with `PRISMA_SKIPPED_NON_DURABLE` info         |
| `entity X { ... }` with no `store` declaration                         | **Skipped** with `PRISMA_SKIPPED_NO_STORE` info            |
| `external entity X { ... }` (sets `external: true` in IR)              | **Skipped** with `PRISMA_SKIPPED_EXTERNAL` info            |
| `property` entries                                                     | Emitted as Prisma fields                                   |
| `computed` entries                                                     | **Never emitted** — derived values must not become columns |

Entities are iterated in IR source order; the projection does not re-sort.

~~<Warning>
  A property literally named `id` becomes the `@id` field. Entities without an `id` property get an
  info diagnostic (`PRISMA_NO_ID_PROPERTY`) because Prisma's schema validator will reject a model
  with no primary key.
</Warning>~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** A property literally named `id` becomes `@id` **unless**
> the entity declares a composite `key [...]`, which emits `@@id([...])` and suppresses
> `PRISMA_NO_ID_PROPERTY`. That diagnostic fires only when there is **neither** an `id` property
> **nor** a composite key (`src/manifest/projections/prisma/generator.ts`).

## Type mapping

The IR carries `type.name` as an arbitrary string (Manifest's type vocabulary is open). The projection holds the mapping table:

| IR `type.name`                | Prisma scalar                                 |
| ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| `string`, `text`, `uuid`      | `String`                                      |
| `boolean`, `bool`             | `Boolean`                                     |
| `int`                         | `Int`                                         |
| `bigint`                      | `BigInt`                                      |
| `float`                       | `Float`                                       |
| `decimal`, `money`            | `Decimal` (with default `@db.Decimal(12, 2)`) |
| `date`, `datetime`, `timestamp` | `DateTime`                                    |
| `time`                        | `DateTime`                                    |
| `duration`                    | `Float`                                       |
| `json`                        | `Json`                                        |
| `bytes`                       | `Bytes`                                       |
| anything else                 | **diagnostic** (`PRISMA_UNKNOWN_TYPE`)        |

The default mapping does **not** include `number`. That is deliberate.

## The `number` ambiguity rule

Manifest's `number` type does not distinguish integers from real numbers from money. Silently mapping it to `Float` is the class of silent rounding bug this project exists to prevent — especially for currency. So the projection refuses to guess:

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property required total: number  // ← ambiguous
}
```

The projection emits an error diagnostic:

```
PRISMA_AMBIGUOUS_NUMBER (Order):
  Property 'Order.total' is typed 'number', which is ambiguous
  (Manifest does not distinguish integers from real numbers from money).
  Pick a precise type in the .manifest source: 'int' or 'bigint' for
  counts and ids, 'float' for measurements where rounding is acceptable,
  'money' or 'decimal' for currency and other exact-decimal values.
  Or supply a 'typeMappings.Order.total' override.
```

The ambiguous column is **skipped** in the output. Fix the source:

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property required total: money  // ← exact-decimal, picks up @db.Decimal(12, 2)
}
```

<Note>
  The escape hatch is the projection config's `typeMappings` — supply an explicit Prisma scalar
  there and the diagnostic disappears. Useful when migrating a legacy `.manifest` file that already
  used `number` for a field whose precision intent is recorded elsewhere.
</Note>

## Money and decimal precision

`money` and `decimal` both resolve to Prisma `Decimal`. Either gets a default `@db.Decimal(12, 2)` — the conservative money default (up to 9,999,999,999.99 with cent precision).

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required total: money       // → Decimal @db.Decimal(12, 2)
  property required fxRate: decimal    // → Decimal @db.Decimal(12, 2)
}
```

Override per-property via `precision`:

```yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    options:
      precision:
        Order:
          fxRate: { precision: 18, scale: 8 } # → @db.Decimal(18, 8)
```

The rule is keyed on the **resolved Prisma scalar**, not the IR type name. A property routed to `Decimal` via `typeMappings` picks up the same default precision.

## Relationships

All four IR relationship kinds emit real Prisma `@relation`:

| IR                  | Emitted Prisma                                                           | Notes                                     |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------- |
| `hasMany name: T`   | `name T[]`                                                               | Parent side of 1:N                        |
| `hasOne name: T`    | `name T?`                                                                | Parent side of 1:1                        |
| `belongsTo name: T` | `nameId String` + `name T @relation(fields: [nameId], references: [id])` | Child side; FK type follows target's `id` |
| `ref name: T`       | same shape as `belongsTo`                                                | "Loose" — no missing-backside warning     |

### 1:1 vs 1:N

The projection detects the cardinality from the opposite side:

```manifest
entity User {
  property required id: string
  hasOne profile: Profile        // 1:1 — declares opposite as hasOne
}

entity Profile {
  property required id: string
  belongsTo user: User           // child side → FK gets @unique
}
```

Emits:

```prisma
model User {
  id String @id
  profile Profile?
}

model Profile {
  id String @id
  userId String @unique          // ← @unique because opposite is hasOne
  user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}
```

For 1:N (parent side is `hasMany`), the FK is **not** `@unique`.

### Foreign key field name

Default is `${relationshipName}Id` (camelCase). Override at IR level via the `foreignKey` annotation, or at projection-config level via `foreignKeys`:

```yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    options:
      foreignKeys:
        Book:
          author: writerId # → Book.writerId instead of Book.authorId
```

The FK type follows the target entity's `id` property type. A `string` PK gets a `String` FK; an `int` PK gets an `Int` FK. No type mismatches.

### Foreign key columns honor `columnMappings`

FK fields are virtual properties from the consumer's perspective; the existing `columnMappings` knob applies:

```yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    options:
      columnMappings:
        Book:
          authorId: author_id # → authorId String @map("author_id")
```

### `belongsTo` alongside an explicit FK property

If your `.manifest` source declares the FK property explicitly **and** declares the relationship, the projection deduplicates — it does NOT emit a second column with the same name:

```manifest
entity Book {
  property required id: string
  property required authorId: string    // ← explicit FK column
  belongsTo author: Author              // ← relationship using that column
}
```

Emits:

```prisma
model Book {
  id String @id
  authorId String                        // ← from the property declaration
  author Author @relation(fields: [authorId], references: [id])
}
```

The relation step sees that `authorId` already exists as a property and reuses it. The FK column line is **not** re-emitted; the `@relation(fields: [authorId], ...)` clause still wires it.

If you supply both an explicit FK property **and** a `foreignKeys` config override that points the relationship at a different column name, the override wins for the relation field — but the explicit property still emits as its own column, so you'll end up with both columns in the model. Pick one approach per relationship.

### Self-relations

A one-sided self-relation triggers `PRISMA_RELATION_MISSING_BACKSIDE`:

```manifest
entity Tree {
  property required id: string
  belongsTo parent: Tree   // no `children` declared → warning
}
```

A two-sided self-relation (`parent` + `children` on the same entity) is the textbook case where Prisma requires `@relation("name")` to disambiguate which `belongsTo` matches which `hasMany`. The projection refuses to invent names and emits `PRISMA_RELATION_AMBIGUOUS` for both sides — hand-add the relation names after generation, or refactor to a single relation:

```prisma
model Tree {
  id String @id
  parentId String?
  parent Tree? @relation("TreeHierarchy", fields: [parentId], references: [id])
  children Tree[] @relation("TreeHierarchy")
}
```

### MongoDB provider

When `provider: mongodb`, Prisma's Mongo connector requires the `@id` column to map to `_id` with ObjectId semantics. The projection applies the canonical pattern automatically:

```yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    options:
      provider: mongodb
```

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property required total: money
}
store Order in durable
```

Emits:

```prisma
model Order {
  id String @id @default(auto()) @map("_id") @db.ObjectId
  total Decimal @db.Decimal(12, 2)
}
```

The Mongo-specific attributes are auto-applied only when you have NOT supplied your own `columnMappings.<Entity>.id` or a literal `defaultValue` on the `id` property. If you want a non-ObjectId pattern (e.g. plain string ULIDs), override via `typeMappings.<Entity>.id` to something other than `String` — the projection will skip the ObjectId attribute but still emit `@map("_id")`.

## Configuration options

All per-property options use the nested `Record<EntityName, Record<PropertyName, ...>>` shape. There are no flattened `"Entity.property"` dotted-string keys anywhere in the config.

```yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    # Path hint for the emitted artifact (default: schema.prisma).
    output: generated/schema.prisma

    options:
      # Optional Prisma datasource provider. When set, the projection
      # emits a `datasource` block at the top of the artifact.
      provider: postgresql # one of: postgresql | mysql | sqlite | sqlserver | mongodb | cockroachdb

      # Per-entity table-name override → @@map("...").
      tableMappings:
        Order: orders
        Customer: customers

      # Per-entity, per-property column-name override → @map("...").
      columnMappings:
        Order:
          createdAt: created_at
          updatedAt: updated_at

      # Automatic identifier casing (opt-in). Emits @map/@@map for any name
      # not already in the target case, so you don't hand-write the mappings
      # above for every field. Explicit tableMappings/columnMappings still win.
      naming: snake_case # shorthand: snake_case columns + pluralized snake_case tables
      # Object form:
      # naming:
      #   table: snake_case        # snake_case | camelCase | PascalCase | preserve
      #   column: snake_case       # snake_case | camelCase | preserve
      #   pluralizeTables: true    # Widget → widgets (default true)

      # Per-entity, per-property decimal precision/scale.
      # Applies to any property whose resolved scalar is Decimal.
      precision:
        Order:
          total: { precision: 14, scale: 2 }
          fxRate: { precision: 18, scale: 8 }

      # Per-entity composite/named index definitions → @@index([...]).
      indexes:
        Order:
          - ['customerId', 'createdAt']
          - { fields: ['status'], name: 'order_status_idx' }

      # Per-entity, per-property type override. Bypasses the default
      # IR-type-name → Prisma-scalar mapping. Value is the literal
      # Prisma scalar (e.g. "Int", "BigInt", "Decimal", "DateTime").
      typeMappings:
        Order:
          legacyAmount: Decimal # opt out of PRISMA_AMBIGUOUS_NUMBER for a `number` field

      # Per-entity, per-relationship FK field name override.
      # Overrides default `${relationshipName}Id` and IR `foreignKey` annotation.
      # Value is a plain string (field name) or a structured object with referential actions.
      foreignKeys:
        Order:
          customer: customerRef # → Order.customerRef instead of Order.customerId
          # Structured form — includes referential actions:
          # customer:
          #   fields: [customerId]
          #   references: [id]
          #   onDelete: Cascade
          #   onUpdate: NoAction

      # Per-entity, per-property native @db.* attribute (without the @db. prefix).
      # Applied after the Prisma scalar but before @default.
      # Use for types the projection can't infer, or to override the default @db.Decimal(12,2).
      dbAttributes:
        Order:
          amount: Decimal(14, 2) # → @db.Decimal(14, 2)
          externalId: VarChar(36) # → @db.VarChar(36)

      # Per-entity, per-property array of verbatim Prisma field attributes.
      # Appended after type and dbAttributes. Use for @unique, @updatedAt, @default(...), etc.
      fieldAttributes:
        Order:
          code: ['@unique']
          updatedAt: ['@updatedAt', '@default(now())']

      # Environment variable name for the database URL in the emitted prisma.config.ts.
      # Default: DATABASE_URL. Only relevant when `provider` is set.
      urlEnvVar: POSTGRES_CONNECTION_STRING
```

## Automatic casing (the `naming` convention)

By default the projection emits IR names verbatim — to render camelCase IR identifiers as `snake_case` database names you'd hand-write a `columnMappings` entry per field. The `naming` option automates that:

```yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    options:
      naming: snake_case
```

`createdAt` emits `@map("created_at")`, `Widget` emits `@@map("widgets")`. The shorthand `snake_case` expands to `{ table: snake_case, column: snake_case, pluralizeTables: true }`; the object form tunes each axis independently.

**The convention only ever adds `@map`/`@@map`.** The Prisma _model name_ and _field identifiers_ stay as the IR name, so relation `fields`/`references`, `@@id`, `@@unique`, and `@@index` are unaffected — only the physical database name changes, and a `@map`/`@@map` is emitted only when the physical name actually differs (so `id` stays bare):

```prisma
model UserAccount {
  id          String   @id
  createdAt   DateTime @map("created_at")
  displayName String   @map("display_name")

  @@map("user_accounts")
}
```

Resolution order per name:

1. explicit `tableMappings` / `columnMappings` (always win — also the **escape hatch** for irregular plurals the built-in pluralizer gets wrong)
2. the `naming` convention
3. the IR name verbatim

A **global** `naming` can also be set once at the top level of `manifest.config.yaml` and is inherited by the Prisma projection; a per-projection `projections.prisma.options.naming` overrides the global default.

## `prisma.config.ts` companion

When `provider` is set, the projection emits **two artifacts** from a single `generate()` call:

| Index          | Path hint          | Content                                                   |
| -------------- | ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| `artifacts[0]` | `schema.prisma`    | The full Prisma schema                                    |
| `artifacts[1]` | `prisma.config.ts` | Prisma 7+ config file with `url: process.env.<urlEnvVar>` |

Prisma 7 moved the connection URL out of `schema.prisma` into a `prisma.config.ts` file. The projection handles this automatically so `prisma migrate` works without edits:

```typescript
// prisma.config.ts — emitted automatically when provider is set
import { defineConfig } from 'prisma/config';

export default defineConfig({
  datasources: { db: { url: process.env.DATABASE_URL } },
});
```

Customize the env var name via `urlEnvVar`:

```yaml
projections:
  prisma:
    options:
      provider: postgresql
      urlEnvVar: POSTGRES_CONNECTION_STRING
```

Without `provider`, only `schema.prisma` is emitted (no datasource block, no `prisma.config.ts`). Use this when you manage datasource configuration externally.

## Diagnostics reference

The projection emits structured diagnostics rather than silently doing the wrong thing. Each carries an entity name (where applicable) and a resolution hint. The CLI's `manifest generate` command **exits non-zero** when any error-severity diagnostic is returned, so CI pipelines detect generation failures.

| Code                                        | Severity | Trigger                                                                                                                               | Resolution                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `PRISMA_AMBIGUOUS_NUMBER`                   | error    | Property typed `number` with no `typeMappings` override                                                                               | Pick `int`/`bigint`/`float`/`money`/`decimal` in source, or supply `typeMappings.<Entity>.<Property>` |
| `PRISMA_UNKNOWN_TYPE`                       | error    | `type.name` not in default mapping and not in `typeMappings`                                                                          | Add a `typeMappings` entry or change the source type                                                  |
| `PRISMA_NO_ID_PROPERTY`                     | info     | Entity has no property named `id`                                                                                                     | Add `property required id: string`                                                                    |
| `PRISMA_SKIPPED_EXTERNAL`                   | info     | Entity marked `external entity`                                                                                                       | Expected — projection intentionally skips external entities                                           |
| `PRISMA_SKIPPED_NO_STORE`                   | info     | Entity has no `store` declaration                                                                                                     | Add `store EntityName in durable` to opt in                                                           |
| `PRISMA_SKIPPED_NON_DURABLE`                | info     | Store target is `memory` or `localStorage`                                                                                            | Flip to `durable` (or `postgres`/`supabase`) to opt in                                                |
| `PRISMA_RELATION_VIA_THROUGH_UNIMPLEMENTED` | info     | `hasMany ... through X` (explicit M2M)                                                                                                | Declare the join entity (`X`) with its own belongsTo relations                                        |
| `PRISMA_RELATION_AMBIGUOUS`                 | info     | Multiple relationships between same entity pair (including two-sided self-relations like `parent` + `children` on the same type)      | Refactor to a single relation, or hand-edit `@relation("name")` after generation                      |
| `PRISMA_RELATION_MISSING_BACKSIDE`          | warning  | One-sided `hasMany` / `hasOne` / `belongsTo` / `ref` — all four emit a full `@relation` that Prisma rejects without the opposite side | Declare the matching relation on the other entity                                                     |
| `PRISMA_RELATION_TARGET_NOT_EMITTED`        | warning  | Relationship targets an entity that is not emitted as a Prisma model (external entity, memory/localStorage store, no store)           | Declare the target as a durable entity, or remove the relationship                                    |

## What the projection deliberately does NOT do

These are not bugs — they're scope boundaries:

- **No database connection.** The projection is pure IR → text. Running migrations is your job (or Prisma's).
- **No `@relation("name")` auto-generation.** Multi-relation pairs require named `@relation` clauses; the projection refuses to invent names. Diagnose, don't guess.
- **No join-entity inference for `through` relationships.** Manifest's `through` annotation declares the join model, but Prisma needs the join entity to be a fully-modeled entity with its own belongsTo declarations. The user provides those.
- ~~**No `id` modifier.** Primary-key designation is by literal property name (`id`). A dedicated `@id` modifier in core grammar is a possible future addition; the projection will pick it up when it lands.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Primary key is still by literal `id` **or** a
> composite `key [...]` declaration (`@@id`). There is still no grammar `@id` modifier.
- **No app coupling.** No defaults reference tenant columns, soft-delete columns, auth fields, or any other application-specific concept.

## CLI usage

The projection registers automatically; `manifest generate -p prisma` works out of the box:

```bash
# Single source file
manifest compile orders.manifest
manifest generate ir/ -p prisma -s prisma.schema -o generated/

# One-step build
manifest build orders.manifest -p prisma -s prisma.schema --code-output generated/
```

The CLI's `--projection` flag accepts `prisma` alongside `nextjs`. The surface name `prisma.schema` is the projection's single emission surface.

## Programmatic usage

For build scripts that need direct control:

```typescript
import { PrismaProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections/prisma';
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
import fs from 'fs/promises';

const source = await fs.readFile('orders.manifest', 'utf8');
const { ir, diagnostics: compileDiags } = await compileToIR(source);

if (!ir) {
  console.error('Compilation failed:', compileDiags);
  process.exit(1);
}

const projection = new PrismaProjection();
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'prisma.schema',
  options: {
    provider: 'postgresql',
    tableMappings: { Order: 'orders' },
    precision: { Order: { total: { precision: 14, scale: 2 } } },
  },
});

for (const d of result.diagnostics) {
  console[d.severity === 'error' ? 'error' : 'log'](`[${d.code}] ${d.message}`);
}

const errors = result.diagnostics.filter((d) => d.severity === 'error');
if (errors.length > 0) process.exit(1);

for (const artifact of result.artifacts) {
  await fs.writeFile(artifact.pathHint!, artifact.code, 'utf8');
}
```

## Related

- [Stores](/language/stores) — the `durable` store target the projection emits for
- [Entities](/language/entities) — the `external entity` modifier the projection skips
- [Projections overview](/integration/projections) — projection pattern, contract, and the Next.js projection


---

## Whole-project (config-driven) — preferred

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/integration/projections

---
title: 'Generate API routes with Manifest projections'
sidebarTitle: 'Projections'
description: 'Projections are a code-generation tool that reads compiled Manifest IR and emits Next.js routes, TypeScript types, and a client SDK for your application.'
---

A **projection** reads your compiled Intermediate Representation (IR) and emits platform-specific artifacts. Projections are tooling — they do not redefine runtime semantics. Adding or changing a projection never changes what a Manifest program _means_; it only changes what code gets generated from it.

## Available projections

~~| Projection | CLI identifier | What it generates | Guide |
| Next.js | `nextjs` | App Router routes, dispatcher, TS types, client SDK | … |
| Prisma | `prisma` | A `schema.prisma` file | … |~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** The table above understated the registry. Shipped projections
> include (among others) `nextjs`, `prisma`, `prisma-store`, `convex`, `drizzle`, `kysely`,
> `zod`, `openapi`, `graphql`, `express`, `hono`, `remix`, `sveltekit`, `mongoose`, and more — see
> `manifest generate -p <name>` / the projections registry. Guides for individual targets live
> under `/integration/*` and `/projections/*`.

| Projection | CLI identifier | What it generates                                   | Guide                                         |
| ---------- | -------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| Next.js    | `nextjs`       | App Router routes, dispatcher, TS types, client SDK | [Next.js projection](/integration/nextjs)   |
| Prisma     | `prisma`       | A `schema.prisma` file (models, relations, indexes) | [Prisma projection](/integration/prisma)    |
| Mongoose   | `mongoose`     | Mongoose schema and model definitions for MongoDB   | [Mongoose projection](/projections/mongoose)  |

Projections share a single contract — `ProjectionTarget.generate(ir, request)` returns one or more `ProjectionArtifact`s plus structured diagnostics. The CLI's `--projection` flag selects which projection runs; dispatch is generic (the registry resolves the name), so adding a new projection means registering it, not editing a hardcoded dispatch branch.

## What projections produce

The Next.js projection ships four surfaces:

| Surface            | CLI identifier   | What it generates                                                               |
| ------------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Entity read routes | `nextjs.route`   | Next.js App Router GET handlers using direct database queries                   |
| Command routes     | `nextjs.command` | POST/PUT/DELETE handlers that call `RuntimeEngine.runCommand`                   |
| TypeScript types   | `ts.types`       | `interface` declarations derived from your IR entity definitions                |
| Client SDK         | `ts.client`      | Typed fetch wrappers for calling your routes from browser or server-client code |

The Prisma projection ships a single surface:

| Surface | CLI identifier  | What it generates                                                                                                                        |
| ------- | --------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Schema  | `prisma.schema` | One `schema.prisma` file with models, `@relation` wiring, `@db.Decimal` precision, and projection-config-driven `@map`/`@@map`/`@@index` |

## Reads vs. writes

This is the most important rule for projections:

- **Read routes (`nextjs.route`) may bypass the runtime.** Generated GET handlers use direct database queries (Prisma by default). That is a **projection** choice for efficiency — those handlers do not call `getInstance` / `getAllInstances`.
- ~~read policies are not enforced by default in Manifest semantics~~
- **Write routes (`nextjs.command`) MUST use `RuntimeEngine.runCommand`.** Bypassing `runCommand` for mutations bypasses guard evaluation, policy checks, constraint validation, and event emission. The spec requires these to run in a fixed order on every mutation.

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Manifest **semantics** do enforce `read` / `all` policies at the
> runtime read gate (`getInstance` / `getAllInstances` — fail closed). Generated Next.js read
> routes that query the DB directly skip that gate unless you wire reads through the runtime or
> re-implement equivalent checks. Do not confuse projection bypass with language semantics.

<Warning>
  Never replace `RuntimeEngine.runCommand` with direct database writes in a command route. Doing so
  silently strips all semantic enforcement from that operation.
</Warning>

## Using the projection CLI

Prefer **config-driven** `--all` after `manifest init` writes `manifest.config.yaml`. Verified in
`packages/cli` @ package **v3.6.41**: `compile --all`, `generate --all`, `watch --all`, and
`build --all` are registered.

```bash
# Whole-project (config-driven) — preferred
manifest compile --all
manifest generate --all
manifest watch --all
# or one shot:
manifest build --all

# Single-file / single-projection (still valid)
manifest build recipe.manifest --projection nextjs --code-output app/api/
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json -p nextjs -s nextjs.route --output app/api/
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json -p nextjs -s nextjs.command --output app/api/
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json -p nextjs -s ts.types --output .
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json -p nextjs -s ts.client --output .
```

~~```bash
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --surface route --output app/api/
# …
```~~

~~<Note>
  The CLI uses `tsx` and is intended for development-time scaffolding. For production build
  pipelines, call the projection functions directly in your build scripts.
</Note>~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Do **not** tell teams to hand-call projection helpers in production
> pipelines as the default. Use `manifest compile --all` / `generate --all` / `watch --all` /
> `build --all` driven by `manifest.config.yaml`. Surface names are registry ids such as
> `nextjs.route`, `nextjs.command`, `ts.types`, `ts.client` (not bare `route` / `types` alone
> unless the CLI resolves them for a selected `-p`).

## Using the projection API programmatically

~~Import `NextJsProjection` and call `generateRoute` / `generateTypes` / `generateClient`.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** `NextJsProjection` exposes the generic
> `generate(ir, request: ProjectionRequest)` contract — there are **no** public
> `generateRoute` / `generateTypes` / `generateClient` methods. Prefer the CLI `--all` path;
> if you must call the API, use `generate` with a `surface` id.

Struck phantom API (do not use): `generateRoute` / `generateTypes` / `generateClient` —
those methods are not on `ProjectionTarget`.

```typescript
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
import { NextJsProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections/nextjs';
import fs from 'fs/promises';

const source = await fs.readFile('recipe.manifest', 'utf-8');
const { ir, diagnostics } = await compileToIR(source);

if (diagnostics.some((d) => d.severity === 'error')) {
  for (const d of diagnostics) console.error(d.message);
  process.exit(1);
}

const projection = new NextJsProjection();
const routeResult = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: { authProvider: 'clerk', appDir: 'app/api' },
});
for (const artifact of routeResult.artifacts) {
  await fs.writeFile(artifact.pathHint, artifact.code);
}
```

### Configuration options

~~Pass an options object as the third argument to `generateRoute`…~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Pass projection options on `request.options` to `generate`, not as
> a third argument to a phantom `generateRoute` method.

```typescript
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: {
    authProvider: 'clerk', // 'clerk' | 'nextauth' | 'custom' | 'none'
    authImportPath: '@/lib/auth',
    databaseImportPath: '@/lib/database',
    responseImportPath: '@/lib/manifest-response',
    includeTenantFilter: true,
    includeSoftDeleteFilter: true,
    tenantIdProperty: 'tenantId',
    deletedAtProperty: 'deletedAt',
    appDir: 'app/api',
    strictMode: true,
  },
});
```

## Auth provider examples

~~`projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', { authProvider: … })`~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Use `generate(ir, { surface, entity, options })` as below.

<CodeGroup>

```typescript Clerk
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: {
    authProvider: 'clerk',
    // Generated import: import { auth } from "@clerk/nextjs/server";
  },
});
```

```typescript NextAuth
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: {
    authProvider: 'nextauth',
    // Generated import: import { getServerSession } from "next-auth";
  },
});
```

```typescript Custom
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: {
    authProvider: 'custom',
    authImportPath: '@/lib/my-auth',
    // Generated import: import { getUser } from "@/lib/my-auth";
  },
});
```

```typescript None
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: {
    authProvider: 'none',
    // Generated: const userId = "anonymous";
  },
});
```

</CodeGroup>

## Tenant and soft-delete filtering

~~`projection.generateRoute(ir, 'Recipe', { includeTenantFilter: … })`~~

```typescript
// Multi-tenant with soft delete (defaults)
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: {
    includeTenantFilter: true,
    tenantIdProperty: 'tenantId',
    includeSoftDeleteFilter: true,
    deletedAtProperty: 'deletedAt',
    // Generated WHERE: { tenantId, deletedAt: null }
  },
});

// Single-tenant, no soft delete
const result2 = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: {
    includeTenantFilter: false,
    includeSoftDeleteFilter: false,
    // Generated WHERE: { }
  },
});

// Custom property names
const result3 = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'nextjs.route',
  entity: 'Recipe',
  options: {
    tenantIdProperty: 'orgId',
    deletedAtProperty: 'removedAt',
    // Generated WHERE: { orgId, removedAt: null }
  },
});
```

## Per-module output file splitting

When your Manifest source uses the `module` keyword to organize entities into modules (e.g., `module Recipe { … }`), several projections support **per-module path hints** that organize generated files by module instead of flattening all output into a single directory.

**Supported projections:**

| Projection | Module path pattern | Notes |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|
| Zod | `schemas/<module>/schema.ts` | Entity/command file organization when `IREntity.module` is set |
| JSON Schema | `schemas/<module>/<entity>.schema.json` | Shared module-path sanitizer with Zod |
| Pydantic | `models/<module>/models.py` + `models/<module>/commands/` | Entity and command file splitting by module |
| Dart | `lib/models/<module>/` + `lib/commands/<module>/` | Type definitions organized by module |
| Storybook | `stories/<module>/` | Command stories inherit entity module |
| Mermaid | `diagrams/<module>/` | State/sequence diagrams per module; ER diagram stays flat at `diagrams/er-diagram.mmd` |
| Analytics | `analytics/handlers/<module>/` | Per-entity tracking handlers; monolith tracking-plan/events stay flat |
| Express | `routes/<module>/` + `types/<module>/` | Router and type definitions; monolith `manifest-router.ts` / `manifest-types.ts` stay flat |
| Hono | `routes/<module>/` + `types/<module>/` | Same pattern as Express; `src/routes.ts` / `manifest-types.ts` stay flat |

**How it works:**

When an entity has `module: "<ModuleName>"` in the IR, the projection generates files into module-prefixed directories. Entities without a module emit to flat paths (preserving existing behavior).

```manifest
module Inventory {
  entity Product { … }
  entity Category { … }
}

module Orders {
  entity Order { … }
  entity LineItem { … }
}
```

Generated output (Zod example):
```
schemas/
  inventory/
    schema.ts       # Product, Category
  orders/
    schema.ts       # Order, LineItem
```

<Note>
  Per-module file trees are **optional** — projections emit flat paths when no module is set. Convex and Next.js per-module splitting remains unimplemented (see COMPLIANCE_MATRIX §6).
</Note>

## The projection boundary

Projections handle transport concerns: authentication, HTTP conventions, response shaping, and read-path strategy. They do not own runtime responsibilities.

| Projection owns                       | Runtime owns                    |
| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Reading IR and emitting code          | Building the evaluation context |
| Configuring auth and database imports | Enforcing policies              |
| Choosing the read-path strategy       | Evaluating guards in order      |
| Adapting to framework conventions     | Executing actions               |
|                                       | Emitting declared events        |

Projections are tooling. They cannot introduce new language semantics — only the runtime and its specification can define how Manifest programs behave. If you find a mismatch between what a generated route does and what the Manifest spec requires, the generated code is wrong, not the spec.


---

## introduction

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/introduction

---
title: 'Manifest: a declarative domain language and runtime'
sidebarTitle: 'Introduction'
description: 'Manifest is a declarative domain modeling language that compiles to IR, letting you define entities, commands, guards, and policies in code.'
---

Manifest is a formal language for expressing domain logic — the rules, constraints, and operations that define how your application's data actually behaves. You write `.manifest` source files that describe entities with typed properties, commands that mutate state, guards that gate execution, policies that enforce authorization, and events that notify downstream systems. The compiler transforms that source into a compact Intermediate Representation (IR), and the `RuntimeEngine` executes the IR deterministically — enforcing every guard and policy in strict order, every time.

## The core mental model

Every Manifest program moves through two stages:

1. **Compile** — your `.manifest` source is parsed and transformed to IR v1 JSON. The IR is the canonical contract between the compiler and everything downstream.
2. **Execute** — the `RuntimeEngine` accepts the IR plus a runtime context (user identity, tenant, etc.) and runs commands. ~~Execution always follows the same order: rate-limit → policies → constraints → guards → actions → event emissions.~~

   > **Correction (2026-07-15):** Full order is parameters/context → rate-limit → policies →
   > constraints → guards → **approval gate (when declared)** → actions → declared `emits` →
   > `CommandResult` (`semantics.md` / `runtime-engine.ts`).

Because the IR is immutable at runtime, all variability enters through the runtime context. Identical IR plus identical context always produces identical results.

## Key language constructs

| Construct   | What it does                                                                                                    |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Entity**  | Defines a data structure with typed properties, defaults, computed values, and relationships                    |
| **Command** | A named business operation with parameters, guards, actions (`mutate`, `emit`, `compute`), and event emissions  |
| **Guard**   | A boolean expression evaluated in order; execution halts immediately on the first falsey guard                  |
| **Policy**  | An authorization rule scoped to an entity action (`read`, `write`, `execute`, `all`)                            |
| **Event**   | A typed definition with a channel name and payload schema, used for outbox patterns and real-time communication |
| **Store**   | Declares where entity instances persist — `memory` for development, or a database adapter for production        |

## A real example

The following entity models a kitchen prep task. It defines computed properties, guards that restrict who can run each command, and events emitted on state transitions.

```manifest
entity PrepTask {
  property required id: string
  property required name: string
  property assignedTo: string?
  property status: string = "pending"
  property priority: number = 1

  computed isUrgent: boolean = priority >= 3

  command claim(employeeId: string) {
    guard self.status == "pending"
    guard user.role == "cook" or user.role == "chef"
    mutate assignedTo = employeeId
    mutate status = "in_progress"
    emit taskClaimed
  }

  command complete() {
    guard self.status == "in_progress"
    guard self.assignedTo == user.id
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit taskCompleted
  }

  policy canClaim execute: user.role in ["cook", "chef"]
}

store PrepTask in memory

event taskClaimed: "kitchen.task.claimed" {
  taskId: string
  employeeId: string
}

event taskCompleted: "kitchen.task.completed" {
  taskId: string
  completedBy: string
}
```

## How Manifest differs from ORMs

ORMs map database tables to objects and handle queries. Manifest models domain _behavior_ — the rules and operations that govern what can happen to your data, not just its shape. Where an ORM lets you call `update()` freely, Manifest requires that every state change pass through a command with explicit guards and policy checks. The runtime refuses to skip them.

This means your business rules live in one place — the `.manifest` file — rather than scattered across route handlers, service classes, and middleware.

## Two integration patterns

Most applications use one or both of:

- **Projections** — run `manifest build` to auto-generate Next.js API routes directly from your `.manifest` files. Best for standard CRUD workflows.
- **Embedded runtime** — import `RuntimeEngine` and call `runCommand()` directly in your own handlers. Best for complex workflows or when you need full control.

## Where to go next

<CardGroup cols={3}>
  <Card title="Quickstart" icon="bolt" href="/quickstart">
    Install the package and run your first command in under five minutes.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Installation" icon="package" href="/installation">
    Full install options, package exports, TypeScript setup, and CLI reference.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Language reference" icon="book" href="/language/entities">
    Complete specification for entities, commands, guards, policies, events, and stores.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>


---

## advanced entities

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/advanced-entities

---
title: 'Advanced entities: inheritance and mixins'
description: 'Extend entity types with single inheritance and mixin composition to build reusable, composable domain models.'
sidebarTitle: 'Advanced entities'
---

Manifest supports two patterns for structuring entity types beyond a flat declaration: single inheritance with `extends` and composition with `mixin`. Both are compile-time features — the runtime sees a flattened entity with no inheritance metadata.

## Entity inheritance (`extends`)

An entity can inherit properties, computed properties, constraints, commands, and policies from a single parent entity using the `extends` keyword:

```manifest
entity BaseEntity {
  property required id: string
  timestamps
}

entity Product extends BaseEntity {
  property required name: string
  property price: number = 0

  command updatePrice(newPrice: number) {
    guard newPrice >= 0
    mutate price = newPrice
  }
}

store Product in memory
```

`Product` inherits the `id` property and `timestamps` from `BaseEntity`. The compiled IR contains a single flattened `Product` entity with all inherited members — there is no runtime polymorphism.

### Overriding inherited members

Child entities can override inherited properties and commands by redeclaring them with the same name. The child's declaration takes precedence.

### Cycle detection

The compiler detects circular inheritance chains (e.g., `A extends B` and `B extends A`) and emits an error diagnostic. Cycles are also caught transitively (A → B → C → A).

<Note>
  Only single inheritance is supported. An entity can extend exactly one parent. For sharing
  behavior across multiple entities, use mixins.
</Note>

## Mixin composition

Mixins inject reusable property and constraint declarations into an entity without creating a parent-child relationship. Declare a mixin with the `mixin` keyword and apply it inside an entity block:

```manifest
mixin Auditable {
  property required createdBy: string
  property required updatedBy: string
}

mixin SoftDeletable {
  property deletedAt: number = 0
  constraint notDeleted: self.deletedAt == 0
}

entity Document {
  mixin Auditable
  mixin SoftDeletable

  property required title: string
  property content: string = ""
}

store Document in memory
```

`Document` receives all properties and constraints from both `Auditable` and `SoftDeletable`. Mixins can be applied to any number of entities.

### Combining extends and mixins

An entity can both extend a parent and apply mixins:

```manifest
entity BaseEntity {
  property required id: string
  timestamps
}

mixin Auditable {
  property required createdBy: string
  property required updatedBy: string
}

mixin SoftDeletable {
  property deletedAt: number = 0
  constraint notDeleted: self.deletedAt == 0
}

entity ArchivedDocument extends BaseEntity {
  mixin Auditable
  mixin SoftDeletable

  property required title: string
}

store ArchivedDocument in memory
```

~~Members are resolved in this order: entity's own declarations → applied mixins → inherited parent members. Conflicts (same name from multiple mixins) produce a compile error.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Composition merge order is **parent → mixins → own**; **own wins** on name conflict (`src/manifest/entity-composition.ts`). Mixin–mixin name clashes are **not** a dedicated compile error — colliding inherited members can both be prepended unless an own declaration overrides them. Prefer disjoint mixin surfaces.

## IR representation

~~Both features are resolved at compile time… The `extends` relationship is not preserved… Mixin names are not recorded.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Members are flattened into the entity for runtime/projections, **and** IR still records lineage: `IREntity.parent` (from `extends`) and `IREntity.mixins` (string array) per `docs/spec/ir/ir-v1.schema.json`. Runtime execution uses the flattened member lists; it does not re-walk inheritance.


---

## approvals

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/approvals

---
title: 'Approvals: multi-stage approval workflows'
description: 'Gate command execution behind multi-stage approval chains. Each stage has its own policy expression and required count. Commands block until all applicable stages are granted.'
sidebarTitle: 'Approvals'
---

An approval declaration attaches a multi-stage approval gate to a command. When the runtime reaches the approval step, it checks whether every applicable stage has been granted. If any stage is still pending, the command blocks and returns an `approvalRequired` result instead of executing actions.

## Declaring an approval

Approvals live inside an entity block. Each approval references a command by name and declares one or more stages:

```manifest
entity PurchaseOrder {
  property required id: string
  property amount: number = 0
  property status: string = "draft"

  command submit() {
    guard self.status == "draft"
    mutate status = "pending_approval"
    emit OrderSubmitted
  }

  approval submitApproval {
    command: submit
    stages {
      manager {
        policy: user.role == "manager" or user.role == "admin"
        required: 1
      }
      director {
        policy: user.role == "director" or user.role == "admin"
        required: 1
        when: self.amount > 10000
      }
    }
    timeout: 72
    on_timeout: cancel
    emit ApprovalRequested
    emit ApprovalGranted
  }
}
```

### Stage fields

Each stage inside the `stages` block has three fields:

| Field      | Required | Description                                                                                                              |
| ---------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `policy`   | Yes      | Boolean expression evaluated against the approver's user context. If the expression is falsey, the approval is rejected. |
| `required` | Yes      | Number of distinct approvals needed for this stage. Typically `1`.                                                       |
| `when`     | No       | Boolean expression evaluated against the entity instance. If falsey, the stage is skipped entirely.                      |

## Execution gate order

The approval gate sits between guards and actions in the command execution pipeline. See [Commands](/language/commands) for the full pipeline description.

<Steps>
  <Step title="Check rate limit">
    If the command declares a `rateLimit`, check whether the caller has exceeded the configured
    window. If the limit is exceeded, execution stops immediately with a `rateLimitDenial` result.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate policies">
    Check every applicable policy. If any policy denies, execution stops immediately with a
    `policyDenial` result.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate constraints">
    Evaluate any `block` constraints declared inside the command. If any fail without an authorized
    override, execution stops with `constraintOutcomes` in the result.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate guards">
    Evaluate each guard expression in declaration order. Execution halts on the first falsey result
    and returns a `guardFailure` with the failing guard's index and expression.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Check approval gate">
    Find matching approval declarations for the command. Evaluate each stage's `when` condition to
    determine which stages apply. If any applicable stage is not fully granted, block with
    `approvalRequired` instead of executing actions.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Execute actions">
    Run each action (`mutate`, `emit`, `compute`, `publish`, `persist`, `effect`) in declaration
    order.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Return CommandResult">
    Return the `CommandResult` with `success: true` and all emitted events.
  </Step>
</Steps>

<Note>
  The approval gate is strictly opt-in. Commands without a matching approval declaration execute
  identically to before — the gate is never evaluated. The `approvals` field is optional on the IR
  entity and is omitted entirely when empty.
</Note>

## Conditional stages with `when`

Use the `when` expression on a stage to make it conditional. The expression is evaluated against the current entity instance at the time the command runs. When the condition is falsey, the stage is excluded from the approval chain for that invocation.

In the PurchaseOrder example above, the `director` stage only activates when `self.amount > 10000`. Orders under $10,000 need only the `manager` stage:

```manifest fragment
stages {
  manager {
    policy: user.role == "manager" or user.role == "admin"
    required: 1
  }
  director {
    policy: user.role == "director" or user.role == "admin"
    required: 1
    when: self.amount > 10000
  }
}
```

This enables a single approval declaration to handle different business rules depending on the entity's state — for example, requiring an additional director sign-off only for high-value orders.

## ApprovalRequiredInfo in CommandResult

When a command is blocked by the approval gate, the `CommandResult` includes an `approvalRequired` field describing the current state:

```typescript
interface ApprovalRequiredInfo {
  approvalName: string;
  pendingStages: string[];
  requestKey: string;
}
```

~~```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand('PurchaseOrder', 'submit', {
  instanceId: 'po-001',
  amount: 15000,
});
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Signature is `runCommand(commandName, input, options?)`.
> `entityName` / `instanceId` go in options; command params go in `input`.

```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'submit',
  {},
  { entityName: 'PurchaseOrder', instanceId: 'po-001' },
);

// result.success === false
// result.approvalRequired === {
//   approvalName: "submitApproval",
//   pendingStages: ["manager", "director"],
//   requestKey: "po-001-manager-director"
// }
```

Callers inspect `result.approvalRequired` to determine which stages still need approval and which have already been granted. This is the same structure returned by `getApprovalRequest()`.

## Runtime API

The runtime engine exposes four methods for managing the approval lifecycle, plus a query method.

### `requestApproval(entity, instanceId, approvalName)`

Creates a pending approval request. Returns the `ApprovalRequestState` with all applicable stages set to `pending`.

```typescript
const state = runtime.requestApproval('PurchaseOrder', 'order-123', 'submitApproval');
```

### `approveStage(entity, instanceId, approvalName, stage, userId)`

Evaluates the stage's policy expression against the current runtime user context. If the policy passes, the stage is marked as granted. If all applicable stages are granted, subsequent command invocations proceed past the approval gate.

```typescript
const result = runtime.approveStage(
  'PurchaseOrder',
  'order-123',
  'submitApproval',
  'manager',
  'user-mgr-001',
);
```

<Warning>
  `approveStage` evaluates the stage policy expression against the approver's user context — not the
  original requester's context. If the policy is falsey, the approval is rejected with an error.
  This means the approver must satisfy the policy they are approving, which prevents privilege
  escalation through the approval API.
</Warning>

### `denyApproval(entity, instanceId, approvalName, deniedBy, reason?)`

Marks the approval request as denied. Subsequent command invocations create a new pending request rather than reusing the denied one.

```typescript
runtime.denyApproval(
  'PurchaseOrder',
  'order-123',
  'submitApproval',
  'user-mgr-001',
  'Amount exceeds quarterly budget',
);
```

### `expireApprovals(now?)`

Expires all pending approval requests whose timeout has elapsed. Timeout is declared in hours via `timeout:` in the approval block and converted to milliseconds internally.

```typescript
runtime.expireApprovals();
```

<Note>
  `expireApprovals()` must be called explicitly — for example, from a cron job or scheduled task.
  The runtime does not automatically expire approvals on a timer.
</Note>

### `getApprovalRequest(entity, instanceId, approvalName)`

Returns the current `ApprovalRequestState` for inspection. This is useful for UI flows that need to display which stages have been granted or are still pending.

```typescript
const state = runtime.getApprovalRequest('PurchaseOrder', 'order-123', 'submitApproval');
```

## Timeout behavior

When an approval declaration includes a `timeout` (specified in hours), the `on_timeout` field determines what happens when the approval window expires:

| `on_timeout` value | Behavior                                                                                 |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `cancel`           | The approval request becomes `expired`. The next invocation creates a fresh request. |
| `escalate { … }`   | Open author-defined routing: required `to` (any expression), `status`, and `timeout` (`hours` / `none` / `reset`). Runtime stores the evaluated `to` as opaque `escalatedTo` metadata — Manifest does not interpret person vs team vs queue. |
| (omitted)          | The approval remains pending indefinitely until explicitly granted, denied, or expired.  |

> **Escalate timeout behavior (2026-07-15):** `on_timeout: escalate` is supported as an open block
> that routes to an author-defined target: `escalate { to: <expr>, status: pending|granted|denied|expired, timeout: <hours|none|reset> }`.
> The runtime stores the evaluated `to` as opaque `escalatedTo` metadata — Manifest does not interpret person
> vs team vs queue. A bare `on_timeout: escalate` (without the block) fails with
> `APPROVAL_ONTIMEOUT_ESCALATE_INCOMPLETE` — see fixture `103-approval-escalate-unsupported`. Full
> example: fixture `111-approval-escalate`.

Denied and expired approvals are reset on the next command attempt — the runtime creates a new pending request rather than reusing the terminal state. This means a denied approval does not permanently block the command; it only blocks the current attempt.

## Lifecycle events

Approval declarations can include `emit` entries for lifecycle transitions:

```manifest fragment
approval submitApproval {
  command: submit
  stages { /* ... */ }
  timeout: 72
  on_timeout: cancel
  emit ApprovalRequested
  emit ApprovalGranted
}
```

These events fire at the corresponding lifecycle transitions (request created, fully granted), giving external systems a way to react to approval state changes without polling. See [Events](/language/events) for declaring event schemas.

## Compile-time validation

The IR compiler validates approval declarations and produces error diagnostics for:

- **Non-existent command reference**: The `command:` field must name a command that exists on the same entity.
- **Empty stages**: An approval must declare at least one stage.
- **Duplicate stage names**: Each stage name within an approval must be unique.

These checks run at compile time, so invalid approvals surface as build errors rather than runtime failures.

## Complete example

```manifest
entity PurchaseOrder {
  property required id: string
  property amount: number = 0
  property status: string = "draft"
  property approvedBy: string = ""

  command submit() {
    guard self.status == "draft"
    mutate status = "pending_approval"
    emit OrderSubmitted
  }

  command finalize() {
    guard self.status == "pending_approval"
    mutate status = "approved"
    mutate approvedBy = user.id
    emit OrderFinalized
  }

  approval submitApproval {
    command: submit
    stages {
      manager {
        policy: user.role == "manager" or user.role == "admin"
        required: 1
      }
      director {
        policy: user.role == "director" or user.role == "admin"
        required: 1
        when: self.amount > 10000
      }
    }
    timeout: 72
    on_timeout: cancel
    emit ApprovalRequested
    emit ApprovalGranted
  }
}
```

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { ir } from './compiled.ir.json';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'requester-1', role: 'employee' },
});

// Submit the order -- blocks at the approval gate
~~const result = await runtime.runCommand('PurchaseOrder', 'submit', {
  instanceId: 'po-001',
  amount: 15000,
});~~

const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'submit',
  {},
  { entityName: 'PurchaseOrder', instanceId: 'po-001' },
);

console.log(result.success); // false
console.log(result.approvalRequired); // { pendingStages: ["manager", "director"] }

// Manager approves the first stage
runtime.approveStage('PurchaseOrder', 'po-001', 'submitApproval', 'manager', 'user-mgr-1');

// Director approves the second stage (required because amount > 10000)
runtime.approveStage('PurchaseOrder', 'po-001', 'submitApproval', 'director', 'user-dir-1');

// Re-submit -- now passes the approval gate
~~const final = await runtime.runCommand('PurchaseOrder', 'submit', {
  instanceId: 'po-001',
  amount: 15000,
});~~

const final = await runtime.runCommand(
  'submit',
  {},
  { entityName: 'PurchaseOrder', instanceId: 'po-001' },
);

console.log(final.success); // true
```

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Both `runCommand` calls above use
> `runCommand(commandName, input, options)` — not the phantom entity-first form.

See [Commands](/language/commands) for the full execution pipeline, [Guards and policies](/language/guards-policies) for the gates that precede approvals, [Events](/language/events) for declaring event schemas used in approval lifecycle notifications, and [Roles](/language/roles) for role-based authorization in policy expressions.


---

## async commands

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/async-commands

---
title: 'Async commands: background execution with job queues'
description: 'Defer command action execution to a background worker queue with the async keyword. Policies, constraints, and guards are validated synchronously before enqueuing.'
sidebarTitle: 'Async commands'
---

Async commands defer action execution to a background worker queue. The `async` keyword prefix tells the runtime to validate the command synchronously (policies, constraints, guards) and then enqueue it for later execution rather than running actions immediately. The caller receives a job ID and a `pending` status without waiting for the work to complete.

## Declaring an async command

Add the `async` keyword before `command`:

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
