  property status: string = "pending"
  property processedAt: number = 0

  async command processOrder(amount: number) {
    guard amount > 0
    mutate status = "processing"
    mutate processedAt = now()
    emit OrderProcessing
  }
}
```

The `async` modifier is available at program-level, module-level, and entity-level command declarations.

## How async commands execute

<Steps>
  <Step title="Synchronous validation">
    The runtime evaluates policies, constraints, and guards synchronously — exactly as it would for a non-async command. If any check fails, the command fails immediately with the normal `policyDenial`, `constraintOutcomes`, or `guardFailure` result. No job is enqueued.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Enqueue job">
    If validation passes, the runtime creates a `JobRecord` and enqueues it via the configured `JobQueue` adapter.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Return immediately">
    The caller receives a result with `{ jobId, status: 'pending', enqueuedAt }`. Actions have not yet executed.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Worker executes">
    When the job is drained (via `drainJobs()` or a production queue consumer), the runtime re-enters the command with `context.source === 'job'`, bypassing the async branch and executing all actions normally.
  </Step>
</Steps>

<Note>
  Fail-fast validation is the key design decision: policies, constraints, and guards are checked
  synchronously so that invalid commands are rejected immediately. Only valid commands are enqueued.
  This means callers get instant feedback on authorization and precondition failures without waiting
  for a background worker.
</Note>

## Return value

~~An async command returns a different shape than a synchronous command. Instead of the normal `CommandResult`, the caller receives:~~

```typescript
// Incorrect flat shape (do not use):
// { success: true, jobId, status: "pending", enqueuedAt }
```

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Async enqueue still returns a normal `CommandResult`. The job metadata is nested under `result`:

```typescript
{
  success: true,
  result: { jobId: "job-abc123", status: "pending", enqueuedAt: 1748131200000 },
  emittedEvents: []
}
```

Read `result.result.jobId` / `result.result.status`. The `jobId` correlates with the synthesized completion/failure events.

## Auto-synthesized events

The compiler automatically creates two events for each async command:

| Event                    | When emitted                        |
| ------------------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| `{CommandName}Completed` | After the job executes successfully |
| `{CommandName}Failed`    | If the job execution fails          |

~~```manifest
// You don't declare these — the compiler creates them:
// event ProcessOrderCompleted: "order.processOrder.completed" { jobId: string; result: object }
// event ProcessOrderFailed: "order.processOrder.failed" { jobId: string; error: string }
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Synthesized events use channel `jobs.<commandName>` (not a domain-style channel), and payloads include timestamps:

```manifest
// Compiler-synthesized (illustrative):
// event ProcessOrderCompleted: "jobs.processOrder" {
//   jobId: string; result: any; completedAt: number
// }
// event ProcessOrderFailed: "jobs.processOrder" {
//   jobId: string; error: string; failedAt: number
// }
```

Names are `{commandName}Completed` / `{commandName}Failed`, appended to the IR `events` array.

<Warning>
  If you declare an event with the same name as a synthesized event, the compiler produces an error
  diagnostic. Rename your event to avoid the collision.
</Warning>

## JobQueue adapter

Configure a job queue in `RuntimeOptions` to enable async command execution:

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine, MemoryJobQueue } from '@angriff36/manifest';

const jobQueue = new MemoryJobQueue();

~~const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'system', role: 'worker' },
  jobQueue,
});~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Constructor is `(ir, context, options)`. Put `user` in context and `jobQueue` in options:

```typescript
const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { user: { id: 'system', role: 'worker' } },
  { jobQueue },
);
```

The `JobQueue` interface defines three methods:

~~```typescript
interface JobQueue {
  enqueue(record: JobRecord): Promise<string>;
  drainPending(): Promise<JobRecord[]>;
  updateStatus(jobId: string, status: string): Promise<void>;
}
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** `enqueue` returns `Promise<void>` (the runtime allocates `jobId` before enqueue). Optional second arg is an opaque transaction handle:

```typescript
interface JobQueue {
  enqueue(job: JobRecord, tx?: unknown): Promise<void>;
  drainPending(): Promise<JobRecord[]>;
  updateStatus(jobId: string, status: string, error?: string): Promise<void>;
}
```

A `JobRecord` carries:

```typescript
interface JobRecord {
  jobId: string;
  commandName: string;
  entityName?: string;
  instanceId?: string;
  input: Record<string, unknown>;
  correlationId?: string;
  causationId?: string;
  enqueuedAt: number;
  status: 'pending' | 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed';
}
```

<Warning>
  `MemoryJobQueue` is for testing and development only. It stores jobs in memory and they are lost
  on process restart. Production deployments should use a durable queue — the shipped
  `PostgresJobQueue` (below), or your own `JobQueue` backed by Redis, SQS, Google Cloud Tasks, or
  similar. If no `jobQueue` is configured and an async command is invoked, the runtime returns a
  `MISSING_JOB_QUEUE` error result.
</Warning>

## Production: durable queue + drain worker

`PostgresJobQueue` is a durable `JobQueue` adapter, and `runJobWorker` is the
shipped drain loop — you no longer hand-write either. Apply the companion
schema (`src/manifest/jobs/stores/postgres.sql`) once, then:

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { PostgresJobQueue } from '@angriff36/manifest/jobs/postgres';
import { runJobWorker } from '@angriff36/manifest/jobs/worker';
import { Pool } from 'pg';

const pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });
const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, context, {
  jobQueue: new PostgresJobQueue({ pool }),
});

// Long-lived worker: each tick drains pending jobs, runs their actions, and
// records completed/failed status. Stop it on shutdown.
const worker = runJobWorker(runtime);
await worker.stop();
```

`PostgresJobQueue.drainPending` claims pending rows with `FOR UPDATE SKIP
LOCKED` and flips them to `running` in one statement, so multiple worker
replicas drain disjoint jobs. For a cron/serverless deployment that wants a
single pass instead of a loop, call `drainJobsOnce(runtime)`.

## Deterministic testing with drainJobs()

The `drainJobs()` method processes pending jobs in FIFO order, making async commands fully testable without real message queues:

~~```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand('Order', 'processOrder', {
  instanceId: 'order-1',
  amount: 50,
});
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** `runCommand(commandName, input, options?)` — not
> entity-first. Command params belong in `input`; `entityName` / `instanceId` in `options`.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine, MemoryJobQueue } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { ir } from './compiled.ir.json';

const jobQueue = new MemoryJobQueue();
~~const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'system', role: 'worker' },
  jobQueue,
});~~
const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { user: { id: 'system', role: 'worker' } },
  { jobQueue },
);

// Seed an order instance
runtime.createInstance('Order', { id: 'order-1', status: 'pending' });

// Invoke the async command
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'processOrder',
  { amount: 50 },
  { entityName: 'Order', instanceId: 'order-1' },
);

console.log(result.success); // true
~~console.log(result.status); // "pending"
console.log(result.jobId); // "job-abc123"~~
console.log(result.result.status); // "pending"
console.log(result.result.jobId); // runtime-allocated id

// The instance has NOT changed yet
const before = runtime.getInstance('Order', 'order-1');
console.log(before.status); // "pending"

// Drain the job queue — executes the enqueued command
await runtime.drainJobs();

// Now the instance reflects the async command's mutations
const after = runtime.getInstance('Order', 'order-1');
console.log(after.status); // "processing"
console.log(after.processedAt); // <timestamp>
```

`drainJobs()` is the primary testing surface for async commands. It processes all pending jobs in order, emitting synthesized completion/failure events as appropriate.

## Re-entry guard

When a job is drained, the runtime re-enters the command with `context.source === 'job'`. The async branch detects this and executes actions normally instead of enqueuing again. This prevents infinite enqueue loops:

1. First call (`context.source` is not `'job'`) -- validate and enqueue.
2. Second call during drain (`context.source === 'job'`) -- skip the async branch, execute actions.

You can also use `context.source` in your own guard expressions if you need to enforce different behavior for job-initiated versus user-initiated calls.

## Complete example

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property amount: number = 0
  property status: string = "pending"
  property processedAt: number = 0

  command submit(amount: number) {
    guard amount > 0
    mutate amount = amount
    mutate status = "submitted"
    emit OrderSubmitted
  }

  async command processOrder() {
    guard self.status == "submitted"
    mutate status = "processing"
    mutate processedAt = now()
    emit OrderProcessing
  }
}
```

~~```typescript
await runtime.runCommand('Order', 'submit', {
  instanceId: 'order-42',
  amount: 250,
});
const result = await runtime.runCommand('Order', 'processOrder', {
  instanceId: 'order-42',
});
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Same `runCommand(commandName, input, options?)` shape.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine, MemoryJobQueue } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { ir } from './compiled.ir.json';

const jobQueue = new MemoryJobQueue();
~~const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'system', role: 'worker' },
  jobQueue,
});~~
const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { user: { id: 'system', role: 'worker' } },
  { jobQueue },
);

// Submit the order synchronously
runtime.createInstance('Order', { id: 'order-42', status: 'pending' });
await runtime.runCommand(
  'submit',
  { amount: 250 },
  { entityName: 'Order', instanceId: 'order-42' },
);

// Process the order asynchronously
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'processOrder',
  {},
  { entityName: 'Order', instanceId: 'order-42' },
);

~~console.log(result.status); // "pending"
console.log(result.jobId); // defined~~
console.log(result.result.status); // "pending"
console.log(result.result.jobId); // defined

// Execute the background job
await runtime.drainJobs();

const order = runtime.getInstance('Order', 'order-42');
console.log(order.status); // "processing"
```

See [Commands](/language/commands) for the synchronous execution pipeline, [Events](/language/events) for event declaration syntax, and [Reactions](/language/reactions) for declarative event-driven command dispatch that can chain with async commands.


---

## commands

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/commands

---
title: 'Commands: business operations and mutations'
description: 'Declare named operations on entities that enforce guards and policies, execute ordered actions, emit events, and return a structured CommandResult.'
sidebarTitle: 'Commands'
---

A command is a named operation bound to an entity. When you call `runCommand`, the runtime executes the command in a strict, deterministic order:
~~rate-limit → policies → constraints → guards → actions → emits → return~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** parameters/context → rate-limit → policies → constraints → guards →
> approval gate (when declared) → actions → declared `emits` → return. Nothing in that order can be
> rearranged or skipped.

## Declaring a command

Commands live inside an entity block. A command takes an optional parameter list and a body of guards and actions.

```manifest
entity Counter {
  property value: number = 0

  command increment() {
    mutate value = value + 1
    emit CounterIncremented
  }

  command setValue(newValue: number) {
    guard newValue >= 0
    mutate value = newValue
    emit CounterSet
  }
}
```

Parameters are typed with the same types as properties: `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `timestamp`. Parameters are available by name in all guard and action expressions.

## Execution order

The runtime executes every command in exactly this order:

<Steps>
  <Step title="Process parameters">
    Apply each declared parameter's `defaultValue` to any omitted argument, then fail closed with a
    `parameterFailure` (`MISSING_REQUIRED_PARAMETER`) when a `required` parameter with no default is
    absent. An explicit `undefined` is treated as absent; `null` counts as supplied.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Build evaluation context">
    Bind `self` and `this` to the current entity instance, inject command parameters (with defaults
    already applied), and attach the runtime context (`user`, `context.*`).
  </Step>
  <Step title="Check rate limit">
    If the command declares a `rateLimit`, check whether the caller has exceeded the configured
    window. If the limit is exceeded, execution stops immediately with a `rateLimitDenial` result.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate policies">
    ~~Check every applicable policy (entity-level defaults first, then command-level). If any policy
    denies, execution stops immediately with a `policyDenial` result.~~

    **Correction (2026-07-15):** Evaluate the command's effective policy list only. Entity
    `default policy` values are expanded into `IRCommand.policies` at compile time; when a command
    already has an explicit IR `policies` array, defaults are **not** merged. If any policy denies,
    execution stops with a `policyDenial` result. (Source has no command-body policy clause —
    per-command stricter checks use guards.)
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate command constraints">
    Evaluate any `block` constraints declared inside the command. If any fail without an authorized
    override, execution stops with `constraintOutcomes` in the result.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Evaluate guards">
    Evaluate each guard expression in declaration order. Execution halts on the first falsey result
    and returns a `guardFailure` with the failing guard's index and expression.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Check approval gate (when declared)">
    **Added (2026-07-15):** If the entity declares an approval for this command and applicable
    stages are not fully granted, execution stops with `approvalRequired` instead of running
    actions. See [Approvals](/language/approvals).
  </Step>
  <Step title="Execute actions">
    Run each action (`mutate`, `emit`, `compute`, `publish`, `persist`, `effect`) in declaration
    order.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Emit declared events">
    Emit the events listed in the `emits` declaration (if any) in order, attaching payload and
    metadata.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Return CommandResult">
    Return the `CommandResult` with `success: true`, the last action result, and all emitted events.
  </Step>
</Steps>

## Actions

Actions are the statements inside a command body that produce effects.

### `mutate`

Assigns a value to an entity property. The expression is evaluated against the current context and stored on the instance.

```manifest
command complete() {
  guard not self.completed
  mutate completed = true
  mutate completedAt = now()
}
```

### `emit`

Emits an event and optionally attaches an inline payload. Also produces an action result with the evaluated value.

```manifest
command increment() {
  mutate value = value + 1
  emit CounterIncremented
}
```

### `compute`

Calculates a value **without mutating** entity state. `compute <name> = <expr>` binds `<name>` as a command-scoped local into the evaluation scope, available to later actions and to event-payload expressions in the same command. The binding is never persisted and never appears in `getInstance`/`getAllInstances`. Bare `compute <expr>` just returns the value. To persist a field, use `mutate`.

The compiler emits a `COMPUTE_USED_AS_MUTATE` warning when a `compute` target matches a declared property — the common sign of a mis-used `mutate`.

```manifest
command addItem(amount: number) {
  compute newQuantity = quantity + amount   // ephemeral local, not persisted
  mutate quantity = newQuantity              // persists
}
```

### `publish`

External delivery of the **named** event `target` (e.g. `publish OrderShipped`). Requires `RuntimeOptions.outboxStore`: when it is absent the command **fails closed** with a `MISSING_OUTBOX_STORE` error and persists nothing. When configured, the named event is delivered in-process (result + listeners + reactions) AND durably enqueued to the outbox, joining the command transaction under a `TransactionProvider`. The compiler errors `EMIT_ACTION_UNKNOWN_EVENT` if `target` is missing or undeclared. In deterministic mode, throws `ManifestEffectBoundaryError`.

### `persist`

Explicitly flushes the command's pending working-copy changes to the store at its point in the action sequence, then clears the pending change set. It does not open or close a transaction. Under a `TransactionProvider` the flush joins the command transaction (atomic-on-failure); without one it is an immediate, non-reversible write. Multiple `persist` actions are permitted. In deterministic mode, throws `ManifestEffectBoundaryError`.

### `effect`

Invokes the host `RuntimeOptions.effectHandler` with the evaluated value and command context (`{ name?, value, commandName, entityName?, instanceId?, context }`); the handler's result becomes the action result. An optional `effect <name> = <expr>` form names the effect. When no handler is configured the command **fails closed** with a `MISSING_EFFECT_HANDLER` error. In deterministic mode, throws `ManifestEffectBoundaryError`.

<Warning>
`publish` and `effect` are fail-closed adapter actions: without `outboxStore` / `effectHandler` respectively the command returns `{ success: false }` (it does not silently no-op). `emit` is in-process and always available. When `deterministicMode` is `true`, calling `publish`, `effect`, or `persist` throws `ManifestEffectBoundaryError` — this is by design to enforce the effect boundary contract.
</Warning>

## The `emits` keyword

You can declare events at the command level using the `emits` keyword outside the action body. This serves as a declaration that the command emits a specific event after all actions succeed.

```manifest
entity Order {
  property status: string = "pending"

  command complete() {
    guard self.status == "in_progress"
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit OrderCompleted
  }
}

event OrderCompleted: "order.completed" {
  orderId: string
  completedAt: number
}
```

See [Events](/language/events) for how to declare event schemas.

## Command constraints (vNext)

Commands can declare their own `constraint` blocks that are evaluated after policies but before guards. The same severity levels (`ok`, `warn`, `block`) apply:

```manifest
entity Order {
  property status: string = "pending"
  property amount: number = 0

  command updateStatus(newStatus: string) {
    constraint notCancelled: self.status != "cancelled" "Cannot update a cancelled order"
    constraint amountVerified:warn self.amount >= 100 "Orders under $100 require manual approval"

    mutate status = newStatus
    emit OrderStatusChanged
  }
}
```

A failing `block` constraint stops execution and surfaces `constraintOutcomes` in the `CommandResult`. A `warn` constraint produces an outcome entry but does not stop execution.

## CommandResult shape

Every call to `runCommand` returns a `CommandResult`:

```typescript
interface CommandResult {
  success: boolean;
  error?: string;

  // Present when a policy denied execution
  policyDenial?: {
    policyName: string;
    expression: string;
  };

  // Present when a guard halted execution
  guardFailure?: {
    index: number; // 1-based
    expression: string;
    resolved: { expression: string; value: unknown }[];
  };

  // Present when constraints were evaluated
  constraintOutcomes?: ConstraintOutcome[];

  // Present when optimistic concurrency detected a conflict
  concurrencyConflict?: ConcurrencyConflict;

  // All events emitted during this run
  emittedEvents: EmittedEvent[];

  // ~~lastActionResult?: unknown;~~
  result?: unknown; // last action value
}
```

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** The field is `result?: unknown`. There is no `lastActionResult` on
> `CommandResult` in `runtime-engine.ts` @ v3.6.41.

## Calling `runCommand` from TypeScript

~~```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { ir } from './compiled.ir.json';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'user-123', role: 'admin' },
});

const result = await runtime.runCommand('Order', 'updateStatus', {
  instanceId: 'order-abc',
  newStatus: 'processing',
});
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Real signature is
> `runCommand(commandName, input, options?)` — not entity-first
> `runCommand(Entity, cmd, args)`. Pass `entityName` / `instanceId` in `options`.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { ir } from './compiled.ir.json';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'user-123', role: 'admin' },
});

const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'updateStatus',
  { newStatus: 'processing' },
  { entityName: 'Order', instanceId: 'order-abc' },
);

if (!result.success) {
  if (result.policyDenial) {
    console.error('Denied by policy:', result.policyDenial.policyName);
  } else if (result.guardFailure) {
    console.error(`Guard ${result.guardFailure.index} failed:`, result.guardFailure.formatted);
  } else if (result.constraintOutcomes) {
    const blocked = result.constraintOutcomes.filter((o) => o.severity === 'block' && !o.passed);
    console.error(
      'Blocked by constraints:',
      blocked.map((o) => o.code),
    );
  }
  process.exit(1);
}

console.log('Events:', result.emittedEvents);
```

## Retry policies

Commands can declare retry policies for transient failures with configurable backoff strategies. When a command fails (guard failure excluded — guards fail fast), the runtime retries according to the declared policy.

```manifest fragment
entity Notification {
  command sendEmail(recipient: string, body: string) {
    retry {
      maxAttempts: 3
      backoff: exponential
      delay: 1000
      jitter: true
    }
    // command body
  }
}
```

### Retry configuration

| Field         | Description                                                                   |
| ------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `maxAttempts` | Maximum number of retry attempts                                              |
| `backoff`     | Strategy: `fixed`, `exponential`, or `linear`                                 |
| `delay`       | Base delay in milliseconds (compiles to IR `delayMs`)                         |
| `jitter`      | Add random jitter to prevent thundering herd (default: `false`)               |
| `retryOn`     | One or more error codes that trigger retry (repeat the field per code)        |

| `maxDelay` | Upper bound on each computed delay (ms) |

> **Note:** Delays are clamped with `min(delay, maxDelay)`. Source field `maxDelay` compiles to IR `maxDelayMs`.
> See `runtime-retry.ts` `computeRetryDelays` and fixture `72-command-retry-policy.manifest`.

~~| `initialDelay` | First retry delay in milliseconds |~~ (use `delay` instead)

### Backoff strategies

| Strategy      | Behavior                                                      |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `fixed`       | Constant delay between retries (e.g., 1000ms, 1000ms, 1000ms) |
| `exponential` | Doubles each time (e.g., 1000ms, 2000ms, 4000ms)              |
| `linear`      | Increases by `delay` each time (e.g., 1000ms, 2000ms, 3000ms) |

### Retry events

~~The runtime emits events for each retry attempt and when retries are exhausted:~~

~~| Event | When emitted |
| `{Command}RetryAttempted` | Each retry attempt, with attempt number |
| `{Command}RetryExhausted` | All retries failed, with total attempts |~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** No `{Command}RetryAttempted` / `{Command}RetryExhausted` events exist
> in the reference runtime. Retry status is returned on `CommandResult.retry`
> (`{ attempts, exhausted, lastErrorCode?, delaysMs }`).

<Note>
  Retry policies only apply when the failed attempt's derived error code appears in `retryOn`.
  Guard failures, policy denials, and blocking constraint outcomes are never retried — they
  represent intentional business rule enforcement, not transient errors.
</Note>

## Rate limiting

Rate limiting constrains how frequently a command can be invoked per user, tenant, or globally.
When the limit is exceeded, the command returns a `rateLimitDenial` on `CommandResult`.

```manifest fragment
entity ApiKey {
  property required id: string

  command makeRequest() {
    rateLimit {
      windowMs: 60000
      maxRequests: 100
      scope: user
    }
    // command body
  }
}
```

### Rate limit configuration

| Field         | Description                                                |
| ------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `windowMs`    | Time window in milliseconds                                |
| `maxRequests` | Maximum allowed requests within the window                 |
| `scope`       | `user`, `tenant`, or `global` (IR enum; not `user.id`)     |

~~| `window` | Time window in milliseconds |~~
~~| `scope` | `user.id`, `tenant.id`, or `global` |~~
~~| `strategy` | `fixed` or `sliding` window (default: `fixed`) |~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** IR `IRRateLimit` requires `maxRequests`, `windowMs`, and
> `scope: "user" | "tenant" | "global"`. Optional `burstAllowance` exists. There is no `strategy`
> field on the rate-limit block.

When the rate limit is exceeded, the `CommandResult` includes:

~~```typescript
{
  success: false,
  rateLimitExceeded: {
    scope: "user.id",
    limit: 100,
    remaining: 0,
    retryAfterMs: 45000
  }
}
```~~

```typescript
{
  success: false,
  rateLimitDenial: {
    scope: 'user', // 'user' | 'tenant' | 'global'
    scopeKey: 'user:user-123',
    limit: 100,
    windowMs: 60000,
    retryAfterMs: 45000,
  },
}
```

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Field name is `rateLimitDenial`, not `rateLimitExceeded`
> (`runtime-engine.ts` `CommandResult`).

<Warning>
  Rate limiting is enforced by the runtime engine before policies/guards run. It is not a
  network-level rate limiter — it operates within the Manifest runtime context. For HTTP-level rate
  limiting, configure your API gateway or middleware. The reference limiter is in-memory only
  (no durable adapter).
</Warning>

<Note>
  Call shape: `runCommand(commandName, input, { entityName?, instanceId?, ... })`. `instanceId` and
  `entityName` belong in **options**, not in the command parameter object. Command parameters are
  only the named values declared in the command signature.
</Note>


---

## computed caching

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/computed-caching

---
title: 'Computed property caching: request, session, and TTL'
sidebarTitle: 'Computed caching'
description: 'Memoize computed properties with request, session, or TTL cache strategies, with dependency-driven staleness surfaced on every read.'
---

A computed property can declare a cache strategy so its value is memoized rather than recomputed on every read. Three strategies are available: per-command (`request`), per-engine-lifetime (`session`), and time-bounded (`ttl`). For how computed properties work in general, see [Computed properties](/language/computed-properties).

> **Verified (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Strategies match runtime cache keys / staleness marking / `evaluateComputedProperty` `{ value, stale, cached }` surface in `runtime-engine.ts`. Package pin **3.6.41**.

## Syntax

The cache modifier follows the computed property's expression:

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required price: number
  property quantity: number = 1
  property taxRate: number = 0

  computed subtotal: number = price * quantity cache request
  computed total: number = subtotal * (1 + taxRate / 100) cache session
  computed margin: number = subtotal * 0.3 cache ttl 300
  computed label: string = "Product"
}

store Product in memory
```

`cache request` and `cache session` are bare strategies. `cache ttl 300` requires a numeric argument interpreted as a TTL in seconds. A computed property with no `cache` clause (like `label`) is recomputed on every read.

## Behavior

`cache`, `request`, `session`, and `ttl` are reserved keywords. The parser runs after the expression: it consumes `cache`, then expects one of `request`, `session`, or `ttl`. For `ttl` it reads a following `NUMBER` token (throwing if absent) and stores `{ strategy: 'ttl', ttlSeconds: <n> }`; the other two store `{ strategy: 'request' }` or `{ strategy: 'session' }`. The cache config is attached to the computed property and carried through to the IR.

The runtime engine implements the strategies with two caches keyed by `entityName:instanceId:propertyName`:

- **`request`**: stored in a request-scoped cache that is cleared per command execution. The value survives repeated reads within a single command but not across commands.
- **`session`**: stored in the long-lived cache for the lifetime of the engine instance.
- **`ttl`**: stored in the same long-lived cache, but on read the entry is only returned when `getNow() - computedAt < ttlSeconds * 1000`; once expired the entry is deleted and the value is recomputed.

On a cache hit the engine returns `{ value, stale, cached: true }`; on a miss it evaluates the expression, stores the result (when a strategy is configured), and returns `cached: false`. Cache entries are not silently wrong when inputs change: when a mutation touches a property that a cached computed depends on, the engine marks the matching cache entries `stale` in both the session/TTL and request caches, and propagates staleness transitively to computed properties that depend on other computed properties. The stale flag is surfaced on the read result rather than forcing an immediate recompute. Because `ttl` uses `getNow()`, expiry honors the deterministic clock supplied via the `now` runtime option.

Caching is a runtime evaluation concern; the cache strategy is metadata carried on the computed property in the IR. There is no dedicated database or schema projection for it.

<Note>
  The `request` cache is cleared on each command boundary, so it is genuinely scoped to a single
  command's evaluation rather than to an HTTP-request abstraction. `session` persists for the
  lifetime of the engine instance — there is no eviction beyond staleness marking. Dependency-driven
  staleness sets a flag rather than evicting; consumers decide whether to act on `stale: true`. A
  `ttl` clause without a numeric argument is a parse error.
</Note>


---

## computed properties

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/computed-properties

---
title: 'Computed properties: derived values'
description: 'Declare derived values with the computed keyword. Manifest evaluates them against the current instance automatically and prevents infinite recursion.'
sidebarTitle: 'Computed properties'
---

A computed property is a read-only value derived from an expression rather than stored directly. You declare it with the `computed` keyword inside an entity block. The runtime evaluates it on demand, using the current instance fields and any declared dependencies.

## Syntax

```manifest
computed <name>: <type> = <expression>
```

The expression can reference `self`, `this`, any property on the instance, other computed properties, and the standard built-ins (`now()`, `uuid()`).

```manifest
entity Item {
  property required price: number
  property quantity: number = 1
  property discountPercent: number = 0

  computed total: number = price * quantity
  computed discountedTotal: number = total * (1 - discountPercent / 100)
  computed hasDiscount: boolean = discountPercent > 0
  computed isExpensive: boolean = total > 100
}
```

## Evaluation context

When the runtime evaluates a computed property, the context contains:

| Binding                       | Value                                                 |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `self` / `this`               | The current entity instance                           |
| All property values           | Direct field access by name                           |
| Any dependencies listed in IR | Other computed properties that have already resolved  |
| `user`                        | The current user object (if the runtime provides one) |
| `context`                     | The runtime context object                            |

```manifest
entity Author {
  property required name: string
  hasMany books: Book

  computed bookCount: number = self.books.length
}
```

Relationship traversal (`self.books`) is fully supported in computed expressions. The runtime resolves the relationship synchronously before evaluating the expression.

## Chaining computed properties

A computed property can reference another computed property on the same entity. The runtime resolves them in dependency order:

```manifest
entity WorkflowStep {
  property status: string = "pending"
  property executedAt: number = 0

  computed isPending: boolean = self.status == "pending"
  computed isInProgress: boolean = self.status == "in_progress"
  computed isCompleted: boolean = self.status == "completed"
  computed canStart: boolean = self.isPending
  computed canComplete: boolean = self.isInProgress
}
```

`canStart` depends on `isPending`, which depends on `status`. The runtime evaluates in bottom-up order.

## When computed properties re-evaluate

~~Computed properties are evaluated fresh each time they are accessed — there is no caching layer in the default runtime.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Without a `cache` clause, values are recomputed on each access. Optional `cache request` / `cache session` / `cache ttl <seconds>` strategies are documented in [Computed caching](/language/computed-caching).

They reflect the state of the instance at the moment of access (or the cached entry when a strategy applies). This means:

- After a `mutate` action inside a command, any subsequent access to a computed property reflects the updated field values.
- Computed properties based on `now()` return a different value on each evaluation if the time source advances.

## Infinite recursion prevention

The runtime detects dependency cycles. If evaluating a computed property would cause infinite recursion, the runtime returns `undefined` for that property instead of throwing. This is defined behavior, not an error condition.

```manifest
entity Circular {
  // If A depends on B and B depends on A, both evaluate to undefined
  computed a: number = self.b + 1
  computed b: number = self.a + 1
}
```

<Warning>
  A computed property that returns `undefined` due to a cycle will silently produce `undefined` in
  guard and action expressions. If you rely on a computed value being non-null, ensure there are no
  cycles in the dependency graph.
</Warning>

## Using computed properties in guards and actions

Computed properties are first-class values in the evaluation context. You can reference them in guards, policies, and action expressions:

```manifest
entity Task {
  property required title: string
  property required status: string = "todo"
  property required assigneeId: string = ""
  property required createdAt: number = 0

  property priority: number = 0

  computed isOverdue: boolean = (now() - self.createdAt) > 86400000 and self.status != "done"
  computed assignedUser: string = self.assigneeId != "" ? self.assigneeId : "Unassigned"
  ~~computed isHighPriority: boolean = self.priority != null and self.priority >= 3~~
  computed isHighPriority: boolean = self.priority >= 3

  command updateStatus(newStatus: string) {
    guard newStatus != null and newStatus != ""
    guard newStatus == "todo" or newStatus == "in-progress" or newStatus == "done"
    mutate status = newStatus
    emit TaskStatusUpdated
  }

  command assignTask(userId: string) {
    guard userId != null and userId != ""
    guard self.assigneeId == "" or user.role == "admin"
    mutate assigneeId = userId
    emit TaskAssigned
  }
}
```

Guards like `self.assigneeId == ""` reference the raw property. A guard referencing `self.isHighPriority` would evaluate the computed property first and use the result.

## Computed properties in the TypeScript output

When you use the Next.js projection or TypeScript types surface, computed properties appear as read-only fields on the generated interface. They are not persisted in the store — they are derived each time the instance is loaded.

```typescript
interface Task {
  title: string;
  status: string;
  assigneeId: string;
  createdAt: number;
  priority: number;
  // Computed — derived at read time
  readonly isOverdue: boolean;
  readonly assignedUser: string;
  readonly isHighPriority: boolean;
}
```


---

## constraints

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/constraints

---
title: 'Constraint built-ins: regex, range, and boundary checks'
sidebarTitle: 'Constraint built-ins'
description: 'Validate string formats and numeric ranges declaratively with the matches, between, min, max, and length built-ins inside Manifest constraints and guards.'
---

Manifest ships built-in functions for the two most common validation needs — matching a string against a pattern and bounding a number or length — so you can express format and range rules declaratively inside constraints and command guards rather than writing custom comparison logic. These built-ins return booleans (or numbers) and slot into the existing constraint and guard evaluation paths unchanged. For how constraints attach to entities and commands and how severity works, see [Entities](/language/entities).

## Regex matching with `matches()`

The `matches(value, pattern)` built-in tests a string value against a regular expression, enabling declarative format validation — emails, phone numbers, postal codes. Invalid literal patterns are caught at compile time; the match itself is enforced at runtime.

```manifest
entity ContactInfo {
  property required id: string
  property email: string = ""
  property phone: string = ""
  property zipCode: string = ""

  constraint validEmail: matches(self.email, "^[^@]+@[^@]+\\.[^@]+$") "Must be a valid email format"
  constraint validPhone: matches(self.phone, "^\\d{3}-\\d{3}-\\d{4}$") "Phone must be in XXX-XXX-XXXX format"
  constraint validZip: matches(self.zipCode, "^\\d{5}$") "Zip code must be 5 digits"
}

store ContactInfo in memory
```

The first argument is the string to test (typically a property via `self.`), the second is the pattern as a string literal. Backslashes are escaped within the string literal (`\\d`, `\\.`). The trailing quoted string is the constraint's failure message.

Regex constraints are validated in two places. At **compile time**, when the callee is `matches`, there are at least two arguments, and the second is a string literal, the compiler attempts `new RegExp(...)`; if construction throws it emits an error diagnostic (`Invalid regex pattern in matches(): "..."`) at the call's source position. Patterns that are not literal strings are not validated at compile time because there is nothing constant to test. At **runtime**, the built-in returns `false` unless both arguments are strings, then evaluates `new RegExp(pattern).test(s)`; a pattern that fails to compile at runtime returns `false` rather than throwing. Because `matches` returns a boolean, it composes with the constraint machinery the same way any other boolean expression does.

A `matches` constraint with a literal pattern is the natural source for a Zod `.regex()` refinement and a database `CHECK` constraint in projections that consume the IR's constraint expressions.

<Note>
  The runtime uses the JavaScript `RegExp` engine, so pattern syntax and semantics are exactly those
  of ECMAScript regular expressions, with no flags applied. A non-string value or non-string pattern
  makes `matches` return `false` — so a constraint over a null or non-string field registers as a
  failed match rather than being skipped.
</Note>

## Range and boundary checks

The `min`, `max`, `between`, and `length` built-ins express numeric range and string-length validation inside constraints and guards.

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required id: string
  property name: string = ""
  property price: number = 0
  property quantity: number = 0
  property discount: number = 0

  constraint priceRange: between(self.price, 0.01, 99999.99) "Price must be between 0.01 and 99999.99"
  // Was incorrect: min(self.quantity, 0) / max(self.discount, 100) — see correction below
  constraint quantityMin: self.quantity >= 0 "Quantity must be at least 0"
  constraint discountMax: self.discount <= 100 "Discount cannot exceed 100%"
  constraint nameMinLength: length(self.name) >= 1 "Name must not be empty"
  constraint nameMaxLength: length(self.name) <= 255 "Name must be at most 255 characters"

  command setPrice(newPrice: number) {
    guard newPrice > 0
    mutate price = newPrice
    emit PriceChanged
  }

  command applyDiscount(pct: number) {
    guard between(pct, 0, 100)
    mutate discount = pct
    emit DiscountApplied
  }
}

event PriceChanged: "product.price.changed" {
}

event DiscountApplied: "product.discount.applied" {
}

store Product in memory
```

`between(value, low, high)` is inclusive on both ends. `length(value)` returns a string's length, which is then compared with ordinary operators (`>= 1`, `<= 255`).

The semantics are:

- `between(value, low, high)` returns `true` only when all three are numbers and `value >= low && value <= high`; otherwise `false`.
- `min(...args)` and `max(...args)` are variadic numeric reducers (`Math.min` / `Math.max`), or `undefined` if none are numeric — **not** boolean boundary predicates.
- `length(value)` returns the length of a string (the same `length` built-in used for strings generally).

In a guard, a falsey result halts command execution in order. In a constraint, pass/fail uses truthiness of the expression result (plus `failWhen` / severity via `constraintExpressionPasses`). A static analysis module also extracts numeric range and length bounds from these expressions and exposes converters for SQL `CHECK`, Zod chains, and OpenAPI `minimum`/`maximum`/`minLength`/`maxLength`.

~~<Warning>
  Note the difference in shape: `between` returns a **boolean**, while `min` and `max` return a
  **number**. The fixture uses `min(self.quantity, 0)` and `max(self.discount, 100)` as constraint
  expressions whose numeric result the checker evaluates…
</Warning>~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Do **not** write `constraint x: min(self.n, 0)` expecting a lower-bound check. `min`/`max` return numbers; constraint polarity is `!!result` (unless `failWhen`). `min(5, 0)` → `0` → **fails**; `min(-1, 0)` → `-1` → **passes**. Use comparisons (`self.quantity >= 0`) or `between(...)` for bounds.


---

## entities

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/entities

---
title: 'Entities: data structures and behavior'
description: 'Define typed data structures with properties, computed values, relationships, constraints, concurrency controls, and state transitions in the Manifest language.'
sidebarTitle: 'Entities'
---

An entity is the primary building block in Manifest. It combines a typed data schema with behavioral constraints, commands, and computed properties into a single declaration. The compiler turns entities into IR that the runtime can instantiate, query, and mutate.

## Basic declaration

Use the `entity` keyword followed by a name and a block. Property declarations go inside the block.

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required name: string
  property price: number = 0
  property inStock: boolean = true
  property description: string = ""
}
```

### External entities

Prefix the declaration with `external` to mark an entity as owned by another system or file. The compiler records `external: true` on the IR, and persistence projections (Prisma, Drizzle, Kysely, Convex, prisma-store) skip it — they emit no table or model for it, only honoring relationships that point at it. Use this to reference data managed outside the current program.

```manifest
external entity ImportedUser {
  property required id: string
  property email: string
}
```

`external` is a contextual modifier rather than a reserved word, so a property may still be named `external` (`property external: string`).

## Properties

Every property has a type.

~~Supported types are `string`, `number`, `boolean`, and `timestamp`. A property without a default is `undefined` unless the caller supplies a value at creation time.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-22 @RYANSIGNED):** `timestamp` **is** a valid property type — it is a normative alias for `datetime` (`docs/spec/semantics.md:281,286-289`). Runtimes MUST treat `timestamp` identically to `datetime`. Common scalar types include `string`, `number`, `boolean`, plus date/time primitives `datetime` / `timestamp` / `date` / `time` / `duration`, `decimal` / `money`, collections, enums, and value-object names. When a property is omitted at create time, the runtime applies the property's `defaultValue` if present, otherwise the **type default** (for example `""`, `0`, `false`) — not `undefined`. An explicit `required` modifier is not satisfied by a zero-filled type default alone (`E_REQUIRED`). See [Types](/language/types).

### Modifiers

Place modifiers between the `property` keyword and the name:

| Modifier     | Meaning                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| ------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `required`   | The caller must supply a value. The runtime rejects a create (`E_REQUIRED`) when the property has no supplied value, `defaultValue`, `autoNow`, auto-managed field, or creating-command action; a zero-filled type default does not satisfy it.                                                                                                                         |
| `optional`   | Explicitly marks the property as not required. Equivalent to the default behavior.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| `readonly`   | Immutable after creation. The runtime rejects an update that changes the value (`E_READONLY`); writes during the creating command and same-value writes are allowed.                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| `unique`     | Values must be unique within the entity collection. The runtime rejects a create/update whose non-null value another instance already holds (`E_UNIQUE`, scanned within the active tenant scope); adapters may additionally emit a database unique constraint.                                                                                                          |
| `encrypted`  | Declares the property for field-level encryption. The runtime encrypts at store-write boundaries and decrypts on read. Requires an `EncryptionProvider` in runtime options.                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| `masked`     | Applies dynamic data masking to the property value on read. Write `masked` alone for the default `redact` strategy, or `masked(strategy, ...numbers)` to pick a strategy with optional numeric parameters. Strategies are exactly: `redact`, `partial`, `email`, `phone`, `last4`. Add a trailing `unmask when <expr>` clause to bypass masking for authorized readers. |
| `searchable` | Declares the property for full-text search indexing (string-only). Adds a `search(text, query)` built-in with AND-semantics whole-word matching. Projections emit platform-specific full-text indexes (e.g., Prisma `@@fulltext`, Drizzle GIN tsvector).                                                                                                                |
| `private`    | Prevents inclusion in public API projections. The property is omitted from generated client types and API response shapes, and the reference runtime strips every `private` property (with or without `masked`) from `getInstance`/`getAllInstances`. Execution paths (guards, constraints, computed properties) use the raw read path and still see the real value.    |
| `indexed`    | Signals to adapters that the property should be indexed for faster lookups. Projections emit platform-specific index declarations.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |

```manifest
entity User {
  property required id: string
  property required email: string
  property optional displayName: string = ""
  property readonly createdAt: number = 0
}
```

### Default values

Any property can carry a literal default. When you create an instance and omit that property, the runtime applies the default. Explicitly supplying a value — including an empty string `""` — skips the default entirely.

```manifest
entity Task {
  property status: string = "pending"
  property priority: number = 1
  property completed: boolean = false
}
```

<Note>
  Defaults apply only when the property is **omitted** from the creation payload. An empty string
  `""` counts as a provided value and overrides the default.
</Note>

## Relationships

Relationships declare connections between entities. The runtime resolves them synchronously from the current store context.

| Keyword     | Cardinality      | Returns                                                          |
| ----------- | ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `hasMany`   | One-to-many      | Array of related instances (never `null`; empty array when none) |
| `hasOne`    | One-to-one       | Single related instance or `null`                                |
| `belongsTo` | Many-to-one      | Single related instance or `null`                                |
| `ref`       | Simple reference | Single related instance or `null`                                |

```manifest
entity Author {
  property required name: string
  hasMany books: Book
  computed bookCount: number = self.books.length
}

entity Book {
  property required title: string
  property year: number = 2024
  belongsTo author: Author
}
```

For `belongsTo` and `ref`, the foreign key property on the source instance holds the target ID. For `hasMany` and `hasOne`, the runtime queries the target entity for instances whose foreign key matches the current instance's ID.

<Tip>
  Accessing a relationship on a non-existent instance returns `null` for singular relationships and
  `[]` for `hasMany`. Circular relationships are handled gracefully.
</Tip>

## Constraints

Constraints are boolean expressions that validate entity state. You can attach them at the entity level (evaluated whenever an instance is created or mutated) or at the command level (evaluated before that command runs — see [Commands](/language/commands)).

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required name: string
  property price: number = 0

  constraint pricePositive: self.price > 0 "Price must be positive"
  constraint nameNotEmpty: self.name != "" "Name cannot be empty"
}
```

### Constraint severity

Each constraint has a severity that controls what happens when it evaluates to `false`:

| Severity | Behavior                                                                                                                                                                              |
| -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `ok`     | Informational only. Does not halt execution. The runtime forces `passed = true` — an `ok` constraint never appears as a failure. The expression is still evaluated for observability. |
| `warn`   | Produces a `ConstraintOutcome` with the real result but does **not** halt execution.                                                                                                  |
| `block`  | Halts execution when the expression evaluates to `false` (default).                                                                                                                   |

```manifest
entity Task {
  property status: string = "pending"
  property priority: number = 1
  property dueDate: number = 0

  constraint okInfo {
    expression: priority > 0
    severity: ok
    message: "Priority is positive"
  }

  constraint warnHighPriority {
    expression: priority <= 5
    severity: warn
    message: "Priority is higher than recommended"
  }

  constraint blockStatus {
    expression: status in ["pending", "in_progress", "completed", "cancelled"]
    severity: block
    message: "Status must be one of the allowed values"
  }
}
```

The long-form block syntax also supports `messageTemplate` and `details` for structured diagnostics:

```manifest
constraint amountLimit:block self.amount > 10000 {
  messageTemplate: "Amount {currentAmount} exceeds limit of {maxAmount}"
  details: {
    maxAmount: 10000
    currentAmount: self.amount
    excessAmount: self.amount - 10000
  }
}
```

### Overrideable constraints

Mark a `block` constraint `overrideable` to allow authorized bypass at runtime. Pair it with an `overridePolicy` to control who can authorize the override.

```manifest
entity FinancialRecord {
  property required amount: number = 0
  property required status: string = "pending"

  command process() {
    constraint overrideable requiresApproval {
      expression: self.status == "approved" or self.amount <= 500
      severity: block
      message: "Must be approved before processing"
      overridePolicy: canApproveLargeAmounts
    }

    mutate status = "processed"
    emit RecordProcessed
  }
}

policy canApproveLargeAmounts override:
  user.role == "manager" or user.role == "admin"
  "Only managers and admins can approve large amounts"
```

When a caller supplies an `OverrideRequest` matching the constraint code, the runtime checks the policy, marks the outcome `overridden: true`, and emits a synthetic `OverrideApplied` event.

## Composite keys and foreign key structure

### Composite primary keys

Declare `key [field1, field2]` inside an entity to define a composite primary key. The listed fields together form the entity's identity — no single `id` field is needed.

```manifest
entity Order {
  key [tenantId, id]
  property required tenantId: string
  property required id: string
  property total: number = 0
}
```

When the Prisma projection sees a `key`, it emits `@@id([tenantId, id])` instead of a single `@id` field. The reference runtime also honors `entity.key`: it derives a deterministic canonical identity string from the key tuple (each component percent-encodes `%` and `|`, joined with `|`) and persists each instance under that string, so a composite-key entity is addressable by it through `getInstance`/`updateInstance`/`deleteInstance` and `runCommand`'s `instanceId`. Instances differing in any key component are distinct even when they share another column's value.

### Alternate keys

Declare `unique [field1, field2]` to define a non-PK uniqueness constraint (an alternate key). You can declare multiple.

```manifest
entity AdminChatParticipant {
  key [tenantId, id]
  unique [tenantId, threadId, userId]
  property required tenantId: string
  property required id: string
  property required threadId: string
  property required userId: string
}
```

The Prisma projection emits `@@unique([...])` for each group, and Drizzle emits the equivalent constraint. Convex, Kysely, OpenAPI, and Zod do not emit uniqueness constraints for alternate keys, and the reference runtime does not enforce alternate-key uniqueness.

### Composite foreign keys

For `belongsTo` and `ref` relationships, declare the FK columns explicitly when your relationship spans multiple columns:

```manifest
// Single-column FK using the shorthand (backward-compatible)
belongsTo author: Author with authorId

// Composite FK — explicit fields and references
belongsTo thread: AdminChatThread fields [tenantId, threadId] references [tenantId, id] onDelete cascade

// Cross-order composite FK
belongsTo proposal: Proposal fields [proposalId, tenantId] references [id, tenantId] onDelete cascade
```

`fields` names properties on the current entity; `references` names the corresponding columns on the target. If `references` is omitted, `[id]` is assumed.

**Runtime note**: The Manifest runtime resolves composite foreign keys. A `belongsTo`/`ref` with `fields.length > 1` (and the inverse `hasMany`/`hasOne`) selects the target row where every mapped `fields`/`references` column is equal, so it picks the exact row even when several targets share a first-column value. Local FK columns are paired with target columns via `references`; when `references` is absent or mismatched, the target entity's declared `key` columns are paired positionally, else the field names are assumed to match. If any local FK column is unset the relationship resolves to `null`/`[]`.

### Referential actions

Add `onDelete` and/or `onUpdate` to any `belongsTo` or `ref` to declare referential integrity behavior. Valid values: `cascade`, `restrict`, `setNull`, `setDefault`, `noAction`.

```manifest
belongsTo account: Account with tenantId onDelete restrict
belongsTo item: LineItem fields [tenantId, itemId] references [tenantId, id] onDelete cascade onUpdate restrict
```

When no action is declared, the projection emits nothing and the target platform uses its own default.

~~(Implied: declaring `onDelete` / `onUpdate` enforces referential integrity in the reference runtime.)~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** ~~The reference runtime does not enforce~~ Referential actions **are** enforced in the reference runtime via `ReferentialActionApplier` (`src/manifest/runtime-referential-actions.ts`, wired at `runtime-engine.ts:1245-1270`). The runtime applies cascade/restrict/setNull/setDefault semantics on `deleteInstance()` based on the IR relationship configuration. Projection adapters also emit referential-integrity constraints (for example Prisma `onDelete`/`onUpdate`).

## Concurrency controls (vNext)

Entities can declare optimistic concurrency controls using `versionProperty` and `versionAtProperty`. The runtime compares the caller-supplied version against the stored value on every mutation.

```manifest
entity Document {
  property required id: string = ""
  property title: string = ""
  property content: string = ""

  versionProperty version: number
  versionAtProperty versionAt: number

  command update(newTitle: string, currentVersion: number) {
    guard self.version == currentVersion
    mutate title = newTitle
    emit DocumentUpdated
  }
}
```

If the caller's version does not match, the runtime returns a `ConcurrencyConflict` and does not apply any mutations:

```typescript
{
  entityType: "Document",
  entityId: "doc-1",
  expectedVersion: 2,
  actualVersion: 3,
  conflictCode: "VERSION_MISMATCH"
}
```

## State transitions (vNext)

Declare allowed state changes with the `transition` keyword. The runtime validates every mutation of the property against the rules before applying it.

```manifest
entity Document {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "draft"

  transition status from "draft"     to ["review", "archived"]
  transition status from "review"    to ["published", "draft"]
  transition status from "published" to ["archived"]

  command submit() {
    mutate status = "review"
  }

  command approve() {
    mutate status = "published"
  }

  command archive() {
    mutate status = "archived"
  }
}
```

<Note>
  Transition validation runs **before** entity constraint validation. If the current value has no
  matching `from` rule, the transition is unconstrained from that state. Properties not mentioned in
  any transition rule are always unconstrained.
</Note>

## Default policies (vNext)

Attach a `default policy` to an entity to apply it to every command that does not declare its own policy list:

```manifest
entity Task {
  property required title: string
  property status: string = "pending"

  default policy RequireAuth execute: user.id != null "Authentication required"

  command complete() {
    guard self.assigneeId == user.id or user.role == "admin"
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit TaskCompleted
  }

  command reassign(newAssigneeId: string) {
    guard user.role == "admin" or user.role == "manager"
    mutate assigneeId = newAssigneeId
  }
}
```

Both `complete` and `reassign` inherit `RequireAuth` because neither declares an explicit policy. See [Guards and Policies](/language/guards-policies) for the full inheritance rules.

## Cross-entity constraints

Constraints can reference properties on related entities through relationship traversal. The runtime resolves the relationship chain and evaluates the constraint against the related instance's properties.

```manifest
entity Customer {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "active"
}

entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property required customerId: string
  property amount: number = 0

  belongsTo customer: Customer with customerId

  constraint customerActive: self.customer.status == "active"
    "Customer must be active to place orders"
}
```

The runtime enriches resolved relationship instances with `_entity` metadata, enabling arbitrary-depth chaining (e.g., `self.order.customer.address.city`). When a relationship resolves to `null` (missing or nonexistent target), any member access on it evaluates to `null`, which typically causes the constraint to fail.

<Note>
  Cross-entity constraints work because the runtime resolves relationships synchronously from the
  current store context. No parser or IR compiler changes are needed -- the existing infrastructure
  handles nested member access syntax and compilation.
</Note>

## Pagination

~~Entity collections support built-in pagination for list queries (limit / offset / cursor). The Next.js projection generates list routes that accept `?limit=N&offset=M` or `?cursor=token` and return `nextCursor` or `hasMore`. Pagination operates after tenant filtering and masking.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** **Not shipped.** `pagination-api` remains in the no-implementation / backlog inventory (`docs/FEATURE-LIST.md` Category 3). The reference runtime's `getAllInstances` has no limit/offset/cursor API, and the Next.js projection does not emit paginated list query parameters as described above. **Documentation gap — not verified against shipped Manifest.** Paginate in the host application or store query layer if needed.


---

## events

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/events

---
title: 'Events: typed outbox and pub/sub primitives'
description: 'Declare typed events with channels and payload schemas. Commands emit them via the emits keyword. The runtime logs each emission with full metadata.'
sidebarTitle: 'Events'
---

Events in Manifest are first-class declarations. You define a schema, assign a channel, and commands reference the event by name using the `emit` action. Every emission is recorded in the runtime's event log with a payload, timestamp, and workflow metadata.

## Declaring an event

Use the `event` keyword followed by a name, a channel string, and an optional payload schema block:

```manifest
event TaskCompleted: "task.completed" {
  taskId: string
  completedBy: string
}

event CounterIncremented: "counter.incremented" {
  counterId: string
}
```

The channel (`"task.completed"`) is a free-form string. It appears on every `EmittedEvent` and can be used by subscribers to route messages.

Payload fields are typed with `string`, `number`, `boolean`, or `timestamp`. They serve as the schema contract — the runtime populates the payload from command input and the last action result.

## Emitting events from commands

Use the `emit` action inside a command body to emit an event:

```manifest
entity Counter {
  property value: number = 0

  command increment() {
    mutate value = value + 1
    emit CounterIncremented
  }

  command reset() {
    mutate value = 0
    emit CounterReset
  }
}

event CounterIncremented: "counter.incremented" {
  counterId: string
}

event CounterReset: "counter.reset" {
  counterId: string
}
```

~~A command can emit multiple events. They are emitted in declaration order after all actions complete successfully.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** `emit` / `publish` **actions** run in action order during the
> actions phase (not after). Separately, a command's declared `emits` list is processed after
> actions succeed (`semantics.md` steps 6–7). Multiple emissions keep declaration / action order
> within that phase.

## EmittedEvent shape

Every emitted event is recorded as an `EmittedEvent` object in `CommandResult.emittedEvents`:

```typescript
interface EmittedEvent {
  name: string; // Event name, e.g. "TaskCompleted"
  channel: string; // Channel string, e.g. "task.completed"
  payload: object; // Command input + last action result
  timestamp: number; // Milliseconds since epoch (from runtime time source)
  emitIndex: number; // Zero-based index within this runCommand invocation (vNext)

  // Workflow metadata (vNext — present when supplied in command options)
  correlationId?: string;
  causationId?: string;
}
```

The `payload` object contains the command's input parameters and the last action result. This gives event consumers enough context to reconstruct what triggered the emission.

## Workflow metadata (vNext)

Pass `correlationId` and `causationId` in the command options to propagate identifiers across a multi-step workflow. The runtime attaches them to every event emitted during that invocation.

~~```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'Order',
  'complete',
  { instanceId: 'order-123' },
  {
    correlationId: 'workflow-abc',
    causationId: 'step-2',
  },
);
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Signature is `runCommand(commandName, input, options?)` — three
> arguments max. `entityName`, `instanceId`, `correlationId`, and `causationId` all go in `options`.

```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'complete',
  {},
  {
    entityName: 'Order',
    instanceId: 'order-123',
    correlationId: 'workflow-abc',
    causationId: 'step-2',
  },
);

// result.emittedEvents[0].correlationId === "workflow-abc"
// result.emittedEvents[0].causationId   === "step-2"
```

`emitIndex` is a per-command zero-based counter. It resets to `0` at the start of each `runCommand` call. It is not a global sequence — cross-command ordering is the caller's responsibility.

<Note>
  Given identical IR, identical runtime context, identical input, and identical options, emitted
  events will always have identical `emitIndex` values. This is a determinism guarantee.
</Note>

## Subscribing to events

Use `runtime.onEvent(listener)` to register a listener. The listener receives **every** emitted event — filter by `event.name` inside the callback. It returns an unsubscribe function.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'worker-1', role: 'system' },
});

const unsubscribe = runtime.onEvent((event) => {
  if (event.name === 'TaskCompleted') {
    console.log('Task completed:', event.payload);
    sendNotification(event.payload.completedBy);
  }
  if (event.name === 'OrderCreated') {
    console.log('New order on channel:', event.channel);
    console.log('Correlation:', event.correlationId);
  }
});

// Call unsubscribe() when the listener is no longer needed.
```

Listeners fire synchronously during `runCommand`, after all actions and before `CommandResult` is returned.

## Complete example

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "pending"
  property total: number = 0

  command addItem(amount: number) {
    mutate total = self.total + amount
    emit OrderUpdated
  }

  command complete() {
    guard self.status == "in_progress"
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit OrderCompleted
  }
}

store Order in memory

event OrderUpdated: "order.updated" {
  orderId: string
  total: number
}

event OrderCompleted: "order.completed" {
  orderId: string
  finalTotal: number
}
```

~~```typescript
const result = await runtime.runCommand('Order', 'complete', {
  instanceId: 'order-001',
});
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Same `runCommand(commandName, input, options)` shape — not entity-first.

```typescript
const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir);

runtime.onEvent((event) => {
  if (event.name === 'OrderCompleted') {
    console.log(`Order ${event.payload.orderId} completed.`);
  }
});

const result = await runtime.runCommand('complete', {}, {
  entityName: 'Order',
  instanceId: 'order-001',
});

console.log(result.emittedEvents);
// [
//   {
//     name: "OrderCompleted",
//     channel: "order.completed",
//     payload: { /* command input + last action result */ },
//     timestamp: 1748131200000,
//     emitIndex: 0
//   }
// ]
```

## OverrideApplied event (vNext)

When an authorized constraint override is applied, the runtime synthesizes and emits an `OverrideApplied` event. This event is not declared in your `.manifest` source — it is injected by the runtime automatically:

```typescript
{
  name: "OverrideApplied",
  channel: "system",
  payload: {
    constraintCode: "requiresApproval",
    reason: "Emergency processing authorized by finance lead",
    authorizedBy: "user-999",
    timestamp: 1748131200000,
    commandName: "process",
    entityName: "FinancialRecord",
    instanceId: "rec-42"
  }
}
```

This event appears in `CommandResult.emittedEvents` alongside any command-declared events.

## Webhook inbound triggers

Webhooks declare inbound HTTP endpoints that trigger commands when an external system sends a request. The reference runtime verifies the HMAC signature (when declared), deduplicates by idempotency key, evaluates the payload transform, and dispatches the command — every step fail-closed.

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "pending"
  property providerRef: string = ""

  command handlePayment(providerRef: string, status: string) {
    guard status == "completed"
    mutate providerRef = providerRef
    mutate status = "paid"
    emit PaymentReceived
  }
}

webhook paymentCallback "/webhooks/payment" run Order.handlePayment
  idempotencyHeader: "X-Idempotency-Key"
  signature {
    algorithm: "hmac-sha256"
    header: "X-Signature"
    secret: "context.webhookSecret"
  }
```

### Webhook configuration

A webhook is declared as `webhook <name> "<path>" run [Entity.]<command>`, where the path string is the generated route and `run` names the command to invoke. The declaration may be followed by optional colon clauses:

| Field                | Description                                                                                                                   |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `method:`            | HTTP method as a string (e.g. `method: "PUT"`); defaults to `POST` when omitted                                               |
| `idempotencyHeader:` | Optional header name carrying an idempotency key for deduplication                                                            |
| `transform:`         | Optional `{ <param>: <expr>, ... }` block mapping request data onto command parameters                                        |
| `signature { ... }`  | Optional verification block with `algorithm` (`hmac-sha256` or `hmac-sha512`), `header`, and `secret` (a context path string) |

<Note>
  When a `signature` block is declared, the reference runtime verifies the HMAC over the exact
  received request bytes before the command is dispatched, comparing with `crypto.timingSafeEqual`.
  The provided signature may be bare hex or a `sha256=`/`sha512=`-prefixed hex (GitHub convention).
  A missing or invalid signature is rejected `401` and no command runs; an unresolvable `secret`
  context path or an unsupported `algorithm` is a fail-closed `500`. A webhook with **no**
  `signature` block is unauthenticated by design — declare one to require authentication. The full
  request pipeline (matching, idempotency, transform, dispatch statuses) is specified in
  `docs/spec/semantics.md` § "Webhooks (Inbound HTTP Triggers)".
</Note>

## Event sourcing store

The `event-sourced` store target persists entity state as an append-only event log rather than overwriting entity records. Each command produces events that are appended to the log, and entity state is reconstructed by replaying events from the beginning.

```manifest
store Order in event-sourced {
  snapshotInterval: 100
  exposeEventLog: true
}
```

The event-sourced store supports:

- **Append-only writes** — events are never modified or deleted
- **Snapshotting** — periodic snapshots (default: every 100 events) avoid replaying the full log
- **Replay** — entity state is reconstructed by replaying events from the last snapshot (or from beginning)
- **getEventLog()** — when `exposeEventLog: true`, returns the append-only log for an entity

<Warning>
  Event sourcing is a storage strategy, not a runtime behavior change. Commands execute identically
  regardless of whether the backing store is memory, postgres, or event-sourced. The difference is
  how state is persisted and recovered.
</Warning>

<Callout type="info">
**Implemented (2026-07-22)**: `EventSourcedStore` ships in the reference runtime (`stores/event-sourced.ts:37-140`, auto-wired at `runtime-engine.ts:1324-1327`). See [Event Sourced Store](/adapters/event-sourced-store) for full documentation.
</Callout>

> **Correction (2026-07-22)** The 2026-07-15 correction above is **stale** — `EventSourcedStore` **is** implemented
> and shipped (`src/manifest/stores/event-sourced.ts:37-140`). The reference runtime auto-wires it for
> `store Entity in eventSourced { ... }` declarations (`runtime-engine.ts:1324-1326`). Features: append-only
> event log, configurable snapshot interval (default 100), `getEventLog()` / `getSnapshot()` methods when
> `exposeEventLog: true`. See the [Event Sourced Store adapter page](/adapters/event-sourced-store) for details.


---

## expressions

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/expressions

---
title: 'Expression built-ins: strings, numbers, dates, and aggregates'
sidebarTitle: 'Expression built-ins'
description: "Manifest's deterministic, side-effect-free expression library — string and numeric helpers, UTC date components, and collection aggregates with mapper and predicate lambdas."
---

Manifest's expression language ships a library of deterministic, side-effect-free built-in functions: string and numeric helpers, UTC date components, and aggregate operations over collections. The aggregates accept optional mapper or predicate lambdas, letting computed properties summarize related entities declaratively. All functions are side-effect-free and produce identical results given identical input and runtime context.

## Scalar and string built-ins

String and numeric helpers can be used anywhere expressions are evaluated — `compute` bindings, guards, computed properties, and constraints:

```manifest
command testStringOps(input: string) {
  guard input != ""
  compute cleaned = trim(input)
  compute partCount = count(split(input, ","))
  compute hasPrefix = startsWith(input, "hello")
  compute hasSuffix = endsWith(input, "world")
  compute swapped = replace(input, "old", "new")
  compute upper = toUpperCase(input)
  compute lower = toLowerCase(input)
  compute charCount = length(input)
  compute alsoCharCount = input.length
  compute slice = substring(input, 0, 5)
  compute foundAt = indexOf(input, "world")
}
```

`input.length` is sugar for `length(input)` on strings and arrays (same for `self.tags.length`). Prefer either form; they evaluate the same.

Numeric helpers `abs`, `round`, `floor`, `ceil`, `min`, and `max` and the array helper `sum` appear in the same fixture, and the date components `year`/`month`/`day`/`hours`/`minutes`/`seconds` operate on a millisecond timestamp.

String helpers wrap the corresponding `String.prototype` methods (returning the input unchanged for non-strings), numeric helpers delegate to `Math`, and `min`/`max` are variadic over numeric arguments. The date components use UTC methods so results are timezone-independent — see [Types](/language/types) for the full list.

> **Note (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Date conversion / duration builtins also exist (`dateOf`, `timeOf`, `datetimeOf`, `addDuration`, `durationBetween`, `durationDays` / `Hours` / `Minutes` / `Seconds`) — see `docs/spec/builtins.md`.

## Aggregate built-ins with lambdas

The aggregates reduce a collection — typically a `hasMany` relationship reached via `self.` — to a single value, optionally transforming or filtering each element with a lambda:

```manifest
entity Order {
  property required customerName: string
  property status: string = "pending"

  hasMany lineItems: LineItem

  computed totalAmount: number = sum(self.lineItems, (item) => item.price * item.quantity)
  computed itemCount: number = count_of(self.lineItems)
  computed averagePrice: number = avg(self.lineItems, (item) => item.price)
  computed cheapestItem: number = min_of(self.lineItems, (item) => item.price)
  computed mostExpensiveItem: number = max_of(self.lineItems, (item) => item.price)
