  computed highValueCount: number = count_of(self.lineItems, (item) => item.price * item.quantity > 50)
}

entity LineItem {
  property required price: number
  property quantity: number = 1

  belongsTo order: Order

  computed lineTotal: number = price * quantity
}

store Order in memory
store LineItem in memory
```

For `sum`, `avg`, `min_of`, and `max_of` the lambda maps each element to a number; for `count_of` the lambda is a predicate selecting which elements to count.

The behavior with a lambda is:

- `sum` adds each mapped number.
- `avg` averages the mapped numbers (`0` for an empty collection).
- `min_of` / `max_of` return the smallest / largest mapped number (`undefined` for an empty collection).
- `count_of` returns the total length without a predicate, or the count of elements for which the predicate is truthy.

Without a lambda, `sum` and `avg` operate on the raw numeric elements and `count_of` returns the collection length. Each aggregate guards against a non-array first argument: `sum` returns its argument unchanged, `avg` returns `0`, `count_of` returns `0`, `min_of`/`max_of` return `undefined`, and `filter`/`map` return an empty array (or the array unchanged when no callback is supplied).

Because aggregating over a `hasMany` relationship requires resolving related instances asynchronously, the lambda-bearing branches return promises that the evaluator awaits. The element mapper is invoked once per element and its result awaited, keeping evaluation deterministic for a fixed runtime context.

<Note>
  Two distinct count helpers exist: `count(array)` (a plain length used above in `count(split(input,
  ","))`) and `count_of(collection, predicate?)` (the aggregate). Likewise `min`/`max` are variadic
  over scalar arguments, which is different from `min_of`/`max_of`, which reduce a collection. Using
  an aggregate on a non-array yields the safe fallbacks listed above rather than an error.
</Note>

## Standard library

The Manifest standard library (`stdlib`) extends the built-in expression functions with additional utilities for common operations. These functions are available automatically — no import is required.

### Full-text search

The `search(text, query)` function performs full-text matching against a `searchable` property:

```manifest
entity Article {
  property required title: string
  property searchable content: string

  constraint relevant: search(self.content, "manifest dsl")
}
```

The `searchable` modifier on string properties signals to projections that a full-text index should be created (e.g., Prisma `@@fulltext`, Drizzle GIN index). The `search()` function is the runtime counterpart that evaluates the match.

### Feature flags in expressions

The `flag(name)` function resolves a feature flag value at expression evaluation time:

```manifest
constraint betaEnabled: flag("new-pricing") == true

guard flag("max-order-limit") == false or self.total < flag("max-order-limit")
```

`flag()` supports boolean, string, number, and object flag values. See [Feature flags](/language/feature-flags) for the full feature flag system.

### Extended built-ins

Additional built-in functions available in the standard library:

| Function  | Signature                   | Description                              |
| --------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `between` | `between(value, low, high)` | Returns `true` if `low <= value <= high` |
| `matches` | `matches(text, pattern)`    | Tests `text` against a regex `pattern`   |
| `search`  | `search(text, query)`       | Full-text search match                   |
| `flag`    | `flag(name)`                | Resolve a feature flag value             |

## Custom expression functions

~~You can register custom expression functions via the plugin API… `registerBuiltin`…~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** There is **no** `registerBuiltin` export. Custom functions are injected via `RuntimeOptions.customBuiltins` (a `Map<string, Function>`), typically collected by the plugin loader from `BuiltinFunctionPlugin` entries (`docs/spec/builtins.md` § Custom Expression Functions). Core builtins always win name collisions.

### Registering a custom function

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { definePlugin } from '@angriff36/manifest/plugin-api';

// Plugin form (preferred for packages):
export default definePlugin({
  manifest: {
    name: '@acme/custom-functions',
    version: '1.0.0',
    pluginApiVersion: '1',
    manifestVersion: '>=1.0.0',
  },
  builtins: [
    {
      name: 'discount',
      purity: 'pure',
      arity: 2,
      fn: (price: unknown, tier: unknown) => {
        if (typeof price !== 'number') return price;
        if (tier === 'premium') return price * 0.8;
        if (tier === 'standard') return price * 0.9;
        return price;
      },
    },
  ],
});

// Direct RuntimeOptions form (tests / hosts):
const customBuiltins = new Map<string, (...args: unknown[]) => unknown>();
customBuiltins.set('discount', (price, tier) => {
  /* ... */
});
const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {}, { customBuiltins });
```

Once wired, the function is available in Manifest expressions:

```manifest
entity Order {
  property total: number = 100
  property tier: string = "standard"

  computed discountedTotal: number = discount(self.total, self.tier)
}
```

### Constraints on custom functions

<Warning>
  Custom functions are not deterministic by default — they can reference external state, make
  network calls, or produce different results for the same inputs. If you use custom functions in
  guards or constraints, you are responsible for ensuring they produce consistent results within a
  single `runCommand` invocation. Violating this breaks the Manifest determinism guarantee.
</Warning>

For this reason, custom functions used in expressions should be pure functions with no side effects. If external data is needed, inject it through the runtime context (`user`, `context.*`) rather than calling out from inside a custom function.


---

## feature flags

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/feature-flags

---
title: 'Feature flags: runtime-resolved gating'
sidebarTitle: 'Feature flags'
description: 'Gate commands and computed properties on named feature flags with the flag() built-in, resolved through a runtime-supplied provider and off by default.'
---

The `flag(name)` built-in resolves a named feature flag through a runtime-provided provider, letting guards and computed properties gate behavior on flags declared in the program but evaluated against external flag state. When no provider is configured, `flag` returns `false`, so features are off by default.

## Syntax

`flag(name)` is used in guards and computed properties. The single argument is the flag name as a string literal, and `flag(...)` composes with other expressions:

```manifest
entity FeatureGatedEntity {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "inactive"

  command activate() {
    guard flag("new-activation-flow")
    mutate status = "active"
  }

  command deactivate() {
    guard flag("allow-deactivation") and self.status == "active"
    mutate status = "inactive"
  }
}

entity FlagInspector {
  property required id: string
  property flagValue: string = ""

  computed isNewUIEnabled: boolean = flag("new-ui")
}

~~store FeatureGatedEntity in memory~~
store ViewerGatedEntity in memory
store FlagInspector in memory
```

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Store name must match the entity (`ViewerGatedEntity`), not a non-existent `FeatureGatedEntity`.

## Behavior

The `flag` built-in returns `false` if the argument is not a string. Otherwise, if a `flagProvider` runtime option is set, it delegates to that provider — `this.options.flagProvider(name)` — and returns whatever the provider returns. When no provider is configured, it returns `false`.

The provider is typed as `flagProvider?: (name: string) => unknown`. Its return value is passed through unchanged, so a flag can resolve to a boolean, string, number, or object depending on the provider — the engine does not coerce it. This is the documented integration point for wiring an external system such as LaunchDarkly:

```typescript
flagProvider: (name) => launchDarklyClient.variation(name, false);
```

Because resolution happens entirely through the runtime-supplied provider and the IR carries only the `flag("name")` call, the same compiled program yields different gating behavior in different runtime contexts without any IR change — consistent with Manifest's rule that variability enters through runtime context, not by editing the IR. In a guard, a falsey `flag(...)` result halts command execution at that guard in order, the same as any other guard expression.

Feature flags are a runtime expression concern, not a schema construct, so there is no dedicated projection mapping; the `flag("name")` call is carried in the IR like any other expression.

<Warning>
  The conformance fixture `66-feature-flags.manifest` has an expected `.ir.json` but **no**
  `results.json`. The IR shape of `flag(...)` is conformance-locked, but the runtime gating behavior
  described above is verified only against the engine source and unit-level reasoning, not against a
  recorded conformance results fixture. Treat the runtime semantics as confirmed in code but not yet
  pinned by an executable conformance result.
</Warning>

<Note>
  The safe default is off: with no `flagProvider`, every `flag(...)` returns `false`, so guards
  depending on a flag deny by default. Provider return values are not validated or coerced by the
  engine, so a provider that returns a non-boolean passes that value straight into the surrounding
  expression.
</Note>


---

## guards policies

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/guards-policies

---
title: 'Guards and policies: access control'
description: 'Use guards for precondition checks and policies for authorization. Both are boolean expressions evaluated in strict order before any action runs.'
sidebarTitle: 'Guards & Policies'
---

Manifest separates authorization into two distinct mechanisms: **policies**, which control who is allowed to execute a command at all, and **guards**, which verify that the current state satisfies the preconditions for the command to proceed. Both are evaluated before any action runs, and neither can be bypassed.

## Guards

A guard is a boolean expression declared inside a command body. Guards are evaluated in declaration order. Execution halts on the first expression that evaluates to `false` — no later guards run, and no actions execute.

```manifest
entity Task {
  property completed: boolean = false
  property priority: number = 1

  command complete() {
    guard not self.completed
    mutate completed = true
    emit TaskCompleted
  }

  command setPriority(level: number) {
    guard level >= 1 and level <= 5
    mutate priority = level
  }
}
```

### Guard diagnostics

When a guard fails, the runtime returns a `guardFailure` in the `CommandResult` with:

- `index` — 1-based position of the failing guard
- `expression` — the source expression as a string
- `resolved` — array of `{ expression, value }` pairs showing what each sub-expression evaluated to

This gives you enough information to explain the failure to the caller without any manual debugging.

```typescript
if (result.guardFailure) {
  console.error(`Guard ${result.guardFailure.index} failed: ${result.guardFailure.expression}`);
  for (const r of result.guardFailure.resolved) {
    console.error(`  ${r.expression} => ${r.value}`);
  }
}
```

### Available bindings in guards

Guard expressions evaluate in the command's evaluation context. You have access to:

| Binding             | Value                                                             |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `self` / `this`     | The current entity instance (or `null` when no instance is bound) |
| `user`              | The current user object, or `null` when unauthenticated           |
| `context`           | The runtime context object                                        |
| `context.tenantId`  | Active tenant ID (if provided by caller)                          |
| `context.orgId`     | Active organization ID (if provided)                              |
| `context.actorId`   | Acting user ID (if provided)                                      |
| `context.requestId` | Caller-supplied request ID                                        |
| Command params      | Named parameters from the command signature                       |
| `now()`             | Current time as milliseconds since epoch                          |
| `uuid()`            | A new globally unique identifier                                  |

<Warning>
  If a guard references `user` and no user is provided to the runtime, the guard correctly fails.
  This is not a bug — it is the expected behavior. The runtime does not inject a permissive default
  `user` to make demos work.
</Warning>

### Operators in guard expressions

Guards support the full expression language:

| Category    | Operators                        |
| ----------- | -------------------------------- |
| Arithmetic  | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`          |
| Comparison  | `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` |
| Logical     | `and`, `or`, `not`, `!`          |
| Membership  | `in`, `contains`                 |
| Conditional | `? :` (ternary)                  |

`==` uses loose equality (`undefined == null` is `true`). Use `!=` for strict inequality. Note: `is not` parses as `x is (not y)` (a type check combined with a logical negation), not as a single inequality operator.

## Policies

A policy is a named boolean expression with a **scope** that controls when it is checked. Policies are declared at entity level (inside an entity block) or at module level (outside any entity block).

```manifest
policy adminOnly execute: user.role == "admin" "Only administrators can execute commands"
```

The expression after the scope keyword is evaluated before any command runs. If it returns `false`, the runtime returns a `policyDenial` result and no further execution occurs.

### Policy scopes

| Scope      | When enforced                                                |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `execute`  | Before any command execution                                 |
| `read`     | At the runtime read gate — `getInstance` / `getAllInstances` |
| `write`    | On entity write operations (not enforced by default runtime) |
| `all`      | Both command execution and the read gate                     |
| `override` | Before authorizing a constraint override (vNext)             |

The runtime enforces `execute` and `all` scopes before command execution. `read` (and `all`) scopes are **also** enforced at a single central read gate in `getInstance` / `getAllInstances`: a denied read fails closed — `getInstance` returns `undefined` (no existence leak) and `getAllInstances` omits the denied rows. A read policy referencing `self.*` is evaluated per row; a context-only policy is evaluated once per call. The internal execution read path (guards, actions, computed properties) is un-gated, so read policies never change command results. `write` and `delete` scopes are still enforced only at command execution (via `command.policies`), not on the store — they are otherwise available for custom adapter or projection logic.

### Binding a policy to an entity

A policy applies to all commands on a specific entity when you reference it inside the entity block or use the entity name in a standalone policy:

```manifest
entity Document {
  property required title: string
  property isPublic: boolean = false

  command makePublic() {
    mutate isPublic = true
    emit DocumentPublished
  }
}

policy adminOnly execute: user.role == "admin" "Only admins can execute"
```

In this example, `adminOnly` is a module-level policy. Use `default policy` inside an entity block to apply a policy to every command on that entity.

### Per-command access restrictions

Policies are entity-level or module-level — there is no inline policy syntax inside a command body
(**confirmed 2026-07-15**; command-body policy clause remains an Appendix E backlog item). To
enforce stricter access on a single command, use a guard inside that command:

```manifest
entity InventoryItem {
  property stock: number = 0

  default policy KitchenRoles execute: user.role in ["kitchen_staff", "kitchen_lead", "manager", "admin"]

  command consume(quantity: number) {
    // Inherits KitchenRoles policy
    guard quantity > 0
    mutate stock = self.stock - quantity
  }

  command adjust(delta: number) {
    // Per-command stricter access: add a guard rather than an inline policy
    guard user.role in ["kitchen_lead", "manager", "admin"]
    mutate stock = self.stock + delta
  }
}
```

<Note>
  Guards produce a `guardFailure` result; policies produce a `policyDenial` result. Use guards for
  state-based preconditions and additional per-command role checks; use policies for entity-wide
  authorization.
</Note>

## Default policies (vNext)

~~Use `default policy` on an entity to apply a policy to every command that does not declare its own:~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** Use `default policy` on an entity to apply a policy to every command
> on that entity. There is **no** source syntax for a command-body / per-command `policies` clause
> (Appendix E backlog). Per-command stricter checks use **guards**. Spec/IR may carry
> `IRCommand.policies`, but the compiler today only expands entity `default policy` names into that
> array.

```manifest
entity Task {
  property required title: string
  property status: string = "pending"
  property assigneeId: string

  default policy RequireAuth execute: user.id != null "Authentication required"

  command complete() {
    guard self.assigneeId == user.id or user.role == "admin"
    mutate status = "completed"
    emit TaskCompleted
  }

  command reassign(newAssigneeId: string) {
    guard user.role == "admin" or user.role == "manager"
    mutate assigneeId = newAssigneeId
  }
}
```

Both `complete` and `reassign` inherit `RequireAuth`. Add a guard inside any command for additional per-command access checks.

### Inheritance rules

1. If the entity declares a `default policy`, every command on that entity inherits it (expanded into IR at compile time).
2. ~~If a command declares its own policies, those replace defaults (not merged).~~ **Correction (2026-07-15):** No `.manifest` syntax currently declares per-command policies; use guards for stricter per-command checks.
3. If no default policy applies, no policy check runs (the scanner will warn).

The IR compiler expands default policies into each command's effective policy list at compile time. The runtime always evaluates an explicit, expanded list — it does not perform inheritance at runtime.

## Override policies (vNext)

The `override` scope is a special policy used to authorize constraint bypasses. When a constraint is marked `overrideable`, the runtime evaluates the referenced override policy before granting the bypass:

```manifest
entity FinancialRecord {
  command process() {
    constraint overrideable requiresApproval {
      expression: self.status == "approved" or self.amount <= 500
      severity: block
      message: "Must be approved before processing"
      overridePolicy: canApproveLargeAmounts
    }

    mutate status = "processed"
    emit RecordProcessed
  }
}

policy canApproveLargeAmounts override:
  user.role == "manager" or user.role == "admin"
  "Only managers and admins can approve large amounts"
```

If the override policy passes, the constraint outcome is marked `overridden: true`. Both override paths are audited: the auto-policy path emits the same synthetic `OverrideApplied` event the explicit-request path emits, with `authorizedBy` derived from the acting user in context (falling back to `policy:<name>` when no user is present) and the event `reason` recording the authorizing policy. Attempts to override a constraint that is not marked `overrideable` are always rejected.

## Default-deny enforcement

When the scanner detects a command with no policy coverage (neither explicit nor inherited from entity defaults), it emits a warning. In strict environments, this warning can be promoted to an error:

```bash
manifest scan --strict
```

The `--strict` flag causes the scanner to exit non-zero when any command lacks policy coverage. This enforces a default-deny posture: every command must explicitly declare who is authorized to run it, or inherit a default policy from its entity.

<Warning>
  A command with no policies is **not blocked** by the runtime — it executes normally. The
  default-deny enforcement is a static analysis check (via `manifest scan`), not a runtime gate. To
  enforce it in CI, add `manifest scan --strict` to your pipeline.
</Warning>

## Roles and permission inheritance

For fine-grained authorization based on role hierarchies (e.g., Admin → Manager → User with inherited permissions), see [Roles and permissions](/language/roles). Roles integrate with policies through the `user.role` binding and the `hasPermission()` / `roleAllows()` builtins.


---

## manifest files

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/manifest-files

---
title: 'Manifest files: anatomy, syntax, and best practices'
description: 'The complete reference for .manifest source files — what a file contains, the declaration grammar, types, expressions, ordering rules, what is not allowed, and house-style best practices.'
sidebarTitle: 'Manifest files (.manifest)'
---

A `.manifest` file is the human-authored source of a Manifest program. The compiler reads it through a lexer → parser → AST → IR-compiler pipeline and produces an **Intermediate Representation (IR)** — a JSON document that is the single source of truth for program semantics. Everything else (TypeScript, Prisma schemas, Convex functions, React Query hooks, and the other [projections](/projections/additional-projections)) is a _view_ generated from that IR.

This page is the canonical reference for the file format itself: what you can put in a `.manifest` file, what you cannot, how the pieces are ordered, and the conventions that keep programs deterministic. For deep dives on individual constructs, follow the cross-links into the rest of the Language section.

<Note>
  The grammar described here is the one the parser actually accepts (`src/manifest/lexer.ts`,
  `src/manifest/parser.ts`). When this page and your intuition disagree, the parser wins — and the
  conformance fixtures in `src/manifest/conformance/` are the executable proof.
</Note>

## Anatomy of a file

A file is a flat sequence of **top-level declarations**. There is no required wrapper, no `package` header, and no semicolons. Whitespace and newlines are insignificant except as token separators.

```manifest
// 1. Cross-file imports first (optional)
use "./shared/money.manifest"

// 2. Any number of declarations, in any order
enum Status { draft, published, archived }

entity Invoice {
  property required id: string
  property status: Status = draft
  property total: decimal(12, 2) = 0

  computed isPayable: boolean = total > 0 and status == published

  command release() {
    guard self.total > 0
    mutate status = published
    emit invoicePublished
  }
}

store Invoice in postgres { table: "invoices" }
event invoicePublished: "billing.invoice.published" { invoiceId: string }
```

The only hard ordering rule at the top level is that **every `use` declaration must come before all other declarations** (see [Ordering rules](#ordering-rules)). Beyond that, declarations may appear in any order; the IR preserves the order it encountered them.

### Comments

```manifest
// line comment

/*
  block comment
*/
```

### Identifiers

Identifiers match `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*`. Entity, enum, and module names are conventionally `PascalCase`; properties, commands, and policies are `camelCase`. [Reserved words](#reserved-words) cannot be used as declaration names, but they _are_ allowed as member-access keys and object-literal keys (so `self.from` or `{ to: ... }` parse fine).

### Literals

| Kind    | Examples                                                                                                    |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| String  | `"hello"`, `'hello'`, `` `hello` `` (all three quote styles; backticks are plain strings, not interpolated) |
| Number  | `42`, `3.14`                                                                                                |
| Boolean | `true`, `false`                                                                                             |
| Null    | `null`                                                                                                      |

## Top-level declarations

Every construct the parser accepts at the top level:

| Keyword                                  | Purpose                                            | Reference                                                          |
| ---------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `use`                                    | Import another `.manifest` file (must be first)    | [Modules](/language/modules)                                       |
| `module`                                 | Namespace grouping of declarations                 | [Modules](/language/modules)                                       |
| `entity`                                 | A typed data structure with behavior               | [Entities](/language/entities)                                     |
| `enum`                                   | A named set of values                              | [Types](/language/types)                                           |
| `value`                                  | A value object (properties only)                   | [Types](/language/types)                                           |
| `tenant`                                 | Declare the tenant property (max one per program)  | [Tenancy](/language/tenancy)                                       |
| `command` / `async command`              | A standalone operation                             | [Commands](/language/commands) · [Async](/language/async-commands) |
| `policy`                                 | A standalone authorization rule                    | [Guards & policies](/language/guards-policies)                     |
| `store`                                  | Bind an entity to a persistence target             | [Stores](/language/stores)                                         |
| `event`                                  | Declare a publishable event                        | [Events](/language/events)                                         |
| `on`                                     | A reaction that runs a command when an event fires | [Reactions](/language/reactions)                                   |
| `saga`                                   | A multi-step workflow with compensation            | [Workflows](/language/workflows)                                   |
| `role`                                   | A role in the authorization hierarchy              | [Roles](/language/roles)                                           |
| `webhook`                                | An inbound webhook bound to a command              | [Events](/language/events)                                         |
| `schedule`                               | A cron/interval-triggered command                  | [Workflows](/language/workflows)                                   |
| `flow` / `effect` / `expose` / `compose` | **EXPERIMENTAL — NOT IMPLEMENTED** (parser accepts, emits warning, not lowered to IR; compile succeeds but has no runtime effect; see fixture 94-unlowered-constructs.manifest) | — |

A `module` block accepts a subset of these: entities, enums, commands, policies, stores, events, reactions, sagas, roles, webhooks, and schedules.

## Entities

The entity is the core construct. Its header can declare inheritance and mixins, and its body holds the rest in any order.

```manifest fragment
entity PurchaseOrder extends Auditable mixin Timestamped {
  // properties
  property required id: string
  property amount: money(12, 2) = 0
  property private internalNote: string = ""

  // computed (a.k.a. derived) values — recomputed, never stored as input
  computed isLarge: boolean = amount > 10000

  // relationships
  hasMany lines: PurchaseLine
  belongsTo vendor: Vendor

  // constraints
  constraint nonNegative: amount >= 0 "Amount cannot be negative"

  // authorization
  policy canApprove execute: user.role == "manager"
  default policy openAccess all: true

  // behavior & commands
  command approve() {
    guard self.amount > 0
    mutate status = "approved"
    emit poApproved
  }

  // concurrency, state machine, lifecycle flags
  versionProperty version: number
  transition status from "draft" to ["approved", "rejected"]
  timestamps
}
```

Sub-blocks an entity may contain: `property`, `computed`/`derived`, relationships (`hasMany`, `hasOne`, `belongsTo`, `ref`), ~~`behavior`/`on`,~~ `command`/`async command`, `constraint`, `policy` (and a `policies { … }` block / `default policy …`), `store … in …`, `versionProperty`/`versionAtProperty`, `key [...]` / `unique [...]`, `transition`, `approval`, the `timestamps` flag, and the bare `realtime` flag. See [Advanced entities](/language/advanced-entities) for `extends`, `mixin`, keys, and transitions.

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Entity `behavior` blocks and bare `on Event { … }` inside an entity are **rejected at compile time** (hard error; conformance fixture 110). They have no IR representation. Use top-level reactions (`on Event run Entity.command`) or command actions instead (`docs/spec/semantics.md` § Entities).

<Warning>
  Events declared *inside* an entity are dropped with a warning. Declare `event` at the top level or
  inside a `module`.
</Warning>

### External entities

Prefix an entity with `external` to mark it as one owned by another system or file. The compiler sets `external: true` on the IR, and persistence projections (Prisma, Drizzle, Kysely, Convex, prisma-store) **skip** it rather than emitting a table or model for it — useful for referencing a table managed elsewhere.

```manifest
external entity ImportedUser {
  property required id: string
  property email: string
}
```

`external` is a contextual modifier, not a reserved word, so `property external: string` still parses as an ordinary property.

## Types

| Category      | Type names                                             |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Primitives    | `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `any`, `void`           |
| Collections   | `list`, `map`, `array<T>`, and the `T[]` postfix sugar |
| Exact decimal | `decimal(precision, scale)`, `money(precision, scale)` |
| Date/time     | `date`, `time`, `datetime`, `duration`                 |
| User-defined  | enum names, `value` object names                       |
| Nullable      | any type followed by `?` (e.g. `string?`, `Money?`)    |

<Note>
  `date`, `time`, `datetime`, and `duration` are **not reserved words** — the parser accepts any
  token as a type name, so they work as types and the corresponding date/time
  [builtins](#built-in-functions) operate on them. See [Date/time types](/language/types).
</Note>

## Property modifiers

Modifiers are written **before** the property name:

```manifest fragment
property required unique email: string
property private apiKey: string
property masked(last4) cardNumber: string unmask when user.role == "admin"
```

Reserved modifiers: `required`, `unique`, `indexed`, `private`, `readonly`, `optional`, `searchable`, `encrypted`. A default value uses `= <expr>`.

The contextual `masked` modifier accepts a strategy — `masked(redact)`, `masked(partial)`, `masked(email)`, `masked(phone)`, or `masked(last4)` — and an optional trailing `unmask when <expr>` clause. The `unmask when` clause is a compile error without `masked`.

<Warning>
  ~~Omitting `required` does **not** imply optional. Use `optional` or a `?` on the type to mark a
  field nullable.~~
</Warning>

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Omitting `required` means the property is **not** required at create time (type / property defaults may apply). The `optional` modifier is a **projection hint** with no independent runtime enforcement (`docs/spec/semantics.md` § Properties). Nullability is separate: append `?` to the type (for example `string?`). Do not conflate "not required" with "nullable."

## Expressions

Expressions appear in guards, computed properties, constraints, policies, defaults, and command actions.

### Operators

Symbolic: `+ - * / %`, `== != < > <= >=`, `&& || !`, the ternary `? :`, and member/access operators `.` `?.` `[]`. Word operators: `and`, `or`, `not`, `is`, `in`, `contains`. Precedence, lowest to highest: ternary → `or` → `and` → equality (`== != is in contains`) → comparison (`< > <= >=`) → additive → multiplicative → unary (`!`, `-`, `not`) → postfix (`.`, `?.`, calls, index) → primary. Lambdas are written `(a, b) => expr` and are used by aggregate builtins.

### Binding namespaces

Expressions may reference exactly four namespaces. These are spec-guaranteed bindings — guards must reference them rather than relying on inferred state.

| Binding    | Meaning                                             |
| ---------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| `self.`    | The current entity instance (e.g. `self.status`)    |
| `this.`    | Alias for the current instance                      |
| `user.`    | The calling principal (e.g. `user.role`, `user.id`) |
| `context.` | Runtime context supplied at execution time          |

<Note>
  Prefer `self.` over `this.` in new code. `self` is a reserved binding; `this` works only because
  it happens to be an ordinary identifier, so `self` is the spec-blessed form.
</Note>

### Built-in functions

The runtime provides a fixed set of builtins (`getBuiltins`, `src/manifest/runtime-engine.ts`). Core builtins always win over a plugin of the same name, so these names are effectively reserved in expressions:

- **General:** `now`, `uuid`
- **Strings:** `trim`, `split`, `count`, `startsWith`, `endsWith`, `replace`, `toUpperCase`, `toLowerCase`, `length`, `substring`, `indexOf`, `matches`, `search`
- **Math:** `abs`, `round`, `floor`, `ceil`, `min`, `max`, `between`
- **Aggregates:** `sum`, `avg`, `min_of`, `max_of`, `count_of`, `filter`, `map`, `flat_map`, `unique_of`
- **Date/time:** `year`, `month`, `day`, `hours`, `minutes`, `seconds`, `dateOf`, `timeOf`, `datetimeOf`, `addDuration`, `durationBetween`, `durationDays`, `durationHours`, `durationMinutes`, `durationSeconds`
- **Feature flags:** `flag`
- **RBAC:** `hasPermission`, `roleAllows`

See [Expressions](/language/expressions) for usage and examples.

## Commands and execution order

```manifest
entity Account {
  property required id: string
  property balance: decimal(12, 2) = 0

  command transfer(amount: decimal(12, 2), optional memo: string = "") {
    retry { maxAttempts: 3 backoff: fixed delay: 1000 }
    rateLimit { maxRequests: 10 windowMs: 60000 scope: user }
    guard self.balance >= amount
    constraint amountPositive: amount > 0 "Amount must be positive"
    mutate balance = self.balance - amount
    emit transferred
  }
}

event transferred: "account.transfer.completed" { accountId: string }
```

At runtime, a command executes in a **fixed order** that cannot be reconfigured:

~~Policies → Guards → Constraints → Actions → Emits → Return~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Per `docs/spec/semantics.md` § Commands:
> 1. Build evaluation context (parameter defaults / required-parameter fail-closed)
> 2. Rate-limit (when declared)
> 3. Policies
> 4. **Command-level constraints**
> 5. Guards
> 6. Actions
> 7. Emits
> 8. Return
>
> Guards run **after** command constraints, not before.

<Steps>
  <Step title="Evaluation context">Parameter defaults apply; missing required parameters fail closed.</Step>
  <Step title="Rate-limit">When declared on the command.</Step>
  <Step title="Policies">Authorization rules are evaluated.</Step>
  <Step title="Constraints">Command-level constraints run with severity (`ok`/`warn`/`block`).</Step>
  <Step title="Guards">
    Guards run in declaration order; execution halts on the first falsey guard.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Actions">Mutations and other effects apply.</Step>
  <Step title="Emits">Declared events publish.</Step>
  <Step title="Return">The result is produced.</Step>
</Steps>

This ordering is part of the language contract. Diagnostics explain failures (which guard, which expression, resolved values) but never change the order or auto-repair a failing program.

## Other declarations at a glance

```manifest fragment
enum Priority { low, normal = "Normal", urgent(2) }      // optional label and ordinal

value Money {                                            // value objects hold properties only
  property amount: decimal(12, 2)
  property currency: string
}

policy adminOnly execute: user.role == "admin"           // standalone policy

store Order in supabase { table: "orders" }              // persistence binding

event orderPlaced: "shop.order.placed" { orderId: string }

on orderPlaced run Inventory.reserve resolve payload._subject.id  // reaction

role manager extends staff { allow approve  deny delete }  // role hierarchy

saga Checkout {                                          // workflow with compensation
  step charge   { command: Payment.charge   compensate: Payment.refund }
  step ship     { command: Fulfillment.ship compensate: Fulfillment.cancel }
  on_failure: "compensate"
  emit checkoutFailed
}

webhook stripe "/hooks/stripe" run Payment.record        // inbound webhook
  method: "POST"
  idempotencyHeader: "Stripe-Idempotency-Key"

schedule nightlyClose cron "0 0 * * *" run Ledger.close  // scheduled command
```

## Ordering rules

- **`use` first.** All `use` declarations must precede every other top-level declaration. A `use` that appears later is an error.
- **`use` paths** must be relative (`./` or `../`) and end in `.manifest`.
- **One `tenant`** declaration per program.
- **Command execution order** is fixed (~~policies → guards → constraints → actions → emits → return~~ **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** policies → **command constraints** → guards → actions → emits → return, after evaluation-context / rate-limit gates); guards halt on the first falsey result.
- **No required ordering** among an entity's sub-blocks, or among top-level declarations other than `use`. IR preserves encounter order.

## What is not allowed

The parser rejects these outright — they are common mistakes, not features:

- **No entity generics.** `entity Paginated<T>` is invalid; type parameters exist only inside property types like `array<T>`.
- **No `import` statements.** Cross-file composition is `use "./file.manifest"` only. There is no `import { X } from "…"`.
- **No `policy` clause inside a command body.** Declare authorization at the entity level (`policy … execute: …`) or as a top-level `policy`.
- **Reserved words as identifiers.** A keyword used as a declaration name produces _"Reserved word 'X' cannot be used as an identifier."_
- **`value` blocks** may contain properties only — no commands, computed, or constraints.
- **Events inside entities** are dropped with a warning.
- **`unmask when` without `masked`** is a compile error.
- **`async`** must be followed by `command`.
- Structural mistakes — missing colons, unclosed braces, a constraint block without an `expression`, a composite `references [...]` without an explicit `fields [...]`, or a saga/approval missing its required command/stage — all surface as parse errors via `Expected X, got Y`.

## Best practices

The language is designed around a small number of [house-style](https://github.com/Angriff36/Manifest) principles. Following them keeps programs deterministic and reviewable.

- **Determinism over convenience.** Identical IR plus identical runtime context must produce identical results. Avoid leaning on ambient state; pass variability through `context.`.
- **Explicitness over inference.** Guards and policies should reference the spec-guaranteed bindings (`self.`, `user.`, `context.`) rather than implicit values. Prefer `self.` to `this.`.
- **Let constraints carry intent.** Give every constraint a message and an appropriate severity (`block` to stop, `warn` to flag, `ok` to annotate). Use `code` and `messageTemplate` when downstream tools consume the failure.
- **Keep authorization at the entity boundary.** Name your policies (`policy canApprove execute: …`) so denials are legible, and use `default policy` for the baseline.
- **Name events in past tense** on a stable channel string (`"billing.invoice.published"`); reactions and webhooks bind to those names.
- **Model money and quantities with `decimal`/`money`**, never `number`, so scale is explicit and exact.
- **Split large programs with `module` and `use`.** Group related entities, keep files focused, and let `use` wire shared value objects and enums.
- **Treat the IR as the contract.** Don't hand-edit generated projections to "fix" behavior — change the `.manifest` source and regenerate. Generated code must not diverge from IR semantics.

## A complete example

```manifest
use "./shared/money.manifest"

module billing {
  enum InvoiceStatus { draft, sent = "Sent", paid(2), cancelled(3) }

  entity Invoice {
    property required id: string
    property status: InvoiceStatus = draft
    property total: money(12, 2) = 0
    property private notes: string = ""

    computed isOverdue: boolean = status == sent and now() > dueDate
    property dueDate: datetime

    hasMany lines: InvoiceLine

    constraint positiveTotal: total >= 0 "Total cannot be negative"

    policy canSend execute: user.role == "billing" or user.role == "admin"
    default policy openAccess all: true

    command send() {
      guard self.total > 0
      guard self.status == draft
      mutate status = sent
      emit invoiceSent { invoiceId: self.id }
    }

    versionProperty version: number
    transition status from "draft" to ["sent", "cancelled"]
    timestamps
  }

  event invoiceSent: "billing.invoice.sent" { invoiceId: string }

  on invoiceSent run Notifier.email resolve self.invoiceId
}

store Invoice in postgres { table: "invoices" }
```

Compile it with `manifest compile`, inspect the IR, and project it to any target — see [Projections](/integration/projections) and the [CLI reference](/cli/commands).


---

## Compile a multi-file project with merging

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/modules

---
title: 'Modules: multi-file projects'
description: 'Organize Manifest programs across multiple files using use declarations, with cycle detection, topological sort, and IR merging.'
sidebarTitle: 'Modules'
---

Manifest supports multi-file projects through `use` declarations. The module resolver discovers all referenced files, detects cycles, sorts them topologically, and merges the compiled IR into a single deterministic output. This lets you split large programs into focused files without sacrificing whole-program validation.

## Use declarations

The `use` keyword includes another `.manifest` file. Use declarations must appear at the top of the file, before any entity, event, or store declarations:

```manifest
// app.manifest
use "./shared/types.manifest"
use "./domains/order.manifest"
use "./domains/product.manifest"
```

The resolver walks `use` declarations recursively to discover the full file graph. A `use` path must be relative and end in `.manifest` — absolute paths and extensions other than `.manifest` produce a parse error.

`use` is the only cross-file mechanism. There is no `import` keyword — every declaration in a `use`d file is brought into the merged program, namespaced by its source file path (see [Namespace isolation](#namespace-isolation)).

## Compilation pipeline

<Steps>
  <Step title="Resolution">
    Walk all `use` declarations starting from the entry file. Build a complete dependency graph
    using BFS discovery.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Cycle detection">
    Detect circular dependencies (A uses B uses A) using DFS with grey/black coloring. Circular
    dependencies produce a compile error diagnostic listing the cycle path.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Topological sort">
    Kahn's algorithm determines the compilation order — dependencies compile before dependents. Ties
    are broken alphabetically for deterministic output.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Per-file compilation">
    Each file is parsed and compiled to IR independently, producing one IR per file.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Cross-file validation">
    The merged IR is validated for cross-file reference integrity: entity types referenced in
    properties must exist, enum members used as defaults must be declared, and event names
    referenced in `emit` must be defined somewhere in the project.
  </Step>
  <Step title="IR merging">
    All per-file IRs are merged into a single output with sorted arrays for determinism. A `sources`
    array in the IR provenance tracks which files contributed to the merged output.
  </Step>
</Steps>

## CLI flags

Use the `--merge` and `--entry` flags to compile multi-file projects:

```bash
# Compile a multi-file project with merging
manifest compile --merge --entry src/app.manifest

# Compile with explicit output path
manifest compile --merge --entry src/app.manifest --output ir/

# Let the compiler auto-detect entry files (files not referenced by any other)
manifest compile --merge src/
```

| Flag             | Description                                                                                        |
| ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--merge`        | Enable multi-file compilation and merge all discovered files into a single IR output               |
| `--entry <path>` | Specify the entry point for module resolution. When omitted, the compiler auto-detects root files. |

## ~~Namespace isolation~~ Cross-file naming

~~Each module's entities, events, and enums are namespaced by the module's file path. Two files can define entities with the same name without collision…~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Multi-file merge does **not** path-prefix entity/enum/event names. Duplicate top-level names across files are a **compile error** (`Duplicate entity` / enum — see `src/manifest/multi-compiler.test.ts`). `module` blocks are a logical grouping only and do not change runtime identity (`docs/spec/semantics.md` § Modules). Keep declaration names unique across the merged project.

## Cross-file validation

After merging, the compiler validates cross-file references and produces error diagnostics with file paths and line numbers:

- Entity types referenced in properties must exist in the merged IR
- Enum members used as default values must be declared in a `use`d file
- Event names referenced in `emit` actions must be declared somewhere in the project
- Store declarations must reference entities that exist in the merged IR

Invalid cross-file references produce compile error diagnostics rather than runtime failures.

## ResolverHost abstraction

The module resolver uses a `ResolverHost` abstraction for file system access. In production, this reads from disk. In tests, you can provide an in-memory host to test resolution without touching the file system:

```typescript
import { ModuleResolver } from '@angriff36/manifest/module-resolver';

const resolver = new ModuleResolver({
  readFile(path) {
    // Return file contents from an in-memory map
    return mockFiles.get(path);
  },
});
```

This makes the resolver testable in isolation and allows the multi-compiler to work in environments without direct file system access.

## Complete example

A multi-file project with shared types, domain entities, and an entry point:

```manifest
// shared/types.manifest
enum Status { draft, submitted, approved, rejected }
enum Priority { low, medium, high }
```

```manifest
// domains/order.manifest
use "../shared/types.manifest"

entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property status: Status = draft
  property priority: Priority = medium
  property amount: number = 0

  command submit() {
    guard self.status == draft
    mutate status = submitted
    emit OrderSubmitted
  }
}

event OrderSubmitted: "order.submitted" {
  orderId: string
}

store Order in memory
```

```manifest
// app.manifest
use "./domains/order.manifest"

// Additional top-level declarations can go here
```

Compile the project:

```bash
manifest compile --merge --entry src/app.manifest --output project.ir.json
```

The resulting `project.ir.json` contains all entities, events, enums, and stores from every discovered file, merged into a single deterministic IR with provenance tracking.

See [Entities](/language/entities) for entity declarations, [Events](/language/events) for event schemas, and [CLI overview](/cli/overview) for the full set of compile options.


---

## reactions

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/reactions

---
title: 'Reactions: declarative event-driven command dispatch'
description: 'Automatically dispatch commands when events are emitted using on Event run Command syntax, with resolve expressions, parameter mappings, and cascade protection.'
sidebarTitle: 'Reactions'
---

Reactions connect events to commands declaratively. When an event is emitted, the runtime evaluates matching reactions in declaration order and dispatches the target command with resolved parameters. This creates reactive workflows without manual event wiring.

## Declaring a reaction

Use the `on` keyword followed by an event name, `run`, and a target command reference:

```manifest
event OrderSubmitted: "order.submitted" {
  orderId: string
  amount: number
}

entity Order {
  property required id: string

  command submit(orderId: string, amount: number) {
    emit OrderSubmitted { orderId: orderId, amount: amount }
  }
}

entity OrderProcessor {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "idle"

  command processOrder(orderId: string, amount: number) {
    guard amount > 0
    mutate status = "processing"
  }
}

on OrderSubmitted run OrderProcessor.processOrder
  resolve "processor-1"
  params {
    orderId: payload.orderId
    amount: payload.amount
  }
```

The `resolve` expression evaluates to the ID of the target entity instance. The `params` block maps values from the event payload into the command's parameters. `resolve` is always required on a single-target reaction; `params` is optional and may be omitted when the target command takes no arguments.

## Resolve expressions

The `resolve` clause provides the ID of the entity instance that receives the dispatched command. It evaluates to a single value — the instance's identifier:

```manifest fragment
on TaskAssigned run Notification.sendAssigned
  resolve payload.assigneeId
  params {
    taskId: payload.taskId
  }
```

~~The runtime looks up the `Notification` instance whose `id` matches the resolved value. If no instance with that ID exists, the reaction is skipped silently — no error is raised.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** The runtime always dispatches `runCommand` with the resolved `instanceId` (`docs/spec/semantics.md` § Reactions). There is **no** silent skip when the instance is missing — the target command still runs; `self`/`this` are unbound when lookup fails, so mutates that need an instance do not persist. Seed target instances (or use a `create` target command) before relying on a reaction.

Use a literal string when the target instance is fixed at design time (a singleton handler, a system processor, etc.):

```manifest fragment
on OrderSubmitted run OrderProcessor.processOrder
  resolve "processor-1"
  params {
    orderId: payload.orderId
    amount: payload.amount
  }
```

Use `payload._subject.id` to target the same entity instance that emitted the event, or `payload.<field>` when the target ID is carried explicitly in the event payload.

## Parameter mappings

The `params` block maps event payload fields to command parameters. Each entry is an expression evaluated in the event's enriched context:

```manifest fragment
params {
  orderId: payload.orderId       // Direct field reference
  total: payload.amount * 1.1    // Expression with arithmetic
  currency: "USD"               // Literal value
}
```

Inside a `params` block, `payload.*` references the event payload fields. The expression language supports the full set of operators: arithmetic, comparison, logical, conditional, and function calls.

## Follow-on emit payloads

A reaction only sees the fields the triggering event actually carries. By default an `emit` carries the command's input plus the last action result — so a follow-on reaction reading `payload.<field>` for anything else resolves `undefined`. To pass real values down a reaction chain, declare them explicitly with an `emit Event { field: expr }` block. The fields are computed **at emit time**, in the emitting command's context:

```manifest
entity ScheduleShift {
  property required id: string
  property scheduleId: string = ""

  command assign(scheduleId: string) {
    mutate scheduleId = scheduleId
    emit ScheduleShiftCreated { scheduleId: self.scheduleId }
  }
}

event ScheduleShiftCreated: "scheduleShift.created" {}

// The follow-on reaction now reads a real value, not undefined
on ScheduleShiftCreated run Schedule.syncShiftCount
  resolve self.scheduleId
```

Each payload field is `name: <expr>`, where the expression is evaluated in the emitting command's context (`self.*`, command inputs). The declared fields are merged into the emitted event's `payload`, where a follow-on reaction's `resolve`, `params`, `fanOut where`, and `count` predicates can read them as `self.<field>` / `payload.<field>`. This closes the gap consumers otherwise papered over with hand-written after-emit middleware.

## Fan-out reactions (1:N cascades)

A single-target reaction dispatches on ONE resolved instance. For a **1:N cascade** — run a command on _every_ related row, e.g. "when a gig is cancelled, unassign every staff member assigned to it" — use the `fanOut` form. It replaces `resolve` with a collection match:

```manifest
entity Gig {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "active"
  hasMany staff: GigStaff

  command cancel() {
    mutate status = "cancelled"
    emit GigCancelled
  }
}

entity GigStaff {
  property required id: string
  property gigId: string = ""
  property status: string = "assigned"
  belongsTo gig: Gig

  command unassign(reason: string) {
    mutate status = "unassigned"
  }
}

store Gig in memory
store GigStaff in memory

event GigCancelled: "gig.cancelled" {}

on GigCancelled fanOut GigStaff where gigId = self.id
  run unassign
  params {
    reason: "gig cancelled"
  }
```

This dispatches `GigStaff.unassign` on **every** `GigStaff` row where `row.gigId == self.id` (the source instance's id, read from the event payload). Each match runs the target command through the normal pipeline — its own guards, policies, actions, and emits fire — exactly like a hand-written after-emit handler, but declared.

- `where <field> = <sourceExpr>` selects the collection; `<field>` is a property on the matched entity (an indexed or foreign-key field is preferred — the Convex projection reads it via `withIndex`, falling back to a filter otherwise).
- `<sourceExpr>` is evaluated against the event payload (`self.id` / `payload.*`), so it sees the source entity's id and any declared `emit Event { field }` values.
- `params` maps the same way as a single-target reaction and is shared across all matches. Inside fan-out, `self` refers to the matched row from the source entity; `target` refers to the target entity instance.
- The reaction depth limit and `correlationId`/`causationId` propagation apply per dispatched command.

Fan-out is the declarative replacement for the common "query children by FK, loop, dispatch" middleware pattern: cancel every line item, create a shipment per line, release every reservation, deactivate every child account, close every tip pool in a period, etc.

## Foreach-create (cross-entity fan-out)

Same-entity `fanOut … run unassign` updates each matched row in place. **Foreach-create** is the cross-entity form: match one collection, then `run Target.create` (or another target-entity command) **without** binding the matched row as `instanceId` / `docId`. Each match allocates a new target row; use matched-row fields in `params` to seed it (`self` is the fan-out source row).

Shipped in Manifest **3.6.36+** (conformance fixture `112-fanout-foreach-create`).

```manifest fragment
on OrderPlaced fanOut LineItem where orderId = payload.orderId
  run Shipment.create
  params {
    lineItemId: self.id
    quantity: self.quantity
  }
```

This is how you declare “for each matching source row, create a child” — not a separate `foreach` keyword. The keywords are still `fanOut` + `run Target.create`.

## Natural-key upsert (`match … else create`)

**Shipped in Manifest 3.6.38+** — natural-key lookups for single-target and fan-out reactions.

Instead of resolving by `instanceId`, match the target entity by one or more field values (natural key). If a match is found, run the command on that instance; if zero rows match and `else create` is set, run the command without `instanceId` (allocate a new row / createVia path).

### Single-target: lookup or allocate

```manifest fragment
event ShoppingCartSubmitted: "cart.submitted" {
  cartId: string
  userEmail: string
}

on ShoppingCartSubmitted run Order.create
  match orderId = payload.cartId
  else create
  params {
    orderId: payload.cartId
    userEmail: payload.userEmail
  }
```

- If an `Order` with `orderId == cartId` exists → run `Order.create` on that row (update path, passing `instanceId`).
- If no match → run `Order.create` without `instanceId` (allocate / create a new row).
- If multiple matches → pick the lexicographically smallest `id` (deterministic tiebreak).
- Soft-deleted rows (`deletedAt != null`) are excluded from the match.

### Fan-out: per-row lookup or allocate

Fan-out can also use `match … else create`. When a source row matches, the runtime looks up the target entity by the match predicates:

```manifest fragment
event OrderPlaced: "order.placed" {
  orderId: string
  items: array
}

on OrderPlaced fanOut LineItem where orderId = payload.orderId
  match sku = self.sku
  else create
  run InventoryAdjustment.allocate
  params {
    sku: self.sku
    orderId: payload.orderId
  }
```

For each `LineItem` where `orderId == payload.orderId`:
- If an `InventoryAdjustment` with `sku == self.sku` exists → run `allocate` on that row.
- If no match → run `allocate` without `instanceId` (allocate new row).

### Syntax

```
on <Event> run <Entity>.<command>
  match <field> = <expression>, ...
  [else create]
  params { ... }
```

- `match` is mutually exclusive with `resolve`. Use `match` when you have natural-key predicates; use `resolve` when you have a direct `instanceId`.
- Predicates are **pure equality**, ANDed together. All must hold for a row to match.
- `else create` is optional. Without it, zero matches = no-op. With it, zero matches = run command without `instanceId`.
- For fan-out, `match` applies to `targetEntity`, not the fan-out source collection. Source rows are selected by the `fanOut … where …` clause; `match` then looks up each selected row's target.

## Aggregate count expressions

A reaction often needs to **recompute a stored count on a parent** after a child event — "how many children reference this parent?" — the pattern behind hand-written "count children where the foreign key equals the parent, then patch the parent" after-emit middleware. The `count(...)` aggregate makes that declarative. It is an expression, so it can appear anywhere a reaction param value does:

```manifest
entity Schedule {
  property required id: string
  property shiftCount: number = 0

  command syncShiftCount(shiftCount: number) {
    mutate shiftCount = shiftCount
  }
}

entity ScheduleShift {
  property required id: string
  property scheduleId: string = ""
  belongsTo schedule: Schedule

  command assign(scheduleId: string) {
    mutate scheduleId = scheduleId
    emit ScheduleShiftCreated { scheduleId: self.scheduleId }
  }
}

event ScheduleShiftCreated: "scheduleShift.created" {}

on ScheduleShiftCreated run Schedule.syncShiftCount
  resolve self.scheduleId
  params {
    shiftCount: count(ScheduleShift where scheduleId == self.scheduleId)
  }
```

When a `ScheduleShift` is assigned, the reaction resolves its parent `Schedule` (`self.scheduleId` is the event payload value), counts `ScheduleShift` rows whose `scheduleId` equals that value, and stores the result via `syncShiftCount`.

### Syntax

```
count(<Entity> where <field> == <value>, <field2> == <value2>, ...)
```

- `<Entity>` is the counted collection. Predicate values (`<value>`) are expressions evaluated in the reaction's context — the event payload, just like other params (`self.*` / `payload.*`).
- Predicates are **pure equality**, ANDed together. A second predicate adds a filter — e.g. counting only in-progress tasks:

```manifest fragment
params {
  currentTaskCount: count(PrepTask where stationId == self.stationId, status == "in_progress")
}
```

- `count` is a **contextual** operator, not a reserved word: it is recognized only in the `count(<Entity> where ...)` shape, so a property can still be named `count`.
- At least one predicate (the foreign-key match) is required. There is **no** group-by, join, multi-hop traversal, or arbitrary SQL — `count` only. Scope is deliberately narrow.

### Projection notes

The runtime evaluates `count` by scanning the entity's collection and counting rows matching every predicate (deterministic — count is order-independent). The Convex projection reads via the counted entity's `by_<field>` index on the foreign-key predicate when one exists (the `belongsTo`/`ref` FK or an `indexed` field), applying any remaining predicates — plus the entity's tenant and soft-delete filters — as post-read `.filter`s, then `.length`. With no indexed predicate it falls back to a table scan and emits an `info` diagnostic.

### What this does NOT replace

`count(...)` covers the **per-parent** recompute (one parent's count, updated by the child event that points at it). It does not cover:

- **Tenant-wide reconcile** (recompute _every_ parent on each event) or **dual-target updates** (a reassignment that moves a child from parent A to parent B needs both updated). These are multi-step / conditional fan-out — model them as sagas, actions, or keep the hand middleware. They are intentional scope boundaries, not gaps in `count`.

## Reaction scopes

Reactions can be declared at three levels:

| Scope   | Location                      | Behavior                              |
| ------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Program | Top-level in `.manifest` file | Global — fires for any matching event |
| Module  | Inside a `module` block       | Scoped to the module                  |
| Entity  | Inside an `entity` block (`on Event run …` / `fanOut`) | Collected into IR with an `entity` field |

~~(Implied: any `on Event { … }` body inside an entity is a valid reaction.)~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Entity-scoped reactions must use the `on Event run …` / `fanOut` form. Bare `behavior` / `on Event { … }` blocks inside an entity are a hard compile error (conformance fixture 110; `docs/spec/semantics.md` § Entities).

```manifest
event OrderSubmitted: "order.submitted" {
  orderId: string
}

event OrderFailed: "order.failed" {}
event OrderReprocessed: "order.reprocessed" {}

entity OrderProcessor {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "idle"

  command processOrder(orderId: string) {
    mutate status = "processing"
  }
}

entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "pending"

  command submit(orderId: string) {
    mutate status = "submitted"
    emit OrderSubmitted { orderId: orderId }
  }

  command fail() {
    mutate status = "failed"
    emit OrderFailed
  }

  command reprocess() {
    mutate status = "pending"
    emit OrderReprocessed
  }

  // Entity-level reaction -- fires within the entity's context
  on OrderFailed run Order.reprocess
    resolve payload._subject.id
}

// Program-level reaction -- fires globally
on OrderSubmitted run OrderProcessor.processOrder
  resolve "processor-1"
  params {
    orderId: payload.orderId
  }
```

Reactions at all three scopes are collected into the IR's `reactions` array. Module-scoped reactions carry a `module` field; entity-scoped reactions carry an `entity` field. The runtime evaluates all matching reactions regardless of scope.

## Cascading reactions and depth limit

When a reaction's target command emits its own events, those events can trigger further reactions. This creates cascading reaction chains:

```
OrderSubmitted
  -> OrderProcessor.processOrder
    -> OrderProcessing
      -> Warehouse.prepareShipment
```

The runtime enforces a maximum reaction depth (`MAX_REACTION_DEPTH = 10`). If a chain exceeds this limit, the runtime throws a `ManifestReactionDepthError` and stops processing further reactions. This prevents infinite loops from circular reaction chains.

<Warning>
  Design your reaction chains to terminate naturally. If event A triggers command B which emits
  event C which triggers command D which emits event A, the runtime will halt after 10 iterations
  with a `ManifestReactionDepthError`. This is a safety net, not a design pattern — circular
  reaction chains indicate a modeling problem.
</Warning>

## correlationId and causationId propagation

Reactions propagate workflow metadata through the chain:

- **`correlationId`** stays the same across the entire workflow. It is set on the initial event and carried through every subsequent reaction.
- ~~**`causationId`** is set to the triggering event's emit ID on each reaction. This changes with each hop, creating a causal chain.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** `causationId` on each reaction-triggered command is the triggering event's **name** (`emitted.name`), not an emit/instance ID (`docs/spec/semantics.md` § Reactions).

This enables cross-reaction tracing for debugging distributed workflows. See [Events](/language/events) for how `correlationId` and `causationId` are attached to emitted events.

## Deterministic ordering

Reactions execute in declaration order. If multiple reactions match the same event, they are dispatched in the order they appear in the source file. This guarantees deterministic behavior: given identical IR, identical runtime context, and identical input, the reaction chain always produces the same result.

The runtime evaluates matching reactions synchronously within the same command execution turn — reactions do not queue asynchronously. This means the full reaction chain completes before the original `CommandResult` is returned to the caller.

## Complete example

```manifest
event OrderSubmitted: "order.submitted" {
  orderId: string
  amount: number
  customerId: string
}

event InvoiceCreated: "invoice.created" {
  invoiceId: string
  orderId: string
  total: number
}

event ShipmentPrepared: "shipment.prepared" {}

entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property status: string = "pending"

  command submit(orderId: string, amount: number, customerId: string) {
    mutate status = "submitted"
    emit OrderSubmitted { orderId: orderId, amount: amount, customerId: customerId }
  }
}

entity Invoice {
  property required id: string
  property orderId: string = ""
  property total: number = 0
  property status: string = "draft"
  belongsTo order: Order

  command createFromOrder(orderId: string, total: number) {
    guard total > 0
    mutate orderId = orderId
    mutate total = total
    mutate status = "issued"
    emit InvoiceCreated
  }
}

entity Shipping {
  property required id: string
  property orderId: string = ""
  property status: string = "pending"
  belongsTo order: Order

  command prepareShipment(orderId: string) {
    mutate orderId = orderId
    mutate status = "preparing"
    emit ShipmentPrepared
  }
}

// Reaction chain: OrderSubmitted -> create invoice
on OrderSubmitted run Invoice.createFromOrder
  resolve "invoice-auto"
  params {
    orderId: payload.orderId
    total: payload.amount
  }

// Reaction chain: InvoiceCreated -> prepare shipment
on InvoiceCreated run Shipping.prepareShipment
  resolve "shipping-auto"
  params {
    orderId: payload.orderId
  }
```

~~Wrong (entity-first): `runtime.runCommand('Order', 'submit', { instanceId, amount })`.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** `runCommand(commandName, input, options?)` — not
> entity-first. `amount` is command input; `entityName` / `instanceId` go in options.
> Do **not** wrap fenced code in `~~…~~` — Mintlify MDX treats the fence as broken and
> parses `{…}` as JSX (`Could not parse expression with acorn`), which blocks deploy.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { ir } from './compiled.ir.json';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'system', role: 'worker' },
});

// Seed instances
runtime.createInstance('Invoice', { id: 'invoice-auto' });
runtime.createInstance('Shipping', { id: 'shipping-auto' });

// Emitting OrderSubmitted triggers the reaction chain
const result = await runtime.runCommand(
  'submit',
  { orderId: 'order-001', amount: 250, customerId: 'customer-123' },
  { entityName: 'Order', instanceId: 'order-001' },
);

// The reaction chain fires automatically:
// OrderSubmitted -> Invoice.createFromOrder -> InvoiceCreated -> Shipping.prepareShipment
console.log(result.emittedEvents.length); // Events from all reactions
```

See [Events](/language/events) for declaring event schemas and [Commands](/language/commands) for the command execution pipeline. For async reactions that defer execution to a background queue, see [Async commands](/language/async-commands).


---

## roles

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/roles

---
title: 'Roles: hierarchy and permission inheritance'
description: 'Define role hierarchies with extends, allow/deny permissions, and transitive inheritance. Deny rules are absolute and always win over allow.'
sidebarTitle: 'Roles'
---

Roles define named permission sets with single-parent inheritance. A role that extends a parent inherits all its permissions transitively. Use roles with the [policy](/language/guards-policies) system to control command authorization based on the user's assigned role.

## Declaring a role

Use the `role` keyword at the program or module level with a block body of `allow` and `deny` declarations:

```manifest
role User {
  allow read
}

role Manager extends User {
  allow write
  allow execute
}

role Admin extends Manager {
  allow delete
  allow override
  deny impersonate
}
```

Each `allow` or `deny` line names an action (or optionally a target) that the role permits or forbids. The `extends` keyword establishes a single-parent inheritance chain.

## Permission inheritance

Roles inherit permissions transitively through the `extends` chain. The compiler walks the chain from root to leaf, unioning all `allow` permissions along the way:

| Role                      | Inherited from | Effective permissions                  |
| ------------------------- | -------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| `User`                    | (none)         | read                                   |
| `Manager` (extends User)  | User           | read, write, execute                   |
| `Admin` (extends Manager) | Manager, User  | read, write, execute, delete, override |

~~The `effectivePermissions` map is precomputed… via O(1) lookups…~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** `resolveRoleGraph()` precomputes an `effectivePermissions: IRRolePermission[]` (allow entries remaining after transitive deny). Runtime checks scan that array (no inheritance walk at execution time).

### Compile-time validation

The compiler validates the role graph and produces error diagnostics for:

- **Duplicate role names**: Each role name must be unique within the program or module.
- **Unknown parent**: A role that extends a name not declared as a role produces a compile error.
- **Circular inheritance**: If the `extends` chain forms a cycle (A extends B extends A), the compiler detects it via DFS coloring and produces an error diagnostic listing the cycle path.

## Deny-is-absolute semantics

If any role in the inheritance chain denies an action, the denial takes precedence over all `allow` rules regardless of where they appear:

```manifest
role User {
  allow read
}

role Support extends User {
  allow write
  allow execute
  deny delete       // Support CANNOT delete, even if a parent allows it
}
```

Deny rules are applied after the full inheritance union is computed. This means:

1. Walk the inheritance chain and union all `allow` permissions.
2. Walk the inheritance chain and collect all `deny` permissions.
3. ~~The result is the `effectivePermissions` map.~~ **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Filter allows against denies → `effectivePermissions: IRRolePermission[]`.

A `deny` at any level is absolute. There is no mechanism to override a deny with a subsequent allow. This is by design — it prevents privilege escalation through role chain manipulation.

<Warning>
  Deny is not a suggestion; it is a hard rule. If `User` denies `impersonate`, then `Admin extends
  User` cannot re-allow `impersonate`. The deny from `User` will always win. Structure your role
  hierarchy so that denials only appear at the appropriate level.
</Warning>

## Unknown roles default to deny

If a user's role does not match any declared role, the runtime returns `false` for all permission checks. There are no permissive defaults — an unrecognized role has no permissions. This is consistent with Manifest's default-deny posture. See [Guards and policies](/language/guards-policies) for the broader authorization model.

<Warning>
  Role matching is **exact and case-sensitive**. `'Admin'` and `'admin'` are treated as different
  roles. A caller passing the wrong casing silently gets `false` with no error — the runtime treats
  it as an unknown role, not a near-miss. Ensure that the role strings your identity system produces
  exactly match the names declared in the `.manifest` file.
</Warning>

## Using roles in policies

Reference roles in policy expressions via the `user.role` binding and the built-in permission functions. Authorization is declared at the entity level with named `policy <name> execute:` rules (or a `default policy`); command bodies do not accept a `policy` clause:

```manifest
entity Document {
  default policy RequireAuth execute: user.id != null "Authentication required"

  ~~policy CanDelete execute: hasPermission(user, "delete")~~
  policy CanApprove execute: roleAllows(user.role, "approve") or user.role == "admin"
  policy CanDelete execute: hasPermission("delete")

  command approve() {
    mutate status = "approved"
    emit DocumentApproved
  }

  command removeDocument() {
    mutate status = "deleted"
    emit DocumentDeleted
  }
}
```

## Runtime builtins

Two built-in functions are registered on the runtime engine when roles are present in the IR:

~~| Function | Signature | Returns |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `hasPermission` | `hasPermission(user, permission)` | … |
| `roleAllows` | `roleAllows(roleName, action)` | … |~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Per `docs/spec/builtins.md` and conformance fixture `71-role-hierarchy.manifest`:

| Function | Signature | Returns |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `hasPermission` | `hasPermission(action, target?)` | `true` if `context.user.role`'s effective permissions grant `action` (optional `target`) |
| `roleAllows` | `roleAllows(roleName, action, target?)` | same check for an explicit role name |

`effectivePermissions` is an `IRRolePermission[]` (not a `Record<string, boolean>`). Lookups scan that array. Passing a user object as the first argument of `hasPermission` always returns `false` (action must be a string).

### `hasPermission(action, target?)`

Uses the bound runtime `user.role`. Returns `false` if there is no role string or the role is undeclared.

```manifest
guard hasPermission("delete")
guard hasPermission("execute", "CreateReport")
```

### `roleAllows(roleName, action, target?)`

Checks whether a named role allows a specific action, without requiring a user object.

```manifest
policy CanExecute execute: roleAllows(user.role, "execute") or user.role == "admin"
```

## Context-sensitive `role` keyword

The `role` keyword is context-sensitive, not a globally reserved keyword. It is emitted as an identifier rather than a reserved token, so existing programs that use `role` as a property name continue to parse correctly:

```manifest
entity UserProfile {
  property role: string = "user"           // property named "role"

  command setRole(newRole: string) {
    mutate role = newRole                   // mutate "role" property
  }
}
```

The parser only treats `role` as a declaration keyword at the top level of a program or module (when followed by an identifier and a block). Inside entity blocks, `role` is an ordinary identifier.

<Note>
  The `role` keyword is only treated as a role declaration at the top level of a program or module.
  Inside entity blocks, `role` is an ordinary identifier. This prevents the keyword from breaking
  existing code that uses `role` as a property name.
</Note>

## IR representation

Roles are stored in the IR as an optional `roles` array on the root `IR` object (and on `IRModule` for module-scoped roles). Each `IRRole` contains:

~~```typescript
interface IRRole {
  name: string;
  extends?: string;
  permissions: IRRolePermission[];
  effectivePermissions: Record<string, boolean>;
}
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** IR shape (`src/manifest/ir.ts`):

```typescript
interface IRRolePermission {
  action: string;
  target?: string;
}

interface IRRole {
  name: string;
  module?: string;
  parent?: string; // from `extends` in source
  allow: IRRolePermission[];
  deny: IRRolePermission[];
  effectivePermissions: IRRolePermission[]; // post inheritance + deny
}
```

The runtime reads `effectivePermissions` directly and never re-computes it.

## Complete example

```manifest
role User {
  allow read
}

role Manager extends User {
  allow write
  allow execute
}

role Admin extends Manager {
  allow delete
  allow override
  deny impersonate
}

entity Document {
  property required id: string
  property title: string = ""
  property status: string = "draft"

  default policy RequireAuth execute: user.id != null "Authentication required"

  ~~policy CanRead execute: hasPermission(user, "read")
