  policy CanWrite execute: hasPermission(user, "write")
  policy CanDelete execute: hasPermission(user, "delete")~~
  policy CanRead execute: hasPermission("read")
  policy CanWrite execute: hasPermission("write")
  policy CanApprove execute: roleAllows(user.role, "execute")
  policy CanDelete execute: hasPermission("delete")

  command view() {
    emit DocumentViewed
  }

  command edit(newTitle: string) {
    mutate title = newTitle
    emit DocumentEdited
  }

  command approve() {
    guard self.status == "draft"
    mutate status = "approved"
    emit DocumentApproved
  }

  command removeDocument() {
    mutate status = "deleted"
    emit DocumentDeleted
  }
}
```

~~```typescript
const result = await adminRuntime.runCommand('Document', 'removeDocument', {
  instanceId: 'doc-42',
});
const mgrResult = await mgrRuntime.runCommand('Document', 'removeDocument', {
  instanceId: 'doc-42',
});
const unknownResult = await unknownRuntime.runCommand('Document', 'view', {
  instanceId: 'doc-42',
});
```~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** `RuntimeEngine.runCommand(commandName, input, options?)` —
> not entity-first. Pass `entityName` / `instanceId` in `options`.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { ir } from './compiled.ir.json';

// Admin user
const adminRuntime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'admin-1', role: 'Admin' },
});

const result = await adminRuntime.runCommand('removeDocument', {}, {
  entityName: 'Document',
  instanceId: 'doc-42',
});
console.log(result.success); // true

// Manager user
const mgrRuntime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'mgr-1', role: 'Manager' },
});

const mgrResult = await mgrRuntime.runCommand('removeDocument', {}, {
  entityName: 'Document',
  instanceId: 'doc-42',
});
console.log(mgrResult.success); // false (Manager has no "delete" permission — removeDocument requires it)

// Unknown role
const unknownRuntime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'guest-1', role: 'Guest' },
});

const unknownResult = await unknownRuntime.runCommand('view', {}, {
  entityName: 'Document',
  instanceId: 'doc-42',
});
console.log(unknownResult.success); // false (unknown role defaults to deny)
```

See [Guards and policies](/language/guards-policies) for the full policy system, [Commands](/language/commands) for the execution pipeline, and [Approvals](/language/approvals) for multi-stage approval workflows that integrate with role-based policies.


---

## manifest.config.yaml

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/stores

---
title: 'Stores: persistence targets for entities'
description: "Declare where each entity's instances are persisted using the store keyword. Choose from memory, postgres, or supabase, or plug in a custom adapter."
sidebarTitle: 'Stores'
---

A store declaration tells the runtime where to persist instances of a given entity. Every entity that participates in runtime command execution needs a corresponding store. The store target determines which adapter the runtime uses to read and write instances.

## Syntax

```manifest
store EntityName in <target>
```

`EntityName` must match an entity declared elsewhere in the same `.manifest` file (or module). The `<target>` is one of the built-in keywords or a custom identifier resolved at runtime configuration time.

## Built-in targets

### `memory`

Stores instances in a plain JavaScript `Map` keyed by instance ID. Data does not survive process restarts. This is the only target that is required to be supported by all conforming runtimes.

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required name: string
  property price: number = 0
}

store Product in memory
```

Use `memory` for:

- Local development and rapid iteration
- Unit and conformance tests
- Demos and prototyping

### `durable`

The **backend-neutral semantic target**. Declares that the entity is persisted in _some_ durable store; the specific technology (Prisma, raw SQL, supabase-js, a custom adapter, …) is chosen by the consumer at runtime via a `storeProvider`, and at compile time via projection config.

```manifest
store Order in durable
```

Use `durable` when you want the entity to participate in storage projections without coupling the `.manifest` source to a specific backend. The [Prisma projection](/integration/prisma) treats `durable`, `postgres`, and `supabase` identically — all three are schema-emission targets. The runtime engine rejects `durable` at command-execution time unless a custom `storeProvider` is bound, with a clear "no store adapter supplied" diagnostic.

<Note>
  `durable` is the recommended target for new code. The legacy `postgres` and `supabase` targets
  continue to work; nothing was removed. Storage projections emit for all three.
</Note>

### `postgres`

Delegates to a `PostgresStore` adapter. The adapter handles SQL reads and writes against a Postgres-compatible database. You must configure the adapter when constructing the `RuntimeEngine`.

```manifest
store Order in postgres
```

<Note>
  `postgres` is a target identifier, not a built-in adapter included in the core runtime. You must
  supply a Postgres adapter via `RuntimeEngine` options. See [Custom
  Stores](/adapters/custom-stores) for the adapter contract.
</Note>

### `supabase`

Delegates to a `SupabaseStore` adapter. Similar to `postgres`, but typed for the Supabase client. Requires adapter configuration at runtime.

```manifest
store User in supabase
```

<Note>
  `supabase` is also an adapter target, not a built-in. Provide a Supabase-backed store via
  `RuntimeEngine` options.
</Note>

## Multiple entities, multiple stores

Each entity gets its own `store` declaration. Entities in the same program can use different targets:

```manifest
entity Author {
  property required name: string
  hasMany books: Book
}

entity Book {
  property required title: string
  belongsTo author: Author
}

store Author in memory
store Book in memory
```

```manifest
entity User {
  property required email: string
  property required role: string = "member"
}

entity AuditLog {
  property required action: string
  property required actorId: string
  property createdAt: number = 0
}

store User in postgres
store AuditLog in postgres
```

## Using a custom store adapter

When you declare `store EntityName in postgres` (or any non-`memory` target), the `RuntimeEngine` must receive a `storeProvider` function that returns the appropriate adapter for that entity name:

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';

class PostgresOrderStore implements Store<Order> {
  async getAll() {
    return await db.order.findMany();
  }

  async getById(id: string) {
    return (await db.order.findUnique({ where: { id } })) ?? undefined;
  }

  async create(data: Partial<Order>) {
    return await db.order.create({ data });
  }

  async update(id: string, data: Partial<Order>) {
    return await db.order.update({ where: { id }, data });
  }

  async delete(id: string) {
    await db.order.delete({ where: { id } });
    return true;
  }

  async clear() {
    await db.order.deleteMany({});
  }
}

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
  ir,
  { user: { id: 'user-1', role: 'admin' } },
  {
    storeProvider: (entityName) => {
      if (entityName === 'Order') return new PostgresOrderStore();
      return undefined;
    },
  },
);
```

When `storeProvider` returns `undefined`, the runtime uses a built-in store only
for a declared `memory` or browser `localStorage` target. Durable targets never
fall back to memory: `postgres`, `supabase`, `durable`, and custom targets fail
and identify the unbound entity.

## Complete generated-companion PostgreSQL setup

A database URL is necessary, but it is not the entire storage configuration.
Bind every durable entity to a store and table:

```env
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/acme
```

```ts
// manifest.config.ts
import { defineConfig } from '@angriff36/manifest/config';
import { PostgresStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/stores';

const connectionString = process.env.DATABASE_URL;
if (!connectionString) throw new Error('DATABASE_URL is required');

export default defineConfig({
  stores: {
    Customer: { implementation: new PostgresStore({ connectionString, tableName: 'customers' }) },
    Invoice: { implementation: new PostgresStore({ connectionString, tableName: 'invoices' }) },
  },
});
```

```yaml
# manifest.config.yaml
projections:
  nextjs:
    output: generated
    options:
      # Relative to the emitted lib/manifest-runtime.ts companion.
      runtimeConfigImport: '../manifest.config'
```

The generated factory imports the TypeScript config, builds the
`storeProvider`, and passes it to `RuntimeEngine`. Without
`runtimeConfigImport`, a generated factory containing durable stores throws a
clear error. Memory and browser localStorage targets need no extra binding.

<Tip>
  See [Custom Stores](/adapters/custom-stores) for the full `Store` interface contract and a guide
  to implementing production-ready adapters.
</Tip>

## Store and `localStorage`

The `localStorage` target is available for browser environments. It is auto-instantiated by the runtime — no `storeProvider` is required. Instances are persisted in the browser's `localStorage` under a key derived from the entity name.

```manifest
store Preferences in localStorage
```

## What the runtime does when a store is missing

If an entity has no store declaration, the runtime uses memory. This is distinct
from declaring a durable target without binding it: an explicitly durable store
fails rather than silently changing persistence.

<Warning>
  Entities without an explicit store declaration have no persistence guarantee. In production,
  always declare an explicit store target to make your persistence intent clear and to enable
  adapter injection.
</Warning>


---

## tenancy

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/tenancy

---
title: 'Tenant isolation: scoping reads and writes to a tenant'
sidebarTitle: 'Tenant isolation'
description: 'Scope entity reads and writes to a tenant value drawn from the runtime context with a single tenant declaration that fails closed when no tenant resolves.'
---

A top-level `tenant` declaration scopes entity reads and writes to a tenant value extracted from the runtime context. Tenant-scoped programs fail closed: a command invoked without a resolvable tenant value is rejected with `MISSING_TENANT_CONTEXT`.

## Syntax

The declaration is a single top-level construct — at most one per program. The parser rejects more than one tenant declaration:

```manifest
tenant tenantId : string from context.tenantId

entity Invoice {
  property required id: string
  property required amount: number
  property required description: string

  command createInvoice(amount: number, description: string) {
    mutate amount = amount
    mutate description = description
  }

  store in memory
}
```

The syntax is `tenant <property> : <type> from <context_path>`.

## Behavior

The compiler emits an `IRTenant` record with `property`, `type`, and `contextPath` fields onto `IR.tenant`. The field is only present when a `tenant` declaration exists, so programs without tenancy compile identically to before. The runtime engine reads `IR.tenant` and applies tenant scoping:

- **`resolveTenantValue()`** walks the configured `contextPath` (for example `context.tenantId`) against the active runtime context and returns the tenant value, or `undefined` when the IR has no tenant declaration or the context lacks the value.
- The **tenant gate** in `runCommand()` activates when **either** the `requireTenantContext` runtime option is set **or** the IR declares a `tenant`. When active and no tenant value resolves — any falsy value (`undefined`, empty string, `null`) counts as missing — the command fails with `MISSING_TENANT_CONTEXT: tenant-scoped command invoked without context.tenantId`, classified as outcome `missing_tenant_context`.
- On create, the resolved tenant value is auto-written into the entity's tenant property.
- **`getAllInstances()`** filters results to instances whose tenant property equals the active tenant value.
- **`getInstance()`** returns `undefined` when an instance's tenant property does not match the active tenant value, preventing cross-tenant reads.

The Prisma projection adds a tenant discriminator column and an index per model, and emits PostgreSQL row-level-security policy statements as comments for consumers to apply manually.

## Reference

- Source keyword: `tenant`; syntax `tenant <property> : <type> from <context_path>`.
- IR field: `IR.tenant?: IRTenant` with `{ property, type, contextPath }`.
- Runtime option: `requireTenantContext?: boolean` — independent of the IR-level declaration; can enforce tenant context without a `tenant` block.
- Failure diagnostic: `MISSING_TENANT_CONTEXT`, classified as outcome `missing_tenant_context`.

<Warning>
  Tenant filtering is enforced by the reference runtime engine's read paths (`getAllInstances`,
  `getInstance`); it is **not** enforced by the database unless the emitted RLS policies are
  applied. The Prisma RLS statements ship as comments, not executed migrations.
</Warning>

<Note>
  The runtime gate is fail-closed by design — a missing or empty tenant value is treated as an error
  rather than a permissive default. The `requireTenantContext` option and the IR `tenant`
  declaration are orthogonal; either activates the gate.
</Note>


---

## timestamps

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/timestamps

---
title: 'Automatic timestamp fields'
sidebarTitle: 'Timestamps'
description: 'The timestamps modifier auto-injects read-only createdAt and updatedAt fields and populates them from the deterministic runtime clock on create and update.'
---

The `timestamps` entity modifier auto-injects read-only `createdAt` and `updatedAt` properties and populates them at runtime, removing the boilerplate of declaring and maintaining audit timestamps by hand.

> **Verified (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Matches `docs/spec/semantics.md` / IR `timestamps` flag / runtime `getNow()` create+update stamping / Prisma `@default(now())` + `@updatedAt`. Package pin `@angriff36/manifest` **3.6.41**.

## Syntax

The modifier is a bare keyword placed inside an entity body. No property declarations for `createdAt` or `updatedAt` are needed:

```manifest
entity Article {
  property required id: string
  property required title: string
  property body: string = ""
  timestamps
}

store Article in memory
```

## Behavior

When an entity declares `timestamps`, the IR compiler sets `IREntity.timestamps: true` and injects two properties — `createdAt` and `updatedAt`, both of type `datetime` with the `readonly` modifier — unless the entity already declares a property of that name. The injection is idempotent and skips names that are already present.

The runtime engine acts on the flag:

- On instance **creation**, when `timestamps` is set, both `createdAt` and `updatedAt` are assigned the value of `getNow()`.
- On instance **update**, when `timestamps` is set, `updatedAt` is reassigned to `getNow()` while `createdAt` is left untouched.

`getNow()` honors the deterministic clock supplied through the `now` runtime option, so tests can pin timestamps to a fixed value. The Prisma projection translates the flag into native column attributes: `@default(now())` on `createdAt` and `@updatedAt` on `updatedAt`.

## Manual `now()` / `today()` defaults

The `timestamps` modifier is the zero-boilerplate path for the `createdAt`/`updatedAt` pair. For a single timestamp field outside that pair, declare it explicitly with a `now()` (or `today()`) default:

```manifest
property createdAt: datetime = now()
```

This call-expression default lowers to the `IRProperty.autoNow` flag (recognized: `now()`/`today()`, called with no arguments). The runtime stamps the field with the current time on create when the caller does not supply a value, and the Prisma projection emits `@default(now())`.

<Note>
  Before this lowering existed, a `= now()` default compiled to **no** default and was null-filled
  at runtime, breaking non-null store columns (the `createdAt must not be null` class of bug). The
  compiler also folds negative numeric literal defaults like `= -1` into a real static default, and
  emits a **warning** for an unsupported call-expression default such as `= uuid()` (which would
  otherwise be silently dropped). See [Types](/language/types) for the full `autoNow` and
  diagnostics behavior.
</Note>

## Reference

- Source keyword: `timestamps`.
- IR field: `IREntity.timestamps?: boolean`.
- Injected properties: `createdAt`, `updatedAt`, type `datetime`, modifier `readonly`.
- Runtime population: `getNow()` on create (both fields) and update (`updatedAt` only).
- Prisma attributes: `@default(now())`, `@updatedAt`.

<Note>
  The injected fields carry the `readonly` modifier, so they cannot be mutated through commands. If
  a program manually declares `createdAt` or `updatedAt`, the auto-injection defers entirely to the
  manual declaration and does not add or override anything for that name. Population is driven by
  the runtime engine's create/update paths — stores or code paths that write instances without going
  through `createInstance`/`updateInstance` will not receive automatic timestamps.
</Note>


---

## types

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/types

---
title: 'Types: enums, decimal/money, value objects, datetime, arrays'
sidebarTitle: 'Types'
description: "Reference for Manifest's richer property types — enums, decimal and money, value objects, datetime with UTC built-ins, and arrays — including what each one enforces at runtime."
---

~~Beyond the four primitive types (`string`, `number`, `boolean`, `timestamp`), Manifest offers richer property types…~~

> **Correction (2026-07-22 @RYANSIGNED):** `timestamp` **is** a valid property type — it is a normative alias for `datetime` (`docs/spec/semantics.md:281,286-289`). Runtimes MUST treat `timestamp` identically to `datetime`. Common scalars include `string`, `number`, `boolean`, plus date/time primitives `datetime` / `timestamp` / `date` / `time` / `duration`, `decimal` / `money`, collections, enums, and value-object names. Type names are open strings, so an undeclared custom name can still compile as a type reference. This page documents what each richer type actually provides — and what it does not.

## Enums

A first-class `enum` declaration defines a closed, named set of values that properties can reference. Each member may carry an optional display label and an optional numeric ordinal, giving entities a typed vocabulary for status fields, priorities, and other fixed value sets.

Enums are top-level declarations:

```manifest
enum Status {
  draft
  published = "Published"
  archived(2)
}

entity Article {
  property required title: string
  property status: Status = draft
  property priority: Priority
}

enum Priority {
  low = "Low Priority"
  medium = "Medium Priority"
  high = "High Priority"
}

store Article in memory
```

A member written as `name` is a plain value; `name = "Label"` attaches a display label; `name(ordinal)` attaches a numeric ordinal. The three forms can be mixed freely within one enum. An enum may be referenced as a property type before or after its own declaration in the file, and a property default such as `= draft` refers to a member by name.

The IR carries the full enum definition — a `name`, optional `module`, and a `values` array where each value records its `name` and, only when present, a `label` and an `ordinal`. Absent metadata is omitted rather than defaulted, so the IR stays minimal. This representation is the input downstream projections use to emit database enum columns and TypeScript union types.

<Warning>
  The IR carries only `name`, `label`, and `ordinal` per member. There is **no** enum-specific
  reference checker in the compiler and **no** transition-constraint machinery on enum members —
  both are aspirational, not present in the source. Because type names are not a closed set,
  referencing an undeclared enum name as a property type is not rejected at compile time by the enum
  machinery itself.
</Warning>

## Decimal and money

The `decimal` and `money` types represent high-precision numbers with optional precision and scale parameters, intended for monetary amounts and other values where binary floating point is unacceptable.

Both types accept an optional `(precision, scale)` parameter list and can otherwise be used like any scalar:

```manifest
entity Invoice {
  property required description: string
  property amount: decimal(10, 2) = 0
  property tax: money(12, 4) = 0
  property total: decimal = 0
  property optionalFee: money?
}

store Invoice in memory
```

`decimal(10, 2)` declares 10 total digits with 2 fractional digits. `money(12, 4)` is the same shape with higher precision. Both types are valid without parameters (`decimal`, `total`), in which case no precision or scale is recorded. The `?` suffix makes the property nullable (`money?`).

The parser special-cases a `(` immediately after the type name only when the name is `decimal` or `money` — any other type name followed by `(` is not treated as parameterized. When present, the precision and scale are stored as a `params` object on the emitted `IRType`; unparameterized types produce no `params`. This `params` object is carried on the `IRType` but is not currently consumed by any shipped projection — the Prisma and Drizzle projections read precision and scale from a separate `options.precision` configuration object, not from `IRType.params`. A downstream consumer could read `params` to emit a Postgres `NUMERIC` column or a `Decimal.js` field, but no built-in projection does so today.

<Warning>
  `decimal` and `money` do **not** introduce runtime arithmetic or validation of their own. The
  runtime engine treats their values as ordinary numbers — there is no decimal arithmetic library
  wired into the evaluator, and precision/scale are metadata carried for projection use, **not
  enforced at runtime or at compile time**. Any high-precision guarantee depends on a downstream
  projection rather than the reference runtime.
</Warning>

## Value objects

A `value` declaration defines a reusable composite type that embeds inline in entity properties rather than living in its own table. Value objects group related fields — money, addresses — into a single named shape that several entities can share.

Value objects are top-level declarations containing only property declarations:

```manifest
value Money {
  property amount: decimal
  property currency: string
}

value Address {
  property street: string
  property city: string
  property country: string
}

entity Product {
  property required id: string
  property name: string
  property price: Money
  property billingAddress: Address
}

entity Order {
  property required id: string
  property total: Money
  property shippingAddress: Address
}

store Product in memory
store Order in memory
```

An entity references a value object by using its name as a property type (`price: Money`). The same value object can be embedded in multiple entities.

The `value` token is context-sensitive — it is emitted as an identifier rather than a reserved keyword, so `property value: number` and `mutate value = 1` continue to parse without reserved-word errors. Every compiled program carries a top-level `values` array (empty when none are declared), and a property whose type name matches a declared value object is identified by checking that name against `ir.values`. The code generator emits a TypeScript interface for each value object, and the Prisma projection emits a matching property as a `Json` (JSONB) column rather than a foreign-key relationship or a separate table.

<Note>
  Value objects are described as "immutable by design," but that is a design statement, not a
  runtime-enforced property: the reference runtime does not freeze or reject mutation of embedded
  value data, and there is no immutability check in the compiler or engine. Value object bodies are
  restricted to properties — relationships, commands, and other members are rejected by the parser.
</Note>

## Datetime (and date / time / duration)

~~Manifest represents points in time with the `datetime` type and offers UTC-based date-component built-ins for extracting parts of a timestamp.~~

~~There is **no** dedicated `date`, `time`, `duration`, or `interval` primitive type — those keywords are not in the lexer. The date type the runtime and projections work with is `datetime`, used like any scalar property type.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Four date/time primitive type names have fixed runtime representations and write-time validation (`docs/spec/semantics.md` § Date/Time Types):

| Type | Representation |
| --- | --- |
| `datetime` | finite number, epoch ms UTC |
| `duration` | finite number, milliseconds (may be negative) |
| `date` | string `"YYYY-MM-DD"` (valid calendar date) |
| `time` | string `"HH:MM:SS"` (`00:00:00`–`23:59:59`) |

Malformed values produce blocking outcomes `E_TYPE_DATETIME` / `E_TYPE_DURATION` / `E_TYPE_DATE` / `E_TYPE_TIME`. `timestamp` is a normative alias for `datetime` (same representation and validation; prefer `datetime` in new docs). There is no `interval` type name. The most common way `datetime` properties appear is via the `timestamps` modifier, which injects `createdAt` and `updatedAt`:

```manifest
entity Article {
  property required title: string
  timestamps
}
```

Because type names are open strings, a property may also be declared `property scheduledFor: datetime` and will compile, but no date-specific parsing or validation is attached to such a declaration.

### `now()` / `today()` defaults (autoNow)

A `datetime` property can default to the current time with a `now()` (or `today()`) call default:

```manifest
entity Article {
  property required id: string
  property createdAt: datetime = now()
}
```

A call-expression default cannot be stored as a static IR value, so the compiler lowers a recognized `now()`/`today()` default (called with no arguments) to the `IRProperty.autoNow` flag. The runtime stamps the field on create when the caller does not supply a value, and the Prisma projection emits `@default(now())`. Negative numeric literal defaults such as `= -1` are folded to a real static default instead of being dropped.

<Warning>
  Before `autoNow` existed, a `= now()` default compiled to **no** default and the field was
  null-filled at runtime, breaking non-null store columns. An **unsupported** call-expression
  default — e.g. `= uuid()` — would still be silently dropped, so the compiler now emits a
  **warning** for it (listing the supported call defaults `now()`, `today()`). Separately, a
  `create` command that leaves a non-null, default-less property unset is **warned** about when the
  runtime would null-fill that field — the types that fill with `null` rather than a zero value:
  `datetime`, `date`, `time`, `enum`, and custom/value-object types. Zero-filling types (`string`,
  `number`, `boolean`, `list`/`array`, `map`) are not flagged, nor are runtime/store-managed fields
  (`id`, composite keys, relationship FKs, the tenant property, version fields, auto timestamps).
  Both are warnings (not hard errors, since the runtime merges caller input on create) and surface
  in `manifest compile` and the LSP.
</Warning>

Date components and conversions are available through expression built-ins. Component extractors operate on a numeric millisecond timestamp:

```manifest
entity DateUtils {
  property required id: string
  property baseTs: number = 0

  computed extractedYear: number = year(self.baseTs)
  computed extractedMonth: number = month(self.baseTs)
  computed extractedDay: number = day(self.baseTs)
  computed extractedHours: number = hours(self.baseTs)
  computed extractedMinutes: number = minutes(self.baseTs)
  computed extractedSeconds: number = seconds(self.baseTs)
}
```

The date built-ins all operate on a number interpreted as milliseconds since the epoch and return a UTC component:

- `year(ts)` returns `getUTCFullYear()`.
- `month(ts)` returns `getUTCMonth() + 1` (so January is 1, not 0).
- `day(ts)` returns `getUTCDate()`.
- `hours(ts)`, `minutes(ts)`, `seconds(ts)` return the corresponding UTC components.

Each returns the input unchanged when it is not a number. UTC methods are used deliberately so results are timezone-independent and deterministic.

~~<Warning>
  This is the most significant area of overclaiming to avoid. There are **no** `Date`, `Time`,
  `Duration`, or `Interval` primitive types, no date column mappings for them, and no date
  arithmetic, comparison, or formatting built-ins beyond component extraction. What genuinely exists
  is the `datetime` type (notably via `timestamps`) and the six UTC date-component built-ins above.
</Warning>~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** In addition to the six component extractors, `docs/spec/builtins.md` ships conversion and duration helpers: `dateOf`, `timeOf`, `datetimeOf`, `addDuration`, `durationBetween`, `durationDays`, `durationHours`, `durationMinutes`, `durationSeconds`. There is still no `interval` type and no general calendar arithmetic library beyond these builtins.

## Arrays

Array properties hold multiple scalar values in a single field, distinct from relationships (which model collections of entities). They are declared with either postfix `[]` sugar or explicit `array<T>` generic syntax.

```manifest
entity TaggedDocument {
  property required id: string
  property tags: string[] = []
  property scores: array<number> = []

  constraint noEmptyTags: self.tags.length > 0
  constraint hasTags: self.tags.contains("published")
}

store TaggedDocument in memory
```

`string[]` and `array<string>` are equivalent. An empty array literal `[]` is a valid default. Constraints can reach into array values with member access such as `self.tags.length` and method-style calls like `self.tags.contains("published")`.

Both forms normalize to an `array` type carrying a `generic` element type in the AST and IR. Array element types may themselves carry parameters — because the inner type goes through the same type parser, the generic element preserves details like decimal precision where applicable. The normalized `array` IR type is the structural input projections consume to emit PostgreSQL array/JSONB columns or Zod array schemas.

<Note>
  The `.length` and `.contains(...)` helpers are expression-level operations rather than dedicated
  array constraint primitives. The two constraints above are independently evaluated rather than
  short-circuited, so both can register failures. Array properties are scalar-valued collections and
  are deliberately separate from `hasMany` relationships, which model entity collections.
</Note>

## Maps / Records

The `map<V>` type declares a key-value dictionary property where all values are of type `V`. Keys are always strings. The form `map<string, V>` is accepted as sugar for `map<V>` (same IR). Other key types are rejected.

```manifest
entity Product {
  property required id: string
  property name: string
  property metadata: map<string> = {}
  property translations: map<string, string> = {}
  property featureFlags: map<boolean> = {}
}

store Product in memory
```

The type parameter is the value type (`map<V>` or `map<string, V>`). An empty map literal `{}` is a valid default. Value types can be any scalar type — `string`, `number`, `boolean`, or a custom type name.

Access map values in expressions using member access:

```manifest fragment
constraint hasEnglishName: self.translations["en"] != ""

computed flagEnabled: self.featureFlags["beta-feature"] == true
```

<Note>
  Map properties are persisted as JSON objects in projections (e.g., Prisma `Json` column, Drizzle
  `jsonb`). The runtime does not enforce key or value types beyond what JavaScript provides — type
  safety is a projection concern.
</Note>


---

## workflows

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/language/workflows

---
title: 'Workflows: saga orchestration and scheduled commands'
description: 'Orchestrate multi-step distributed workflows with saga compensation, and trigger commands on time-based schedules using cron expressions or fixed intervals.'
sidebarTitle: 'Workflows'
---

Manifest provides two workflow primitives for coordinating execution across time and services: **saga orchestration** for distributed transactions with compensation, and **scheduled commands** for time-based triggers.

## Saga orchestration

A saga declares a sequence of steps that execute across entities or services. Each step names a `command` and an optional `compensate` command for rollback. If any step fails, the saga runs compensate for all previously completed steps in reverse order.

### Declaring a saga

Use the `saga` keyword at the program or module level. Each `step` is a block naming its `command` and optional `compensate` as `Entity.command` references. The `on_failure` clause takes a single identifier — `compensate` (the default) or `abort` — and `emit` declares the saga's lifecycle events:

```manifest
event SagaStarted: "saga.started" { sagaName: string }
event SagaCompleted: "saga.completed" { sagaName: string }
event SagaFailed: "saga.failed" { sagaName: string }
event SagaStepCompleted: "saga.step.completed" { sagaName: string }

saga ProcessOrder {
  step reserveInventory {
    command: Inventory.reserve
    compensate: Inventory.release
  }

  step chargePayment {
    command: Payment.charge
    compensate: Payment.refund
  }

  step shipOrder {
    command: Shipping.dispatch
  }

  on_failure: "compensate"
  ~~emit OrderProcessingFailed~~
  emit SagaStarted
  emit SagaCompleted
  emit SagaFailed
  emit SagaStepCompleted
}
```

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** List the fixed lifecycle event names you want emitted (and declare matching `event`s). A single custom `emit OrderProcessingFailed` does **not** substitute for `SagaFailed` / `SagaCompleted`. Start with `runtime.runSaga('ProcessOrder', stepInputs)`.

### Saga lifecycle

<Steps>
  <Step title="Start">
    ~~The saga begins when the first step's command is invoked.~~ **Correction (2026-07-15)
    @RYANSIGNED:** The saga begins when the host calls `runSaga(name, stepInputs)`. Steps then run
    in declaration order via `runCommand`.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Execute steps">
    Each step's command runs in sequence. If the command succeeds, the saga advances to the next
    step.
  </Step>
  <Step title="On failure — compensate">
    If any step's command fails, the saga enters compensation mode. It runs each completed step's
    compensate command in reverse order (last completed first).
  </Step>
  <Step title="Complete or failed">
    After all steps complete (or all compensations run), the saga emits a completion or failure
    event.
  </Step>
</Steps>

### Best-effort compensation

Compensation is best-effort. If a compensate command itself fails, the saga logs the failure and continues compensating remaining steps. This ensures partial rollback rather than aborting the compensation process entirely.

### Saga lifecycle events

~~| Event | When emitted |
| --- | --- |
| `{SagaName}Started` | … |
| `{SagaName}StepCompensated` | … |~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Lifecycle event names are **fixed** (`SagaStarted`, `SagaStepCompleted`, `SagaCompleted`, `SagaFailed`) — not `{SagaName}…`. The runtime emits a lifecycle event **only if** that name appears in the saga's `emit` list **and** a matching `event` is declared in the IR (`emitSagaLifecycle`). There is **no** `SagaStepCompensated` lifecycle emission. Invoke with `runtime.runSaga(name, stepInputs)`.

```manifest
event SagaStarted: "saga.started" { sagaName: string }
event SagaCompleted: "saga.completed" { sagaName: string }
event SagaFailed: "saga.failed" { sagaName: string }
event SagaStepCompleted: "saga.step.completed" { sagaName: string }

entity Order {
  property required id: string
  command charge() {
    mutate charged = true
  }
  command refund() {
    mutate charged = false
  }
  store in memory
}

saga ProcessOrder {
  step chargePayment {
    command: Order.charge
    compensate: Order.refund
  }
  on_failure: "compensate"
  emit SagaStarted
  emit SagaCompleted
  emit SagaFailed
  emit SagaStepCompleted
}
```

<Note>
  Declared saga lifecycle events carry `correlationId` / `causationId` like other emissions when
  present on the emitted record.
</Note>

## Scheduled commands

Scheduled commands trigger entity commands on a time-based schedule using cron expressions, fixed intervals, or periodic timers.

### Declaring a scheduled command

> **Correction (2026-07-22 @RYANSIGNED):** The accepted duration units for `every`/`interval` are `ms`, `s`, `m`, `h`, `d`, `week`, `weeks` only. Long forms like `minutes`/`hours`/`seconds` are NOT accepted and will throw an error. **Important:** Entity-embedded schedules (inside `entity { … }` blocks) are silently dropped from the IR and do not execute. Only top-level single-line schedules are supported — see the corrected example below.

Use the `schedule` keyword at the top level of a `.manifest` file:

```manifest
entity Report {
  property required id: string
  property lastRunAt: number = 0
  property status: string = "idle"

  command generate() {
    mutate status = "running"
    emit ReportGenerationStarted
  }

  command checkHealth() {
    mutate lastRunAt = now()
  }

  command purgeExpired() {
    mutate status = "cleaned"
  }
}

// Top-level schedules (single-line syntax required)
schedule generateDaily cron "0 6 * * *" run Report.generate
schedule healthCheck interval "5m" run Report.checkHealth
schedule cleanup every 24 h run Report.purgeExpired
```

### Trigger types

| Type       | Syntax                             | Description                                                      |
| ---------- | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `cron`     | `cron "0 6 * * *"`                | Standard 5-field cron expression (minute hour day month weekday) |
| `interval` | `interval <ms>` \| `interval "<dur>"` | Fixed interval in milliseconds or duration string (e.g., `"5m"`, `"1h"`) |
| `every`    | `every <n> <unit>`                | Human-readable period where `<unit>` is `s`/`m`/`h`/`d`/`week`/`weeks` |

### Schedule resolution

Schedules are resolved at compile time and emitted to the IR as `IRSchedule` objects. The `RuntimeEngine` class has no built-in timer, but the package ships an optional worker (`startScheduleWorker` from `@angriff36/manifest/schedule-worker`) that drives schedules on a tick loop and sweeps approval expiry; projection targets also consume the metadata:

- **Next.js projection**: Generates a cron route per schedule (`/api/cron/[name]/route.ts`) plus a `vercel.json` registering them, so the platform invokes them on schedule.
- **Express/Hono projections**: Emit route handlers intended for external scheduler invocation.
- **Infrastructure projections** (Terraform): Generate CloudWatch Event rules or similar scheduled resources.

### Inline parameters

Scheduled commands can pass static parameters to the target command:

```manifest
schedule warmCache cron "0 */6 * * *" run Cache.refresh(keys: "all")

entity Cache {
  command refresh(keys: string) {
    // ...
  }
}
```

<Warning>
  The `RuntimeEngine` class has no built-in timer. On Vercel, the generated cron routes and
  `vercel.json` fire schedules automatically; on other hosts, run `startScheduleWorker(runtime)`
  from `@angriff36/manifest/schedule-worker`, or invoke `runSchedule(name)` from your own scheduler.
  Cron triggers are matched in UTC to the minute.
</Warning>

## Combining sagas and schedules

~~Sagas and schedules can work together. A scheduled command can initiate a saga…~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Schedules invoke an entity **command** (`run <command>`). They do **not** call `runSaga` automatically. To start a saga from a schedule, the scheduled command (or a reaction it triggers) must call into host code that invokes `runtime.runSaga(...)`, or model the workflow as ordinary commands/reactions. Emitting an event alone does not start a saga.


---

## additional projections

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/projections/additional-projections

---
title: 'Additional projections'
sidebarTitle: 'More projections'
description: 'Kysely, Terraform, materialized views, analytics, Elasticsearch, Storybook, and Python Client SDK projections for specialized infrastructure and tooling targets.'
---

Beyond the primary framework and ORM projections, Manifest ships several specialized projections for infrastructure, analytics, search, and developer tooling targets. Each follows the same `ProjectionTarget` interface and deterministic output contract.

> **Verified (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Names `kysely`, `terraform`, `materialized-views`,
> `analytics`, `elasticsearch`, `storybook`, and `pydantic` (client via `pydantic.client`) are
> registered in `src/manifest/projections/builtins.ts`. Invoke with
> `manifest generate -p <name>`. Package pin: see `package.json` SoT
> (~~**3.6.4**~~ **3.6.41** in the 2026-07-22 checkout).

### Kysely

> **Correction (2026-07-22 @RYANSIGNED):** The registered surface name is `kysely.types` (not `kysely.schema`). The projection generates TypeScript type definitions for the [Kysely](https://kysely.dev) type-safe SQL query builder.

The projection is registered under the name `kysely` and emits one surface: `kysely.types` (content type `typescript`, default output `kysely.types.ts`).

**Key characteristics:**

- IR types map to Kysely/TypeScript types with `Generated<T>` for ID columns and columns with `defaultValue`, and `ColumnType<SelectType, InsertType, UpdateType>` for Date/datetime columns.
- `belongsTo`/`ref` relationships auto-generate FK columns (skipped if an FK property already exists). `hasMany`/`hasOne` are expressed through Kysely's relation API, not as columns.
- Bare `number` produces a `KYSELY_AMBIGUOUS_NUMBER` diagnostic and the column is skipped, requiring an explicit `int`/`bigint`/`float`/`decimal`/`money` type.
- Skips non-SQL store targets (`memory`, `localStorage`, `mongodb`, `dynamodb`, `eventSourced`).
- Supports `dialect` (`postgresql`, `mysql`, `sqlite`), `tableMappings`, `columnMappings` (applied to property and synthesized FK keys — 2026-07-22), `typeMappings`, and `emitFactory` options.

```ts
import { getProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections';

const projection = getProjection('kysely');
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'kysely.types',
  options: { dialect: 'postgresql' },
});
```

### Terraform

> **Correction (2026-07-22 @RYANSIGNED):** The registered surface name is `terraform.hcl` (not `terraform.main`). The projection generates HCL resource definitions from Manifest IR. It supports three cloud providers: **AWS** (emits `aws_db_instance`, `aws_s3_bucket`, `aws_sns_topic`), **GCP** (emits `google_sql_database_instance`, `google_storage_bucket`, `google_pubsub_topic`), and **Supabase** (emits `supabase_project` with documented table schemas).

The projection is registered under the name `terraform` and emits one surface: `terraform.hcl` (content type `hcl`). It supports both single-file (`main.tf`) and multi-file (`database.tf`, `storage.tf`, `messaging.tf`) output modes.

**Key characteristics:**

- Walks `ir.stores` to find entities with persistent store targets (`postgres`, `supabase`) for database resources.
- Walks `ir.events` to emit pub/sub topic definitions.
- Accepts `storageBuckets` in options for S3/GCS bucket definitions.
- Skips non-persistent targets (`memory`, `localStorage`, `durable`) with diagnostic codes.
- Emits proper HCL with primary keys, unique constraints, `NOT NULL`, and encryption settings.

```ts
const projection = getProjection('terraform');
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'terraform.hcl',
  options: { provider: 'aws', outputMode: 'multi-file' },
});
```

### Materialized View SQL

The materialized views projection generates PostgreSQL `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW` DDL from Manifest IR entities. It supports three refresh strategies: **on-demand** (emits `REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW`), **scheduled** (emits `SELECT cron.schedule(...)` for pg_cron), and **trigger-based** (emits `CREATE TRIGGER ... AFTER UPDATE OF col ... EXECUTE FUNCTION`).

The projection is registered under the name `materialized-views` and emits one surface: `materialized-views.ddl` (content type `sql`).

**Key characteristics:**

- Includes an expression-to-SQL translator that converts `IRExpression` trees to PostgreSQL: literals, identifiers, binary operations (`===` maps to `=`), function calls (`SUM`, `COUNT`), and conditionals (`CASE WHEN`).
- Supports schema-qualified view names and `CREATE [UNIQUE] INDEX` statements (btree, hash, gin, gist).
- All relational concepts (view names, column aliases, refresh strategies, index definitions) enter via projection options, never into the IR.
- Deterministic output for a given IR + options pair.

```ts
const projection = getProjection('materialized-views');
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'materialized-views.ddl',
  options: {
    views: [
      {
        name: 'order_summary',
        source: 'Order',
        strategy: 'scheduled',
        schedule: { cron: '0 * * * *' },
        columns: [{ expression: 'SUM(total)', alias: 'revenue' }],
      },
    ],
  },
});
```

### Analytics Event Schema

The analytics projection generates typed event schemas from Manifest IR for Segment, Amplitude, Mixpanel, or Snowplow. It produces three surfaces:

- `analytics.tracking-plan` — A JSON tracking plan document following the Segment Tracking Plan spec format, with events derived from command emits and standalone event declarations.
- `analytics.events` — A TypeScript module with typed property interfaces per event, `AnalyticsEvents` constants, `AnalyticsEventMap` type mapping, and provider-specific `track()` functions.
- `analytics.handlers` — TypeScript command handler stubs that inject typed `track()` calls after command execution.

The projection is registered under the name `analytics` and supports the `provider` option (`'segment'` | `'amplitude'` | `'mixpanel'` | `'snowplow'`, default `'segment'`). Events are deduplicated by name across command emits and standalone declarations. All output is deterministic with sorted iteration.

```ts
const projection = getProjection('analytics');
const result = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'analytics.tracking-plan',
  options: { provider: 'segment' },
});
```

### Elasticsearch / OpenSearch

The Elasticsearch projection generates index mappings, templates, ingest pipelines, an outbox-driven indexer worker, and a typed search client from Manifest IR entities stored in Elasticsearch. It emits five surfaces:

- `elasticsearch.mapping` — Per-entity ES index mappings with proper field types, settings, and analyzers.
- `elasticsearch.indexTemplate` — Composable index templates with index patterns and priority.
- `elasticsearch.ingestPipeline` — Ingest pipeline processor configurations.
- `elasticsearch.indexer` — TypeScript worker that consumes outbox entries and bulk-indexes entity state changes with retry logic and exponential backoff.
- `elasticsearch.client` — TypeScript search query builders with `multi_match` full-text search and typed document interfaces.

The projection is registered under the name `elasticsearch`. It maps Manifest types to ES field types (string to `keyword`/`text`, int to `integer`, float to `scaled_float`, etc.). Bare `number` produces an `ELASTICSEARCH_AMBIGUOUS_NUMBER` diagnostic. Computed properties are excluded from index mappings by default. Multi-tenant support automatically adds `tenantId` as a required `keyword` field when tenant context is configured.

```ts
const projection = getProjection('elasticsearch');
const mapping = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'elasticsearch.mapping' });
const indexer = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'elasticsearch.indexer' });
```

### Storybook

The Storybook projection generates Component Story Format (CSF3) stories from Manifest IR entities and commands. It emits three surfaces:

- `storybook.entity` — Default stories for each entity with all properties mapped to Storybook controls (text, number, boolean, date, select for enums). Computed properties are rendered as `control: false`. Private properties are excluded.
- `storybook.command` — Interaction stories with `GuardsPass` and `GuardFails` scenarios using play functions. Guard expressions are analyzed heuristically to produce meaningful pass/fail args.
- `storybook.all` — Combined entity and command stories in a single artifact.

The projection is registered under the name `storybook`. It supports `componentImportPattern` (with `{Entity}` and `{Command}` placeholders), `titlePrefix`, `includeGuardScenarios`, and `includeConstraintStories` options. Constraint violation stories are generated for entities with constraints.

```ts
const projection = getProjection('storybook');
const stories = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'storybook.all',
  options: { titlePrefix: 'Manifest/' },
});
```

### Python Client SDK

The Python Client SDK is delivered through the `pydantic.client` surface of the [Pydantic projection](/projections/python-pydantic). It generates a `ManifestClient` class with `httpx.AsyncClient` integration, entity query methods (`list_<entity>s`, `get_<entity>`), typed command invocation methods, optional bearer token auth, and module-level convenience functions. The generated code targets Python 3.10+.

```ts
const projection = getProjection('pydantic');
const client = projection.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'pydantic.client',
  options: { clientBaseUrl: 'https://api.example.com', emitConvenienceFunctions: true },
});
```


---

## one surface

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/projections/convex

---
title: 'Convex backend projection'
sidebarTitle: 'Convex'
description: 'Generate a complete Convex backend from Manifest IR — defineSchema/defineTable validators, reactive queries, governed mutations, crons, HTTP webhooks, and saga orchestrators, all standalone with no runtime dependency.'
---

The Convex projection emits a complete, self-contained Convex backend from compiled Manifest IR. It started as a schema-only projection (`convex.schema`, shipped in 2.9.0) and now spans eight surfaces covering schema, reads, governed writes, schedules, webhooks, sagas, computed properties, and React clients. Everything it emits is standalone Convex code with no Manifest runtime dependency, and typechecks against `convex@1.41`. Like the [Prisma](/integration/prisma) and [Drizzle](/projections/drizzle) projections, all Convex-specific interpretation (table names, validators, references, indexes) lives in the projection and its options — never in Manifest core grammar or IR.

## What it generates

The projection registers under the name `convex` and declares eight surfaces:

| Surface            | Path hint                                      | Emits                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `convex.schema`    | `convex/schema.ts` (configurable via `output`) | `defineSchema` / `defineTable` with `convex/values` validators, enum unions, `v.id` references, and indexes. Optionally a system events table.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| `convex.queries`   | `convex/queries.ts`                            | Reactive reads over `ctx.db`: `list<Entity>`, `get<Entity>` (by Convex `_id`), and `list<Entity>By<Field…>` over `.withIndex` for **every** schema index — indexed/tenant/reference fields _and_ composite `options.indexes` entries (`list<Entity>By<A>And<B>`). `list`/`get` are tenant-scoped and soft-delete-filtered by default (see [Read filtering](#read-filtering)). When the events table is emitted, adds `listRecentEvents` plus `listEventsByType` / `listEventsByEntity` / `listEventsByEntityId`.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| `convex.mutations` | `convex/mutations.ts`                          | One governed `mutation` per IR command. Runs policies → guards → constraints (runtime order, fail-closed), applies `mutate` actions, appends an event row, and fires matched reactions. Honors G7 `emit Event { field: expr }`: declared fields populate the event-row and reaction payloads from the post-action instance.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| `convex.crons`     | `convex/crons.ts`                              | IR schedules → `cronJobs()` (`crons.cron` / `crons.interval`) referencing the command mutations.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| `convex.http`      | `convex/http.ts`                               | IR webhooks → `httpRouter` / `httpAction` routes that read the request body, map `transform` params, and `ctx.runMutation` the command.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| `convex.sagas`     | `convex/sagas.ts`                              | IR sagas → orchestrator `action`s that run each step via `ctx.runMutation`, tracking completed steps and compensating in reverse on failure (`onFailure: compensate`) or rethrowing (`abort`).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| `convex.computed`  | `convex/computed.ts`                           | Computed property definitions: `isFieldComputed()` helpers and `get<Field>()` selectors for each entity's computed properties. (Shipped 3.6.17+)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| `convex.react`     | `convex/react.ts`                              | React hook wrappers: `useQuery<Entity>`, `useQuery<Entity>By<Field>`, `useMutation<Entity,Command>`. Emits only when `convex.react` is enabled via options. (Shipped 3.6.19+)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |

Only persistent entities are projected. Entities marked `external`, entities with no `store` declaration, and entities whose store target is `memory` / `localStorage` are skipped; `durable` / `postgres` / `supabase` targets are emission targets. `computed` properties are never emitted as fields, and the IR `id` is dropped because Convex's built-in document `_id` is identity.

## Usage

```ts
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
import { getProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections';

const { ir } = await compileToIR(source);

const projection = getProjection('convex');
const result = projection?.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'convex.schema',
  options: {
    referenceMode: 'convexId',
    tableMappings: { CateringEvent: 'events' },
  },
});

for (const artifact of result?.artifacts ?? []) {
  // write artifact.code at artifact.pathHint
}
```

`ConvexProjection` can also be imported directly from `@angriff36/manifest/projections/convex`. Through the CLI it is dispatched by registry name. The CLI accepts either the full surface id (`convex.schema`) or its short suffix (`schema`), and `--surface all` walks every surface in one run:

```bash
# one surface
manifest generate ir/app.ir.json --projection convex --surface convex.schema --output convex

# every surface (schema, queries, mutations, crons, http, sagas, computed, react)
manifest generate ir/app.ir.json --projection convex --surface all --output convex
```

Each artifact is written at its `pathHint` (e.g. `convex/schema.ts`, `convex/queries.ts`) relative to `--output`.

## Type mapping & behavior

The IR `type.name` string maps to a `convex/values` validator through `DEFAULT_TYPE_MAPPING` in `type-mapping.ts`:

| Manifest type                                | Convex validator | Notes                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| -------------------------------------------- | ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `string`, `text`, `uuid`                     | `v.string()`     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| `boolean`, `bool`                            | `v.boolean()`    |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| `int`, `bigint`, `float`, `decimal`, `money` | `v.number()`     | Convex Float64. Matches Manifest runtime semantics, which treat every numeric type as an ordinary JS number — decimal/money precision and integer width are projection metadata, not runtime-enforced. Storing them as `v.int64()` (bigint) or `v.string()` (text) would diverge from the runtime and break generated guard/mutation arithmetic (mixed bigint/number throws; string operands concatenate or compare lexically). Opt into `v.int64()` / `v.string()` per property via `typeMappings` where lossless transport is genuinely required. |
| `date`, `datetime`, `time`, `duration`       | `v.number()`     | Epoch milliseconds, the Convex-idiomatic timestamp. Override to `v.string()` for ISO-8601.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| `json`                                       | `v.any()`        |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| `bytes`                                      | `v.bytes()`      |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |

As with Prisma and Drizzle, bare `number` is intentionally absent — it is ambiguous between integers, reals, and money. The projection emits a hard `CONVEX_AMBIGUOUS_NUMBER` diagnostic and skips the field; an unknown type with no override emits a hard `CONVEX_UNKNOWN_TYPE` diagnostic. There is no silent fallback.

Validators compose: enums become `v.union(v.literal("a"), v.literal("b"))`, `array<T>` becomes `v.array(<element>)`, nullable properties union with `v.null()`, and non-required properties wrap in `v.optional(...)` (enum + nullable flatten into a single union). For `belongsTo` / `ref` relationships in the default `convexId` mode, a property that backs the relationship is retyped from its declared scalar to `v.id("<targetTable>")`, preserving its own optional/nullable. Indexes are emitted for `indexed` properties (`by_<field>`), the tenant column, and every reference, plus any consumer-supplied composite/named indexes. Referential actions (`onDelete` / `onUpdate`) emit a deferred-info diagnostic — Convex has no schema-level cascade.

## Options

The options object is `ConvexProjectionOptions` in `convex/options.ts`, normalized through `normalizeOptions`. Per-entity and per-property options use the nested `Record<EntityName, Record<PropertyName, X>>` shape — there are no dotted-string `"Entity.property"` keys anywhere on this surface.

| Option                    | Type                                               | Default                                         | Purpose                                                                                                                                                                   |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `output`                  | `string`                                           | `"convex/schema.ts"`                            | Path hint for the emitted schema artifact (flows to `ProjectionArtifact.pathHint`).                                                                                       |
| `tableMappings`           | `Record<EntityName, string>`                       | `{}`                                            | Per-entity table-name override; always wins over the naming convention.                                                                                                   |
| `typeMappings`            | `Record<EntityName, Record<PropertyName, string>>` | `{}`                                            | Per-property validator override; the value is the literal Convex validator expression, e.g. `"v.number()"` or `"v.int64()"`.                                              |
| `indexes`                 | `Record<EntityName, IndexEntry[]>`                 | `{}`                                            | Per-entity composite/named indexes. Each entry is a `string[]` (composite) or `{ fields, name? }`, becoming a `.index("name", [...])` call.                               |
| `references`              | `Record<EntityName, Record<string, string>>`       | `{}`                                            | Per-relationship foreign-key field-name override (default is the non-tenant `foreignKey.fields` column, or `${relationshipName}Id`).                                      |
| `referenceMode`           | `'convexId' \| 'stringId'`                         | `'convexId'`                                    | How `belongsTo` / `ref` references are represented. `'convexId'` → `v.id("<targetTable>")`; `'stringId'` → `v.string()` for app-level string ids (UUIDs).                 |
| `naming`                  | `NamingConventionInput`                            | `{ table: 'camelCase', pluralizeTables: true }` | Table-name casing/pluralization. The Convex-idiomatic default is applied when omitted; `tableMappings` is the escape hatch for irregular plurals.                         |
| `emitEventsTable`         | `boolean`                                          | `true`                                          | Emit a system events table (the reactive event log governed mutations append to).                                                                                         |
| `eventsTable`             | `string`                                           | `"manifestEvents"`                              | Name of the system events table. If the resolved name collides with an entity table it is deterministically suffixed (with a `CONVEX_EVENTS_TABLE_COLLISION` diagnostic). |
| `policyMode`              | `'enforce' \| 'skip'`                              | `'enforce'`                                     | Authorization-policy enforcement in generated mutations — see below.                                                                                                      |
| `includeTenantFilter`     | `boolean`                                          | `true`                                          | Scope `list<Entity>` / `get<Entity>` reads to the current tenant. Field-aware — see [Read filtering](#read-filtering).                                                    |
| `includeSoftDeleteFilter` | `boolean`                                          | `true`                                          | Exclude soft-deleted rows from generated reads. Field-aware.                                                                                                              |
| `tenantIdProperty`        | `string`                                           | IR `tenant.property`                            | Override the tenant property name used for read scoping.                                                                                                                  |
| `deletedAtProperty`       | `string`                                           | `"deletedAt"`                                   | Soft-delete property name; a read drops rows whose value is non-null.                                                                                                     |
| `authContextImport`       | `string`                                           | unset                                           | Module specifier of an author-owned auth-context module — see [Auth context seam](#auth-context-seam).                                                                    |

### Auth context seam (`authContextImport`)

By default (unset), generated functions read identity from an inline `(ctx as any).auth` bag — a shape the real Convex runtime **never populates** (`ctx.auth` only exposes `getUserIdentity()`), so role/tenant reads evaluate against `undefined` and fail closed until you wire auth by hand. Setting `authContextImport` (e.g. `"./lib/authContext"`) routes all identity reads through your module's exported `getAuthContext(ctx)` — which should derive role and tenant from the real authenticated identity (e.g. a Clerk/Auth0 JWT via `ctx.auth.getUserIdentity()`).

With the seam active, two write-side protections also turn on for tenant-scoped entities:

- **`create` mutations derive the tenant server-side.** The tenant column is filled from the auth context and removed from the mutation's client arguments (a create action targeting it is overridden, with a `CONVEX_TENANT_SERVER_DERIVED` diagnostic).
- **Instance mutations verify document tenancy.** A command against a document from another tenant throws the same "not found" error as a missing document (no cross-tenant existence oracle).

Contract: `getAuthContext(ctx)` returns (or resolves to) an object carrying at least `role` and the tenant property. Crons, sagas, and webhooks invoke the same governed mutations, so the module must also handle identity-less system calls (e.g. return a system context).

### Read filtering

~~Generated reads are **tenant-scoped and soft-delete-filtered by default**, mirroring the [Next.js projection](/integration/nextjs).~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Convex defaults `includeTenantFilter` and
> `includeSoftDeleteFilter` to **`true`**. Next.js defaults both to **`false`** — they do **not**
> mirror each other. Both Convex filters are _field-aware_: a clause is emitted only for entities
> that actually declare the column, so entities that don't follow the tenancy / soft-delete
> conventions get plain unfiltered reads.

<Note>
The tenant id for `list<Entity>` and `get<Entity>` is derived from the authenticated identity (via `getAuthContext(ctx)` when `authContextImport` is set, otherwise the legacy inline `ctx.auth.<tenantProp>` read), **never from a client argument** — so an un-scoped `list<Entity>()` cannot return rows across tenants (it fails closed: with no auth wired, it matches no tenant and returns nothing rather than everything). `get<Entity>` returns `null` on a tenant mismatch or a soft-deleted row. `list<Entity>By<Field…>` reads additionally drop soft-deleted rows and apply the auth-derived tenant filter unless the index already constrains the tenant column. Turn either filter off with `includeTenantFilter` / `includeSoftDeleteFilter`.
</Note>

### policyMode

`policyMode` (added in 2.10.x) controls whether generated mutations enforce authorization policies. Its accepted values are exactly `'enforce'` and `'skip'`, defaulting to `'enforce'`.

<Note>
  With `policyMode: 'skip'`, the role/policy (authorization) checks are omitted from generated
  mutations, while guards and constraints (state validation) are still enforced. When skipped, the
  `ROLE_PERMISSIONS` map and `checkRole` helper are not emitted, so there is no dead code. This is
  intended for dev/demo backends that have no auth context configured, so the generated files don't
  need hand-editing. Production builds should keep the default `'enforce'`.
</Note>

## Notes & limitations

<Note>
Mutation governance is rendered **fail-closed**: each policy, guard, and constraint is rendered from IR via a pure expression resolver, and any expression the resolver cannot fully map emits a hard `CONVEX_UNRESOLVED_{POLICY,GUARD,CONSTRAINT}` diagnostic together with a denying `throw` — never a silent pass. Roles become a `ROLE_PERMISSIONS` map plus a `checkRole()` helper (with an `all` wildcard). Reactions that create tenant-scoped targets automatically thread the source `tenantId` (2.10.3) unless an explicit reaction param sets it.
</Note>

<Note>
  The `convex.http` surface generates `httpRouter` / `httpAction` webhook routes that read the
  request body and invoke the command mutation, but does **not** verify `IRWebhook.signature` (HMAC
  sha256/sha512) — generated Convex `httpAction`s have no signature verification. It also does not
  implement `idempotencyHeader` deduplication: duplicate webhook deliveries re-run the command
  mutation. For verified, deduplicated webhook handling, use the reference webhook handler
  (`@angriff36/manifest/webhooks`) with a Next.js, Express, or Hono projection instead.
</Note>

### G7 emit payloads (`emit Event { field: expr }`)

A command may declare explicit event-payload fields (G7). The projection evaluates each declared field against the **post-action instance** and populates both the emitted event row's `payload` and the shared reaction `payload` with them — mirroring the reference runtime (`runtime-engine.ts` emit-time payload construction). This matters for reactions on **mutate** commands: the runtime's base event payload is `{ ...input, result }`, where `input` is only the command's parameters, so an entity-owned field the reaction needs (`payload.invoiceId`, `payload.amount`) or a computed field (`payload.total = self.qty * self.price`) is `undefined` unless declared. Declaring it via G7 makes the reaction resolve instead of silently no-op'ing — which is what lets a cross-entity cascade (e.g. "when a payment is processed, apply it to its invoice") be authored as a reaction rather than hand-written as after-emit middleware. Create commands evaluate against the built `doc`; non-create commands introduce a post-patch `{ ...doc, ...updates }` instance. It is strictly opt-in: commands with no `emitPayloads` generate byte-identical output. Unresolved field expressions emit a `CONVEX_UNRESOLVED_EMIT_PAYLOAD` warning and are omitted (non-governance, so no denying throw).

This projection's behavior is taken directly from `src/manifest/projections/convex/generator.ts`, `options.ts`, `type-mapping.ts`, `functions.ts`, and `orchestration.ts`. The schema surface shipped in 2.9.0; the five functions/orchestration surfaces, `listRecentEvents`, the create-mutation completeness fix, and `policyMode` landed across the 2.10.x releases. Schema↔query index parity (every schema index — single-field, reference FK in both reference modes, and composite — has a matching read), the events-table indexed reads, and default tenant + soft-delete read filtering landed in a later 2.10.x release.

## Rate limiting

Command- and policy-level `rateLimit` is implemented via a sliding-window bucket table. When any command or write/execute/delete policy declares `rateLimit`, the projection emits:

**Schema table** (`commandRateLimitBuckets` by default):

```ts
commandRateLimitBuckets: defineTable({
  scopeKey: v.string(),      // "user:user-123", "tenant:org-abc", or "global"
