- **`clerk`** — Imports from `@clerk/remix`, uses `getAuth()` to retrieve the current user session.
- **`custom`** — Imports a `requireUser(request)` function from the configured `authImportPath`.
- **`none`** — No auth checks in generated code; suitable for public APIs or when auth is handled at a higher layer.

~~Unauthorized users receive a redirect to the configured `unauthorizedRedirect` path (default `/login`) with the appropriate HTTP status code from the `unauthorizedStatus` option.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Default `authProvider` is **`remix-auth`**, not `clerk`
> (`REMIX_DEFAULTS`). There is **no** `unauthorizedRedirect` option — unauthorized responses use
> `unauthorizedStatus` (default `401`) without a configured redirect path.

## Generated file structure

For an entity named `Order` with a `placeOrder` command, the projection emits:

```
app/routes/
  orders/
    index.tsx          # remix.list — GET /orders
    $id.tsx            # remix.detail — GET /orders/:id
    place-order.tsx    # remix.command — POST /orders/place-order
app/types/
  manifest.ts          # remix.types — entity & command interfaces
app/utils/
  manifest-client.ts   # remix.client — response helpers
```

Route segment names are derived from the lower-cased entity name and kebab-cased command names, following Remix file-based routing conventions.

## Type mapping

The projection maps Manifest IR types to TypeScript: `string` to `string`; `number`/`integer`/`int` to `number`; `boolean`/`bool` to `boolean`; `date`/`datetime`/`uuid`/`email`/`url`/`uri`/`decimal` to `string`; `any` to `unknown`; `object` to `Record<string, unknown>`. Generic `array<T>` becomes `T[]` and `map<V>` becomes `Record<string, V>`. Nullable types append ` | null`.

## Options

~~`RemixProjectionOptions` accepts `authProvider` (`'clerk'` | `'remix-auth'` | `'custom'` | `'none'`, default `'clerk'`), `authImportPath` (default varies by provider), `runtimeImportPath` (default `./lib/manifest-runtime`), `runtimeFactoryName` (default `createManifestRuntime`), `basePath` (default `/api`), `remixVersion` (`'v2'` | `'v7'`, default `'v2'`), `includeTenantContext` (default `true`), `tenantIdProperty` (default `tenantId`), `errorBoundaries` (default `false`), `unauthorizedStatus` (default `401`), `unauthorizedRedirect` (default `/login`), `includeSoftDeleteFilter` (default `false`), `publicReads` (default `false`), and `includeComments` (default `true`).~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Real options from `REMIX_DEFAULTS`
> (`src/manifest/projections/remix/generator.ts`): `authProvider` default **`'remix-auth'`** (not
> `clerk`); `includeTenantFilter` (not `includeTenantContext`) default **`true`**;
> `includeSoftDeleteFilter` default **`true`** (not `false`); `includeErrorBoundary` (not
> `errorBoundaries`) default **`true`**; `remixVersion` default `'v2'`; `unauthorizedStatus`
> default `401`. There is **no** `unauthorizedRedirect`, `publicReads`, `basePath`, or
> `runtimeFactoryName` option. Defaults also set `authImportPath: '~/utils/auth.server'`,
> `runtimeImportPath: '~/utils/manifest-runtime'`, `routesDir: 'app/routes'`, `emitCompanions: true`.

Command handlers map ~~runtime error codes to HTTP status: `GUARD_FAILED` to 403, `CONSTRAINT_VIOLATION` to 422, `CONCURRENCY_CONFLICT` to 409, `NOT_FOUND` to 404, and anything else to 500~~.

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Generated Remix actions map diagnostic **kinds**:
> `policy_denial` → 403, `guard_failure` → **422**, `constraint_block` → 422, else 400 — not the
> legacy `GUARD_FAILED`/`CONSTRAINT_VIOLATION` string codes above.

## Notes & limitations

<Note>
  The generated modules import auth and runtime factory from the configured paths; they do not
  define those modules. Read routes (`list`/`detail`) bypass the runtime engine and call the
  database directly. All writes go through
  `runtime.runCommand(commandName, body, { entityName, instanceId })`, matching
  `RuntimeEngine` (not an entity-first signature).
</Note>

The projection supports Remix v2 and React Router v7 file conventions via `remixVersion`.
Tenant and soft-delete filtering default **on** (`includeTenantFilter` /
`includeSoftDeleteFilter`); set them to `false` to disable.


---

## sveltekit

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/projections/sveltekit

---
title: 'SvelteKit server routes projection'
sidebarTitle: 'SvelteKit'
description: 'Generate SvelteKit +server.ts route handlers, +page.server.ts load functions, and typed API surfaces from Manifest IR — with Lucia, Auth.js, or custom auth integration.'
---

The SvelteKit projection generates server routes and load functions from compiled Manifest IR that follow SvelteKit conventions. Use it when you are building a SvelteKit application and want Manifest entities wired into your server-side routes, form actions, and page data. The projection mirrors the [Next.js projection's](/integration/nextjs) read/write split but targets SvelteKit's `+server.ts` and `+page.server.ts` file conventions.

The projection is registered under the name `sveltekit` and lives at `src/manifest/projections/sveltekit/generator.ts`.

## What it generates

The projection exposes six surfaces.

`sveltekit.server` emits a `+server.ts` file with `GET` and `POST` `RequestHandler` exports. The `GET` handler lists entity instances (with optional auth checks, tenant filtering, and soft-delete filtering). The `POST` handler dispatches a command through ~~the Manifest runtime via `runtime.runCommand`~~.

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Generated POST/form handlers emit entity-first
> `runtime.runCommand(entity, command, { params, instanceId })` — see Notes. That is not the
> `RuntimeEngine` signature.

`sveltekit.load` emits a `+page.server.ts` file with a `load: PageServerLoad` function and an `actions: Actions` export. The `load` function fetches entity data for page rendering. Form actions decode `event.request.formData()`, extract `instanceId` from form fields, and invoke commands. SvelteKit's `./$types` provides type-safe `PageData`.

`sveltekit.command` emits a per-command `+server.ts` route under `commands/[command]/+server.ts`. This is a legacy alias for the command-specific route pattern.

`sveltekit.types` emits entity and command type interfaces plus `ManifestActionResult` and `ManifestDiagnostic` utility interfaces. Computed properties are emitted as `readonly` fields.

`sveltekit.client` emits a `$lib/manifest-client.ts` module with an `invokeManifestCommand()` helper function for client-side command invocation. `sveltekit.companions` emits a companion facade module with `runCommand(entity, command, { params, instanceId }, context?)` that adapts to `RuntimeEngine.runCommand`.

## Usage

```ts
import { getProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections';

const projection = getProjection('sveltekit');

// Generate server route handlers
const server = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'sveltekit.server' });

// Generate page load functions and form actions
const load = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'sveltekit.load' });

// Generate type definitions
const types = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'sveltekit.types' });

// Generate the client helper
const client = projection.generate(ir, { surface: 'sveltekit.client' });
```

Through the CLI:

```bash
manifest generate ir/widget.ir.json --projection sveltekit --surface sveltekit.types --output src/lib/manifest-types.ts
```

## Auth integration

The projection supports four auth providers, configured via the `authProvider` option:

- **`lucia`** (default) — Reads `event.locals.session` for the current user session, following the Lucia auth convention.
- **`auth-js`** — Uses `getServerSession(event)` from NextAuth/SvelteKit integration.
- **`custom`** — Imports a `requireUser(event)` function from the configured `authImportPath`.
- **`none`** — No auth checks in generated code.

Tenant filtering is activated via the ~~`includeTenantContext`~~ **`includeTenantFilter`** option. When enabled, read queries include a `tenantId` predicate. Soft-delete filtering adds `deletedAt: null` to read queries when `includeSoftDeleteFilter` is enabled.

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Option name is `includeTenantFilter` (default **`true`**).
> `includeSoftDeleteFilter` also defaults to **`true`** (`SVELTEKIT_DEFAULTS`), not `false`.

## Generated file structure

For an entity named `Task` with an `updateStatus` command:

```
src/routes/
  tasks/
    +server.ts         # sveltekit.server — GET /tasks, POST /tasks
    +page.server.ts    # sveltekit.load — load + actions
    commands/
      update-status/
        +server.ts     # sveltekit.command — POST /tasks/update-status
src/lib/
  manifest-client.ts   # sveltekit.client — invokeManifestCommand()
src/lib/
  manifest-types.ts    # sveltekit.types — interfaces
```

## Type mapping

The projection maps Manifest IR types to TypeScript using `irTypeToTs`: `string` to `string`; `number`/`integer`/`int` to `number`; `boolean`/`bool` to `boolean`; `date`/`datetime`/`uuid`/`email`/`url`/`uri`/`decimal` to `string`; `any` to `unknown`; `object` to `Record<string, unknown>`. Generic `array<T>` becomes `T[]` and `map<V>` becomes `Record<string, V>`. Nullable types append ` | null`.

## Options

~~`SvelteKitProjectionOptions` accepts `authProvider` (`'lucia'` | `'auth-js'` | `'custom'` | `'none'`, default `'lucia'`), `authImportPath`, `runtimeImportPath` (default `./lib/manifest-runtime`), `runtimeFactoryName` (default `createManifestRuntime`), `basePath` (default `/api`), `includeTenantContext` (default `true`), `tenantIdProperty` (default `tenantId`), `includeSoftDeleteFilter` (default `false`), `publicReads` (default `false`), `validationImportPath` (default unset), `databaseImportPath` (for direct database reads), `dispatcherImportPath`, and `includeComments` (default `true`).~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Real defaults from `SVELTEKIT_DEFAULTS`: `authProvider`
> `'lucia'`; `authImportPath` `$lib/server/auth`; `runtimeImportPath`
> `$lib/server/manifest-runtime`; `runtimeFactoryName` `createManifestRuntime`;
> `databaseImportPath` `$lib/server/database`; `routesDir` `src/routes`;
> `includeTenantFilter` **`true`** (not `includeTenantContext`); `includeSoftDeleteFilter`
> **`true`**; `emitFormActions` / `emitTypeImports` / `emitCompanions` **`true`**. There is **no**
> `basePath`, `publicReads`, or `dispatcherImportPath` option. `validationImportPath` exists and
> is optional.

## Notes & limitations

~~<Note>
All writes flow through `runtime.runCommand()`, preserving guard, policy, and constraint enforcement. Reads may bypass the runtime via direct Prisma-style `database.<entity>.findMany()` calls when the `databaseImportPath` option is configured, which improves read performance at the cost of bypassing Manifest-level read policies.
</Note>~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Generated SvelteKit handlers call a **phantom**
> entity-first form
> `runtime.runCommand(entityName, commandName, { params, instanceId })`. The default companion
> from `generateRuntimeFactoryModule` returns a raw `RuntimeEngine`, whose API is
> `runCommand(commandName, input, { entityName, instanceId })` — **not** entity-first. Unlike
> Express/Hono, SvelteKit does **not** emit an adapting facade. Treat generated write call sites
> as scaffolding that must be aligned with the engine (or a hand-written facade) before use.
> Reads may still bypass the runtime via `database.<entity>.findMany()` when
> `databaseImportPath` is set.

Form actions decode `event.request.formData()` and extract `instanceId` from form fields. The `+page.server.ts` returns typed `data` through SvelteKit's generated `./$types`, providing full type safety from server to client. The output is deterministic: the same IR and options always produce the same generated code.


---

## wiring

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/projections/wiring

---
title: 'Product wiring projection'
sidebarTitle: 'Product wiring'
description: 'Machine-readable command wiring contracts and safe bindings so apps wire UI to Manifest without guessing — not a UI generator.'
---

The **wiring** projection emits a deterministic product-wiring contract and TypeScript binding helpers from compiled IR. Use it so applications and agents connect real UI to Manifest commands without inventing types, lifecycle states, or client-side identity fields.

> **Verified (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Registry name `wiring`; CLI
> `manifest generate -p wiring --surface all`, plus `manifest wiring-inspect` /
> `manifest wiring-remediate`. Package pin **3.6.41**.

This is **not** a UI generator. It does not create pages, forms, buttons, layouts, or copy. Your app still owns presentation.

## Generate

```bash
manifest generate -p wiring --surface all
```

Default artifacts:

- `src/generated/manifest-wiring-contract.json`
- `src/generated/manifest-wiring-bindings.ts`

## What the contract answers

For every entity command:

- Exact parameter types (including `string[]` vs `string`)
- Required vs optional
- Enum / finite values when statically known
- Numeric and non-empty constraints when statically known
- Which fields are **server-owned** (`from context.*`) vs client-owned
- Dispatcher route identity
- Proven lifecycle transitions (from `transition` + literal `mutate`)
- List/detail invalidation hints after success
- Structured error states to handle

Unprovable metadata is omitted — never guessed.

## Trusted server-owned inputs

```manifest
command markCompleted(completedBy: string from context.actorId) {
  mutate completedBy = completedBy
}
```

- Client input types omit `completedBy`
- Runtime strips spoofed client values and injects `context.actorId`
- Missing trusted context fails with `MISSING_TRUSTED_CONTEXT`

Ownership is declared explicitly. Naming heuristics are not used.

## Automatic consumer inspection

Manifest does **not** design the interface. It proves whether application code correctly consumes declared capabilities.

```bash
manifest wiring-inspect \
  --contract src/generated/manifest-wiring-contract.json \
  --root apps/app \
  --root apps/api \
  --strict
```

Primary coverage comes from source inspection (generated client calls, `executeCommand`, server actions → `runManifestCommand`, API route chains, imported helpers). Explicit registries are overrides for `backend-only` / `deferred` / accepted ambiguous cases.

Default CI fails on stale consumers and proven contract mismatches. Unwired capabilities fail only with `--strict-coverage`. Ambiguous findings never fail by default.

Repo-wide scans are supported: Capsule-Pro-scale roots complete in a few seconds with indexed import resolution and parallel loading. Include filters are optional for debugging, not required for the gate.

See the full reference in the repo: `docs/projections/wiring.md`.

## Automatic remediation

Manifest does **not** design the UI. When the correct integration is derivable from Manifest truth and an existing product surface, Manifest plans and applies a deterministic repair:

```bash
manifest wiring-remediate --contract src/generated/manifest-wiring-contract.json \
  --root apps/app --root apps/api --mode one-defect
```

Modes: `plan`, `dry-run`, `apply`, `one-defect`. Auto-fixable findings are patched via AST-aware edits and re-inspected. Ambiguous product placement is never auto-edited. `add-required-input` applies only when a unique proven in-scope value source exists (never invented defaults). Failed verification discards the patch.

## Boundary

| Manifest                                     | Application                                    |
| -------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| Command truth, types, ownership, constraints | Layout, visuals, labels                        |
| Safe bindings + invalidation metadata        | Button vs menu vs gesture                      |
| Coverage truth + deterministic wiring repair | Explicit consumer registry / product placement |

Full reference: [docs/projections/wiring.md](https://github.com/Angriff36/Manifest/blob/main/docs/projections/wiring.md) in the repository.


---

## zod

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/projections/zod

---
title: 'Zod validation schema projection'
sidebarTitle: 'Zod'
description: 'Generate Zod validation schemas from Manifest IR — one z.object() per entity and command, with constraint-derived refinements and type coercions.'
---

The Zod projection generates Zod validation schemas from compiled Manifest IR — one `z.object()` per entity and per command parameter set, with constraint-derived refinements and type coercions. Use it for runtime validation in any TypeScript environment without coupling to a specific framework.

## What it generates

The projection registers under the name `zod` and declares three surfaces, `['zod.entity', 'zod.command', 'zod.schemas']`.

`zod.entity` emits a schema file per entity (`id: zod.entity.<Entity>`, content type `typescript`, written at `schemas/<Entity>.schema.ts`). `zod.command` emits a schema file per command (`id: zod.command.<command>`, written at `schemas/<command>.schema.ts`). `zod.schemas` emits a single combined module containing all schemas (`id: 'zod.schemas'`, written at `schemas/manifest-schemas.ts`).

Each entity schema is a `z.object()` with constraint refinements applied. When enabled, the projection also emits an `<Entity>ComputedSchema` that extends the base schema with the entity's computed properties, and `export type X = z.infer<typeof XSchema>` type aliases.

## Usage

```ts
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
import { getProjection } from '@angriff36/manifest/projections';

const { ir } = await compileToIR(source);

const projection = getProjection('zod');
const result = projection?.generate(ir, {
  surface: 'zod.schemas',
  options: { emitTypes: true, zodImportPath: 'zod' },
});

const code = result?.artifacts[0]?.code;
```

`ZodProjection` can also be imported directly from `@angriff36/manifest/projections/zod`, or dispatched through the CLI by name:

```bash
manifest generate ir/recipe.ir.json --projection zod --surface zod.schemas --output src/schemas/manifest-schemas.ts
```

## Type mapping & behavior

~~IR types map to Zod expressions through `TYPE_MAP`. `string` and `text` become `z.string()`; `boolean`/`bool` become `z.boolean()`; `number`, `float`, `decimal`, and `money` become `z.number()`; `int`/`integer` become `z.number().int()`; `bigint` becomes `z.bigint()`; `date`/`datetime` become `z.coerce.date()`; `uuid` becomes `z.string().uuid()`; `email` becomes `z.string().email()`; `url`/`uri` become `z.string().url()`; `json` and `any` become `z.unknown()`; `bytes` becomes `z.instanceof(Uint8Array)`; and `object` becomes `z.record(z.unknown())`. Arrays and maps with a generic recurse into `z.array(...)` and `z.record(...)`. An unknown type emits a `ZOD_UNKNOWN_TYPE` warning and falls back to `z.unknown()`.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** Same base map, plus: `timestamp` aliases `datetime`
> (`z.coerce.date()`); `time` → regex-validated `z.string()`; `duration` → `z.number()`;
> `list<T>` aliases `array<T>`; IR enum type names resolve to `z.enum([...])` from `ir.enums`
> (member **names** as string literals). Value objects inline as `z.object({...})`. Truly unknown
> types still emit `ZOD_UNKNOWN_TYPE` → `z.unknown()` (`src/manifest/projections/zod/generator.ts`).

Note that, unlike [Prisma](/integration/prisma) and [Drizzle](/projections/drizzle), the Zod projection maps bare `number` to `z.number()` without an ambiguity diagnostic — Zod has no distinct integer-versus-decimal concern at this layer. Constraints on an entity are analyzed (via `src/manifest/constraint-analysis.ts`) and emitted as `.min()` / `.max()` refinement chains.

## Options

The options object is `ZodProjectionOptions` in `zod/types.ts`. All four options default to enabled or a sensible string: `emitTypes` (default true) emits `z.infer` type aliases, `emitComputedSchemas` (default true) emits the `<Entity>ComputedSchema` variants for entities that have computed properties, `zodImportPath` (default `zod`) sets the import source, and `emitHeader` (default true) prepends a generation-metadata comment.

## Notes & limitations

<Note>
  Computed-property schemas are emitted only when both `emitComputedSchemas` is enabled and the
  entity actually declares computed properties.
</Note>

The behavior documented here comes directly from `src/manifest/projections/zod/generator.ts` and `types.ts`.


---

## Interactive project setup (creates manifest.config.yaml)

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/quickstart

---
title: 'Quickstart: install Manifest and run your first command'
sidebarTitle: 'Quickstart'
description: 'Install @angriff36/manifest, write your first .manifest file, compile it to IR, and execute a command with RuntimeEngine in under five minutes.'
---

This guide walks you from a blank project to a running Manifest program. You will install the package, write a `.manifest` source file, compile it to IR, and call `RuntimeEngine.runCommand()` to see the result. All code examples use real APIs from the package — nothing is invented.

<Steps>

<Step title="Install the package">

~~Add `@angriff36/manifest` to your project. Node.js 18 or later is required.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** `package.json` `engines.node` is `>=20`. Use Node.js 20 or later.

<CodeGroup>

```bash npm
npm install @angriff36/manifest
```

```bash pnpm
pnpm add @angriff36/manifest
```

</CodeGroup>

</Step>

<Step title="Write your first .manifest file">

Create `my-program.manifest` in your project root. This entity models a simple to-do item with two commands and an authorization policy.

```manifest
entity Todo {
  property id: string
  property title: string = ""
  property completed: boolean = false
  property createdAt: number = 0

  command create(newTitle: string) {
    guard newTitle != null and newTitle != ""
    guard length(newTitle) < 100

    mutate title = newTitle
    mutate completed = false
    mutate createdAt = now()
  }

  command complete() {
    guard self.completed == false

    mutate completed = true
    emit TodoCompleted { todoId: self.id }
  }

  policy AdminRead read: user.role == "admin"
  policy Authenticated execute: user.id != null "Authentication required"
}
```

Each `command` defines a named business operation. The `guard` lines gate execution — if any guard evaluates to `false`, the command stops immediately and reports which guard failed. `mutate` assigns a bare property name; expressions read the current instance through `self.*`. The `policy` lines are entity-level authorization rules — each has a name, a scope (`read`, `write`, `execute`, `all`, or `override`), an expression, and an optional denial message — and they are enforced before guards even run.

</Step>

<Step title="Compile the source to IR">

Use the `compileToIR` export to parse the source and produce an IR object. Always check diagnostics before proceeding — compilation errors are surfaced here, not thrown as exceptions.

```typescript
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';
import { readFileSync } from 'fs';

const source = readFileSync('my-program.manifest', 'utf-8');

const { ir, diagnostics } = await compileToIR(source);

if (diagnostics.some((d) => d.severity === 'error')) {
  console.error('Compilation errors:');
  diagnostics.forEach((d) => console.error(`  ${d.message}`));
  process.exit(1);
}

console.log('Compiled IR:', JSON.stringify(ir, null, 2));
```

You can also compile inline source directly — useful for tests and prototyping:

```typescript
import { compileToIR } from '@angriff36/manifest/ir-compiler';

const source = `
  entity Todo {
    property id: string
    property title: string
  }
`;

const { ir, diagnostics } = await compileToIR(source);
```

<Note>
  The IR is plain JSON. You can serialize it to disk, cache it, or pass it to any downstream
  consumer — including the `RuntimeEngine`, projections, and conformance tooling.
</Note>

</Step>

<Step title="Execute a command with RuntimeEngine">

Import `RuntimeEngine` from the package root and construct it with your compiled IR and a runtime context. `user.*` bindings in guards and policies resolve from `context.user` (an object with at least an `id`; `role` and other keys are up to you), and `context.tenantId` scopes tenant-aware commands.

```typescript
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, {
  user: { id: 'user-123', role: 'admin' },
  tenantId: 'tenant-456',
});

// Run the "create" command on the Todo entity.
// Signature: runCommand(commandName, input, { entityName })
const createResult = await runtime.runCommand(
  'create',
  { newTitle: 'Learn Manifest' },
  { entityName: 'Todo' },
);

if (!createResult.success) {
  console.error('Failed:', createResult.guardFailure ?? createResult.error);
  process.exit(1);
}

console.log('Created:', createResult.instance);
```

When the command succeeds, `createResult.instance` contains the resulting entity state. When it fails — because a guard evaluated to `false` or a policy denied access — inspect `createResult.guardFailure` (and `policyDenial`, `constraintOutcomes`, etc.) to see exactly which check failed and what values were resolved. `CommandResult` has no `diagnostics` field.

</Step>

<Step title="Chain a second command and inspect events">

Use the instance returned by `create` to run `complete`. Check `completeResult.emittedEvents` to see the `TodoCompleted` event that the command emitted.

```typescript
const completeResult = await runtime.runCommand(
  'complete',
  {},
  { entityName: 'Todo', instanceId: createResult.instance.id },
);

if (completeResult.success) {
  console.log('Events:', completeResult.emittedEvents);
  // Output: Events: [{ name: 'TodoCompleted', channel: 'TodoCompleted',
  //   payload: { todoId: '...', ... }, subject: { entity: 'Todo', ... }, ... }]
}
```

You can also subscribe to events as they are emitted. `runtime.onEvent(listener)` receives every event (filter by `event.name` yourself); `runtime.subscribe(entityName, listener)` receives only events whose subject is that entity. Both return an unsubscribe function.

```typescript
const unsubscribe = runtime.onEvent((event) => {
  if (event.name !== 'TodoCompleted') return;
  console.log('Todo completed:', event.payload.todoId);

  // Trigger side effects
  sendEmail(event.payload.todoId);
  updateAnalytics(event.payload.todoId);
});
```

</Step>

</Steps>

## Running the CLI instead

If you prefer a command-line workflow, use the `manifest` CLI that ships with the package:

```bash
# Interactive project setup (creates manifest.config.yaml)
manifest init

# Config-driven whole project (preferred after init)
manifest compile --all
manifest generate --all
# or: manifest build --all / manifest watch --all

# Single-file compile / one-shot build still work
manifest compile my-program.manifest --output ir/
manifest build my-program.manifest --projection nextjs --code-output app/api/
```

<Tip>
  Run `manifest init` once to create a `manifest.config.yaml`. After that, prefer
  `manifest build --all` (or `compile --all` + `generate --all`) so every configured projection
  stays in sync — verified CLI flags @ v3.6.41.
</Tip>

## Troubleshooting

~~**"Entity not found in IR"** — Entity names are case-sensitive. Make sure the name you pass to `runCommand` exactly matches the `entity` declaration in your source file.~~

> **Correction (2026-07-15):** `runCommand`'s first argument is the **command** name. Pass the
> entity as `options.entityName` (case-sensitive match to the `entity` declaration).

**"Guard failed but no detail"** — Check that you aren't silently catching errors before inspecting `result.guardFailure`. The runtime populates it for every guard failure, including the guard index, the formatted expression, and resolved sub-expression values.

**Compilation errors after a small change** — Conformance tests treat compilation output as executable semantics. If something stops compiling, you've changed language meaning. Review your source against the language specification.

## Next steps

- **[Installation](/installation)** — full install options, all package exports, TypeScript configuration, and CLI setup
- **[Language reference](/language/entities)** — complete specification for entities, commands, guards, policies, events, and stores
- **[Integration patterns](/integration/overview)** — how to use projections and the embedded runtime together in a real application


---

## troubleshooting

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/troubleshooting

---
title: 'Troubleshoot common Manifest issues'
description: "Diagnose and fix the most common errors you'll encounter when compiling, running, or generating code with the Manifest SDK and CLI."
sidebarTitle: 'Troubleshooting'
---

The issues below cover the most common problems reported when working with Manifest — from CLI configuration mismatches to runtime guard inspection and Windows-specific path bugs. Each entry describes what you see, why it happens, and how to fix it.

<AccordionGroup>

  <Accordion title='"Entity not found in IR"'>
    **What you see**

    An error like `Entity 'todo' not found` or `Entity X not found` when compiling or running a command.

    **Why it happens**

    Entity names in Manifest are case-sensitive. If your source defines `entity Todo` but your code references `'todo'` or `'TODO'`, the lookup fails.

    **How to fix it**

    Check the exact casing of the entity name in your `.manifest` source file. Then confirm the entity is present in the compiled IR:

    ```typescript
    // Print all entity names from the compiled IR
    console.log(result.ir.entities.map(e => e.name));
    ```

    Make sure the name you pass to `runCommand` matches exactly.

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title='"Guard failed but no diagnostic"'>
    **What you see**

    A command returns `success: false` but you cannot find any diagnostic information. Accessing `result.diagnostics` returns `undefined`.

    **Why it happens**

    `CommandResult` does not have a `diagnostics` field. Guard failure details are returned in `result.guardFailure`. Policy denial details are in `result.policyDenial`. General errors are in `result.error`. Inspecting the wrong field produces nothing.

    **How to fix it**

    Check the correct fields on `CommandResult`:

    ```typescript
    const result = await runtime.runCommand('create', input, { entityName: 'Todo' });

    if (!result.success) {
      if (result.guardFailure) {
        console.error('Guard failed:', {
          index: result.guardFailure.index,
          expression: result.guardFailure.formatted,
          resolved: result.guardFailure.resolved
        });
      } else if (result.policyDenial) {
        console.error('Policy denied:', {
          policy: result.policyDenial.policyName,
          message: result.policyDenial.message
        });
      } else {
        console.error('Error:', result.error);
      }
      return;
    }

    console.log('Success:', result.result);
    ```

    <Note>
      The runtime does not emit events like `runtime.on('guardFailed')`. All diagnostic information is returned in the `CommandResult` object returned by `runCommand`.
    </Note>

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title='"No .manifest files found"'>
    **What you see**

    Running `manifest compile` or `manifest build` prints `No .manifest files found` and exits without producing any output.

    **Why it happens**

    The CLI looks for `.manifest` files using either the `src` glob in `manifest.config.yaml` or a default discovery pattern. If neither is configured and no path is passed explicitly, no files are found.

    **How to fix it**

    Pass the source path directly:

    ```bash
    manifest compile path/to/your/file.manifest
    ```

    Or set the `src` glob in your config file:

    ```yaml
    # manifest.config.yaml
    src: "modules/**/*.manifest"
    output: "ir/"
    ```

    Then run without arguments:

    ```bash
    manifest compile
    ```

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title='"Cannot find module" errors during manifest compile'>
    **What you see**

    The compile command starts and then fails with:

    ```text
    Cannot find module .../dist/manifest/parser imported from .../dist/manifest/ir-compiler.js
    ```

    **Why it happens**

    You are on a build where ESM relative imports were emitted without `.js` file extensions. Node's ESM resolver requires explicit extensions and cannot fall back to bare specifiers.

    **How to fix it**

    Upgrade to a version where the runtime ESM imports use explicit `.js` extensions (`./parser.js`, `./lexer.js`, `./ir-cache.js`, and so on). Then verify the fix:

    ```bash
    pnpm exec manifest compile
    ```

    Expected result: compile runs across discovered manifests without module-resolution errors.

    If you are working inside the Manifest repo itself, make sure you have built the library before running the CLI:

    ```bash
    npm run build
    pnpm exec manifest compile
    ```

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Windows: manifest exits 0 with no output">
    **What you see**

    On Windows, any `manifest` CLI command exits with code `0` and prints nothing — no help text, no error, no output.

    **Why it happens**

    The CLI uses direct-run detection to avoid double-execution when invoked through pnpm shims. Before version 0.3.10, the detection compared normalized (but not resolved) paths. On Windows, the pnpm shim path and the `.pnpm` real target path differ, so the comparison always failed — the CLI detected itself as a passthrough and exited silently.

    This is fixed in **0.3.10**. The CLI now compares normalized **realpaths** and performs a case-insensitive comparison on Windows.

    **How to fix it**

    Upgrade to the latest release (current package pin for this audit: **3.6.41**; the Windows shim fix has shipped since `0.3.10`):

    ```bash
    pnpm add @angriff36/manifest@latest
    ```

    ~~If you are still on a pre-0.3.10 build, treat silent exit-0 as the known Windows shim bug.~~

    > **Note (2026-07-15) @RYANSIGNED:** `@angriff36/manifest` requires **Node.js >= 20** (`package.json` engines). Historical `0.3.x` version numbers below describe when CLI bugs were fixed; they are not current release pins.

    If you cannot upgrade immediately, use one of these workarounds to verify the CLI is reachable:

    ```bash
    pnpm exec manifest --help
    node .\node_modules\@angriff36\manifest\packages\cli\dist\index.js --help
    .\node_modules\.bin\manifest.cmd --help
    ```

    Each of these should print the Manifest help text. Use whichever form works in your environment until you can upgrade.

    <Warning>
      `manifest init --force` is interactive and requires terminal input. In non-interactive or headless shells it may wait for prompts or exit without rewriting the config file. Run it in an interactive terminal.
    </Warning>

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title='TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading "output") in manifest compile'>
    **What you see**

    Running `pnpm exec manifest compile` fails around line 48 of `packages/cli/dist/index.js` with:

    ```text
    TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'output')
    ```

    **Why it happens**

    You are on a build where Commander argument declarations were duplicated — the same argument was registered in both `.command(...)` and `.argument(...)`. The duplicate caused the options object to be `undefined` when the action handler ran.

    **How to fix it**

    Upgrade to a version where command signatures no longer duplicate arguments and action handlers use safe option defaults. Verify the fix by checking the command shapes:

    ```bash
    pnpm exec manifest --help
    ```

    Expected command shapes after the fix:

    ```text
    compile [options] [source]
    generate [options] <ir>
    build [options] [source]
    validate [options] [ir]
    ```

    If `compile` still shows the argument listed twice, you are on an older build. Upgrade the package and rebuild.

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Constraint is not blocking execution">
    **What you see**

    A constraint you defined is violated, but the command succeeds and the entity is persisted anyway.

    **Why it happens**

    The constraint's severity level controls whether a violation halts execution. Only `block` severity stops the command. If the severity is `warn` or `ok`, a violation is recorded but execution continues.

    **How to fix it**

    Check the severity declaration in your `.manifest` source. If you want the constraint to halt execution, make sure it uses the default severity (`block`) or explicitly sets it:

    ```manifest
    constraint titleNotEmpty {
      severity: block
      expression: this.title is not empty
    }
    ```

    After a successful command, check `result.constraintOutcomes` filtered by `severity: 'warn'` to see any warning-level violations that were recorded but did not block:

    ```typescript
    const warnings = result.constraintOutcomes?.filter(o => !o.passed && o.severity === 'warn');
    if (warnings?.length) {
      console.warn('Constraint warnings:', warnings);
    }
    ```

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Read policies are not being enforced">
    **What you see**

    You defined a `policy ReadOnly read: ...` rule on an entity, but the policy is never enforced — reads succeed regardless of the policy condition.

    **Why it happens**

    `read`-scoped policies ARE enforced by the runtime — but only at the engine's read gate (`getInstance` / `getAllInstances`). If your reads bypass the engine — for example generated Next.js GET routes that query the database directly — they are not gated. The policy is enforced only when a read flows through `runtime.getInstance` / `runtime.getAllInstances`.

    **How to fix it**

    Route reads through the engine's `getInstance` / `getAllInstances` to have `read` (or `all`) policies enforced (a denied read returns `undefined` / omits the row). If you instead want a policy enforced during command execution, use the `execute` or `all` scope. Policies are entity-level declarations with the form `policy Name scope: expression`:

    ```manifest
    entity Resource {
      property required title: string
      property createdBy: string = ""

      policy AdminOnly execute: user.role == "admin"
      policy OwnerAll all: user.id == self.createdBy

      command update(newTitle: string) {
        mutate title = newTitle
      }
    }

    store Resource in memory
    ```

    `execute` is enforced during `runCommand`. `all` is enforced during command execution AND at the read gate. `read` is enforced at the read gate only.

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Generated Next.js routes don't work">
    **What you see**

    Running the generated route handler returns a 500 error, a module-not-found error, or silently fails with no output.

    **Why it happens**

    Generated routes import helper modules from paths configured during `manifest init` (or passed via CLI flags). If those files don't exist at the expected paths — for example `@/lib/manifest-runtime`, `@/lib/database`, or `@/lib/manifest-response` — the route fails at import time.

    **How to fix it**

    Verify all three lib files exist in your project:

    - `lib/manifest-runtime.ts` — wraps `RuntimeEngine`
    - `lib/database.ts` — exports your database client
    - `lib/manifest-response.ts` — exports `manifestSuccessResponse` and `manifestErrorResponse`

    If `manifest-response.ts` is missing, create it:

    ```typescript
    // lib/manifest-response.ts
    export function manifestSuccessResponse(data: unknown, status = 200) {
      return Response.json(data, { status });
    }

    export function manifestErrorResponse(
      message: string | object,
      status = 400
    ) {
      return Response.json(
        typeof message === 'string' ? { error: message } : message,
        { status }
      );
    }
    ```

    For workspace monorepos, confirm the import paths in `manifest.config.yaml` match your actual package names (for example `@repo/manifest/runtime` instead of `@/lib/manifest-runtime`).

    You can also verify the entity exists in the compiled IR before regenerating:

    ```bash
    pnpm exec manifest compile modules/recipe.manifest
    cat ir/recipe.ir.json | grep -o '"name":"[^"]*"'
    ```

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title='"Unsupported storage target" error'>
    **What you see**

    The runtime throws or returns an error like `Store not supported` or `Unsupported storage target`.

    **Why it happens**

    The runtime only supports built-in targets that are explicitly configured. If you reference a target that has not been set up — or pass an unrecognized string — the runtime emits a diagnostic instead of silently falling back to memory.

    **How to fix it**

    `memory` and browser `localStorage` are built in. `postgres`, `supabase`,
    `durable`, and custom targets require a provider; a database URL alone is
    not a provider.

    For custom databases, implement the `Store` interface and pass it via `storeProvider`:

    ```typescript
    import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';

    const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(
      ir,
      { actorId: 'user-123' },
      {
        storeProvider: (entityName) => {
          if (entityName === 'Todo') return new MyCustomTodoStore();
          return undefined;
        },
      },
    );
    ```

    Returning `undefined` uses built-in behavior only for `memory` and browser
    `localStorage`. Generated companions load providers from
    `manifest.config.ts` through `runtimeConfigImport`. See the
    [complete setup](/language/stores#complete-generated-companion-postgresql-setup).

  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title='manifest validate reports "Missing required field: metadata" or "Schema not found"'>
    **What you see**

    Running `manifest validate` fails with either:

    ```text
    Missing required field: metadata
    ```

    or:

    ```text
    Schema not found at docs/spec/ir/ir-v1.schema.json
    ```

    **Why it happens**

    You are running a stale global CLI binary (pre-0.3.23). The old `validate` command resolved the schema relative to `process.cwd()`, which only works inside the Manifest repo itself — consumer projects don't have that path. The old validator also ran hardcoded validation against a `metadata` field that does not exist in the IR spec. The IR itself is valid; the CLI was wrong.

    **How to fix it**

    Stop using the global binary and use `pnpm exec manifest` instead, which resolves from the installed package:

    ```bash
    pnpm exec manifest validate path/to/output.ir.json
    ```

    If you are on an old version, upgrade to the latest release:

    ```bash
    pnpm add @angriff36/manifest@latest
    pnpm exec manifest validate path/to/output.ir.json
    ```

    Since `0.3.23`, the schema is bundled inside the package and resolved relative to the CLI binary, not the working directory. Do not copy schema files, modify provenance, or create symlinks as workarounds.

    <Tip>
      Always use `pnpm exec manifest` (or `npx manifest`) rather than a globally installed binary. Run `pnpm exec manifest --version` to confirm the version matches the installed package.
    </Tip>

  </Accordion>

</AccordionGroup>

<Note>
  If your issue is not listed here, check the [FAQ](/faq) for answers about Manifest's design and
  semantics. To report a new issue, open a GitHub issue and include your `.manifest` source, the
  compiled IR, and any diagnostic output.
</Note>


---

## Just the companions

URL: https://manifest-b1e8623f.mintlify.app/whats-new

---
title: "What's new in 3.0"
sidebarTitle: "What's new (3.0)"
description: 'Manifest 3.0 makes generated backends runnable with zero hand-written glue: emitted companions, shipped outbox/job/schedule workers, an atomic transaction boundary, coherent routes and clients, webhooks, and a runtime event bus. Includes breaking changes.'
---

> **Note (2026-07-15, updated 2026-07-22):** This page is a **3.0 release note**, not the
> current package pin. Repo / npm `@angriff36/manifest` is **v3.6.41** at verification
> time. Prefer `manifest compile --all` / `generate --all` / `build --all` /
> `watch --all` for config-driven projects (those flags remain valid).
>
> **3.6.36+ language:** cross-entity fan-out foreach-create —
> `on Event fanOut Source where … run Target.create` — is documented under
> [Reactions → Foreach-create](/language/reactions#foreach-create-cross-entity-fan-out).
>
> **3.6.38–3.6.41 language:** natural-key upsert (`match … else create`) —
> single-target lookup-or-allocate and fan-out per-row lookup-or-allocate reactions —
> is documented under [Reactions → Natural-key upsert](/language/reactions#natural-key-upsert-match-else-create).
> Also includes Convex identity-filter fix for `db.get`, else-create allocate, and soft-delete exclusion.

Manifest 3.0 closes the gap between "code was generated" and "the backend actually
runs." Where earlier releases emitted types and route bodies but left you to
hand-write the runtime factory, response helpers, drain loops, and worker wiring,
3.0 ships those pieces — and makes the generated routes, clients, and schemas agree
on one contract.

<Note>
  This release contains **breaking changes** to generated output (zod schema names, the routes
  projection's default paths, hono/express `basePath`, and the react-query command envelope). See
  [Breaking changes](#breaking-changes) before upgrading.
</Note>

## Docs are now gated against the compiler

~~Every ` ```manifest ` snippet in the published docs is compiled by the real
compiler in CI (`scripts/check-doc-snippets.mjs`), internal links are resolved
(`check-doc-links.mjs`), and page frontmatter and spec headers are enforced
(`check-doc-metadata.mjs`, `check-spec-integrity.mjs`). Doc examples that stop
compiling now fail the build, so what you read here matches what the compiler
accepts.~~

**Correction (2026-07-15):** CI does run `pnpm run docs:check` (metadata + links +
spec integrity + snippets) via `.github/workflows/ci.yml`. Scope and limits:

- **`check-doc-snippets.mjs`** compiles ` ```manifest ` blocks with the real
  compiler (`compileToIR` via jiti) for `mintlify/**` plus selected `docs/`
  trees — **not** every snippet, and **not** all docs. ` ```manifest fragment `
  blocks are skipped; ` ```manifest invalid ` blocks must fail to compile.
  `docs/internal` and `docs/spec` are out of snippet scope. TypeScript /
  runtime API examples are **not** typechecked.
- **`check-doc-links.mjs`** resolves internal markdown link *paths* only
  (anchors and external URLs are ignored).
- **`check-doc-metadata.mjs`** requires Mintlify `title` frontmatter and the
  codedocs AUTO-GENERATED banner — not full frontmatter on every page, and not
  YAML frontmatter on `docs/spec/`.
- **`check-spec-integrity.mjs`** enforces plaintext headers (`Last updated`,
  `Status`, `Authority`) and relative links under `docs/spec/*.md`.

So the gate keeps standalone Manifest source examples honest for compile
errors; it does **not** guarantee that everything you read matches the
compiler or runtime API.

## Generated companions: zero hand-written files

The five server projections — **Next.js, Hono, Express, Remix, and SvelteKit** —
now emit the modules their generated route code imports, so a fresh `generate`
produces code that compiles with no hand-written files. Emission is on by default
(`emitCompanions: true`).

For the Next.js projection the companions surface writes:

- **Runtime factory** — `createManifestRuntime` (default `@/lib/manifest-runtime`), which embeds the compiled IR and constructs a `RuntimeEngine` with your `RuntimeOptions`.
- **HTTP envelope helpers** — `manifestSuccessResponse`, `manifestErrorResponse`, and `normalizeCommandResult` (default `@/lib/manifest-response`).
- **Database client**, **auth stub**, and **tenant lookup helper** companions when the generated routes need them.

Generate them explicitly, or as part of a normal build:

```bash
# Just the companions
manifest generate ir/app.ir.json --projection nextjs --surface companions --output app/api/

# Or the whole surface set (companions included)
manifest generate ir/app.ir.json --projection nextjs --surface all --output app/api/
```

To keep your own implementations instead, set `emitCompanions: false` — the
projection then emits nothing and leaves your hand-written modules untouched.

## Drains, jobs, cron, and idempotency ship as workers

You no longer hand-write the loops that drive durable delivery and async work.

- **Outbox drain** — `runOutboxWorker(store, deliver)` (a polling worker) and `drainOutboxOnce(store, deliver)` (one pass) from `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/worker` own the claim/deliver/mark loop; pass your publish function as `deliver`. A Redis-backed outbox store is available at `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/redis`.
- **Async job queue** — `PostgresJobQueue` (`@angriff36/manifest/jobs/postgres`) is a durable `JobQueue`; `runJobWorker` / `drainJobsOnce` (`@angriff36/manifest/jobs/worker`) drain and execute enqueued async commands. Apply `src/manifest/jobs/stores/postgres.sql` once.
- **Schedules** — a real 5-field cron matcher backs `startScheduleWorker` and `runSchedulesOnce` (`@angriff36/manifest/schedule-worker`). On Vercel, the Next.js projection emits one cron route per **cron-kind** schedule plus maintenance routes for approval expiry and async-job draining, and a `vercel.json` that registers them — cron-kind schedules fire with no extra setup. `interval` and `every` schedule kinds are not emitted as Vercel cron routes; use `startScheduleWorker` from `@angriff36/manifest/schedule-worker` for those.
- **Idempotency** — `MemoryIdempotencyStore` (`@angriff36/manifest/idempotency/memory`) and `PostgresIdempotencyStore` (`@angriff36/manifest/idempotency/postgres`) record command results keyed by an `idempotencyKey` so retried commands do not double-execute.

See [Async commands](/language/async-commands) and the [Outbox adapter](/adapters/outbox).

## Atomic transaction boundary

Set a `transactionProvider` in `RuntimeOptions` and the engine wraps each command
attempt — the command body, its outbox enqueue, and its idempotency record — in a
single `withTransaction` call. Those writes now commit or roll back together; a
failed outbox enqueue rolls the mutation back and returns `OUTBOX_ENQUEUE_FAILED`
instead of silently persisting state without a durable event. `PostgresTransactionProvider`
ships at `@angriff36/manifest/transactions/postgres`.

```typescript
import { Pool } from 'pg';
import { RuntimeEngine } from '@angriff36/manifest';
import { PostgresOutboxStore } from '@angriff36/manifest/outbox/postgres';
import { PostgresTransactionProvider } from '@angriff36/manifest/transactions/postgres';

const pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });

const runtime = new RuntimeEngine(ir, context, {
  outboxStore: new PostgresOutboxStore({ pool }),
  transactionProvider: new PostgresTransactionProvider({ pool }),
});
```

Without a provider, the prior default behavior is unchanged: mutation and outbox
enqueue run on separate connections and the gap is logged, not enforced.

## Wiring, config, exports, and CLI

- **One shared route contract.** Routes, clients, zod schemas, and hono/express handlers now derive their paths, envelope keys, and schema names from a single contract module, so a client generated from the same `appDir` as its routes cannot target a stale prefix.
- **Plugins load.** Plugins declared in config now register their CLI commands, and a runtime seam threads plugin registries into `RuntimeOptions`. The config schema covers all projection targets.
- **New CLI subcommands** are registered: `analyze`, `seed`, `profile`, `pack`, `unpack`, and `generate-from-prompt`, alongside `manifest build --all` and `manifest generate --all` for config-driven, whole-project builds.
- **New export subpaths** back the features above: `@angriff36/manifest/outbox/worker`, `/jobs/postgres`, `/jobs/worker`, `/schedule-worker`, `/idempotency/memory`, `/idempotency/postgres`, `/transactions/postgres`, `/webhooks`, `/events`, `/events/redis`, `/outbox/redis`, and `/federation`.

## Route and client coherence

The projections that make up an HTTP surface now agree on one contract:

- The **routes** projection emits dispatcher-style command paths by default.
- **ts.client** emits typed command callers with configurable base paths.
- **react-query** command hooks call the dispatcher path using the raw command name, resolved through the shared contract.
- **zod** command-params schema names are now entity-prefixed so hono/express imports resolve against them.

## Webhooks execute

`handleWebhookRequest` (`@angriff36/manifest/webhooks`) turns an inbound HTTP
request into a Manifest command execution. It is **fail-closed**: HMAC signature
verification, idempotency, and the payload transform all run before any command
executes, and a missing or invalid signature is rejected. The Next.js, Hono, and
Express projections emit webhook routes (`--surface webhook`).

## Realtime and the event bus

The runtime now publishes committed events to a connected `EventBus` **after**
the transaction commits. `connectEventBus` wires a bus onto a `RuntimeEngine`;
`EventBus` and a Redis-backed implementation ship at `@angriff36/manifest/events`
and `@angriff36/manifest/events/redis`. The Next.js shared runtime wires the bus
automatically, and `realtime.requireEventBus` makes a missing bus a hard error
rather than a silent single-instance fallback (`--surface realtime`). See
[Realtime subscriptions](/extensibility/realtime-subscriptions).

## Breaking changes

<Warning>
  These change the shape of **generated code**. Regenerate all surfaces together (`manifest generate
  --all` or `manifest build --all`) so routes, clients, and schemas stay on the same contract, and
  review the diff before committing.
</Warning>

- **Zod schema names are entity-prefixed.** The zod projection now emits `${Entity}${Command}ParamsSchema` (e.g. `RecipeCreateParamsSchema`) instead of the un-prefixed `${Command}ParamsSchema` (e.g. `CreateParamsSchema`). Update any code importing the old names. This is what lets hono/express imports resolve against the emitted schemas.
- **The routes projection emits dispatcher paths by default.** Generated command paths now default to the dispatcher layout. If you depended on the previous default path shape, set your client and server to the same surface or update your callers.
- **hono/express `basePath` now applies.** The `basePath` option (default `/api`) is now prefixed onto emitted routes; previously it was effectively ignored. Routes generated with a non-default `basePath` will move.
- **react-query command envelope/paths changed.** Command hooks now target the dispatcher path with the raw command name via the shared contract; regenerate client and server together.

## Upgrading

1. Regenerate every surface from one build: `manifest build --all` (or `manifest generate --all`).
2. Review the diff, paying attention to zod schema imports, route paths, and `basePath`.
3. If you had hand-written a runtime factory or `manifest-response` helper, delete it and let the companions surface emit it — unless you intentionally keep `emitCompanions: false`.
4. To close the outbox transactional gap, configure a `transactionProvider`.


---
